text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ with PMH signficant for COPD, Behcets disease, and depression who presents from ___ s/p elective L TKR with a ___ anterior STEMI, s/p 1 DES, now receiving post-STEMI care and pain control/safe dispo plan for TKR. #) ANTERIOR STEMI: The patient presented from an OSH after elective L TKR with perioperative anterior STEMI with STE in V2-V3, s/p 1 DES. Risk factors include prior smoking history, family history of MI. Initial troponin was 1.12, and upon arrival to ___ was 0.27. The patient was initially started on ticagrelor but out of concern for bleeding risk with ASA + ticagrelor +lovenox (for knee prophylaxis), on ___ discontinued ticagrelor and the patient was loaded with 600 mg clopidogrel and subsequently continued on 75 mg daily instead of ticagrelor. She also was started on metoprolol and lisinopril. #) Systolic heart failure: The patient's TTE on ___ demonstrated akinesis of apex/distal anterior septum and hypokinesis of the basal mid anterior septum and distal inferior wall, with an EF 35-40%. She was started on metoprolol and started lisinopril on ___. She was started on furosemide 20mg daily to help manage her hyperkalemia of 5.4 on ACEi, but then she became mildly dehydrated and furosemide was stopped. However, over time she began to become volume overloaded and developed mild pulmonary edema and an oxygen requirement and received several doses of 20mg IV lasix and was discharged on oral lasix 20mg daily. Due to LV apical hypokinesis found on TTE, the patient was started on warfarin with a presumed course of 6 months. #) S/P L TKR: The patient underwent elective L total knee replacement ___. She was started on enoxaparin with a bridge to warfarin which should be continued for 6 months due to her cardiac condition (apical hypokinesis). For pain control was on PCA, converted overnight ___ to oxycodone. Per recommendations from her OSH orthopedist, the patient continued with physical therapy (weight bearing as tolerated). Physical therapy recommended rehab after hospital discharge. #) COPD: The pt is a former smoker, also has Behcet's disease which may cause obstructive pulmonary symptoms. On admission she was saturating 97% on 2L NC. She was continued on home albuterol, tiotropium, and fluticasone. #) DEPRESSION: The patient was continued on home escitalopram oxalate 20 mg daily. She developed some anxiety during hospitalization requiring lorazepam; however, due to waxing and waning mental status, this medication was discontinued. #) BEHCET'S DISEASE: Per OSH notes, the patient had a Behcet's flare 10 weeks ago that responds to azithromycin PRN. Behcet's disease may cause coronary arteritis, which may lead to MI. Her Behcet's disease was not an active issue during this hospitalization. #) ALTERED MENTAL STATUS: Patient had waxing and waning mental status which developed during her hospital stay. She appeared somnolent and was receiving lorazepam for anxiety and oxycodone for pain. Her lorazepam was stopped and her oxycodone was decreased in dose. Her mental status and level of consciousness improved significantly suggesting her altered mental status was due to medication side effects. She had a UA which was negative with culture pending at discharge and chest xray had no infiltrate so infectious causes were felt to be unlikely. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: -Please check Chem-7 and INR on ___. -Weight bearing as tolerated. -Anticipate less than 30 day stay -Monitor weights daily. If gains more than 3 pounds in 1 day or 5 pounds in 3 days then would start diuretics. Discharge weight 109kg standing with knee brace on -For any questions regarding post-op care of knee replacement call Dr. ___ ___
summary: You were admitted with a heart attack and a blockage was found in your left anterior coronary artery. A drug eluting stent was placed to open the artery and you have done well since the procedure. It is extremely important that you take aspirin and clopidogrel every day for the next year to prevent the stent from clotting off and causing another heart attack. Do not stop taking aspirin and clopidogrel for any reason unless Dr. ___ ___ you it is OK to do so. Your heart is weaker after the heart attack and it is possible that you will retain fluid. Check your weight daily and report a weight gain more than 3 pounds in 1 day or 5 pounds in 3 days to Dr. ___.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Mr. ___ is a ___ year old gentleman with a past medical history of CAD s/p CABG ___, aflutter, CHF, and poorly controlled diabetes who presented to an OSH with chest pain, found to have worsening CHF and concern for possible thrombus on TEE at OSH. Transferred to ___ where TEE did not show any thrombus, and patient underwent catheter-directed ablation, post-procedure course complicated by alternating junctional and sinus rhythms. # Atrial Fibrillation/Flutter: TEE prior to ablation demonstrated no thrombi. Patient underwent catheter-directed ablation on ___, with post-procedure course complicated by junctional bradycardia which improved within 24 hours. Metoprolol was held while patient was bradycardic. Patient was then in accelerated junctional rhythm and normal sinus rhythm, without any further marked bradycardic episodes and with stable vital signs. Patient was started on warfarin 5mg daily, and was maintained on heparin gtt, which was subsequently transitioned to Lovenox 90mg BID while bridging to therapeutic INR. Patient will follow-up with ___ and ___ for further warfarin dose adjustments. # Acute on Chronic Systolic heart failure: Previous echo notable for EF of 47%, but at OSH had worsening systolic heart failure with EF of ___. Concern for tachymyopathy secondary to atrial flutter/fibrillation. Patient appeared volume overloaded on physical examination and was subsequently treated with IV Lasix, and transitioned to his home dose of 60mg furosemide daily prior to discharge. Metoprolol was held post-ablation out of concern for bradycardic junctional rhythm, and was restarted at lower dose (50mg succinate XL daily) at the time of discharge. # CAD s/p CABG: Patient with a history of CAD, was chest pain free while hospitalized. Patient was maintained on aspirin and atorvastatin, and restarted on metoprolol succinate XL at 50mg daily (reduced dose) at the time of discharge. # ___: Patient had Cr bump to 2.4 on ___ (previous baseline 1.5-1.8), which improved with diuresis to baseline of 1.7 at the time of discharge. # Type II diabetes: Patient maintained on lantus and Humalog sliding scale while hospitalized. # Hyperlipidemia: Continued atorvastatin 80 mg daily, ezetimibe 10 mg daily TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: ==================== Discharge Weight: 92.9kg Discharge Cr: 1.7 # Atrial Fibrillation s/p Ablation: patient currently in normal sinus rhythm. - Cardiac Medications: Metoprolol succinate 50mg daily, atorvastatin 80mg QHS, ezetimibe 10mg daily, furosemide 60mg daily, warfarin 5mg daily, Lovenox 90mg BID (while INR subtherapeutic) # Warfarin: Patient was initiated on warfarin 5mg daily at recommendation of electrophysiology and was bridged with heparin gtt while subtherapeutic. Patient was discharged with Lovenox 90mg BID bridge with Warfarin 5mg. He will have repeat INR drawn on ___ when he follows up with ___ pharmacist. Rx for twice weekly INRs given to patient at the time of discharge. - INR to be drawn on ___ - Results to be sent to ___ (Fax ___, who will follow his INR Name: ___. Location: ___ PRIMARY CARE ___ Address: ___, ___ Phone: ___ Fax: ___ # Medication Changes: Started Lovenox 90mg BID bridge to therapeutic warfarin (Warfarin dosed at 5mg daily), switched to metoprolol succinate 50mg daily (previously tartrate 75mg BID). # CODE: Full (confirmed) # CONTACT: ___ (son) ___
summary: You were admitted to the hospital because you were having an abnormally fast heart rhythm (atrial fibrillation and atrial flutter) for which you underwent a procedure (ablation) to prevent your heart from going back into this arrhythmia. After the procedure, you had a briefly slow heart rate, which normalized over time while in the hospital. You were started on a medication called warfarin (Coumadin) which you will continue to take after discharge. You were also started on a medication called Lovenox, which is also a blood thinner. It is important that you take this medication until your warfarin level (called the INR) is therapeutic. It is very important that you follow-up with your outpatient providers, and follow their recommendations regarding dosing of these medications. Failure to do so can put you at higher risk of bleeding.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Mr. ___ is a ___ year old gentleman with history of non-ischemic cardiomyopathy (EF 20%), severe AS, DM and afib on digoxin admitted to CCU in cardiogenic shock, received IV diuresis and transferred to the ___ service, underwent TAVR. Course complicated by right foot hematoma (initially thought to be cellulitis), post-TAVR bradycardia, and asymptomatic hypotension. #Severe Aortic Stenosis s/p TAVR: Peak velocity 3.8 m/sec, peak/mean 54/36, Valve area 0.9 cm2. Symptomatic due to decompensated heart failure. He was declined for surgical AVR. TAVR was thus performed ___ and procedure technically went well. Post-op course was complicated by asymptomatic bradycardia to the ___'s with aberrant conduction, for which he was monitored in the CCU, but the bradycardia resolved without intervention. He was able to ambulate the halls without symptoms for over a week prior to discharge. He will follow up with Dr. ___ as an outpatient. #Acute on Chronic Heart Failure with Reduced EF #Cardiogenic Shock: Patient presented in cardiogenic shock, likely secondary to worsening heart failure (h/o non-ischemic cardiomyopathy) in the setting of not taking medications after loss of insurance, worsened by severe AS. He received Lasix drip and tailored diuresis with swan ganz catheter in CCU. Euvolemic upon transfer to the floor. He had several instances of asymptomatic hypotension which improved upon titration of his medications. He was ultimately transitioned to a regimen of torsemide 10mg qOD, lisinopril 2.5mg BID, and metoprolol succinate 25mg QHS, which he tolerated for several days without issue. #Atrial Fibrillation: Rates well controlled. Continued on metoprolol, digoxin. On warfarin prior to admission. Transitioned to heparin gtt for left atrial thrombus (see below) for anticoagulation leading up to TAVR procedure and required extended period of time for bridging back to warfarin. #Left Atrial Thrombus: Noted incidentally on TAVR workup CT. Anticoagulated with heparin gtt while briding to warfarin. #Insulin Dependent Diabetes: New diagnosis. ___ consult was obtained. Received diabetes teaching and will have followup outpatient at ___. Discharge regimen is Glargine 10 Units at Bedtime, 2u Humalog with meals, no sliding scale, no oral agents. Had excellent control of blood sugars with this regimen. #Right Foot Hematoma: Erythema noted on dorsum of R foot while on CTX for Klebsiella UTI. Started Vancomycin for presumptive MRSA cellulitis, continued ___, then Doxy ___, then Keflex for strep coverage ___. Overall it improved initially, but then a patch of erythema seemed increased from prior. Ultrasound was obtained and showed a complex fluid collection on ___, which was drained by ___ ___ with prompt improvement in symptoms, and was likely a hematoma based on bloody appearance. Gram stain was negative and preliminary cultures had no growth from the fluid collection. #UTI: Urine culture grew klebsiella. Treated with 7-day course of ceftriaxone. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES ===================== - Please draw INR ___ and fax results to Dr. ___ for ___ titration at ___ - Discharge INR: 2.4 - Discharge Warfarin plan: Warfarin 12.5 mg 4X/WEEK (___) Warfarin 15 mg 3X/WEEK (___) - Please note that this Warfarin plan is *SUBJECT TO CHANGE* based on subsequent INR values - Lisinopril increased to 2.5 mg BID. - Metoprolol Succinate 25 mg QHS started for heart failure; Metoprolol Tartrate 25 mg BID was discontinued. - Discharge Insulin regimen: Glargine 10 Units at Bedtime, 2u Humalog with meals. No sliding scale, no oral agents at this time. - Has f/u appointment in process of being scheduled with ___ for diabetes - Was previously being evaluated for AICD, likely still indicated, should be further discussed as outpatient - ___ weight: 114.0kg - Discharge diuretic: 10mg Torsemide every other day # Code - full
summary: You had lots of extra fluid in your body, and your blood pressure was quite low. You had severe aortic stenosis, a problem with your aortic valve. You were treated with various medicines to improve your blood pressure and to get the extra fluid out. You were evaluated by our heart surgeons, who felt that you would benefit from having your heart valve replaced. They decided to do a TAVR, or a procedure where the valve was replaced without having to do open-heart surgery. You were started on Insulin for diabetes. You had a fluid collection on your foot drained. It was a pocket of blood, and you received antibiotics for an infection of this as well. You restarted Warfarin to lower your risk of stroke from Atrial Fibrillation and a blood clot in your heart. Please weigh yourself every morning, first thing when you wake up, after you go to the bathroom.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ yo F with hx of HTN, HLD, newly diagnosed MDS, depression and dementia who presented with shortness of breath and lower extremity swelling found to have transudative effusion likely ___ to newly diagnosed CHF. . # Acute on chronic systolic CHF exacerbation: Patient presented with shortness of breath and found to have large right sided pleural effusion. She underwent a thoracentesis which revealed a transudative effusion. Cytology and cultures were negative. The cardiology team was consulted. She had a TTE which showed a mild symmetric LVH with moderate global hypokinesis (EF 35-40%) c/w diffuse process, 4+ TR, mild/mod MR, and pulmonary hypertension. Unclear etiology of new CHF. Ddx includes viral process vs tachycardia mediated cardiomyopathy. She was initially diuresed with IV lasix, however developed ___. When renal function improved she was started on po lasix with good effect. She was continued on her bblocker and ACE inhibitor. She has plans to follow up with cardiology as an outpatient. . # Atrial Fibrillation - Patient found to be in Afib on ECGs and telemetry. Initially it was unclear if it was new or old diagnosis, as there was only an old ECG from ___ showing Afib in OMR. Per cardiology discussion with PCP, ___ is new. Her CHADS score was calculated to be 3 (CHF, hypertension, age). The decision was made to start anticoagulation with coumadin 2.5 mg daily. She will be followed in ___ clinic for INR monitoring. . # ___: During admission Cr increased from 0.8 to 1.3. This was likely ___ prerenal azotemia from diarrhea and diuresis. Diuretics were held for a few days and diarrhea improved. Lasix was restarted prior to discharge without further effect on Cr. . # Diarrhea: During admission, patient noted to have diarrhea. This was thought to be due to possible norovirus given recent exposure. Diuretics were held and diarrhea resolved without further intervention. . # Anemia/thrombocytopenia - Recently diagnosed with MDS. ___ concerning for hemolysis with elevated bili, ldh and low haptoglobin. H/H remained stable. She has plans to follow up with heme/onc as an outpatient. . # elevated INR - Unclear etiology given that not on Warfarin on admission. Most likely ___ to malnutrition. Remained stable. . # RUE swelling - Patient noted to have RUE swelling. Per daughter, patient has intermittent swelling of Right arm since lymph node dissection in ___. RUE ultrasound showed no evidence of clot . # hypertension - Continued ace, betablocker and lasix as above . # hyperlipidemia - Continued pravastatin . # depression/anxiety - Continued lexapro . # dementia - continued donepezil . # osteopenia - continued calcium and vitamin D . transitional issues - patient will need outpatient cardiology follow up - lasix dose may need further titration - patient will need frequent INR checks until stable in therapeutic range - no ___ pending at time of discharge - patient was full code on this admission - contact: ___ ___
summary: You were admitted because you were short of breath. You were found to have fluid in your lungs which was drained off. You underwent an echocardiogram to evaluate the function of your heart which showed that it was not pumping optimally. You were evaluated by the cardiology team and you were started on some medications to help maximize your heart function and improve your symptoms. While you were admitted you were also found to have an abnormal heart rhythm called atrial fibrillation. You were started on a medication called coumadin to held reduce your risk of stroke. This medication will require frequent blood draws for monitoring. Your next blood draw should be ___.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ y/o female with CAD, CHF, CKD, and DM2 who was admitted for hyponatremia. # Hyponatremia/Polydipsia: Etiology of hyponatremia secondary to polydipsia as the patient admitted to drinking excess water. She is afraid she will need dialysis in the future, therefore drinks water whenever she feels her urine output is not adequate. This is supported by dilute urine osmolality on admission (144). The patient was placed on fluid restriction of 1.5L and her sodium trended to normal upon discharge. Patient also has an element of volume overload secondary to systolic CHF. Therefore, torsemide was restarted at her previous dose (5mg). The patient was informed that her kidney function is stable, and she should continue with 2L fluid restriction. # Hyperkalemia: K+ peaked at 5.6 during this admission. EKG did not have any hyperacute T waves. Per OMR, the patient may type IV distal RTA. This is evidenced by history of hyperkalemia as well as urine pH >5. Irbesartan was decreased by half to prevent further episodes of hyperkalemia. The patient was also restarted on torsemide. # Systolic CHF: Secondary to coronary artery disease. Repeat echo with EF 50-55%, though EF was 34% on perfusion stress test. BNP was elevated, though at baseline. Patient appeared asymptomatic and compensated, though volume status was difficult to assess due to body habitus. The patient's carvedilol was increased to 25mg BID as she was hypertensive. Irbesartan dose was also decreased in half. Patient was restarted on torsemide 5mg (previous home dose). # Coronary artery disease: The patient had one episode of chest pain with radiation down her left arm. EKG and troponins were negative. Echocardiogram showed decreased EF and new hypokinesis in the inferior walls. Stress test did not produce angina or EKG changes. Perfusion stress test was unremarkable. The patient was continued on aspirin and simvastatin. Cardiology was consulted and felt patient may have an element of demand ischemia in the setting of hypertension. Therefore, carvedilol dose was increased to 25mg BID. Patient was also started on imdur 30mg. # Hypertension: Patient was hypertensive with SBP up to 180s. Therefore carvedilol dose was increased to 25mg BID. Irbesartan was decreased to 37.5mg due to hyprekalemia. Imdur 30mg was also started. # Chronic Kidney Disease Stage III: The patient's baseline Cr is 1.4, though ranges from 1.3-1.6. As above, counseled patient that her Cr has remained stable and we don't anticipate her needing dialysis in the near future. # DM Type II on insulin: HbA1c 6.7 in ___. The patient only takes glargine when her FSBG is >200. The patient's sugars were well controlled and she never required insulin. # Anemia of chronic disease: H/H remained stable. She was continued on ferrous sulfate. # Crohn's Disease: The patient was continued on sulfasalazine and ciprofloxacin. # Peptic ulcer disease: Patient was continued on omeprazole. # Alcoholic cirrhosis: No evidence of decompensation. Last EGD ___ with no evidence varices. # Chronic pancreatitis: Continued on creon. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: - The patient will follow up with Cardiology. - Please repeat Chem 7 at ___ appointment. - Encourage patient to adhere to 2L fluid restriction. - Carvedilol dose increased to 25mg BID. - Patient started on imdur 30mg as patient remained hypertensive. - Irbesartan dose decreased to 37.5mg due to hyperkalemia, and patient has a history of type IV renal tubular acidosis. - Patient restarted on torsemide 5mg. - Consider decreasing aspirin dose to 81mg.
summary: As you recall, you were admitted with nausea and vomiting. Your sodium was found to be low, which is likely the cause of your symptoms. Your low sodium is due to your excess water intake. Your kidney function tests have been stable, therefore we do not anticipate you needing dialysis in the near future. Please limit your water intake to 2L per day (equivalent to the size of one large soda bottle). As discussed, excess water will cause your sodium to be low, which can be dangerous. It can also cause your heart function to worsen. You also had some chest pain in the hospital. EKGs and lab tests indicate no acute damage to your heart. You had a stress test, which showed your heart function is decreased from prior tests. Because of this, we increased the dose of carvedilol and started you on a new blood pressure medication called imdur. Your irbesartan dose was decreased by half to prevent your potassium too be high. We also restarted you on torsemide to decrease swelling in your legs.

text: Brief Hospital Course: #Atrial Fibrillation with rapid ventricular response-Ms. ___ was admitted with new A fib with RVR and rates to the 150's. She responded to IV and PO metoprolol and was controlled with metoprolol 75 PO TID and diltiazem 60 PO TID. She was in atrial fibrillation on telemetry monitoring during her entire hospitalization. She was treated with a heparin drip and then bridged to warfarin for further anti-coagulation as an outpatient. She had a cardioversion which she tolerated well and has been in NSR since. A TEE performed prior to the cardioversion showed no clot. She is being discharged off diltiazem but on metoprolol succinate 100mg qdaily. Because she has just had her TEE/cardioversion, it is essential that she maintain a therapeutic INR of ___ on warfarin for the next month. #Sepsis-Ms. ___ was febrile to 102 while hospitalized on two occasions. She had a urinalysis which was negative for nitrate and leukocyte esterase but two urine cultures which grew E. Coli sensitive to ampicillin. Blood cultures were positive for listeria which was also sensitive to ampicillin. She will require 2 weeks of ampicillin 2gm q6hours to treat these infections. Her last day of antibiotics will be ___. A PICC line was placed so that she can receive the antibiotics at rehab. After beginning antibiotic therapy, her WBC count decreased to normal, and she remained afebrile. #SOB-On presentation, the patient had a respiratory rate of 30's and was very uncomfortable with a new oxygen requirement of ___ nasal cannula. She received furosemide and after diuresis her breathing improved dramatically. As she was tachypneic and tachycardic on presentation, a CT-A was done to rule out a PE. The CT was negative for PE. It seems that the new atrial fibrillation caused by sepsis put her into congestive heart failure and flash pulmonary edema. With diuresis, the patient improved and no longer required any supplemental oxygen. #Diastolic CHF-EF 55-60%-This CHF exacerbation was likely caused by sepsis from the UTI vs bloodstream infection with listeria. The patient was diuresed and her pulmonary edema and lower extremity edema resolved. #DVT-The patient was noted to have assymetrical edema of bilateral lower extremeties with L>R. A doppler ultrasound of the lower extremities revealed a DVT of the L tibeal vein. She was anti-coagulated with a heparin drip and was bridged to warfarin which she will take as an outpatient.
summary: You were admitted because you lost consciousness and had a fall in your bathroom. We think your fall was caused by an abnormal heart rhythm called atrial fibrillation which can cause your heart to beat very quickly. We have treated your abnormal rhythm with a procedure called a cardioversion and now your are back in normal sinus rhythm. You should continue to take your metoprolol, but you should take it at a higher dose to help control your heart rate. You were also found to have a urinary tract infection caused by E Coli and a blood stream infection caused by listeria. Listeria is a bacteria that can be acquired by eating soft cheeses or deli meat in the refrigerator. These infections are likely the cause of your atrial fibrillation and the cause of your difficulty breathing which was due to fluid buildup in your lungs.

text: Brief Hospital Course: -Drug reaction: Presumed due to Bactrim, as it was the only new medication. She did not develop mucosal involvement, and dermatology and allergy consults did not feel prednisone was indicated. Her Fever curve spiked to a maximum of ___, and sugsequently trended down with supportive care. Bactrim was added to Outpt and inpt allergy lists. -Transaminitis: Also likely related to the allergic reaction to the Bactrim. Trending downwards at the time of discharge, and PCP aware to followup and ensure resolution. -Fever: Resolved during the course of the admission. Likely related to allergic reaction. No exidence of associated infection, including urine, bloodstream, or pulmonary. -Volume overload: She developed the signs and symptoms consistent with diastolic heart failure, with cardiac wheeze, and peripheral edema. Her symptoms and oxygen requirement resolved with lasix, and she was not hypoxic with ambulation on room air prior to discharge. She was felt to be euvolemic on discharge, although her chronic ___ edema was present. -Hypertension: The patient was continued on a lower dose of her home propranolol. She was encouraged not to keep taking the HCTZ, which was also discussed with her internist. -Sleep apnea: The patient was continued on CPAP during the admission - and brought her home machine for increased comfort.
summary: We believe you had an allergic reaction to the antibiotic bactrim. You had fevers and were dehydrated. Your kidneys were irritated, but this improved with fluids. You developed some swelling and fluid in your lungs, as a result of all of the fluid you needed to get through the fevers. We suggest that you call Dr ___ to schedule an ultrasound of your heart, to evaluate the pump function, given your history of hypertension. Your kidneys returned to normal before you went home, and your liver function tests still need to be monitored. We suggest that Dr ___ these when she sees you on ___. They are likely related to your allergic reaction. You received a single dose of Sumatriptan (Imitrex) for a headache while you were here, and it helped the headache resolve. Please continue to use your CPAP as you regularly do. We have suggested that you continue to drink 2Liters of water daily, and not to include caffeinated and sugary drinks in this.

text: Brief Hospital Course: The patient is a ___ year-old woman with a complicated medical history that includes heart failure, MI x 2, HTN, and systemic sclerosis who presents today with exertional dyspnea. . # Dyspnea on exertion/atypical chest pain - The patient was found to be grossly volume overloaded on admission with recent increase in her weight. She was diuresed with multiple doses of IV lasix 20mg with good urine output to each dose. Due to her chest pain as well as some diffuse flattening of ST segments in V5-V6, she was also ruled out for an MI with serial troponins which were negative. Initial Chest X-ray was also concerning for possible right lung pneumonia and therefore the patient was started on levofloxacin. However, repeat films with left lateral decubitus position showed a mobile effusion with no pneumonia and the antibotics were stopped. A diagnostic and therapeutic thoracentesis was considered and interventional pulmonary was consulted, but given the patient's advanced age and anticoagulation on coumadin, this was not done. The effusion was thought to be likely secondary to congestive heart failure. Upon discharge, the patient's dyspnea was well controlled with lasix and morphine. She was discharged with lasix 40mg PO bid and Morphine sulfate instant release 15mg q4h prn pain. # Goals of care - A frank discussion was had with the patient concerning her declining clinical status and increasing rate of hospital admission. The patient was very clear and had good insight into her condition; initially she had gone home from ___ in the hopes of passing away at home. A palliative care consultation was obtained, with the recommendation that the patient return to Epoch where she is well known to the staff, with care to be directed primarily towards relief of symptoms. # Paroxysmal atrial fibrillation Patient currently on warfarin therapy, but on admission was supratherapeutic at 3.4. Held warfarin at first and checked INRs. INR returned to 3.0; warfarin restarted at 1mg qday. Patient remains therapeutic at 2.3 at discharge. . # Angina - The patient was continued on imdur and ranolazine. # HTN - Controlled on home medications # Systemic sclerosis - Continued on nifedepine.
summary: You came in with chest pain and difficulty in breathing that we found was due to congestive heart failure. You were also found to have some fluid around your right lung, which we did not drain because we felt the risks of the procedure outweighed the benefits. We also discussed the situation in that you are most likely nearing the end of your life. We agreed that our goals for your care should be towards treating your symptoms. You are being discharged to Epoch at ___, where Dr. ___ ___ work with your primary care provider ___ to continue to treat your symptoms of pain and shortness of breath.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Mrs. ___ is a ___ ___ lady with DM2, CAD, paroxysmal afib, COPD, ___, and CKD who presented to the ED with toenail pain and was admitted for hypoxia to the floor. #Hypoxia: Pt was hypoxic on admission. She was saturating in the low 90's on 2L oxygen. Weaning oxygen trials failed on the day of admission. A CTA in the ED ruled out PE. There was concern that the hypoxia was secondary to hypoventilation. Differential included acute COPD decompensation, body habitus, CHF (pt has diastolic CHF), versus fluid overload with pulmonary edema. Lasix was admininstered without improvement in symptoms and CXR did not reveal fluid overload/pulmonary edema. Pt was continued on oxygen but experienced AMS episode (see below). On return to the floor, patient diuresed steadily with improvements in O2 sat and was ultimately weaned off oxygen with saturations in the mid ___ on room air. Physical therapy saw patient and found ambulatory saturations in the high ___, which improved to the ___ with deep breathing. She was deemed safe for discharge home with ___ assistance and without the need for home oxygen. #AMS: Pt experienced an alteration in mental status on the night of ___. Blood glucose was normal. An ABG was attempt, but ultimately a VBG was obtained. Blood glucose, EKG, trops, CXR were all normal. Pt was then transfered to MICU in hypercarbic respiratory failure with decreased responsiveness for Bipap. #Toenail Pain: Pt was seen by podiatry and determined that she had an ingrown toenail that could be managed as an outpatient. #Acute on Chronic Respiratory Acidosis: Pt was transfered to MICU in hypercarbic respiratory failure wih decreased responsiveness. In the MICU she was treated for possible fluid overload and pulmonary edema, she did not improve with diuresis with iv lasix. She did improve with Bipap, IV steriods, nebs, and azithromycin for a COPD exacerbation and improved clinicially. She was then started on her home torsemide and transferred back to the floor. She was continued on her home COPD meds and albuterol and ipratroprium nebs. She was transitioned to prednisone to 20 mg, with plan for rapid taper on day of transfer to floor. She was also started on azithromycin x ___nding ___. Should f/u with pulmonologist on discharge. ___ on chronic kidney disease: Creatinine is markedly increased on admission (2.6)from baseline of 1.4. Likely result of CTA dye. Now improved to 2.2. Home torsemide restarted as above. Lisinopril was held while she was in the unit and restarte on the floor when Cr normalized. On discharge Cr was 1.4, which is baseline per OMR. #HTN: Anti-hypertensives were initially held on admission, but restarted her home meds prior to transfer to the floor, except lisinopril was held as above and restarted only after improvement in renal function. #DM2: Home insulin regimen of 70/30 QAM, QHS was continued and pt also covered with SSI Humalog. She had two episodes of nighttime hypoglycemia (to 46 and ___ that were treated with orange juice and ___ amp d50. These events were potentially resulting from diabetic diet as inpatient. Her dinnertime 70/30 dose was decreased which prevented additional episodes of hypoglycemia. # Atrial fibrillation: Continued her Warfarin and coumadin and followerd INR. Supratherapeutic to 3.___uring admission, so coumadin was held x 2 days and lowered dose to 3mg on third day. INR dropped to 1.6 and normal home regimen of 5mg was restarted. ___ clinic was notified and patient was given instructions to follow up for INR check. # Hypercholesterolemia: stable, continued home statin. # h/o seizure: stable, continued Keppra. Transitional issues - She need to be set up with outpatient f/u for her COPD with a pulomonalgist or a ___ clinic. - She needs to get to a podiatrist for her toenail pain. - INR fluctuations during hospitalization, will need ___ ___ clinic f/u - PCP follow up - Discharged on prednisone taper with prescription and instructions
summary: You were initially seen in the ED for toe pain, but were admitted to the medicine floor for difficulty breathing. Your shortness of breath was severe, so you were transferred to the intensive care unit, where you were managed non-invasively before returning to the general medicine floor. You were treated for a flare of your COPD and your congestive heart failure. You were given diuretics which helped removed fluid from your lungs and were given a course of antibiotics, steroids (which you should continue at home as instructed), and nebulizers for your COPD. Your breathing improved and you were seen by the physical therapists who felt you would benefit from 24 hour services at home.

text: Brief Hospital Course: BRIEF HOSPITAL COURSE AND ACTIVE ISSUES ___ year old female with history of non-ischemic dilated CM (EF 25%), DM type 2, and dementia, presenting with hypoxemia, dyspnea on exertion, and lower extremity edema consistent with a CHF exacerbation with BNP in 20,000s. # Acute on chronic heart failure: Pt w/ non-ischemic CMP w/ EF ___ on last TTE in ___. She had been maintained on furosemide 20 mg daily, but was recently switched to torsemide 10 mg PO daily per outpatient cardiology notes on ___ as she was volume overloaded at that time. She was further diuresed with a net total output of about 4.2L over course of admission. She was continued on her spironolactone and lisinopril. We are discharging her on Furosemide 40mg with plans for chem 7 draw on ___ and ___ to be faxed to Dr. ___ in cardiology. ON DISCHARGE HER WEIGHT IS 136 LBS. # Hypothyroidism: She had an elevated TSH and normal T4 during last admission and her Levothyroxine had been increased to 150 mcg daily. However on this admission TSH remained elevated at 65. It should be confirmed after discharge that she takes her levothyroxine separately from her other medications and on an empty stomach. If TSH remains elevated after these interventions, her dose should be further uptitrated. INACTIVE ISSUES # Asymptomatic Bradycardia: Pt with previous admission for reported bradycardia to ___ recorded at ECF. Stable for now. Not AICD candidate. # Diabetes mellitus: Insulin dependent on home ISS and fairly well controlled w/ last A1c 6.3% in ___. Metformin was discontinued during last hospitalization ___ ___ but was restarted in the nursing home at 250 mg daily. Metformin was held in-house. # Hyperparathyroidism with hypercalcemia: Stable. No further intervention per endocrine. # CKD: stable # Dementia: Pt with relatively advanced dementia that has been progressive. Currently oriented x1 which seems to be new baseline. Continued on quetiapine and remeron. # Gout: Stable. Continued on home allopurinol TRANSITIONAL ISSUES -- DAILY WEIGHTS, adjust Lasix based on volume status and weight. ON DISCHARGE SHE IS 136LBS. If >3lb weight gain, call and let Dr. ___ know at ___. -- O/P chem 7 on ___ and ___ to be faxed to Dr. ___ in cardiology -- Make sure Levothyroxine is taken on empty stomach without other medications -- Changed ASA to enteric coated
summary: You were admitted for difficulty breathing. We treated you for a heart failure exacerbation with diuretics (medicines to increase your urination). We also monitored your heart rate and blood oxygen levels in the hospital and they have been stable. We also noticed that your thyroid function continues to be decreased despite treatment. Please make sure the thyroid replacement medicine Levothyroxine is taken on an empty stomach, as food can impair its absorption. Going forward, the nursing home should weigh you every morning. If you gain more than 3lbs in a day, your cardiologist Dr. ___ be notified.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ yo female with diabetes, HTN, CKD who presented with chest pressure and respiratory distress, found to have elevated BP and pulmonary edema. # HYPOXEMIC RESPIRATORY DISTRESS / ACUTE PULMONARY EDEMA / ACUTE SYSTOLIC CONGESTIVE HEART FAILURE: The patient's respiratory distress was thought to be due to flash pulmonary edema in the setting of hypertensive emergency. She improved dramatically with BIPAP and nitroglycerin drip in the ED. Upon arrival to the ICU, the patient was weaned immediately off her nitro drip. She received 20mg IV lasix once with moderate response. Please see below regarding management of HTN. An echo was performed showing a new EF of 35% with new wall motion abnormalities. EKG showed no ST segment changes. Troponins were unremarkable. She underwent a catheterization on ___, which was negative for significant CAD. She was discharged on Furosemide 20mg daily and Metoprolol 25mg daily and Valsartan 80mg BID. # HYPERTENSIVE EMERGENCY: As mentioned above, nitro drip was weaned off quickly. One dose of labetolol was too agressive for the patient and dropped her blood pressures too much. She was instead started on valsartan and her dose was uptitrated to 80mg BID. She was also given Metoprolol 25mg daily # COMPLICATED URINARY TRACT INFECTION: The patient has a history of left atrophic kidney with chronic right hydronephrosis requiring percutaneous nephrostomy. Her nephrostomy tube was recently exchanged on ___. Her UA showed possible UTI, so she was started on ceftriaxone based on prior pansensitive organisms. On imaging, her recently exchanged tube was noted to be malpositioned despite good urine output. ___ performed a perc nephrostomy tube exchange on ___. Ceftriaxone was ultimately discontinued when her culture grew Yeast. She should follow up with her nephrologist and her urologist. # DIABETES: SSI, holding metformin. # CKD: At or better than baseline creatinine on admission. Recent outpatient creat 2.9. She only received 6cc of contrast during her LHC. Cr was 1.9 at discharge. Transitional Issues: ======================= -The patient will need follow up Chem 10 on ___ -The patient will need to follow up with her cardiologist as an outpatient -Need follow-up with her Urologist -Needs follow-up with her nephrologist
summary: You were admitted to the hospital with shortness of breath. You were found to have very high blood pressure which seemed to have caused fluid to back up into your lungs. We gave you a medication to help you get rid of that fluid and you improved. We also took a picture of your heart called an echocardiogram that showed that the function of you heart was decreased. That prompted us to perform a catheterization of your heart to make sure there were no blockages in the arteries around your heart. We found that your coronary arteries are clear. You were started on Valsartan 80mg twice a day and Metoprolol 25mg daily to help control your blood pressure.

text: Brief Hospital Course: This is an ___ yo woman with a history of A-fib, Diastolic Heart Failure, UGIB ___ Duodenal Ulcer, Dementia who was transferred from her Nursing home for tachycardia. . # Diastolic heart failure. Some clinical signs and supportive evidence from echo of ___. Left ventricular function was good in ___ (LVEF = ~60%). Some fluid overload during stay, therefore increase furosemide to once daily and added supplementary KCl. Started lisinopril 5 mg daily (also for hypertension). . # Rhythm. EP study yesterday diagnosed AVNRT - two sites were ablated with subtotal ablation of a slow conduction pathway. Beta blockers have been well tolerated so these are also to continue to help reduce epidsodes of arrhythmia. She has also exhbited atrial fibrillation, premature ventricular contractions with trugminy and bigeminy while here. These abnormal rhythms have resolved now with use of metoprolol to convert her from atrial fibrillation to sinus rhythm. Given her DNR/DNI code status and previous hypotensive reaction to diltiazem, this drug was avoided - a code would be unrecoverable. . # Hypertension. When in slow SR is hypertensive - an active problem during this admission. We added lisinopril 5 mg daily and this may be adjusted to blood pressure (with concomitant adjustment of potassium intake). . # Dementia. Significant issue on the evening of her first day of stay. She was very tired from being in the ED and was agressive at times and refused oral meds. This was not a problem subsequently, particularly when stopping the tramadol and moving her medication times forward to 6 and 6. More lucid without tramadol - pain has not been a significant issue. Tramadol, sedative, may defeat the purpose of memantine (faciliating transmission from cholinergic basal forebrain). . # Arthritis. Was not worse at rest when holding Ultram given dementia, as above. We continued acetominophen (< 2g / d). This regimen may need to be adjusted in the light of less analgesic cover. . # Anemia. Stable. No evidence of GIB in house. Not investigated during admission. . # Communication. Sons are jointly Health Care Proxies: ___ (c) ___ ___ (c) ___ They were very helpful and inquisitive during this admission. . # Code status. DNR/DNI was discussed with the sons and confirmed in OMR.
summary: You came to the hospital with a rapid heart rate. While you were here we recognized that your heart actually had several stable rhythm states. The transitions between these states were rapid. We used electrophysiological methods to study the conduction system of your heart and found that there was more than one conduction pathway for impulses within the atria of the heart. The abnormal pathways were ablated. This results in a much higher threshold for state change and potential eradication of abnormal rhythms. To additionally ensure that the heart stays in a normal rhythm we have increased one medication, metoprolol. Your blood pressure was mildly elevated and you have early signs of heart failure so we took the evidence based approach of adding an ACE inhibitor to your medication regimen, lisinopril. We noted that you were taking a sedative medication, tramadol, which is likely to work against the beneficial effect of memantine (Namenda). We held this medication and found that you did not suffer from more pain at rest.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Mr. ___ is a ___ yo man with a h/o a-fib on coumadin, dCHF (EF >55% in ___, stroke, stage III CKD and a recent admission in ___ for R foot MSSA osteomyelitis, who presented with ~3 weeks of progressive. ACUTE ISSUES: # acute on chronic diastolic CHF exacerbation Given symptoms (orthopnea, PND and ankle swelling), moderate evidence of volume overload on exam and response to lasix, his SOB was felt to be most consistent with an acute on chronic diastolic CHF exacerbation. Possible contributing factors include dietary changes (increased salt with take-out) and significant Na load in his recent frequent nafcillin infusions. His EKG did not show overt signs of interval ischemic damage and cardiac enzymes were negative x2. He had a CXR, which showed a RLL infiltrate concerning for a pneumonia. In the ED he received levofloxacin, but given that he was not experiencing fever, chills, productive cough, leukocytosis and had preserved oxygen saturation, treatment for pneumonia was discontinued. On discharge, he was instructed to reduce Na/fluids, weigh himself daily and if >3 lb gain, call his PCP. His discharge weight was 94.5kg. He was not discharged on home lasix as he preferred to attempt dietary modifications before starting another pill. Of note, he will need a CXR at ___ weeks to ensure that there is interval resolution of the infiltrate. # Prolonged QTc The patient's QTc was monitored during admission and was consistently 450-470s, notably longer than previous ECGs. Pharmacy was consulted, who felt that contributing medications may include venlafazine and oxycodone, the latter of which is new for him. Of note, his electrolytes were normal. Given his existing heart disease, worsening structural heart disease could not be excluded as a contributing factor. Please trend this closely when administering QTc prolonging agents. # R surgical wound The patient wound was healing well with erythema or purulent drainage to suggest infection. He was given his home equivalent of oxycodone and tylenol. CHRONIC ISSUES: # Atrial Fibrillation The patient was in atrial fibrillation and hemodynamically stable during admission. The patient was continued on diltiazem, carvedilol and warfarin 7.5mg daily. Incidentally, for unclear reasons, he was noted to be sub-therapeutic to 1.6 on discharge, so he was started on a lovenox bridge (100mg bid) and his warfarin was increased to 10mg for 2 days with the plan for him to have his INR checked 2 days after discharge. # CKD Stage 3: The patient's Cr was stable, and 1.6 at time of discharge. # CVA The patient was continued on aspirin. # Depression The patient was continued on venlafaxine. # h/o traumatic seizure The patient was continued on oxcarbazepine. ISSUES OF TRANSITION IN CARE: # CODE: Full # CONTACT: ___ (wife) ___ # ISSUES TO DISCUSS AT FOLLOW UP - diastolic CHF, consider starting lasix depending on weights and symptoms - Repeat CXR in ___ weeks to ensure resolution of RLL infiltrate - orthopedics follow up as scheduled - close follow up with INR. continue lovenox bridge until INR >2. # PENDING STUDIES AT DISCHARGE - ___ BLOOD CX x2
summary: You were admitted with shortness of breath that was likely due to an exacerbation of your underlying congestive heart failure, possibly due to increased sodium in your diet and recent IV nafcillin infusions. You received lasix, which caused you to urinate extra fluid, resulting in improvement in your breathing. A chest xray was performed, which showed a possible pneumonia, but given that you were not having fevers, chills, productive cough or requiring oxygen, you were not treated with an extended course of antibiotics, though did receive one dose in the hospital. You will need a repeat chest xray in ___ weeks. Please weigh yourself every morning and record it.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ year old woman with past medical history notable for HTN, HLD, OSA, PVD, pAF, and chronic back pain who was transferred from ___ with NSTEMI now s/p cath with DES x1. #NSTEMI: Patient had cath ___ with DES to left circ without complication. She was started on clopidogrel 75 mg ___ 81 ___, and high dose atorvastatin. She was also started on a low dose of metoprolol, which was titrated up to 50mg BID over her hospital course. Her troponin trended upward through her hospital stay and peaked at 6 (on ___. We had concern for low clearance versus an ischemic process. She remained without symptoms, her ECG did not show any ischemia, and her trop down-trended on the day of discharge. Please follow-up this elevated troponin especially in light of her asymptomatic NSTEMI. #Chronic Paroxysmal Afib: Patient had episodes of afib with concurrent ST depressions in the anterior leads. She spontaneously converted back to sinus rhythm. Patient was started on Coumadin 2.5 daily with lovenox bridging until she was therapeutic. She will need her INR drawn on ___ with close follow-up. #Acute on chronic systolic heart failure: Patient presented with peripheral edema, exertional dyspnea, elevated BNP, and pulmonary edema on CXR, which is most likely in the setting on known HCTZ non-compliance. Patient received several doses of Lasix while inpatient and pulmonary edema was resolved. Please consider restarting diuretic as outpatient if needed. (Patient has been euvolemic off diuretic for last 4 days.) The patient's episodes of afib may have also contributed to the transient heart failure. #Nausea with intermittent diarrhea and constipation: Patient presented to the ___ with complaints of nausea and diarrhea, but this resolved upon admission to the floor. Patients states that this is a chronic problem. She had a KUB, which showed a non-obstructive bowel gas pattern and stool throughout the colon. Her symptoms are most likely consistent with IBS. #HTN: Patient was continued on her home dose of valsartan. #Depression: Patient was continued on home dose of sertraline. #OSA: Patient was continued on her home CPAP. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: Medication changes [ ] Aspirin 81 mg and Plavix 75 mg were started s/p cath; these need to be continued for at least ___ year after discharge [ ] Patient was started on metoprolol succinate 50mg BID for afib rate control. We considered rhythm control with amiodarone, but held off for now given she is asymptomatic and her LFTs were mildly elevated upon discharge. She remained in sinus rhythm for the last two nights. [ ] Warfarin was started with lovenox bridging. Her INR was therapeutic at time of discharge at 2.5. She will need follow up in ___ clinic. [ ] Next INR to be drawn on ___ by ___ and sent to ___ until follow up with ___ clinic. [ ] Patient's LFTs were noted to be elevated during admission. They down-trended over the hospital course, but the etiology of these elevations should be followed up [ ] Please evaluate patient for heart failure and restart diuretic as needed. She was non-compliant with thiazide. (She was treated effectively in hospital with IV Lasix) and so it was held on discharge. Discharge labs: Cr: 0.6 INR: 2.5 Trop T: 5.37 CK-MB: 2 Weight: 66.2 kg
summary: You presented to ___ because you had nausea and chest pressure. A procedure was performed to look at your heart arteries, and we found a blockage in one of them. A stent was placed to keep the blood vessel open. You also had fluid in your lungs when you came to the hospital. We gave you medicine and you began to feel better. You were started on aspirin 81 mg and clopidogrel (also known as Plavix) 75 mg. It is very important that you continue these medicines after you leave the hospital for at least one year, unless your cardiologist instructs you otherwise. If you don't take these medications consistently, you will be at risk of a heart attack.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ woman with metastatic adenocarcinoma of lung, chronic dyspnea, h/o PE, pleural effusions, recently noted decrease in LVEF to 25% with ___ HK, admitted for dyspnea and hypotension. Hypotension: Felt to be secondary to recently starting a combination of atenolol and furosemide. Patient did not appear septic as no fever, wbc, or lactate. Hypotension was fluid responsive. It was felt patient was intra-vascularly dry, however total volume overloaded. Cardiology was consulted in help with management of patient's hypotension in setting of severe heart failure. It was advised to slowly re-introduce lisinopril, metoprolol, and lasix. As this was performed patient again became hypotensive in the ___. She was asymptomatic during these low blood pressures. Her blood pressure improved with very small fluid boluses. Finally she was started on only 6.25mg po bid metoprolol but again became hypotensive. She was discharged on no BP medications for this reason but may be able to restarted as an outpatient. Dyspnea: Felt to be multifactorial given lung cancer, bilateral pleural effusions, systolic congestive heart failure, anemia, and pulmonary emboli. IP was consulted and felt her pleural effusions were minimal and removed her pleurex catheter. She was continued on lovenox BID for her pulmonary emboli. She was given 2 units of red blood cells for anemia. She is undergoing treatment in the outpatient for lung cancer. Her medications have been adjusted as above for patient's heart failure. She was given supplemental oxygen (which she uses at home) for comfort. She was not noted to be hypoxic. Patient stated ativan aides in her dyspnea. This medication was increased in frequency with improvement of her dyspnea. Chronic systolic CHF: Pt with new markedly decreased LVEF to 25% on TTE, with significant ___ HK/AK c/w ___ MI. ECG changes incl biphasic Twaves in similar distribution. BNP elevated. No signs of recent acute event. She was continued on aspirin 81 mg daily. Further medication adjustment as above. She will be followed closely in the heart failure clinic in the outpatient with Dr. ___. Anemia: Initial Hct 24.1 with a baseline likely closer to 30. She was transfused 2 units of red blood cells with improvement of her hematocrit. Pleural effusion: Left pleural effusion had been reaccumulating slowly, necessitating Pleurex catheter placement, which is no longer draining. IP removed catheter without incident. It was felt the patient's left pleural effusion was minimal. Pulmonary malignancy: Pt with pleural-based adenocarcinoma, s/p 6 cycles of carboplatin / pemtrexed, now s/p 3 cycles Taxol and due for fourth cycle in one week. Changed to Taxol as CA-125 was rising, currently at 1036. No active issues during this hospitalization.
summary: You were admitted because of shortness of breath and low blood pressure. You were given fluids and your blood pressure increased. Your low blood pressure was likely from the medications that were started for your heart. The cardiologists evaluated you and felt you should be started on medications that will help your heart failure. We tried to start these medications but your blood pressure again became low. You should discuss these medications with your cardiologist at your appointment next week. While you were in the hospital, the interventional pulmonologists pulled your pleurex catheter. You had no complications after this procedure.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms ___ is an ___ year old female with history of TIA and PE on coumadin who presented with hypoxia. Hypoxia was believed to be secondary to volume overload based on clinical exam. Pt has known right heart failure and pulmonary HTN from chronic PEs, with a patent PFO causing right to left shunting that seems to become clinically significant in setting of volume overload. She was evaluated by the cardiology service who recommended against closure of PFO given need for permanent anticoagulation for PE. They also felt that the PFO was unlikely to be clinically significant if patient maintained euvolemic status. She was started on Sildenafil and diuresed in the MICU and the floor, home amlodipine was held to allow room for diuresis with blood pressure, not restarted given pressures in the 120s. At the time of discharge she was euvolemic on 1L oxygen by nasal cannula, dry weight today is 39.1kg. She will require daily weights and weekly electrolyte checks with addition of 20mg po if there is concern for increased volume. She also developed multifactorial delirium ___ hypercalcemia, prolonged hospital stay, possibly poor sleep and underlying dementia. An infectious work-up on the floor was negative for UTI, pneumonia, cellulitis. She was found to be hypercalcemic to 11.3, PTH high normal, started on cinacalcet for hypercalcemia/primary hyperparathyroidism per the endocrine team. There was concern about bisposphonate use with GERD and immobility. Mirtazipine for chronic insomnia was reduced from 12.5mg to 7.5mg. She was started on seroquel 12.5mg as the most sedating antipsychotic to improve sleep and hopefully reduce daytime somnolence. ___ be stopped on ___ if pt is sleeping well. During her hosptialization she was evaluated by speech and swallow who found her to be aspirating food and liquid with two failed video swallows. She had a dobhoff placed temporarily to maintain nutrition. Repeat video swallow evaluation when patient's mental status was improved allowed patient to tolerate modified diet . She was restarted on thin liquids, Ensure plus three times a day, and moist ground solids and crushed meds. Aspriation precautions should be maintained. Her hospitalization was also complicated by electrolyte disturbances from overdiuresis, which was easily corrected with free water flushes. She was noted to have an EColi UTI treated with ceftriaxone X 7 days. To do: - daily weights, weekly assement of volume status and weekly electrolye checks, pt demonstrate any signs of volume overload lasix 20mg po should be added to maintain euvolemic state. - calcium check in q weekly X 1month to monitor for efficacy treatment of cinacalcet. - cinacalcet should be increased to 30mg bid in 2 weeks if persistent hypercalcemia - d/c seroquel as mental status improves - encourage oral nutrition - daily physical therapy - will need four week follow up with Dr. ___ ___
summary: You were admitted to the hospital because you were having trouble breathing and the levels of oxygen in your blood were found to be very low. It was thought that your low oxygen level was due to having too much fluid in your body, building up in your lungs, as well as due to increased pressure in the blood vessels in your lungs. You were also found to have a hole in your heart between two compartments which allowed blood without oxygen to go straight to the rest of your body without going to your lungs. Because of the increased fluid in your body you were given a pill to make you urinate to reduce your fluids. You were also started on a medication called sildenafil to treat the increased pressure in your lung blood vessels. As you had less fluid in your body, the amount of fluid going through the hole in your heard decreased. When we spoke to the cardiologists, they recommended against closing the hole because they felt it was not contributing much to your low oxygen. You improved as the amount of oxygen in your blood increased. You also became a little confused and sleepy while you were in the hospital which we think was due to being in the hospital for a long time, as well as having high calcium in your blood. You were started on a medication called cinacalcet to reduce your calcium levels. You were also started on a medication called sildenafil to reduce the high blood pressure in your lungs to improve your breathing.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ y/o female with history of hypertension, hyperlipidemia, previous left shoulder injury, who presented with worsened left shoulder pain for one day. ACTIVE ISSUES: 1. Left shoulder pain: History more consistent with musculoskeletal pain as patient had previous injury to that shoulder and her pain is worse with movement and reproducible on palpation. Patient ruled out for myocardial infarction in ED with 2 negative troponins prior to admission. EKG showed no evidence of ischemia on admission. No fevers, erythema, or warmth that would be consistent with septic joint. The patient's pain improved on the day of admission and she did not require any pain meds. X-Ray did not show any fracture. Patient has planned follow-up with her PCP for further evaluation of the shoulder pain. 2. ___: No chest pain, but patient was concerned that scapular pain may represent ischemia. She had never had similar pain in the past. History of CAD was listed on patient's medication list, however she is unaware of any past history of MI/catheterization in the past. Patient did have emotional stressor prior to presentation as close friend passed away, but negative troponin x2 make ACS/Takotsubo's unlikely. Attempted to obtain outpatient records from Dr. ___, but office staff did not have any record of stress test or cardiac catheterization. Continued home medications of plavix, metoprolol, isosorbide and statin while admitted. 3. Pulmonary edema on CXR: Patient was comfortable at rest, without hypoxia or tachypnea. She reports intermittent shortness of breath as an outpatient, but this is long standing and does not appear consistent with a CHF history. Also, BNP was < 400 making CHF less likely. She received a dose of lasix in the emergency department prior to arrival to floor. Patient did not receive any further lasix as she had clear lungs on exam and did not have any dyspnea, PND, or orthopnea. CHRONIC/INACTIVE ISSUES 1. Hypertension: Continued metoprolol, isosorbide. 2. Hyperlipidemia: Continued statin. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: 1. Shoulder pain: Should pain persist, patient may warrant further work-up for pain. She is following up with her primary care doctor in 2 weeks. 2. Interstitial edema on CXR: Patient was asymptomatic with clear lungs on exam and low BNP. Consider repeating CXR as outpatient.
summary: ___. You were admitted to the hospital with shoulder pain. You had an x-ray of your shoulder that did not show a fracture. You had blood tests that did not show any evidence of damage to your heart. We did an EKG of your heart that did not show evidence of a heart attack. Your chest x-ray showed that there is some extra fluid on your lungs, but you were not having any trouble breathing and your oxygen saturation was normal. Your shoulder pain was worse with movement and it was painful to touch. The cause of your shoulder pain is likely musculoskeletal and may get better over time. You are scheduled to see Dr. ___ on ___. If the pain does not improve in the next 1 - 2 weeks, please see Dr. ___ further evaluation of the shoulder pain and the chest x-ray findings.

text: Brief Hospital Course: The patient was admitted with shortness of breath that was likely an acute exacerbation of chronic diastolic heart failure consistent with his increased edema and recently decreased lasix dose. The patient was treated with lasix, however his blood pressure dropped to the 80's systolic after 0.3L urine output. Therefore his Norvasc and Valsartan and lasix were held. Also during this episode it was noted that he was bradycardic with a heart rate into the 30's, with a baseline heart rate in the 60___s. The patient's drop in blood pressure was attributed to over diuresis and he was treated with a fluid bolus and transferred to the cardiology service for possible evaluation for a pacemaker. . He was also wheezing on admission and his shortness of breath was improved with the diuresis and with albuterol and ipratropium treatments. The symptoms were from a combination of heart failure, and worsened by underlying asthma. The patient required oxygen and at discharge was at 2 liters nasal cannula when walking. The patient may require home oxygen when discharged from rehab. . On the cariology service the patient was found to have bradycardia, and while on telemetry monitoring he had a heart rate into the low 20's transiently multiple times, with no symptoms. Pt was also not increasing his heart rate appropriately with activity, it would not exceed the mid-70's while walking, even while short of breath. His echocardiogram showed mild symmetric left ventricular hypertrophy with preserved global and regional biventricular systolic function, Mild aortic regurgitation, Moderate mitral regurgitation, Moderate pulmonary hypertension, and enlarged atria and an ejection fraction of >55%. Also his EKG's had very low voltage. . Due to this the patient was tested for amyloidosis- his SPEP, UPEP, and fat biopsy were negativef for signs of amyloid. Due to the continued bradycardia the patient was evaluated for a pacemaker, he received a single lead left sided pacemaker. He tolerated the procedure without problem. He also was slowly diuresised with lasix and put on a 2 liter fluid restriction to treat his heart failure. The patient was on telemtry monitoring. Lasix was given conservatively due the patients elevated creatinine with too much lasix. He was started back on his Diovan, but his Norvasc was held due to the bradycardia. If the patient becomes hypertensive after discharge, there should be consideration to restart his Norvasc. . Since the patient has received a pacemaker he was treated prophylactically with vancomycin, and he will be discharged with 1 days of treatment on Keflex BID. He has a follow up appointment with the device clinic and with his cardiologist to monitor his pacemaker function and symptoms of heart failure. . Of note, the patient was noted to have a very congested voice, which was new for the patient for the last month. He had a speech and swallow evaluation before discharge who recommended soft solids for his diet. This will need further evaluation at rehab, to see if the patient still requireds this restriction. Patient was tolerating this diet without problems at discharge, and stated that it was much easier for him to consume. . The patient was also noted to have a bullous pemphigoid rash, (has had it for ___ years). Pt had recently been decreased from Prednisone 15 every day to every other day. However, likely secondary to the stress of being in the hospital the patient's rash increased and had more bullae on his forearms. This was non-painful, not pruritic, and did not appear to be infectous. The prednisone was increased to everyday and clobetasol ointment was started BID, this improved the rash and the bullae were resolving at discharge. Pt is to follow up with his Dermatologist this week to evaluate for futher treatment and long term managment of this skin condition. . He was hemodynamically stable at discharge and able to ambulate with assitance.
summary: You were admitted to ___ with heart failure. Your heart rate was also decreased. You were recommended to have a pacemaker, therefore, you received the pacemaker and toleraed the procedure well. You will need to go to a rehab center once you leave the hospital since you are not strong enough to be home alone at this time. As you were instructed after your pacemaker was placed, do not get your bandages wet, or shower, or raise your left arm above your head until you are seen in the Device clinic in 1 week. During your stay you also had more blisters and rash related to you skin condition, your predinisone was increased to everyday and you were given steriod cream. You will need to see your dermatologist for further care of your skin.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ with h/o morbid obesity (BMI=68), CAD, CVA, DM, PE, CHF, Pulm HTN, AFib on Coumadin, and a prior GI bleed from ulcerated lipoma, who is being admitted from her PCP's office for worsening bilateral lower extremity edema concerning for CHF exacerbation, with likely mild traumatic hematuria from foley insertion. . ACTIVE DIAGNOSES: . #R-sided diastolic CHF Exacerbation/Morbid Obesity: Patient was admitted with gross fluid overload over baseline extreme morbid obesity. She had severe lymphedema with peau-de-orange skin changes on her inner thighs bilaterally and pitting up to the waist. She had 1 negative set of cardiac enzymes, no chest pain, and an unchanged, reassuring EKG. TTE was performed and showed primarily R-sided HF and pulmonary HTN and she was with minimal crackles on lung exam. She was started on nighttime O2 and diuresed aggressively. Admission weight was not obtained (the weighing mechanism on her bariatric bed malfunctioned and she was unable to be walked at her admission weight which we estimate to be 450-460lbs. On Torsemide 80mg daily she was routinely putting out 5+ L of urine without significant drops in her blood pressure (without any reduction of her home anti-hypertensive regimen) or bump in her creatinine. She did have progressively rising bicarb level with measured alkalemia on ABG (mixed metabolic alkalosis and respiratory acidosis) but was without any symptoms of hypercarbia. Unfortunately, her ABG and VBGs (attempted twice) closely correlated with regards to pH but not with regards to pCO2. She was set up with home O2 and discharged with 40mg torsemide daily to continue her diuresis as well as given a script for acetazolamide to be administered at the discretion of Dr. ___ her primary care doctor to counteract her alkalemia. She refused home services despite multiple attempts to convince her but was set up with the ___ intensive CHF program. Her discharge weight was 430lbs. She was also advised to make it to her CPAP titration study in order to initiate CPAP at home. . #Hematuria: Patient developed gross hematuria late on the day of admission likely related to her supertherapeutic INR and difficult foley placement. She was managed with CBI, her INR was allowed to drift back to within her goal of ___ for afib, and her hematuria cleared. . CHRONIC DIAGNOSES: . #Afib on coumadin: Stable. She was admitted with a supertherapeutic INR which was allowed to drift to within her goal range of ___. Her coumadin regimen was changed to 7.5mg alternating with 10mg coumadin daily. She will continue to ___ with ___ ___ clinic. . #CAD: Stable. No chest pain or clinical syndrome of ACS. Not on aspirin b/c bleeding previously and on coumadin. She was continued on simvastatin and her anti-hypertensives. . #DM2: Stable. Lantus was reduced down to 36 unit QAM in house given her strict diet but she was discharged on her home 42units QAM. Metformin was held in-house but re-initiated on discharge. . TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: . -DIURESIS: She was discharged on torsemide 40mg daily with enrollment into the ___ intensive CHF program. She will need continued diuresis but care must be taken that she have her electrolytes repleted and her bicarb/acid-base status checked periodically to prevent her from becoming overly alkalotic. She was given a script for acetazolamide but instructed to hold off on taking it unless instructed to do so by her PCP or Dr. ___. She refused home services such as ___ and ___ despite several offers. . -CPAP Titration: She was encouraged to ___ with her outpatient providers and ___ with a CPAP titration study as an outpatient. She was setup for home O2 to aid in the meantime. . -LANTUS: She required a lower lantus dose of 36 (down from 42) in-house related to her strict diabetic diet. We discharged her on her home dose of 42 expecting her dietary control not to be as strict as it was in-house.
summary: You were admitted to ___ for evaluation and treatment of worsened lower extremity swelling. We did an echocardiogram which showed pulmonary hypertension and predominantly right-sided heart failure. You were diuresed aggressively first with IV lasix then with oral torsemide in addition to acetazolamide (another diuretic). You also developed some transient hematuria (blood in the urine) likely related to the placement of your foley catheter which resolved. You were offerred placement at a rehab facility for continued monitored diuresis but refused. We are discharging you home to continue diuresis more gradually at home.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ year old man with history of morbid obesity, non-ischemic cardiomyopathy, systolic HF (EF 15% s/p ICD), and OSA, who presented on ___ with Vtach s/p ICD firing with worsening lactic acidosis. Patient noted to be volume overloaded on exam and was started on bumex drip. He was ultimately diuresed approximately 30 liters over the course of the admission. Additional therapies included afterload reduction with maximal doses of hydralazine and isordil as well as the addition of digoxin to improve contractility. A swan-ganz catheter was placed for tailored-therapy monitoring. Due to a persistently low cardiac output and episodes of symptomatic hypotension, the patient required IV inotrope therapy with dobutamine initially at 5 mcg/kg/min. As the patient was diuresed and adjustments were made to his other oral medications, several attempts were made to wean the patient off of the dobutamine drip. These attempts were unsuccessful as reflected by worsening hemodynamic parameters measured via the ___, worsening lactic acidosis, episodes of hypotension, and subjective discomfort reported by Mr. ___. Several meetings were held with Mr. ___ involving the primary team, social work, and palliative care to discuss the limited options for his end stage heart failure and to address goals of care given his overall poor prognosis. It was once again determined that he would not be a candidate for transplant or a ventricular assist device. The patient opted to become DNR/DNI but decided to not turn off the ICD. Mr ___ decided that he would prefer to be discharged home with ___ services rather than go to a ___ facility. He was discharged on dobutamine at a rate of 3.5 mcg/kg/min indefinitely. He will require daily ___ care and close follow up with the heart failure team. Discharge weight was 154.3kg. Discharge Bumex 4mg QPM, 5mg Daily # Acute decompensated HF c/b cardiogenic shock: pt w/ hx sHF and NICM EF 15%: patient volume overloaded on exam w/ elevated BNP and pulmonary edema as well as lactic acidosis. PA catheter placed for tailored therapy with decreased MvO2 and elevated PCWP c/w cardiogenic shock. Patient started on dobutamine for ionotropic support. Afterload reduction achieved with nipride gtt which was transitioned to captopril. Pt diuresed with bumex gtt w/ diuril IV boluses. Spironolactone increased to 200 BID in setting of hypokalemia. Home metoprolol initially held. Patient monitored with daily weights, strict I/Os with ___ cc fluid restriction, 2g sodium diet. Patient was stabililized on dobutamine and continued bumex gtt. Attempts made to wean the dobutamine were unsuccessful, prompting the addition of hydralazine/imdur along with digoxin to allow a very slow wean of the dobutamine, which ultimately could be weaned only to 3.5. Discharged on Bumex 4mg QPM 5 mg daily. # ICD firing: Patient presents after feeling ICD firing. Notably he had a prior admission a week prior where he had VT, thought to be in the setting of electrolyte abnormalities. Pacer interrogated and had one shock day prior to admission. Mg was 1.4 on admission and K to 3.7 though he claims he did not miss any doses of his home supplemenation. EP consulted and recommened continuing amiodarone 400 BID and he was ultimately decreased to requiring 400 mg QD on discharge. # Anxiety/depression: home sertraline continued. SW consulted- goals of care conversations as above. # TRANSITIONAL ISSUES - needs CPAP at home -considered for CRT for cardiomyopathy. QRS is widened but not >120. non ischemic. -CONTACT: ___ (wife) ___ -CODE STATUS: DNR/DNI, keep ICD on for now
summary: You were admitted to the hospital for management of your severe heart failure. Unfortunately, your heart was not pumping blood well enough to meet the demands of your body. We placed a monitor in your neck to measure important information about your heart as we made changes to your medications to find the best regimen. Several therapies were tried. First, we gave you medicine to remove the excess fluid from your body. We also added or increased the dose of several other medications. During your stay, you required a continuous IV medication called dobutamine to assist your heart's ability to pump blood. Despite all the changes we tried, you were not able to come off dobutamine. You will need to remain on this medication indefinitely. In order to help with your care at home, you will have a visiting nurse to assist with all medications and other therapies.

text: Brief Hospital Course: PATIENT SUMMARY ================== ___ w/ history of HFpEF, atrial fibrillation/flutter, and HLD admitted for dyspnea c/w acute CHF exacerbation and rapid a-fib. Symptoms rapidly improved with diuresis and converted back to sinus rhythm. # Tachycardia # Atrial fibrillation, history of pAF/flutter - Longstanding history of afib/flutter, rate controlled on Metoprolol for ___ years. Patient presents with increased frequency of palpitations and worsening exertional shortness of breath with signs of volume overload ___ edema, elevated JVP; CXR with pulmonary edema, BNP elevated). There was no clear precipitant for his worsening atrial fibrillation at this time other than possible viral URI two weeks ago. History was not suggestive of ischemia, he has no heavy EtOH use, no severe electrolyte derangements, TSH was normal, and he was taking metop regularly. Trops neg x2. His rates were not controlled with IV metoprolol, so he was started on IV diltiazem and apixaban in the ED. Unfortunately he then had worsening respiratory status, requiring oxygen, but rapidly improved with diuresis and converted back into sinus rhythm. The diltiazem was stopped, and he was restarted on his home metoprolol. On discharge, his home metoprolol dose was increased to try and prevent recurrence of afib. His baby aspirin was discontinued in the setting of starting apixaban. # Hypoxia # Volume overload Presented with shortness of breath, and volume overloaded on exam, with worsening respiratory status and chest xray after diltiazem as above. The patient reported a ___ pound weight gain over the weekend. He diuresed 16 liters over length of stay with BID dosing of 20 mg IV Lasix. His breathing improved to baseline. # Macrocytic anemia - No clinical signs of bleeding, suspect dilutional effect given volume overload. =============== CHRONIC ISSUES: =============== # Dyslipidemia Continued home pravastatin. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES ==================== --- Discharge weight: 195.99 lbs (88.9 kg) --- Discharge creatinine: 0.9 --- Discharge diuretic: Furosemide 20 mg PO daily PRN [ ] Patient was discharged on increased dose of metoprolol to prevent recurrence of afib. Heart rate and symptoms should be monitored as outpatient. [ ] Patient was discharged with Rx for furosemide 20 daily PRN, and instructions to call PCP if weight gain >3lbs in 24 hours. [ ] suggest checking Hgb A1c as outpatient given rapid diuresis. # Emergency contact: ___ (wife) Phone: ___ ___ on Admission: The Preadmission Medication list is accurate and complete. 1. Metoprolol Succinate XL 25 mg PO DAILY 2. Pravastatin 80 mg PO QPM 3. Aspirin 325 mg PO DAILY
summary: You were admitted because your heart rate was very fast and you were feeling short of breath You were found to be in a heart rhythm called atrial fibrillation that was making your heart beat too fast. You were given medications through the IV (diltiazem and metoprolol) to slow your heart rate down. Your heart reverted from atrial fibrillation to the normal rhythm (sinus rhythm). Your heart was having trouble pumping blood, and this caused fluid to back up around your lungs and made it difficult for you to breath. You were given oxygen through nasal cannula to help you breath. You were also given medications through the IV (furosemide, "Lasix") to help you urinate out the extra fluid.
