You had an ultrasound done of your heart ("echocardiogram" or "TTE") that showed the pump and valves are normal. Your weight in the hospital on day of discharge was 195.99 lbs (88.9 kg). You should weigh yourself every morning after you urinate and before you have breakfast.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ============================= PRIMARY REASON FOR ADMISSION ============================= Mr. ___ is a ___ yo man with hx of repaired Type A aortic dissection, aortic insufficiency, and recent hospitalization for multifocal pneumonia in ___ who presents again with dyspnea secondary to congestive heart failure ============================= ACTIVE ISSUES: ============================= #)Dyspnea secondary to Acute Systolic Congestive heart failure with Aortic Regurgitation: The patient presented with dyspnea, orthopnea and bilateral lower extremity pitting edema consistent with an exacerbation of congestive heart failure. BNP >6000 but without a baseline. Given his recent history of multifocal pneumonia requiring an ICU stay, he initially received vancomycin and cefepime in the ED but this was stopped once he was admitted to the medical floor as our suspicion for infection was low. He was given on Lasix IV boluses and had subjective and clinical improvement in symptoms and was euvolemic by discharge. He had a TTE that was significant for regional left ventricular systolic dysfunction consistent with CAD (troponins negative, cath report ___ reviewed with no significant CAD); moderate-to-severe aortic regurgitation; moderate mitral regurgitation; and moderate pulmonary hypertension. Compared with his previous echo on ___, the inferior wall motion abnormalities were more pronounced with a decline in ejection fraction. The severity of mitral regurgitation had progressed and the estimated pulmonary artery pressure was higher. Cardiothoracic surgery evaluated and will follow closely as an outpatient to determine need for further intervention. He will likely need a TEE after discharge. Lasix dose was increased from 20mg to 40mg daily and he had Nutrition teaching about heart failure diets. He was also instructed to weigh himself and let his doctors know ___ develops edema or more dyspnea. . #)Asymmetric bilateral edema: Given that his left leg was larger than his right on admission, duplex ultrasound was performed and ruled out DVT. The edema of both legs improved prior to discharge with Lasix as per above and low Na diet. . #) Hypertension: The patient had systolic blood pressures in the 130s-150s on his home lisinopril 20mg and amlodipine 5mg. His lisinopril was increased to 30mg (Cr stable around baseline 1.3). . . ============================= CHRONIC ISSUES: ============================= #) Anemia: Currently stable on iron supplements. . #) OSA: Uses CPAP at home and continued during hospitalization. . #) Depression: Stable. Continued home medications. . #) Hypercholesterolemia: Continued home Pravastatin. . . ============================= TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: ============================= - Patient refused aspirin inpatient. Given his risk factors, he would likely benefit from taking aspirin and this should be addressed again with him as an outpatient - Lasix increased to 40mg daily, lisinopril increased to 30mg daily. Will need electrolytes and blood pressure monitored and may need to increase K+ supplementation - Discharge weight: 100.6 kg - Full code
summary: You were admitted on ___ for shortness of breath. You were found to have a lot of fluid in your lungs and swelling in your legs likely secondary to congestive heart failure. You were given IV lasix and your symptoms improved. We increased your home dose of lasix to 40mg and you should take this new dose on discharge. We also increased your lisinopril to 30mg as your blood pressure was high during this admission. You had a repeat echocardiogram, an ultrasound of your heart, that showed some progression in worsening valve function and overall heart squeeze than when you were last here in ___. You were evaluated by the Cardiothoracic Surgery Service and will have close follow-up as an outpatient. It will be very important for you to follow a low sodium diet. You met with our nutritionist while you were here.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Patient is a ___ year old male with CHF, sarcoid, cardiomyopathy and atrial fibrillation who presents with volume overload since discontinuation of a number of his medications on ___ secondary to Cr elevation. . #. CAD: No known history of CAD with ETT limited only by DOE with uninterpretable ECG. However, no recent nuclear imaging or cath to confirm continued clean coronaries. Some LV WMA seen on echo. Continued on Aspirin 325mg daily . #. Pump: Non-ischemic cardiomyopathy with most recent EF of 40%. Now appeared to be in acute on chronic systolic congestive heart failure likely in setting of holding his Lasix, Aldactone +/- Lisinopril. Per patient, he has been compliant with fluid and sodium restrictions. CXR shows mild prominence of pulmonary vasculature but no overt heart failure. Differential diagnosis, however, includes CHF exacerbation vs. methotrexate effect vs. substantial restrictive/obstructive lung disease causing severe diffusion defect resulting in hypoxemia with exertion (from TBM, sarcoid, pulmonary HTN and subglottic stenosis). Ultimately, proBNP was in the 1900s and patient was 19 pounds over his dry weight. Patient was diuresed to negative ~2 liters daily and lost 12 pounds in fluid. ECHO showed generally stable cardiac function/anatomy, although was suboptimal. Sleep saw the patient in-house for possible in-patient sleep study but given the patient's obstructive and central sleep apnea components, he requires a remote physician sleep study that can not be coordinated in-house. The patient will follow up in sleep clinic on ___ with possible improvement in his pulmonary status and ultimately, improvement/alleviation of cardiac issues. The patient was instructed to continue sleeping upright to manage the hypoxemia associated with his sleep apnea and pulmonary conditions (tracheobronchomalacia, subglottic stenosis etc). . # Acute on chronic renal failure: Creatinine baseline 1.3-1.5. Patient's creatinine bumped to 1.9 but ultimately was 1.6 upon discharge. Slight bump may have been due to poor forward flow/perfusion due to hypervolemic state, which responded to diuresis. Patient was also likely experiencing poor PO intake secondary to general malaise over the weekend from volume overload. The patient's bactrim had recently been switched to moxifloxacin and his lisinopril and spironolactone continue to be held. Also avoided nephrotoxins and renally dosed Allopurinol at 150mg daily vs 300mg (CrClearance 55) . #. Rhythm: Patient is ventricularly paced with no new rhythm changes on EKG. Patient does have ICD in place, also and a history of atrial fibrillation. Patient's primary cardiologist, Dr. ___ failure physician, ___ both notified of his admission and recommended ongoing intravenous lasix diuresis. He was monitored on telemetry and did have a few episodes of nonsustained VTach. The patient's electrolytes were repleted aggressively and he was continued on Coumadin (for difficult to control, unable to cardiovert in the past) atrial fibrillation. Patient was also continued on Metoprolol tartrate and Digoxin for rate control . # Right lateral shin wound: Followed in Wound Care clinic and by ___ services, healing slowly but appropriately. Had been on three week course of Bactrim, switched to Moxifloxacin on ___ for 10 day course in setting of acute renal insufficiency. Wound care consult was called inhouse with recommendations. Patient did also inadvertently kick his table with bruising, ecchymosis and tenderness/swelling of his left second toe. The patient declined xray to evaluate for possible fractures to the area that could potentially be intervened upon, demonstrating understanding of the risks and benefits of declining further imaging. . # Depression: Stable. Continued on Zoloft . #. Code: FULL, confirmed with patient
summary: You were admitted with significant shortness of breath and leg swelling. It was felt you had an exacerbation of your heart failure and was retaining fluid, given the recent stopping of your Lasix, Lisinopril and Spironolactone. Your fluid was removed by intravenous Lasix with good effect and your breathing/moving improved by time of discharge. ECHO (ultrasound of the heart) showed that your heart function is unchanged from before. Your kidney function (creatinine) had also improved. The Sleep Doctors saw ___ in the hospital. They will schedule the special sleep study for you, likely for ___ and contact you with time/instructions.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ w/ hx of afib, PPM, hypothyroid, CKD, PE presents after fall x 2 at home. Active Issues: #s/p mechanical fall on coumadin: Pt has long hx of mechanical falls. Fall does not appear to be syncopal or cardiac in nature: denies any dizziness/lightheadedness/CP/palpitations prior to fall. Imaging all negative. Neuro exam remained unchanged throughout hospital stay. Pt kept on coumadin due to her hx of recurrent PEs. Pt evaluated by ___ and deemed to need rehab. #recurrant UTIs: has extensive hx of multi-drug resistant UTIs, possible assymptomatic bactiuria vs true UTI. Pt currently assymptomatic--no dysuria, no hematuria, no foul smelling urine. No systematic leukocytosis or fevers. Does have frequency and incontinence but this seems to be baseline for her. Urine cx did show >100,000 e.coli. Pt received 1 dose of ceftriaxone in ED and 1 dose of zosyn on the floor but abx stopped as pt thought to be assymptomatic. A foley was placed to monitor her I/O after lasix started as pt is incontinent. She has follow up with Dr. ___ urology. She has outpt cystoscopy scheduled. # Hypothyroid: Pt was lethargic, although denies cold intolerance or constipation. TSH elevated to 12 with low T3 (38), T4 (3.9). Synthroid increased to 150 mcg daily. Repeat TSH needs to be checked in 4 weeks. # dCHF, acute on chronic: Pt's lasst exacerbation in ___ (>60%) with worsening mitral regurg. Pt noncompliant with lasix--states it makes her urinate too frequently. On admission, pt fluid overloaded on exam and admits 2 pillow orthopnea plus DOE Her weight was about 6 lb up from admission in ___. Pt received 40 IV lasix BID for 2 days and 40 IV lasix daily for 2 days. Pt d/c home on 40 mg lasix PO daily. #generalized fatigue: Pt states she has been more tired in past weeks to months. Most likely multifactoral--untreated CHF with volume overload vs hypothyroid vs deconditioning with poor po intake vs depression vs infection. Pt seems much improved with diuresis and it seems untreated CHF playing large part. Hypothyroid could have also been also contributing: TSH 12, T3 38, T4 2.9--> pt's synthroid increased to 150 mcg daily. Infection was ruled out as pt was afebrile, ___ ct wnl, has cough but is chronic, CXR wnl, denies urinary symptoms. Statin myopathy was also ruled out as CK was in normal range. Although pt endorsed decrease PO intake, her albumin was 3.8 Chronic Medical issues: # afib: Pt in rate controlled afib. Continued coumadin: 5 mg ___, 7.5 mg all other days. -Pt subtherapeutic on ___ with INR of 1.7 -coumadin increased on ___ to 10 mg -f/u INR on ___ was 2.0 -home regimen resumed #tachy/___ syndrome s/p PPM: fall appears mechanical and not syncopal. Thus pacemaker interrogation was not deemed to be necessary. #Hypertensive: Metoprolol 50 daily was continued. #asthma: Continued albuterol PRN #GERD: Continued home omeprazole 40 mg daily #HLD: Continued simvastatin 20 mg daily #chronic knee pain s/p bilateral knee replacements: Continue tramadol 50 mg Q4 PRN and acetaminphen 500 mg Q4 PRN #depression: Continue home sertaline 100 mg daily Transitional Issues: -Pt should have TSH rechecked in ___ weeks (mid ___ due to recent synthroid adjustment. -Pt should follow up with urology about her persistant UTIs vs assymptomatic bactiuria, has outpt cystoscopy. -Dry weight unclear at the time of discharge. Discharged on 40mg po furosemide. Would continue to monitor daily weights and uptitrate furosmide if weight increasing. -Please check INR on ___ and adjust coumadin dose accordingly -Pt lives alone with home services. She is looking for an assisted living facility. -Repeat CXR or CT Chest in 4 weeks to assess for resolution of RUL infiltrate / opacity seen on CT C-spine on ___ (see above in PERTINENT RESULTS or in OMR for report). -PENDING STUDIES AT TIME OF DISCHARGE: NONE
summary: You were admitted to the hospital after you fell twice. We did multiple X-rays which showed you did not break any bones after your falls. You were also found to have bacteria in your urine. Since you did not have any symptoms of a urinary tract infection (UTI) such as burning when you urinate, smelly urine, or blood in your urine, we decided to not give you antibiotics. You were also very tired when you came to the hospital and had some trouble breathing. This was because you had fluid in your lungs because you have heart failure. We gave you lasix which helped get the fluid off your lungs and made you breath better. Please make sure you continue to take the lasix when you leave the hospital. This will help keep fluid off your lungs and help you to breath better and be less tired.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ w/ COPD, AFib, HTN, DMII, GERD, stress CM (clean cath ___ at ___ per daughter), ___, now who presented from NH with dyspnea. # Dyspnea: Likely multifactorial from diastolic CHF exacerbation and baseline COPD: She presented with clinical CHF exacerbation given new peripheral edema and crackles on lung exam, though CXR without substantial pulmonary edema. BNP 200s 1 month ago at OSH, now proBNP 20,000+ here. No new pneumonia on admission CXR. PE was less likely given anticoagulation on warfarin (though subtherapeutic recently). ECHO showed isolated apical left ventricular hypertrophy with normal left ventricular cavity size is normal. No left ventricular apical aneurysm is seen. Overall left ventricular systolic function is normal with EF 55%, mild MR, moderate TR. Echocardiographic findings were consistent with apical (___) hypertrophic cardiomyopathy. She was diuresed with IV Lasix until she showed signs of euvolemia and contraction alkalosis. This resulted in improvement in shortness of breath. She was transitioned to PO regimen prior to discharge. She received inhaled Fluticasone, received guaifenesin / dextromethorphan and Tesselon perles for cough. She was given Duonebs for relief of wheezing / SOB. She was discharged on metoprolol 100XL, lisinopril 5mg. Her Oxygen was weaned on discharge from 4LNC to 2LNC, which she was wearing primarily for comfort. # NSTEMI: Trops elevated to 0.25 at OSH, now downtrending. Dynamic EKG changes noted new from prior. Also had intermittent chest pain though not different from baseline. Has history of Takotsubo cardiomyopathy. She had normal left heart catheterization in ___ without evidence of substantial coronary stenosis. She was initially placed on heparin and nitroglycerin drips, which were subsequently discontinued given low suspicion for ACS. # Abdominal pain: Chronic for months to years. Previous workup has included gastric motility study and CT abd/pelvis which did not show etiology of pain. Omeprazole was continued. Symptoms were chronic for months to years. Previous workup has included gastric motility study and CT abd/pelvis which did not show etiology of pain. GI consulted who recommended EGD as outpatient after optimization of respiratory status and outpatient work up for lactose/fructose intolerance, and bacterial overgrowth. CHRONIC ISSUES: # Anemia: Stable on anticoagulation # T2DM: Held home nateglinide, restart as outpatient # Constipation: Continue senna / colace standing, bisacodyl / miralax prn # Chronic cough: Continue guaifenesin # Atrial fibrillation: Anticoagulation as above # Anxiety: Held ALPRAZolam 0.25 mg PO TID:PRN on admission, resumed prior to discharge. TRANSITIONAL: - patient should followup with PCP - patient ___ followup with GI to arrange EGD as outpatient if patient continues to have bloating and abdominal distention with meals. - Continue low lactose, low fat diet as may be triggering patient's IBS - please weight patient in morning at same time everyday, if patient gains more than 1kg (3lbs), please give 80mg IV lasix. Her dry weight is 62.4kg (137.6lb). - please monitor electrolytes once a week, ensure K>4, Mg>2. - please consider discharge on home oxygen therapy per daughter and patient's preference. Room air saturation was 88% in the hospital. CORE MEASURE: # CODE: DNR/DNI # CONTACT: ___ (son) ___
summary: You were admitted to the hospital due to shortness of breath. You had elevated cardiac labs concerning for a type of heart attack ___ elevation myocardial infarction). You had an echocardiogram that showed a heart disorder called "Apical (___) hypertrophic cardiomyopathy" which is due to hypertrophy ("thickening") of part of the base of the heart. This was consistent on your prior echocardiogram in ___. In ___, you also had a cardiac catheterization what showed no coronary artery disease so we did not repeat this. We felt that your shortness of breath was likely caused by a combination of a resolving pneumonia, chronic obstruction lung disease and heart failure exacerbation. You were also seen by our gastroenterologists who felt that you bloating and abdominal distention were concerning for lactose intolerance or irritable bowel syndrome. They recommended avoiding lactose or high fat foots.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ yo female w/ history of lifelong history of insulin-dependent diabetes complicated with nephropathy, neuropathy, and CAD w/AMI in ___ requiring stenting, and extended rehabilitation at ___ for ___, presents with a brief syncopal episode that occurred this morning. ACUTE: #Presyncope/syncope presumed Autonomic Dysfunction: The differential for this is broad and includes orthostatic, vasovagal, arrhythmias, cardiomyopathy, hypoglycemia, seizure, Carotid Sinus Hypersensitivity, MI, PE, and outflow obstruction. She was monitored on telemetry without arrythmia, no evidence of EKG changes or MI (by EKG or biomarkers). Orthostatics negative. She had 1 symptomatic episode with no changes on telemetry but BP fell to 87/40. Did not lose consciousness or fall. Rested for 5 min and BP came back up to 100s. Prior EEG with no seizure activity, and Brain MRA/MRI and Neck MRA that showed some diffuse atherosclerosis and narrowing but did no carotid stenosis or overwhelming vascular narrowing). We made some changes to BP meds as below and recommended that she follow up in Autonomic Neurology Clinic. CHRONIC: #DM1, controlled with comps: Continued home Lantus and HISS scale. #Anemia (___): At baseline, continued to monitor. #Hypothyroidism: Continued home Levothyroxine. #CAD, native: s/p NSTEMI ___. s/p PCI to RCA with Integriti stent ___. Continued ASA, Plavix, metoprolol, isosorbide. #Ischemic cardiomyopathy/systolic CHF: Inf wall hypokinesis and LVEF 45% per echo ___ post NSTEMI, ___ class I-II. Continued home metoprolol, Lasix TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: Recommended that she follow up in Autonomic Neurology Clinic
summary: You were admitted due to these episodes that ___ were having where ___ felt like ___ were going to pass out. Your family reported that ___ were not acting like yourself following these events. ___ did not have any blood pressure changes on going from laying to sitting to standing. ___ were monitored on telemtry (heart monitoring). ___ had one event and your blood pressure fell to 87/40. ___ sat down and let it pass and your blood pressure came back into the normal range. ___ had lab tests that did not show any damage to your heart or any other causes for these episodes. ___ did not have any of these episodes while ___ were in the ___. During your last hospitalization ___ had a Brain MRI and EEG that did not show any cause for these episodes either. In clonclusion we believe these episodes are likely due to changes in blood flow to your brain due to transient changes in your blood pressure likely related to your heart failure and autonomic dysfunction.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ year old man with past medical history significant for COPD, congestive heart failure, recent pulmonary embolism, and pneumonia admitted with respiratory distress, bacteremia. . # Bacteremia/Sepsis: Blood cultures drawn on admission grew Enterococcus faecalis. He was started on broad spectrum antibiotics including linezolid given concern for potential VRE as he had recently been on vancomycin for treatment of pneumonia. Sensitivities later revealed pan-sensitive enterococcus, and linezolid was changed to ampicillin. He did have an episode of tachycardia and hypotension on the floor, which responded to intravenous fluids. The infectious disease service was consulted. A trans-thoracic echo was performed to evaluate for possible vegetation, which showed an echodense structure on the aortic valve. To further evaluate, an transesophageal echo was obtained, which showed no endocarditis. Subsequent cultures were negative, although the patient's VRE rectal swab was positive. . # COPD/Respiratory Distress: On ___, the patient was transferred to the ICU for tachypnea and a worsening oxygen requirement in the setting of IV fluid administration for hypotension. It is likely that, in this patient with heart failure and advanced COPD on 2L home O2, he has limited pulmonary reserve so that even mild pulmonary edema result in significant distress. He was given IV lasix boluses and nebulizer treatments with significant improvement in his respiratory status. There was no evidence of pulmonary infection on imaging and urine legionella was negative. He was transferred back to the medicine floor on ___. He was continued on outpatient tiotropium and inhaled steroids. . # Lower GI Bleed: On ___, the patient passed bloody bowel movements. His hematocrit dropped to 22. He was transferred back to the MICU. He underwent EGD, which was normal. He required transfusion of four units of packed RBCs. He underwent CT angiogram of his abdomen, which was negative for aortoenteric fistula. The patient subsequently underwent colonoscopy which was unrevealing for acute bleed, but erythematous mucosa was biopsied to evaluate for arteriovenous malformation. His aspirin and warfarin had been held, but were restarted on his day of transfer to rehab, as his hematocrit had been stable with no further bleeds for about a week. . # UTI. Urine culture with sensitive Klebsiella. He completed a seven day course of ciprofloxacin. . # Hyponatremia: Variably attributed to SIADH and hyervolemic hyponatremia. The patient was placed on conservative fluid restrictions. Renal was consulted and the patient was also diuresed with IV and PO furosemide. Sodium levels subsequently normalized. . # Atrial Fibrillation: He was in atrial fibrillation with variable rate control. He was continued on rate control with lopressor and diltiazem. He was anticoagulated with warfarin for thromboembolic prophylaxis with a goal INR of ___. Following his lower GI bleed, his systemic anticoagulation was held. He and the rehabilitation center were instructed to restart his warfarin (and aspirin) on the day of transfer. . #. Weakness/tremor: Believed to be related to recent addition of citalopram. The patient also takes trazodone, but did not display any other signs of serotonin syndrome such as nausea/vomitting/diarrhea. His tremor persisted to varying degrees . #. Pulmonary hypertension/PE: No formal workup in setting of subacute pulmonary embolus. Will need outpatient follow up. PE Likely contributing to right ventricular volume overload and pulmonary hypertension as above. Patient tolerated systemic anticoagulation until he experienced his GI bleed (see above). Warfarin was subsequently held as noted above. . #. CLL: The patient's WBC count trended down from its baseline level (___) when the patient developed bacteremia (see above). As his bacteremia was treated, his WBC count rose to a level consistent with his baseline level.
summary: You came to the hospital for weakness, tremor and fatigue. You were found to have bacteria in your blood. You were given antibiotics intravenously and your symptoms improved. You also had a urinary tract infection that improved with antibiotics. You had respiratory distress and had to go to the intensive care unit, where your breathing improved with medications to help remove fluid from your body. You later developed gastrointestinal bleeding and you had an upper endoscopy as well as a colonoscopy. Your bleeding stopped, and your blood count was subsequently stable. Please complete a 14-day course of antibiotics to end on ___.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Mr. ___ is a ___ year old man with PMHx of recurrent SVT (on atenolol prior to admission), type II diabetes mellitus (on metformin), ___, HTN, HLD, current smoker, who presented with ___ edema and weight gain, found to have new AF+RVR and HFrEF. == ACTIVE ISSUES == # Acute-on-chronic Systolic CHF: First episode of HFrEF, with EF 28%. Has known history of SVT (on atenolol). Notable lab findings negative (TSH, HIV, Tox screen), no significant CAD on coronary angiography. As such, presentation concerning for tachycardia-induced cardiomyopathy. Possibly Takotsubo cardiomyopathy, i/s/o recent life stressors, although appearance on TTE/TEE less typical. He was diuresed with IV furosemide and transitioned to torsemide for discharge. Atenolol converted to metoprolol. Spironolactone added. Lisinopril increased for afterload reduction. Did not tolerate hydralazine given hypotension. Digoxin loaded, and continued at 0.125 mg daily. -- Admission weight: 98.4 kg -- Discharge weight: 86 kg -- Discharge diuretic regimen: torsemide 10 mg daily, spironolactone 25 mg daily -- Will need digoxin level & Chem-10 checked on ___ # Atrial fibrillation with RVR: Hx of SVT for years, per patient, for which he was taking atenolol. Noted here to be in AF with rapid response (rates up to 180s) but hemodynamically stable (even with HRs in the 180s). Underwent TEE in preparation for DCCV, though ___ thrombus identified; DCCV abandoned at that point. Apixaban started (10 mg BID x7 days, then 5 mg BID [thrombus dosing]). Plan to delay DCCV until resolution ___ thrombus. -- Anticipate TEE/___ after 1 month of apixaban therapy, if no thrombus identified # ___: found on TEE, secondary to AF. Apixaban started. == CHRONIC ISSUES == # Demand ischemia Slight troponin elevation on admission (.01<-.02). Likely ___ CHF and afib/RVR. Cardiac cath without significant CAD. Pt initially received aspirin, which was transitioned to apixaban (as above). Also started atorvastatin 40mg daily. # R flank ecchymosis: Noted after sleeping on telemetry box. Hgb remained stable. Suspect traumatic, and no evidence to consider RP bleeding. # ___: Patient with baseline ___ (Cr 1.1-1.3). Remained stable while in house. -- Discharge Cr: 1.3 # Type II diabetes mellitus: A1C 7.1% but uptrending. ISS while in house, but metformin at home. Resumed metformin at discharge. Will need PCP follow up for optimization. # Cigarette smoking: Smokes ___ ppd. Counseled on smoking cessation. == TRANSITIONAL ISSUES == -- Admission weight: 98.4 kg -- Discharge weight: 86 kg -- Discharge diuretic regimen: torsemide 10 mg daily, spironolactone 25 mg daily ## LEFT ATRIAL THROMBUS: Continue loading of apixaban 10 mg BID until ___. On ___, start maintenance dosing of apixaban 5 mg BID ## SYSTOLIC HEART FAILURE: will need to check digoxin level as well as Chem-10 (___). ## ATRIAL FIBRILLATION: given ___, unable to cardiovert during admission. Will need apixaban for 1 month prior to TEE/___ ## GERD: Patient with abdominal distension, started on pantoprazole and Sucralfate as outpatient. Not currently reporting GI upset. Suspect these symptoms were symptoms of CHF. Stopped pantoprazole and sucralfate. == NOTABLE MEDICATIONS == ** PLEASE REVIEW DISCHARGE MEDICATION WORKSHEET ** -- STOPPED medications: atenolol, tadalafil, aspirin, simvastatin, pantoprazole, sucralfate -- NEW medications: spironolactone, torsemide, metoprolol succinate, apixaban, digoxin, atorvastatin -- CHANGED medications: lisinopril (increased to 40 mg) == LABS == Will need labs drawn on ___ -- digoxin level, Chem-10
summary: You were admitted to ___ because you had leg swelling. Your symptoms were related to heart failure and atrial fibrillation. We gave you Lasix, which helped remove the fluid. We gave you Metoprolol, ******Lisinopril******, Spironolactone, Hydralazine, and Atorvastatin, which also treated the heart failure. In addition to the Metroprolol, we also gave you Digoxin and Apixaban to treat the atrial fibrillation. Based on the echocardiogram that showed blood clots in your heart, you were unable to undergo cardioversion for the atrial fibrillation. The large bruise on your right side was determined to be not clinically concerning based on your stable blood lab values. Your diabetes was treated with insulin, and was switched back to your home Metformin upon discharge.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ y/o male with PMHx of Ischemic cardiomyopathy, severe AS, Severe PVD and other medical comorbidities admitted after PEA arrest with CHF exacerbation s/p diuresis and hemodynamic optimization # PUMP: ECHO shows LVEF of 45% with mild MR ___ AS. ___ patient was aggressively diuresed and put on nitro gtt titrated to appropriate BPs while in the ICU. He was optimized hemodynamically with captopril and metoprolol tartrate. These medications were then switched to Lisinopril 10 mg daily and PO Lasix as well as Sprironolactone. Patient was over net 6.5 L out, on nasal cannula and discharge. # CORONARIES: The patient was originally billed as a STEMI when transferred to ___ and sent directly to cath lab where 80% occlusion of LAD, 40% LCx, and 100% RCA although with adequate collaterals were found. Given the collaterals supported adequate flow, the CAD was thought to be chronic in nature and no intervention was performed in cath lab. ST depressions noted on EKGs from OSH. The patient was placed on heparin. The patient refused CABG but will discuss the issue further with his outpatient cardiologist. Carotid ultrasound was obtained for pre-op evaluation should the patient decide to pursue coronary artery bypass. The right carotid was found to be occluded and the left 40% stenosed. Aspirin and atorvastatin were continued. The patient's heparin was discontinued on ___ as the patient's clinical status improved. The patient did not have any remarkable arrhythmias secondary to condition during this hospital stay. # PERIPHERAL VASCULAR DISEASE: A CTA of the lower extremities was done, showing the following: 1. Filling defect in a moderate segment of the right superficial femoral artery may represent thrombus versus dissection. This was discussed with Dr. ___ by phone at 10:20 a.m. on ___ after attending review. 2. Occluded dorsalis pedis and plantar arteries in the right foot. 3. Significant atherosclerotic disease in the lower extremity vasculature as described above. 4. Diverticulosis without diverticulitis. On the night of admission his right foot was noted to be cool to touch without an appreciation for a dorsalis pedis pulse. Vascular surgery was consulted which led to the CTA of the ___ as above. A heparin drip was started. The vascular surgery team did not recommend intervention as he is a poor surgical candidate and the operation would be major. His right lower extremity became warmer over the next few days and a pulse returned without clinical consequence to his R foot. Inactive issues: DM2: The patient has known non-insulin dependent diabetes mellitus, type 2. His metformin was discontinued on admission to the hospital and his glucose was kept under good control with insulin. HTN: The patient's ACE inhibitor was continued. His metoprolol was increased to 125 mg BID. Transitional Issues: Severe three vessel disease: The patient deferred CABG while in hospital but should discuss the issue further with his outpatient cardiologist. Diabetes: The patient will be discharged on his pre-admission dose of metformin without any insulin.
summary: You came to the hospital after a cardiac arrest at an outside hospital with congestive heart failure and intubated (tube in your airway). We gave you lasix (a water pill) and helped you diurese off the extra fluid. You improved with the diuresis and also with control of your blood pressure using Metoprolol Tartrate 125mg twice a day and decreased your previous dose of Lisinopril to 10mg daily. The Metoprolol was higher than your home dose because we wanted to tightly control your heart rate given your severe aortic stenosis. As you were being treated for your heart failure, we were able to wean you off the ventilator that you came to ___ requiring and eventually extubate it. You will be discharged on Metoprolol Tartrate 125 mg BID, Lasix 20 mg PO daily, Spironolactone 12.5 mg daily (new medication), and Lisinopril 10 mg. These are for your congestive heart failure and blood pressure. For your coronary artery disease, we put you on medical management including Aspirin 325mg, Atorvastatin 20mg, and the above doses of your Metoprolol and Lisinopril. After your cardiac catherization, you developed significant ischemia in your right foot. You had lost pulses and it was becoming cold. CT scan showed a possible dissection of your R femoral artery. We consulted vascular surgery who suggested we put you on a Heparin drip (a blood thinner) and your pulses returned and your foot became warm and pink. We removed the Heparin on ___ with a plan of continuing to examine the foot to ensure adequate blood flow. After your catherization, you also developed a hematoma in your right thigh. The source was unsure at first with an unidentified reason for your hemoglobin dropping. Later, with a CT scan, your thigh was found to have a 10x10cm hematoma. You were transfused with a total of 4 units of Red Blood Cells and your hemoglobin was checked to make sure that you no longer were bleeding. Your Heparin was also adjusted at that time. Your blood counts have recovered from that episode. You have significant coronary artery disease and valve disease that we believe cannot be treated appropriately with medicines alone. We have recommended to receive open heart surgery to help treat these problems. You refused these procedures on multiple occasions. A carotid artery ultrasound (a non-invasive test looking at the blood vessels in your neck) was done showing complete blockage of your right carotid artery and a 40% narrowing of your left.

text: Brief Hospital Course: The patient is a ___ year old male with CHF (LVEF unknown), atrial fibrillation, hypertension, back pain, osteoarthritis, cataracts, hearing loss, and memory loss who presented with two weeks of progressive dyspnea and a 15 lb weight gain consistent with a CHF exacerbation. ACTIVE ISSUES: # Acute on Chronic Diastolic CHF: He has a history of CHF, RV predominant, likely secondary to left-sided diastolic CHF. At presentation he had bilateral pleural effusion, distended neck veins, and significant pedal, scrotal, and penile edema consistent with biventricular failure. Echo showed preserved LVEF, markedly dilated right ventricle with severe pulmonary hypertension and moderate-to-severe tricuspid regurgitation, marked ventricular interaction secondary to severe right ventricular pressure and volume overload, and prominent pleural effusions and ascites. ___ cardiology was consulted. He was diuresed with Lasix IV and then switch to high PO regimen for discharge. After being stable on PO for 24 hours he was discharged to rehab for continued care. Weights obtained were not consistent but based on UOP and clinically he was markedly improved. He will follow up with ___ cardiology. # Atrial Fibrillation, Bradycardia: No longer anticoagulated with warfarin out of concern for falls at home (confirmed with HCP and home health aide). He was monitored on telemetry, which showed irregularly irregular bradycardia, at times to the ___, though asymptomatic wit stable blood pressures, with frequent PVCs and intermittent, brief runs of asymptomatic NSVT. During his episodes of bradycardia he was completely asymptomatic when awake and while sleeping. His electrolytes were monitored and replaced as needed. His home ASA was continued. # Concerns for falls at home: He worked with ___ during this admission, who recommended it was safer that he go to rehab for further recovery and ___ after discharge. He has a ___ for when he goes home. Per ___, he should ambulate with a walker. # Change of code status: The patient decided to become full code during this admission. He cited as his reasons the desire to help those who would be treating him learn from the experience. CHRONIC ISSUES: # Depression / Dementia: His mood was bright during this admission and he remained well-oriented and communicative during this admission. His home sertraline and risperidone were continued. # Benign Hypertension: Pressures were stable during diuresis. He was continued on his home Losartan. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: - He should follow-up with Atris cardiology as an outpatient. ## Contact information: Outpt case manager, ___ (___) Phone ___, ___ HCP and power of attorney ___ ___
summary: You were admitted to the ___ because of increasing shortness of breath and leg swelling. We determined that you have an exacerbation of congestive heart failure (a condition in which fluid backs up from the heart), resulting in fluid in the lungs as well as, quite prominently, in your legs. We treated you with intravenous diuretics (like your water pill) with close monitoring of your electrolytes. Your swelling diminished markedly and you are ready to be discharged. You have been switched to a new oral lasix regimen (increased dose) that you can take at home. Before going home, though, we have discharged you to a rehab facility where you can work with physical therapists to regain your strength and balance. Please review your medication list closely.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ with afib s/p pacer for tachy/brady, b/l fem artery occlusion s/p thombectomy, dCHF, hemorrhagic and ischemic strokes with residual global aphasia, and PEG tube placement on ___, who presented with increased somnolence after recent discharge, blood-tinged vomiting x1 day, and intermittent agitation. Active Issues: ==================== #Encephalopathy: Toxic-metabolic. Pt was agitated, more somnolent, and less interactive than usual. Head CT showed no acute changes. CXR showed pneumonia, which was treated with abx (see below). Workup was otherwise negative for infection. Normal EEG x24 hours. Due to sub-therapeutic tegretol level, dose was increased to 100mg TID (previously BID). Her neurological and mental status changes were thought to be related to infection, including recrudescence of stroke findings. Pt improved and was discharged at mental status baseline. #Pneumonia: Elevated white count, elevated lactate, change in mental status in the setting of bilateral PNA on CXR. Presentation consistent with hospital-acquired pneumonia (recent hospitalization ___ vs. aspiration pneumonia i/s/o poor swallowing function and recent vomiting. Pt was afebrile during admission, and blood cultures were negative. She received broad spectrum antibiotics, which were narrowed to Levaquin after clinical improvement and a negative MRSA swab. She completed her 8 day abx course on ___. # Bruising # GI bleed: # Acute blood loss anemia: The patient's H&H improved after one unit of blood and remained stable. Melena improved and G-tube output remained clear. GI bleed was likely due to slow-ooze from PEG i/s/o coagulopathy while on lovenox, which may be have been over-dosed given patient BMI and/or ineffectively cleared. Gastroenterology was consulted and recommended no need for EGD given clinical improvement and no need outpt followup of G-tube. Her proton-pump inhibitor was discontinued as she does not need ulcer prophylaxis. Hgb on discharge 9.1. #Peripheral artery disease with arterial thromboses #Stroke: INR was subtherapeutic at 1.1, possibly related to some Vit K in tube feeds. Her dose was increased to 2mg while in house, will be followed closely by ___ clinic after d/c. Hematology was consulted and recommended avoiding lovenox bridging in the future. INR remained subtherapeutic at 1.2 after resuming tube feeds. #Acute Renal Failure: Likely prerenal i/s/o infection and emesis. Pt was volume resuscitated to euvolemia. Cr improved slightly to 1.2 with fluids and resuming tube feeds. #Moderate Malnutrition: s/p PEG tube placement on ___. Pt will continue bolus tube feeds at home: Jevity 1.2@ 4.5 cans/day. Per nutrition, Vit K stable when tube feeds provided consistently. Chronic Issues: ================== #dCHF: LVEF >55%. Torsemide was held. Pt did receive 40mg IV Lasix x1 dose given concern for volume overload on exam. Pt remained euvolemic thereafter, and did not receive further IV fluids or diuretics. #HTN- continued amlodipine, carvedilol. #Afib #CVA #PAD s/p bilateral ileofemoral thrombolectomies Recent admission on vascular service and warfarin initiated. Will continue warfarin until ___ **TRANSITIONAL ISSUES** ========================= -Discuss code status with family, currently remains FULL CODE after discussions while in house -Neurology recommended repeating tegretol level in ___ weeks after discharge (was 4.2 on ___ which is lower limit of normal) -Measure INR on ___ and follow-up with ___ clinic so that they can titrate warfarin dose -D/c warfarin after 3 months postop: Pt was originally prescribed warfarin for only 3 months post-thrombectomy.
summary: You came to the hospital because you were very sleepy and were not acting like yourself. You also came in because you were bleeding from your G-tube and had blood in your stool. While you were in the hospital, you were found to have pneumonia (infection in your lungs). Your neurological symptoms were most likely related to your infection. You were treated with antibiotics and intravenous fluids. Your Lovenox was stopped, and you should not be bridged with Lovenox again in the future. Your tube-feeds were initially stopped due to bleeding. Your Coumadin and tube feeds were restarted when you were no longer bleeding.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ASSESSMENT AND PLAN: ___ yo woman with dCHF, CAD, HLD admitted for worsening DOE, weight gain, and pedal edema in the setting of inadequate home diuresis most consistent with decompensated dCHF. Also found to have ___ and ___ that is consistent with prior exacerbations without CP or EKG changes. # Acute decompensated ___: Patient has had difficult to manage CHF given borderline blood pressures and history of orthostatic hypotension. On her last admission her carvedilol and torsemide were held for this reason. Her dry weight is likely around 110 lbs and she was 126.8 lbs on admission, now ___ lbs. Had episode of acute tachypnea/hypoxemia overnight that lasted <30 minutes. Unclear if this was flash pulmonary edema (no ischemic change on EKG), as no enzymes or CXR. She was eventually diuresed to around 104 lbs (46.8kg), which may be below her dry weight. She had an increase in BUN, Cr with this. Her diuretics were held for a day and her kidney function improved. She was discharged home with Rx for 10mg torsemide daily and lab check in a few days. Spironolactone, atorvastatin, and metoprolol were started. BPs tolerated this with sBP in 100-110s. # Hypoxemic respiratory distress: Subacute to chronic. Has home O2 now on 2L. PFTs ___ showed mild obstructive pattern, likely from 30 pack years. CXR on admission shows mild congestion and edema w/ R pleural effusion. Had episode of acute tachypnea/hypoxemia a couple of days into admission that lasted <30 minutes. Unclear if this was flash pulmonary edema (no ischemic change on EKG), as no enzymes or CXR. Was tolerating room air on discharge without hypoxemia, however has some desats with exertion. Home O2 (2L) was continued. # ___: NSTEMI not likely from plaque rupture but from demand ischemia ___ high wall stress in setting of hypervolemia. Did not start heparin on admission and patient remains CP free with flat enzymes. Has had low level troponin elevations in the setting of CHF exacerbations but has declined cath in past and is CP free. Trop 0.17-0.19 x3, CKMB 3 x3. She was started on metoprolol on discharge. Her aspirin/clopidogrel was continued. # Acute on chronic renal insufficiency: Likely secondary to dCHF and renal venous congestion. Improved with diuresis, to 1.1, then worsened as she became slightly hypovolemic to 1.6. Improved again with holding diuretics x1 day prior to discharge. Discharge Cr 1.5. # POAG: Continued glaucoma meds while in house, though some home meds not formulary and were substituted. # Hypothyroidism: On home levothyroxine. TSH normal. # HLD: Started on fish oil ___ because history of statin myalgias. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: -needs assessment of BP on metoprolol, spironolactone -needs outpatient chemistry f/u to make sure she has improved renal function and does not become hyperkalemic on spironolactone
summary: you were admitted with worsening shortness of breath and edema in your legs. This was due to an exacerbation of your heart failure. While you were here there was some death of some of your heart muscle, which is probably associated with the fluid overload of your heart failure. You were treated with diuretics to remove fluid. You lost weight, felt better, and required less oxygen. At discharge your weight was 102 lbs which is slightly lower than your normal dry weight of 107 lbs. Your kidney numbers were slightly elevated from diuresis but are improving at the time of discharge. You should have these checked later in the week to make sure it continues to improve.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Pt is a ___ y.o woman h.o CAD, s/p CABG, CHF (EF 40-45%), HTN, HL, DM, IBS who experienced presycope. . #Presyncope: The most likely etiology for the patient's presyncopal episode is orthostasis. The patient is on diuretics at home, and admitted to one week of diarrhea and poor PO fluid intake. The patient was found to be persistently orthostatic in ED despite 3L NS, though her vital signs had normalized without orthstasis at the time of assessment on the floor. The patient had a negative ischemic workup, and there was no other cardiac or neurologic etiology for her symptoms. She was maintained on telemetry while hospitalized, in NSR with occasional PVCs. No atrial fibrillation. Enalapril and lasix were held on admission, with blood pressures in the normal range thoughout her hospitalization. She was restarted on enalapril at the time of discharge, and was instructed to begin taking her home lasix dose the day following discharge, and to discuss dosing with her PCP at her ___ visit. . #CAD: The patient is s/p CABG/stenting. She is on metoprolol and enalapril, as an outpatient. As above, enalapril and lasix were held on admission. Recent persantine MIBI (___) showed no reversible defects but a known irreversible defect. The patient had a negative ischemic workup. . #Chronic systolic CHF: She has had no recent overt signs of decompensated acute systolic heart failure, and was presumed hypovolemic on admission. She is known to have chronic systolic heart failure, with chronic dyspnea and fatigue, chronic for years. She is on metoprolol, enalapril, and lasix as an outpatient. As above, enalapril and lasix were held on admission. Recent persantine MIBI (___) showed left ventricular ejection fraction is 32% which has decreased from 43% on ___. . #Paroxysmal SVT: Coumadin was continued in-house for goal ___. Of note, the patient was recently seen by her cardiologist at the end of ___, and had ICD interrogation, which revealed no episodes of ventricular tachycardia. She was found at that time to have episodes of atrial tachycardia up to 8 minutes in length. . #DM2- The patient's last HbA1c was 9 in ___ and 9.8 previously. She was continued on her outpatient lantus dosing with HISS. She was maintained on a diabetic diet with fingersticks QID. . #Hypothyroidism: The patient was continued on her home levothyroxine dose, with no clinical signs of hyper- or hypothyroidism.
summary: You were admitted for evaluation of your lightheadedness. In the emergency department, you were found to have elevated heart rate with standing, and were given three liters of intravenous fluids. Your creatinine was also mildly elevated from your baseline over the past few months. Chest x-ray revealed no acute process, and laboratory data showed that you had not had a heart attack. You were admitted to the hospital for rehydration. Your blood pressure and heart rate were stable with standing after the intravenous fluids, and your symptoms were improved. Your enalapril was initially held in the emergency room, and restarted on discharge. Your lasix was held during hospitalization as below.

text: Brief Hospital Course: This is a ___ F with history of ischemic, dilated cardiomyopathy (EF35%), DMII, HTN, HL, and CKD (baseline creat 1.1-1.5) who presents with a 10 pound weight gain, vomiting episodes concerning for CHF exacerbation. . ACTIVE ISSUES # Acute on Chronic systolic failure: Pt has known EF 35-40% with inferior/inferilateral hypokinesis. Her CHF exacerbation most likely developed in the setting of excessive salt/water intake at home (2 liters fluid daily). Given the history of hypotension, there might be a component of cardiogenic shock with transient lactic acidosis, which resolved within 24 hours since admisison. Pt was treated with lasix gtt, with a good response. As part of the medical manageent, we held her lisinopril in the setting of hypotension. We increased her metoprolol to 25 mg qd. Upon discharge, pt has been switch to oral torsemide. She maintained fluid neutroality upon discharge. Her dry weight at discharge is about 65 kg. . # Coronary artery disease: Pt has known CAD w/ LCX angioplasty without stent in ___. Upon admisison, pt had chest pain with SOB. Her EKG showed transient junctional rhythm, likely a result of vagal tone. Pt was also found to have intermittent TWI in precordial leads. However, her cardiac enzymes were negative. We continued her medical management including aspirin and ezetimibe. . # Nausea/emesis: Pt presented with severe persistent nausea and vomiting. Her symptoms improved in 24 hours, as her blood sugar gets better controlled. Gastroparesis is the most likely cause of her nausea and vomiting. Pt was seen by the gastroenterology team, who recommended gastric emptying study. The study showed normal gastric emptying rate. Her symptoms appear to respond well with ativan, as we tried to avoid zofran given the QTc is already in the 470s. We decreased her erythromycin to 250 mg bid with lunch and dinner as a precaution for long QTc. We also increased her omeprazole to 40 mg bid. Her H.pylori serology was negative. Her symptoms largely resolved in two days after hospitalization. A viral gastroenteritis is also on the differential. Of note, pt has never had EGD. As part of the workup, this could be considered as outpatient. . # Acute on chronic renal insufficiency: Pt presented with Cr above her baseline. This is likely secondary to ATN vs hypoperfusion in CHF exacerbation. Her Cr improved subsequently to baseline with supportive care and diuresis. . # Leukocytosis: Pt presented with mild leukocytosis that has persisted for 2 weeks. Her blood and urine culture were both negative. It is unclear whether there is a source of infection. Her leukocytosis resolved spontaneously shortly prior to discharge. . # Diabetes: Pt has poorly controlled diabetes with A1c 8.6 6 weeks ago. During her last admission, pt required insulin drip for volatile blood sugar, including hyperglycemia with anion gap. Her blood sugar remained challenging to control during this admission. Pt was seen by ___ consult service, and was discharged on lantus 20 u in AM and 5 u in ___ with humalog sliding scale. We discontinued her regular insulin as the sliding regimen choice. . CHRONIC ISSUES # Anemia: Pt has normocytic anemia on admission. She received one unit of pRBC transfusion for a HCT of 23 and 24 hours of N/V, chest discomfort. Iron studies are consistent with chronic disease. . # Hyperlipidemia: In setting of statin allergy, ezetimibe is continued. . TRANSITIONAL ISSUES # CODE: Full # PENDING STUDIES AT DISCHARGE: Blood culture on ___ - no growth to date # MEDICATION CHANGES - STOPPED lisinopril - INCREASED metoprolol succinate to 25 mg qd - INCREASED torsemide to ___ m qd - INCREASED omeprazole to 40 mg bid - DECREASED erythromycin to 250 mg bid - STARTED potassium 30 mg qd # FOLLOW UP PLAN: - PCP and cardiology ___ at ___ - Pt was educated about fluid restriction, low salt diet and BP measurement at home - Pt was given a list of QT prolonging medications upon discharge.
summary: You came to our hospital for nausea, vomiting, weight gain and low blood pressure. You were found to be in exacerbation of your heart failure. We gave you IV medication to remove the excessive fluid, which you tolerated very well. For you nausea and vomiting, you underwent a gastric emptying study which showed did not show significant delay in food digestion. We also adjusted your insulin regimen to better control your blood sugar. You are on several medications that can potentially put your heart at risk for a condition called long QT. You have been extensively monitored during this admission. Your current regimen is proven to be safe. We provided you with a list of medication that can potentially further prolong QT, and you should discuss with your provider before taking these medications. Your blood sugar was elevated and we adjusted your insulin dose as below.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ===================== BRIEF HOSPITAL SUMMARY ===================== ___ with ___ COPD s/p 2 intubations w/prior exacerbations, diastolic CHF, moderate pulm HTN, cryptogenic/alcoholic cirrhosis c/b portal HTN and varices admitted with presumed COPD exacerbation +/- ___ transferred to MICU with respiratory distress and concern for sick sinus. In the MICU, patient refused intubation and invasive treatment. Discussion was held with the patient, palliative care, and his health care proxy (___). As per patient wishes, care was not esclated and care was transitioned to focus on comfort only. Patient was transferred to the floor and did well, becoming more responsive, and able to interact with loved ones and health care providers. His care was focused on comfort. ======================== ACTIVE ISSUES ======================== # Goals of care: ___ CARE Patient was transferred to ICU for respiratory distress early in his hospital course. Patient made wishes very clear that he wanted to be DNR/DNI and did not want any invasive or heroic treatment. Palliative care was consulted and held a family meeting with patient's daughters and ___ (health care proxy) ___. As per patient's wishes BIPAP was discontinued and dopamine was discontinued. Patient was made comfort measures only. Patient received boluses of PRN morphine for dyspnea and pain in the ICU. Patient remained comfortable while in the ICU. He was transferred to the floor, continued to receive morphine and ativan as needed. The patient was extremely tenuous when all invasive measures were removed in the ICU, yet he improved and became interactive while on the medical ward. Nonessential medications were discontinued. The patient's nadalol and spironolactone were been held. For comfort, the patient's lasix and nebulizers (in addition to the morphine and ativan for respiratory comfort). Given his advanced COPD and cirrhosis among other comorbid conditions, his life expectency is certainly <6 months. . #Respiratory Distress: Both hypercarbic and hypoxic. Pt with history of COPD with 2 intubations, pulmonary HTN and diastolic CHF p/w cough and dyspnea ___. Pt with suspected COPD exacerbation aggravated by CHF exacerbation (BNP 5003, previously as high as 13K) in setting of medication ___ and high salt load. CXR with bilateral fluffy infiltrates c/w pulmonary edema. Less likely PE (negative LENIS, bradycardia); BS bilaterally with no evidence of pneumothorax on CXR. . Patient was initially on BIPAP in ICU. He received IV steroids, nebs, and levoquin. He declined intubation. Based on patient wishes, care was transitioned to comfort care. Palliative care was consulted and held a family meeting with patient's daughters and ___ (health care proxy) ___. As per patient wished for BIPAP to be removed and he was transitioned to nasal cannula. Patient remained comfortable throughout ICU stay. Was transitioned to the medical ward, and remained comfortable on room air. . ___ Syndrome: Pt with alternating sinus bradycardia and sinus tachycardia. On tele, pt found to have HR between ___ repeat EKG showed sinus tachycardia but no ST changes, subsequently with HR dropped to ___ with hypotension requiring pressors; pt A&Ox3 with bradycardia, though lethargic. Otherwise asx, with no focal neuro concerns or chest pain/pressure. Cardiology consulted and felt no indication for temporary pacer and it was not in line with patient's wishes as above. ====================== TRANSITIONAL ISSUES ====================== 1. # CONTACT: ___ cell ___, pager ___ 2. Patient and HCP ___ made it very clear the patient is comfort measures only. The patient should not be rehospitalized unless specifically requested by the patient or HCP.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital for a severe COPD exacerbation. You were managed in the medical ICU, and then you and your family decided to withdrawal all invasive measures and to concentrate on comfort care. While on the general medical ward, your condition improved, and you became interactive, responsive, yet still in guarded condition. You and your family stressed to the team that in the future, you would not want to be hospitalized or given invasive measures or escalate your care. It was decided that you will concentrate on optimizing the quality of your remaining life, and making you comfortable.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ M with history of severe AS ___ - ___ 0.7, mean gradient 41), CAD s/p 3V-CABG (SVG to RCA, SVG to LAD and SVG to OM1), HTN, sCHF EF ___, ischemic cardiomyopathy s/p ICD placement presents with increasing chest pain, now s/p cath with DES x2. Developed GIB following cath with hypotension and tachycardia as well as episodes of SVT. Found to have angioectasias on EGD treated with electrocautery. #. Severe AS - ___ 0.7 with mean gradient of 41 during previous cath. Echo results here are similar. Initially this was thought to be cause of patient's chest pain and increased fatigue. However, pt did have a RHC on this admission with wedge pressure only 7 so did not get valvuloplasty as this is unlikely to be cause of patient's symptoms given hemodynamic results. Surgery also declined intervention. The patient will follow with Dr. ___ potential ___ in the future. In the setting of GI bleeding, the patient was given IV fluids without heart failure symptoms. He will continue on low dose lasix, beta blocker, and ___. #. NSTEMI/CAD - history of 3V-CABG with only RCA graft still patent. He is now s/p 2 DES to ostial and mid ramus. No current chest pain. Cardiac enzymes downtrending. #.GIB - in setting of receiving heparin and integrellin for cath. Hct drop from 34.0 to 29.1. Given 5 units total of PRBCs. Endoscopy revealed bleeding AVM in duodenum (which was sclerosed) and mild gastritis. He will continue PPI and carafate BID and f/u with gastroenterology for further care and colonoscopy. # SVT: Goes into 140s with lightheadedness. Adequately treated with vagal maneuvers such as ice and carotid massage. Increased metoprrolol for control. . # Acute on Chronic Systolic CHF - EF ___, ischemic cardiomyopathy s/p ICD. Crackles BB but ? r/t fibrosis. Appears euvolemic at present. Was on lasix 40 mg at home and was sent home on lasix 20 mg daily as he appeared dry at discharge. Metoprolol was changed to XL. . #. Acute on Chronic Kidney injury- resolved. . # IPF: incidentally discovered on CT scan. Assessed by pulmonology team yesterday. PFT's show restrictive pattern. Will F/u with pulmonolgy after discharge for futher management. Stable with no O2 requirement. . #. Thrombocytopenia- resolved. # Transitional Issues**** Incidental findings - has CT Chest with extensive fibrosis with many of features suggestive of UIP. - attenuation difference between liver and spleen may suggest prior treatment such as amiodarone - this could also be considered in context of patients fibrosis. - discussion of risk/benefits of anticoagulation in setting of LV aneursym
summary: You had chest pain and a heart attack and was taken to ___ for treatment. A cardiac catheterization found some blockages. One of the arteries was cleared and a stent was placed to keep the artery open. It is extremely important that you take aspirin and plavix every day for at least one year and possibly longer. Do not stop taking aspirin and plavix for any reason unless Dr. ___ that it is OK. You were seen by the heart surgeons but they do not think an operation is appropriate for you. You will continue to see Dr. ___ may need to have your aortic valve fixed in the future. Your heart function is weak and it is important to watch for any signs of fluid overload such as swelling in your legs or ___, trouble breathing or sleeping.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ h/o hx of CAD, HTN, NSTEMI ___, 3VD (not ammenable to CABG or PCI) and DMII, who presented for hypertensive emergency (SBP 210s) with flash pulmonary edema and NSTEMI type 2. # Acute on chronic diastolic congestive heart failure: Presentation consistent with flash pulmonary edema ___ hypertensive emergency (SBPs in the ___). CXR and exam consistent with significant volume overload. Was given 40mg IV lasx in ED and additional 40mg IV on the floor, putting out 2.7L. Transitioned back to home PO Lasix on ___. Has been relatively euvolemic since then. At discharge, states that her respiratory status and DOE are at her baseline. Weight on discharge is 76kg. # NSTEMI type 2 (3VD, MI ___, anatomy not amenable for CABG): Patient with anginal pain/nausea at rest and troponin rising from 0.04 to 0.14. Likely exacerbated by hypertensive emergency and resulting demand ischemia. She has baseline anginal pain with minimal exertion so not surprising that she has chest pain in setting of hypertensive emergency. Currently is able to be relieved with nitro. Unlikely to benefit from cath given anatomy that was not ammenable to intervention in ___. Treated medically with ASA 325mg, pravastatin 20mg daily (did not tolerate Lipitor in the past), and continued labetalol 300mg BID. TTE showed new focal inferior wall area of akinesis consistent with new infarction. Still has preserved EF of 55%. # Hypertension/ Hypertensive emergency: BP has been very difficult to control on 4 antihypertensives. Current presentation consistent with hypertensive emergency (SBPs in 210s) with resultant flash pulmonary edema and NSTEMI type 2 (i.e demand ischemia). Unclear how compliant patient is with current antihypertensive regime. She states that on day of presentation, she may have forgotten to take her amlodipine and Imdur. BPs improved on morning of admission with SBPs in 130s to 150s. She is already maxed out on amlodipine and lisinopril. Would not want to increase labetalol as patient with HR in low ___. Added hydralizine 25mg TID on admission. Increased to 50mg TID on ___. BPs much better controlled now with SBPs ranging in 120s to 130s. # UTI - Patient with purulent appearing uop from Foley. UA showed many RBCs, WBCs, and moderate bacteria, and positive ___. D/C'd Foley and treated as complicated UTI w/ Bactrim for 10 day course. # Type II DM: Poorly controlled with last few A1Cs in ___. Is on a 70-30 mix with 50 units Qam. Glucose significantly elevated in ER to 428 on chem 7. On day of admission, FSGs very difficult to control with FSG's persistently in 400 range. Received 69 units humalog and 60 units 70/30. Morning of ___, experienced hypoglycemia to 47 which quickly recovered with orange juice and peanutbutter sandwich. Gave 35 units 70/30 on ___ and FSGs much better throughout the day. Hyperglycemia likely ___ adrenergic surge in setting of hypertensive emergency, flash pulmonary edema, and NSTEMI. Discharged on home insulin regimen. Will require close f/u with ___ clinic upon discharge. # Mild ___ on CKD, creatinine 1.8-2.0 at baseline: Mildly up from baseline with Cr of 2.2. Likely due to poor forward flow in setting of heart failure. Cr on day of discharge increased from 2.3 to 2.7. This was likely a falsely elevated creatinine caused by Bactrim which blocks creatinine secretion into proximal tubule via organic ion transporter. Will need f/u lytes, BUN, creatinine within 1 week to ensure that Cr is returning toward baseline. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: #DM: FSGs difficult to control during hospitalization (see above). Being discharged on her home insulin regimen. Will require close ___ follow-up #Acute on chronic renal insufficiency: initial bump in Cr likely ___ hypertensive emergency/acute CHF. Had increase in creatinine on day of discharge from 2.3 to 2.7, likely falsely elevated ___ bactrim starting the day before (see above). Will need f/u lytes, BUN, creatinine within week of discharge. #Urine culture pending
summary: You were admitted for dangerously high blood pressure, shortness of breath, and chest pain. Your blood pressure was very high, in the 200s, which likely caused a small heart attack and fluid to back up into your lungs making you very short of breath. We treated you with IV lasix to remove the water from your lungs and your breathing improved. We also added a new blood pressure medication, hydralizine, and your blood pressure was much better controlled. We did an echocardiogram that the small heart attack that you had, did not do any significant damage to your heart. You were also found to have a bladder infection and this will be treated with 10 days of the antibiotic Bactrim.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ICU COURSE: Patient presented to ___ after a mechanical fall and then was admitted to the Neuro ICU for monitoring given her INR of 2.0 and her status of being on Coumadin. She received 2 units of FFP with an INR drop to 1.8. She was transferred to the floor but was transferred back to the ICU for rapid AFib and respiratory distress. On the morning of 6.6 she was stable in the ICU and on an Amiodarone drip. Her HR remained stable between ___ on the Amiodarone drip. On ___, she was started metoprolol and amiodarone was discontinued. CXR was concerning for flash pulmonary edema. She received 20 mg of Lasix IV and 100 mg of torsemide in the AM with good response. Medicine team was consulted in Neuro ICU, and patient was transferred to Medicine for further management. MEDICINE COURSE: # Acute on Chronic Systolic Heart Failure: Patient appeared to have an episode of flash pulmonary edema on the neurology floor and responded well to IV and po diuretics. TTE confirmed EF 45% with mild global systolic function, likely secondary to CAD. Diuretics were held upon transfer to medical floor, and patient maintained negative fluid balance. Prior to discharge, Torsemide was restarted at lower dose of 20 mg daily to which she responded with fair urine output. She will need to follow up with her primary care physician in one week to monitor fluid status and check creatinine. Patient was felt to be euvolemic upon discharge with dry weight of 171 lbs. While working with ___, she had intermittent ambulatory oxygen desaturation to the high ___, but would increase back to the mid ___ if encouraged to take a few deep breaths. # Intraparenchymal Hemorrhage: Head CT scan on presentation showed a 5 x 6 mm hyperattenuating focus involving the left frontal region, compatible with intraparenchymal hemorrhage, though the differential also included AVM, cavernoma, or hypercellular neoplasm. She remained neurologically intact without focal findings besides old CN VI palsy. Repeat head CT after two days was stable. Followup CT scan will be needed in ___ weeks with Neurosurgery followup. # Acute on Chronic Renal Failure: Per information from her PCP, her recent baseline creatinine is somewhere between 2 and 3. Her creatinine was 2.8 on admission and remained stable around this level for several days, but decreased to 2.4 on the day of discharge, after several days off diuretics. # s/p Mechanical Fall: The patient's fall was in the setting of not using cane while walking up stairs at home. She was evaluated by physical therapy who felt that she would benefit from ___ rehabilitation prior to returning home. She was encouraged to use an assistive device when walking in the future. # Atrial Fibrillation: Coumadin was held on presentation in setting of intraparenchymal head bleed and should be held for a total of 7 days. Coumadin should be restarted on ___ at her home dose with regular INR monitoring. Her heart rate was well controlled with her home regimen of Metoprolol and Amiodarone during her stay on the Medicine floor in the ___ bpm. She was monitored on telemetry without any concerning events noted. # Transitional Care: -- Blood cultures from ___ showed no growth to date but final results were pending on discharge. -- Restart Warfarin anticoagulation on ___ with regular INR checks -- Electrolyte panel in one week and PCP followup after restarting Torsemide -- Neurosurgery followup with CT head prior to appointment
summary: You were admitted to the hospital after a mechanical fall in which you hit your head and developed a small hemorrhage in the brain. You were briefly in the ICU, but were soon transferred to the general Medicine floor. During your stay, you had several episodes in which the oxygen level in your blood dropped to a low level. You also had evidence of fluid buildup in your lungs. An echocardiogram (ultrasound of the heart) was performed and showed a somewhat decreased ability of your heart to pump. This likely contributed to the fluid buildup in your lungs and in your legs and ankles. Several changes were made to your medications. You were restarted on a lower dose of Torsemide to help prevent fluid buildup in your body. Your Simvastatin was decreased since at recent research has shown that the 80 mg has a higher risk of muscle pain and other complications. Your Oxazepam was stopped since it can make it more likely for you to fall and injure yourself. Your Warfarin (Coumadin) was temporarily stopped after your head injury. You will need to start taking it again on ___. You should restart your prior regimen at that time with frequent INR checks until the level is stable again.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ M with hx of HFrEF w single vessel disease on cath, questionably due to tachy-mediated CM and recurrent atrial flutter s/p DCCV x 3, most recently on ___ who presented with significant SOB, DOE and ___ edema. # CORONARIES: LAD with 50% proximal to mid LAD stenosis # PUMP: LVEF less than 30 % # RHYTHM: multifocal atrial tachycardia with occasional aflutter given at least different p wave morphologies #Acute on chronic systolic heart failure Pt with h/o HFrEF with single vessel disease on cath, likely ___ tachy-mediated CM and recurrent atrial flutter s/p DCCV x 3, most recently on ___ presenting with significant SOB, DOE and ___ edema since pm of ___. Patient was volume overloaded on exam with ___ edema, increased JVP, reduced breath sounds in bl lung bases and diffuse crackles. CXR c/w pulmonary edema and bilateral pleural effusions. Etiology likely HFrEF exacerbation iso tachymediated CM. BNP elevated to 9201 and trops negative x2. TTE showed biventricular dilation with severe global biventricular hypokinesis consistent with diffuse process, EF ___. He was diuresed with IV Lasix and hypoxia improved. He remained in sinus rhythm during hospitalization. While inpatient he had scheduled pulmonary vein ablation on ___. After procedure he required further IV diuresis and ideally would have stayed at least one extra day but patient refused. Team felt it was safe to discharge patient as he was nearing euvolemia albeit not there yet. He was discharged with higher diuretic dose and strict return criteria. #Multifocal atrial tachycardia with occasional aflutter ___ recurrent aflutter s/p DCCV x 3, on amiodarone, Lopressor and eliquis. EKG with three different p wave morphologies noted c/f multifocal atrial tachycardia. Continued home amiodarone, home Lopressor and home eliquis #Subclinical Hypothyroidism Pt with TSH of 11, but free T4 1.5. no past ___ thyroid disease likely amiodarone mediated. Started on 25mcg levothyroxine daily. #Diabetes Mellitus Type 2 HbA1C found to be 6.7%, no past ___ DM. HISS while in house, will need PCP ___ for further mx TRANSITIONAL ISSUES ======================= [] Please check Cr at ___ clinic appointment. Cr at discharge 1.4 [] Please monitor Alk phos, found to be mildly elevated during admission. [] Please re-check TSH and free T4 to assess necessity of synthroid. Started in hospital for subclinical hypothyroidism [] HbA1c 6.7% during admission, patient was not started on medication for diabetes. Please recheck and consider initiating therapy if unable to control with lifestyle modifications. [] Pt EF ___, consider ICD placement in future [] Consider starting ACE-I ___ if patient's blood pressure tolerates []Please monitor TFTs every three- to four-month while patient is on Amiodarone []Please monitor LFTs every six months while patient is on Amiodarone [] Patient discharged with increased diuretic dose #Discharge weight: 88.2kg/194.4Lb #Discharge creatinine: 1.4
summary: You were admitted to the hospital because you had been feeling short of breath and you were found to have fluid on your lungs. This was felt to be due to a condition called heart failure, where your heart does not pump hard enough and fluid backs up into your lungs. You were given a diuretic medication through the IV to help get the fluid out. You also had a procedure called an ablation that will help keep you out of your irregular heart rhythm. You were also found to have low thyroid levels and were started on a medication to replace the thyroid hormone. Your blood sugar was found to be elevated Your weight at discharge is 194Lbs. Please weigh yourself today at home and use this as your new baseline. Please weigh yourself every day in the morning.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ year old woman with a history of HTN and pulmonary HTN who presents with SOB and hypoxia. . # Hypoxia, SOB: CHF versus pneumonia were on differentials. Pneumonia was considered with pt's history of progressively worsening cough and shortness of breath with leukocytosis and subjective fevers. On hospital Day 2, pt was febrile to ___ and was started on Levaquin. CXR shows pleural effusions but no lobar consolidations, and given that pt recently stopped diuretics, it was likely pt was fluid overloaded. This was supported by pt's elevated BNP. TTE showed moderate mitral regurgitation and pulmonary hyptertension (stable from prior). Pt was diuresed with Lasix and given captopril for BP control but was discontinued after UOP decreased and creatinine increased. Pt did well with antibiotic and her oxygen supplementation was weaned. She was discharged on levofloxacin to complete a 7-day course, as well as furosemide 10mg daily until she sees her PCP ___ ___. . # HTN: Pt was on felodipine, atenolol and Losartan as an outpatient. Pt came in with SBP in 190s and we continued felodipine and started TID captopril. On hospital day 2 pt became oliguric and Cr bump to 1.3 and thus stopped captopril. SBP in 130s and we restarted home atenolol. Once ___ resolved, all home antihypertensive medications were resumed, as well as furosemide as noted above. . # Anxiety: Pt was having multiple anxiety episodes upon admission and we continued her home diazepam. During these episodes, pt's BP increased significantly with tachycardia and had periods of nausea. Pt received valium at night and an ativan when she was particularly anxious. # Hyponatremia: Pt's sodium remained low throughout MICU course and has a history of having diuretics changed due to hyponatremia. Urine lytes, osm and urea were checked which showed low Na and osm consistent with dehyradation. Pt was hydrated and hypoNa resolved. # Anemia: Pt has h/o of anemia which had been worked up recently with EGD and colonoscopy. Would recommend outpatient follow-up given that her Fe was 7 and TIBC was low during admission. . # Hypothyroidism: Chronic and stable. We continued Synthroid at home dose. . # Renal lesions: Two renal lesions were seen incidentally on CT. Recommend that PCP get renal ultrasound to further characterize and to eval for renal cell carcinoma (considering her iron deficiency anemia of unclear etiology). . # Transitional Issues: - Needs renal ultrasound - Anemia workup
summary: You were admitted because you have been having shortness of breath and fevers. The chest x-ray and cat scan show that you have pneumonia as well as fluid in your lungs which is likely related to the pneumonia. You also have pulmonary hypertension which has been a longstanding issue, but which may be contributing. We are treating you with an antibiotic called levofloxacin and restarted lasix (a diuretic) to remove the fluid from your lungs until you see your primary care doctor and/or cardiologist at which time they may decide to stop this. You are anemic which is not new, but which is slightly worse than prior, and you were found to have low iron. Your primary care doctor ___ need to do further workup for this. The cat scan showed two lesions in your kidneys which may be benign cysts, however your primary care doctor ___ need to do further workup for this.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Mr. ___ is a ___ with PMH significant for severe valvular heart disease, diastolic CHF (EF 55%) with pacemaker, CABG x 5, Afib not on anticoagulation, heart block s/p PPM, DM2, and dementia who presented to ___ on ___ after an unwitnessed fall and was found to have broken ribs. # Goals of Care: Mr. ___ daughter, ___, and son, ___, met with the team regarding goals of care for Mr. ___. His current clinical status was discussed, including evidence of poor perfusion on labs and on exam. The prognosis of both his acute fractures/injuries as well as his longstanding underlying, irreversible heart disease were described to the best of our knowledge, and the family demonstrated understanding. ___ elected to pursue comfort-centered care, focusing on symptom based treatment. Regular scheduled labs, disruptive vitals, and telemetry were discontinued (temperature and respiratory rate were continued). His DNR/DNI/Do not escalate/Do not transfer to ICU status was confirmed by both of his children. Plans were made to transfer to a long-term care facility with hospice care. Any treatments that could help his symptoms should be considered, including antibiotics to treat symptomatic infections (such as fever or rigors not controlled by tylenol). # Unwitnessed fall: CT head at OSH was negative for intracranial bleed. CT neck did not show dispalced fracture and the Acute Care Surgical Team cleared his C spine prior to transfer to cardiology. CT chest and abdomen showed multiple Rt rib fractures (___), moderate and small left nonhemorrhagic effusions and intra-abdominal ascites.
