She required several MICU stays for recurrent hypoxia and desaturation. There was initial concern for multifocal pneumonia, HCAP vs. aspiration. She was empirically treated with Vanc/Cefepime/Azithromycin on admission to the MICU. Vancomycin was discontinued on ___ as MRSA screen was negative. She completed a 7 day course of HCAP coverage. Video swallow was negative for oropharyngeal aspiration, but there was concern for increased secretions and possible mild bulbar dysfunction leading to aspiration. Glycopyrrolate was trialed to attempt to reduce secretions but this led to worsening confusion and was stopped. Neurology was consulted and recommended evaluation by her outpatient neurologist for possible botox injections to control secretions. She was diuresed with IV Lasix boluses, and transitioned to 20mg PO Lasix at discharge due to concern for a component of volume overload given elevated BNP of 4929. Pulmonology and cardiology were consulted and felt that her clinical picture was consistent with amiodarone toxicity. Amiodarone was stopped, and she was started on prednisone. She was also initiated on calcium, vitamin D, and Bactrim given projected prolonged steroid course. Respiratory function improved, and she was maintaining O2 saturation > 95% on room air at time of discharge. # Anemia: The patient had an acute drop in hemoglobin to 6.7 from recent baseline which appears to be around ___. Low haptoglobin, elevated LDH and bilirubin were suggestive of transient hemolysis, which resolved without intervention. Direct Coombs was negative, and peripheral smear revealed only rare schistocytes. Hematology was consulted and felt that hemolysis was secondary to a transient transfusion reaction. Iron studies were suggestive of anemia of chronic disease. B12 level was borderline with elevated methylmalonic acid, suggestive of B12 deficiency. She was started on 1000 mcg of cyanocobalamin daily. # Afib on dabigatran: CHADS2VASC score is 7 (HTN, Age x ___, female, HTN, TIA, CHF). Amiodarone was discontinued given concern for amiodarone toxicity as above. She was started on low dose metoprolol 12.5mg XL daily for rate control. # Transaminitis: Mild, new from prior labs. RUQ US was without abnormalities. Transaminitis resolved without intervention, and was likely secondary to hemolysis given marked elevation of LDH and indirect hyperbilirubinemia. # ___ disease: Continued home carbidopa-levodopa, entecapone, and pramipexole # HTN with labile BP in the setting of autonomic dysfunction ___ ___ disease: Continued home amlodipine # Mood disorder: Continued home sertraline Transitional Issues: - started on prednisone 30mg daily. Also started on calcium, vitamin D, and prophylactic Bactrim while taking prednisone. Will likely require prolonged taper, to be directed by Dr. ___ - discharge on 20mg PO Lasix. Should have electrolytes and creatinine checked on ___ - amiodarone discontinued secondary to pulmonary toxicity - consider botox as an outpatient to treat excess secretions - borderline low B12 level. Methylmalonic acid high so started on high dose B12 supplementation. Intrinsic factor Ab pending at discharge - started on low dose metoprolol for rate control of atrial fibrillation
summary: You came in because you were having problems breathing. We think this was due to aspiration (food going down the wrong way into the lungs), fluid from heart failure, and a side effect of your amiodarone. We stopped your amiodarone and started you on a medication called prednisone, which can help treat the lung problems caused by amiodarone. We are discharging you on predisone, which you should take until you see Dr. ___ in clinic. Since prednisone can also cause you to retain fluid, we are discharging you on a water pill called furosemide. You should have your electrolytes and kidney function checked at your next primary care visit on ___, since these can be affected by furosemide.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ yo man with history of non-ischemic CMP (EF 50->30%), alcohol and tobacco abuse, AF on Coumadin, HTN, and OSA, presenting with dyspnea, worsening ___ edema, and confusion. # Acute on chronic systolic non ischemic CHF exacerbation: Patient has a history of non-ischemic (neg cath ___ CM with EF 30%, ___ class I per history, now at least class II. Patient's recently depressed EF is suspected to be due to alcohol. Pt appearred overloaded on exam and on CXR, BNP 6826 (none prior in our system). Exacerbation may be due to increased etOH intake vs pna vs afib vs salt indiscretion. Troponins negative, EKG shows LBBB but negative for Sgarbossa's criteria. During the course of hospitalization was diuresed effectively with 40mg IV Lasix, lost ~6kg, discharge weight 84.2kg. Discharged on torsemide 20mg daily as 40mg daily was too much. Decreased lisinopril to 5mg daily because of border line low BP (100s-110s systolic) and metoprolol XL 100mg daily instead of the atenolol. Stopped home HCTZ/triamterene on d/c. # Toxometabolic encephalopathy: Patient presented with confusion per wife in the setting of unintentional overdose in the setting of ambien/wine/nyquil. Appears improved. # Cough/RLL infiltrate: Plus coarse rhonchi in R lung base on exam, consistent with PNA. DDx includes pneumonia vs aspiration which patient is high risk for with alcoholism and unintentional medication overdose. Negative legionella. Treated with CTX/azithro x5 days # Atrial fibrillation on Xarelto: s/p ablation (___), pt with recent ZioPatch ___ to assess afib burden and rates when in Afib given worsening EF and concern that afib/tachycardia may be causing CM. ZioPatch results show Afib throughout, average ventricular rate 115/min, IVCD, no pauses. Large amount (10%) of ventricular ectopy, mostly VPBs. No patient events. Switched atenolol to metoprolol and continued on rivaroxaban. # Elevated INR: 5.8. Likely due to combination of poor PO intake, xarelto. Consider cirrhosis from ETOH as well. Improved with vitK, likely nutritional in setting of alcohol abuse. At discharge under 2 # ___: creatinine 1.5 from 1.1 baseline. Improved with mild diuresis. Possibly combination of fluid overload plus medication overdose. # Alcohol abuse: Patient with history of etOH abuse, has been strongly advised by MDs to discontinue etOH use. Patient has been poorly compliant and drinks about ___ drinks/day for ___ years. On CIWA for several days in the hospital but did not score. Given thiamine/folate/MVI and consulted SW. Discharged with rehab f/u. #chronic tophaceous gout: on colchicine but not allopurinol. Uric acid level 7.5, tophi apparent by b/l elbows L>R, started allopurinol ___ daily #OSA: continued CPAP while in house #HTN: restarting lisinopril at 5 mg # GERD: continued PPI #Elevated transaminases: unclear etiology. Negative hepatitis serologies, non-alcoholic pattern (ALT >AST). RUQ U/S normal. Trending down before discharge. Recommend f/u at outpatient visit. #CAD prevention: no CAD seen on most recent cath (___). Continue asa, statin Transitional issues: -Given conduction dx, PVCs, consider ICD and/or resynchronization -switched atenolol to metoprolol -decreased lisinopril from 12.5mg to 5mg daily -added torsemide 20mg daily -started allopurinol for chronic tophaceous gout, should recheck uric acid in 6 weeks for goal <4 -recheck transaminases at outpatient f/u
summary: You were admitted because of confusion that was likely related to an unintentional overdose of medications. We also found, however, that you likely had pneumonia and were fluid overloaded from your heart failure. We gave you IV medications to remove excess fluid that is distributed in the lungs and legs as a result of decreased heart muscle function. You will need to take a medication called torsemide every day that will keep the fluid from re-accumulating in the lungs and legs. We are also changing some of your other medications to optimize your regimen for heart failure. Atenolol has been changed to Metoprolol. Triamterene-HCTZ is discontinued. Allopurinol has been started for gout. The dose of lisinopril has been decreased.

text: Brief Hospital Course: #. SOB/cough: The patient reports he has had productive cough for the past 2 weeks prior to admission, which started during his recent admission. Cough is worse when lying flat and he has had to sleep sitting up. At baseline he sleeps on two pillows. He was treated for possible pneumonia/bronchitis during his previous admission. CXR on admission was notable for bibasilar atelectasis. No leukocytosis. Afebrile. Breath sounds are coarse on exam and has significant ___ edema. His SOB and cough were felt to be most likely secondary to a CHF exacerbation, despite the fact that he had a recent TTE in ___ that was essentially normal. His BNP was elevated to 656. He was treated with albuterol and atrovent nebulizers, and IV lasix. A TTE was performed and revealed no significant change from prior (no wall motion abnormalities, no significant valvular disease, and likely normal EF though suboptimal study). He was not discharged on standing diuretics. . #. LLE swelling: The patient noted onset of LLE swelling on the morning of admission. He had swelling in his RLE as well, but not as much. This swelling is most likely secondary to volume overload. He was evaluated by podiatry in the emergency room who felt there was no evidence for infection. The wound site was healing well with granulation tissue and no purulence or malodor. He likely has poor lympatic drainage from his multiple surgeries at his left foot resulting in his left leg swelling significantly more than the right in the setting of congestive heart failure exacerbation. He was continued on vancomycin 1g every 12 hours per podiatry to be completed at rehab after a total of 4 week course on ___. He was fitted for a cloth pneumatic boot while in the hospital. He does need to go to orthopedics clinic on ___ to have a more permanent bi-valve cast placed. . #. Acute renal failure: On admission, his creatinine was 1.5. His baseline creatinine appears to be 1.1 however during his last admission, his creatinine ranged 1.3-1.5. He may have had mild acute renal failure. On day 4, his creatinine came down to 1.2, without any intervention. He was diuresed with IV lasix as above. . #. Anemia: His hematocrit ranged from ___. Baseline appears to be in the low ___, however was 26.5 on discharge on ___ so is currently stable. Previous work-up consistent with iron deficiency anemia. The patient will likely need outpatient colonoscopy to look for occult bleeding source. . #. CAD: H/o LAD stent in ___. No chest pain currently. Has ___ degree AV nodal conduction delay, but this is seen on prior EKG. One set CE negative in the ED. He was continued on ASA, BB. . #. DM2: He was continued on his outpatient lantus regimen and a regular insulin sliding scale. . #. HTN: He was continued on his outpatient Imdur, Norvasc, metoprolol. . #. GERD: He was continued on his outpatient PPI. . #. Code: He was full code for this admission.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital with left leg swelling and cough. You had an ultrasound of your left leg which showed no evidence of a blood clot. You were also followed by the podiatry team while you were in the hospital and fitted with a special boot to help stablize your ankle. You will need to get a more permanent cast placed on your left leg. You should get this done on ___ in the ___ clinic on the second floor of the ___ building. You were also treated for a flare of your congestive heart failure (or retention of fluid) with diuretics. You had an echocardiogram which showed normal pumping function of your heart.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ year old with known CAD, s/p CABG, multiple MI's, multiple PCI's, HTN, HL, DM, cardiomyopathy (with LVEF ___, OSA, hypothyroidism, TIA, s/p dual-chamber ICD in ___ with chest pain for past 4 months presents for elective cardiac cath, s/p DES to SVG-LAD graft, with recent ICD firing. . # Rhythm: The patient was in a normal sinus rhythm during admission. Interrogation of the ICD when he presented to the ED revealed that the patient had undergone an episode of VT that failed ATPx3 and successfully resolved with 25J CV. His metoprolol was increased to 75mg daily upon discharge. He did not have any electrolyte disturbances, heart failure, or ICD breaks that might otherwise cause disturbances to his rhythm. # Coronary artery disease: Patient denied any chest pain or signs or symptoms consistent with CAD. He has an extensive CAD history with CABG, multiple MIs, multiple PCI. He was ruled out for MI, and was continued on his home ASA 325 mg PO daily, Plavix 75 mg Daily, and Lisinopril 5 mg PO daily. His Metoprolol Succinate was increased to 75 mg Daily on discharge. # Systolic congestive heart failure: The patient has a history of CHF with most recent echo (___) showing EF of 25%. The patient has ICD placement to presvent SCD, but did not have any signs or symptoms of heart failure currently. His home lisinopril and furosemide at 80 mg daily were continued. His digoxin was likewise continued, and a digoxin level revealed a therapeutic level. His Metoprolol Succinate was increased to 75 mg Daily on discharge. # Primary Hyperparathyroidism: On admission, the patient's calcium was elevated, and phosphorus was low, suggestive of hyperparathyroidism. The patient's PTH level as checked and foudn to be elevated in the 100s. Endocrine was consulted, and indicated that there were no clear indications for parathyroidectomy (age not < ___, Ca level not 1 mg/dl above upper limit of nl, no osteoporosis history, CrCl >60). On discharge, they recommended PCP ___ for ___ to evaluate for osteoporosis, in addition to a Vitamin D level, a well as a 24 h urinary calcium level. Dr. ___ patient's endocrinologist, was notified by the endocrine fellow of these findings. The team also suggesting having annual calcium and renal function checked to determine if he develops indication for surgery, with ___ q ___ years. # Hypothyroidism: Thyroid functions repeated in house were within normal limits. The patient was continued on his home dose of 25 mcg levothyroxine daily
summary: You were admitted because your ICD had fired. We admitted you to the hospital for further monitoring; your chest x-ray was clear, and your electrolytes were all within normal limits. Our electrophysiologists evaluated you, and did not find any problems with your ICD. They recommended increasing your dose of Metoprolol to 75 mg Daily. We noticed on your laboratory values that your calcium was high, and your phosphorus was low. Our endocrinologists evaluated you, and we diagnosed you with a condition called hyperparathyroidism. As of now, this condition is asymptomatic, meaning it does not appear to be affecting you, but you should have your physicians work this up as described below. CHANGE your dose of Metoprolol to 75 mg Daily (previously you had beent taking 50 mg Daily) We did not make any other changes to your medications, so pelase continue to take them as you normally do.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ is a ___ year-old man with diabetes mellitus, hypertension, and hyperlipidemia admitted with pneumonia. ACTIVE DIAGNOSES ================ # Community-acquired pneumonia: He had fever, productive cough, and SOB, along with opacities on chest radiography indicative of a community-acquired pneumonia. He was treated with levofloxacin 750 mg for a 7 day course with day one on ___ (last dose on ___, supplemental oxygen, and albuterol and ipratropium nebulizers as needed. His symptoms improved and he was weaned off supplemental oxygen. # Systolic heart failure: He has had symptoms of orthopnea and PND for the past ___ weeks. He has had findings consistent with pulmonary edema on exam (crackles) and on CXR (bibasilar infiltrates) as well as pitting edema. TTE on ___ showed mild global left ventricular hypokinesis, moderate dilatation of the left ventricle, and a LVEF of 44%. The cause is not clear, but perhaps post-viral or hypertension. His respiratory status improved after diuresis with 40 mg IV lasix. His amlodipine was discontinued, and he was started on lisinopril 10 mg. He was started on a low sodium (2g) diet. He should have a repeat TTE in ___ months. He should follow-up with his PCP regarding the management of the systolic heart failure, including consideration for cardiology referral. Bblocker was not started this admission given that depressed EF could be due to acute illness, however if systolic dysfunction persists on follow up ECHO would plan to start at that time. # CKD: He has had elevated BUN and Cr since at least ___ per PCP ___. His Cr has remained stable at ~2 since then. His urine protein/creatinine ratio of 3.2 is close to nephrotic range. He has hypoalbuminemia. He had periorbital and pretibial edema. He was evaluated by the nephrology service. Given his history of DM, diabetic nephropathy is likely the primary cause, with possible contribution from HTN. Renal US revealed no structural abnormality of the kidneys. Medications were renally dosed. Amlodipine was discontinued and lisinopril 10 mg was initiated. He should have his creatinine measured ___ days after discharge. Follow-up will be with ___ in nephrology. # Pulmonary hypertension: Moderate pulmonary hypertension was found on TTE, with PASP 52-54 mmHg. Given the history of snoring and his body habitus, his pulmonary hypertension is likely due to OSA. We recommend an outpatient sleep study. # Normocytic anemia: He has a normocytic anemia, likely due decreased EPO production in CKD. His H/H remained stable during his hospitalization. He should follow-up with his PCP and nephrologist regarding the anemia. CHRONIC DIAGNOSES ================= # DM: His home glipizide was held while inpatient. He had evidence of poor glycemic control with an A1C of 11.8%. His fingerstick glucose was monitored, and an insulin sliding scale was instituted while inpatient. He should restart his home glipizide on discharge and follow-up with his PCP regarding the management of his DM. # HLD: He was continued on his home simvastatin. # HTN: He was initially maintained on his home amlodipine, which was later discontinued. He was started on lisinopril 10 mg as discussed above. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES =================== # Continue levofloxacin through ___ (7 day course) # Please recheck Cr at next PCP visit given recent transition to ACEI # D/c'ed amlodipine and started lisinopril 10mg given renoprotecitve effect # Will need to follow-up with nephrology for recent diagnosis of near nephrotic range proteinuria # Will need repeat TTE in next ___ months to re-eval LVEF and need for cardiology referral # Will need sleep study to evaluate for OSA # He be taking asprin 81 mg daily for primary prevention of CAD
summary: You were hospitalized for fevers, cough, and shortness of breath. You had a chest x-ray that showed pneumonia, and you were treated wtih antibiotics, oxygen, and nebulizers. Your symptoms improved. You should continue to take the antibiotics (levofloxacin) until your last dose on ___. Your laboratory tests revealed decreased kidney function, which was previously seen by your primary care provider. This is likely due to her diabetes and high blood pressure. This may explain your symptoms of puffy eyes and leg swelling. You were evaluated by the kidney specialists (nephrologists) while you were here. We started you on a medication called lisinopril to help perserve your kidney function.

text: Brief Hospital Course: PRIMARY REASON FOR ADMISSION: ___ female with insulin-dependent diabetes, hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and high grade atrioventricular block status post dual chamber pacemaker p/w dyspnea and evidence of congestive heart failure. ACUTE ISSUES: # Acute on chronic decompensated heart failure: Pt presented with shortness of breath, pulmonary edema and elevated BNP c/w acute on chronic systolic heart failure. TTE showed moderate-severe regional and global left ventricular systolic dysfunction c/w multivessel CAD along with functional mitral regurgitation and mild pulmonary artery systolic hypertension. She was given IV lasix and with improvement in symptoms transitioned to PO lasix. Discharged on 10mg daily lasix dose. She also underwent cardiac cath that showed non obstructive insignificant coronary artery disease. Based on TTE results, there was concern for pacemaker induced cardiomyopathy. Patient was scheduled for follow up with Dr. ___ ___ for further management. # Fever/leuckocytosis: Patient was febrile to 101.2 in the ED with leukocytosis to a high of 21.8. No clear source so antibiotics were not given. No UTI or PNA. She also underwent left UE that showed no clot as a cause of her fever, and there were no other localizing symptoms. WBC normalized during hospitalization and her fever did not recur. # Troponin elevation: most likely demand ischemia in the setting of heart failure and possible infection. No CP or EKG changes currently that would be concerning for ACS. Trop stable X3. CHRONIC ISSUES: #Diabetes Mellitus, type 2: Her oral hypoglycemics were held and she was put on glargine and HISS. Discharged on home meds. #Hypertension: - Hydrochlorthiazide 25mg and amlodipine 2.5mg were held during hospitalization. Not continued on discharge given SBP 90-120 during hospitalization. Metoprolol succinate 25mg XL increased to 50mg given systolic CHF #High grade AV block s/p pace maker: Follow up with EP arranged to explore possible pacemaker induced cardiomyopathy. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: - Hydrochlorthiazide 25mg and amlodipine 2.5mg were held during hospitalization. Not continued on discharge given SBP 90-120 during hospitalization. Consider restarting as outpatient as needed - Metoprolol succinate 25mg XL increased to 50mg given systolic CHF - Discharged on lasix 10mg PO daily for maintainace diuretic - Pt will follow up with Dr. ___ given concern reduced EF on TTE could be due to pacemaker induced cardiomyopathy - Follow up ACE level for evaluation of sarcoidosis as a possible caused of reduced EF - Discharge weight: 57.1kg CODE: DNI ok to resuscitate Contact: Daughter ___ ___ ___ on Admission: The Preadmission Medication list is accurate and complete. 1. Alendronate Sodium 70 mg PO 1X/WEEK (FR) 2. Amlodipine 2.5 mg PO DAILY 3. Atorvastatin 40 mg PO QPM 4. ciclopirox 0.77 % topical BID 5. Epinephrine 1:1000 0.5 mg SC ONCE MR1 severe allergy 6. Fluticasone Propionate NASAL 2 SPRY NU DAILY 7. HYDROmorphone (Dilaudid) ___ mg PO Q4H:PRN pain 8. Glargine 20 Units Bedtime 9. MetFORMIN XR (Glucophage XR) 1000 mg PO DAILY 10. Metoprolol Succinate XL 25 mg PO DAILY 11. Omeprazole 20 mg PO DAILY 12. sitaGLIPtin 100 mg oral daily 13. Valsartan 320 mg PO DAILY 14. Aspirin 81 mg PO DAILY 15. Hydrochlorothiazide 50 mg PO DAILY 16. Calcium 600 + D(3) (calcium carbonate-vitamin D3) 600 mg(1,500mg) -400 unit oral daily 17. GlipiZIDE XL 10 mg PO DAILY
summary: You were admitted on ___ for shortness of breath. You had extra fluid in your lungs from heart failure and were given medication to help remove this fluid so that you could breath more easily. You also underwent a procedure to look at the blood vessels that feed your heart, called a cardiac catheterization, which showed no major obstruction. You had imaging of your heart which showed that your heart is not working as well as it should which is the most likely cause of your shortness of breath. Based on the imaging, there is concern that the weakness of your heart might be related to your pacemaker.

text: Brief Hospital Course: . #. CHF - Ischemic vs nonischemic cardiomyopathy. Risk factors for CAD include age and HTN. Substernal chest pain resolved without intervention, biomarkers negative, EKG not suggestive of ischemia. Also possible are amyloid, sarcoid and hemochromatosis although much less likely. The patient responded well to lasix 20mg IV daily with improvement in fatigue and dyspnea on exertion. Echo showed a global hypokinesis, with EF approx 20%. She was fluid restricted, continued to diurese to 20mg IV lasix, and started on metoprolol and lisinopril. Lasix was switched to oral dose prior to discharge. Other work-up including iron studies, B12/folate, TSH were normal. She will have outpatient cardiology follow-up within one week of discharge for medication adjustments, and blood draw to follow resolution of hypokalemia. . #. Rhythm - irregular rhythm w/ atrial ectopy, noisy baseline, multiple P morphologies on admission. On telemetry during admission the patient appeared to have a regular rhythm with frequent ectopy, possibly secondary to her electrolyte abnormality. She was started on metoprolol as above. She remained asymptomatic during her stay. . #. Anemia - known pernicious anemia, receives B12 injections monthly per her PCP.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital for shortness of breath and one episode of chest pressure. Based on crackles heard in your lungs, an elevated blood level of BNP, and an x-ray that showed fluid in your lungs, it was felt that you were in congestive heart failure (CHF). To treat your heart failure, you were given lasix, which causes an increase in urine production, and your symptoms of CHF improved. It is not clear the cause of your CHF or your chest pressure. To rule out worrisome causes, an echocardiogram was performed, which confirmed heart failure. In addition, your potassium level was low and was repleated.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Outpatient Providers: ___ female with a past medical history of T2DM, hyperlipidemia, hypertension, HFpEF, moderate mitral stenosis, and dementia who presented for evaluation of dyspnea. =============== ACTIVE ISSUES: =============== #Dyspnea: #Acute on chronic HFpEF: Patient presented with dyspnea, CXR with volume overload, and elevated BNP higher than prior. Trigger could be undertreated hypertension or inadequate home diuretic. Responded well to diuresis for total fluid balance -5945 cc, though had minor ___ (below). TTE with improved MS though functional MR and severe pulmonary HTN. Nuclear stress was obtained to evaluate for ischemia, which showed partially reversible deficit in LAD distribution. Cardiac catheterization showed 40% LAD stenosis; no intervention performed. Was symptomatically improved and tolerating exercise well on discharge. Hydralazine was added for escalation of anti-hypertensive regimen, and torsemide was increased to 30 mg in the morning and 20 mg in the evening. ___ on CKD: Cr uptrended to 1.9 when torsemide 30 mg BID trialed. Patient received 1L fluids after cath and Cr improved to 1.8. #Mitral stenosis: Moderate on last TTE in ___ mild-moderate on TTE during admission. HRs were appropriately controlled on home labetalol. ================ CHRONIC ISSUES: ================ #DM2: -Continued glargine, ISS. #HTN: -Continued home labetalol, amlodipine, losartan. Added hydralazine 50 mg TID. #Normocytic anemia: likely multifactorial ___ CKD, DM Transitional Issues [] Please obtain BUN, Cr, K at outpatient follow-up on ___. [] Discharge weight = 81.9 kg (180.56 lb), discharge Cr = 1.8. [] Severe pulmonary hypertension noted TTE ___, worsened from prior. Seemed out of proportion to left heart failure. Consider outpatient sleep study if not already obtained, and treatment for OSA. [] Consider workup of secondary hypertension if not already done, given requirement of 4 anti-hypertensive agents.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital because you were having shortness of breath with activity. You were found to have too much fluid in your body and were admitted for monitoring while we removed the extra fluid. You were given medications to make you pee more to remove fluid from your body. We increased your daily torsemide dose to 30 mg in the morning and 20 mg in the evening. We started a new medication for your blood pressure. You had a stress test of your heart which showed that a part of your heart was not receiving enough blood flow when you exercise. Because of this, you had a procedure called a cardiac catheterization, which did not find any severe blockages in your heart.

text: Brief Hospital Course: 1. Acute diastolic heart failure/Hypoxia: CTA negative for PE or infiltrate. Ruled out for MI by enzymes. CTA with bilateral effusions as evidence of mild volume overload. Echocardiogram done and demonstrated hyperdynamic LV with elevated filling pressures. Responded well to diuresis. No oxygen requirement at discharge. She did well with lasix every other day. 2. Septicemia, MSSA bacteremia and likely endocarditis: three days into the hospital stay, Ms. ___ became diaphoretic, febrile and tachycardic. She responded to fluid. Blood cultures were positive for Methicillin-Sensitive S. aureus (___). Transthoracic echo did not demonstrate any vegetations. Her ___ and ___ surveillance cultures were all positive. She was treated with vancomycin from ___ as she had a reported penicillin allergy. Given her persistently positive blood cultures despite antibiotic therapy, discussion with her family revealed an unknown penicillin reaction in the past. She was transitioned to Nafcillin with monitoring, and she did well. The decision was made not to pursue a TEE as our management would not change. Given her high grade bacteremia, it is likely that she has endocarditis and she will be treated for 6 weeks with Nafcillin. Her PCP ___ monitor safety labs--weekly CBC/diff and Chem 7. PICC was placed ___. Initially, it ended in her Right atrium, and lead to NSVT. It was pulled back 3 cm by the ___ nurse at the request of the Radiologist. 3. Volume status: Ms. ___ presented volume overload, but given systemic illness with septicemia during the hospitalization, she fluctuated between dehydration and overhydration. At discharge, she was on lasix 20 mg every other day. Her creatinine at discharge was 1.7. 4. Acute on chronic renal failure, chronic stage III kidney disease: dehydration at admission with creatinine of 1.2. Improved with hydration, but fluctuated with lasix. Cr at discharge 1.7. Lasix changed to every other day. Urine eosinophils were checked prior to discharge and were negative, as she was receiving Nafcillin. 5. Rash: Chronic and long standing. Seen by dermatology on this admission. All nonessential medications were stopped including statin and aricept. Over the hospitalization, her rash improved. She was treated with Clobetasol cream. 6. Hypotension: prior to admission, resolved prior to the hospitalization and was never an active issue. 7. Hypertension: continued home lisinopril and restart toprol XL at discharge. 8. Atrial Fibrillation: no signs of RVR. Patient's ooutpatient regiman was aspirin and metoprolol for rate control. These we contiued during the stay. 9. Anemia: This was thought to be secondary to chronic disease. Fe studies were obtained and were consistent with this diagnosis. HCT on discharge was 28, stable for several days. 10. Hyperlipidemia, dementia and GERD remained stable during admission.
summary: You came to the hospital with shorteness of breath and reported hypotension. We believe your shortness of breath is due to volume overload from your heart disease. We treated you with diuretics and you responded well. We were also concerned about your rash and thought it might be caused by a medication you are taking, so we stopped some of your medications to see if that would help. We gave you medications to help reduce the itching. Also you were noted to have an infection in your blood. We gave you antibiotics (Nafcicillin) to help fight your infection. You need to take this for 6 months. Please check your labs (CBC and Chem7) at your rehab facility every week and have your results evaluated by a physician.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ F with nonischemic cardiomyopathy (systolic CHF, EF 20%) presenting with dyspnea, weight gain, hyponatremia consistent with CHF exacerbation, greatly improved after diuresis. # sCHF exacerbation: Increased dyspnea likely secondary to CHF exacerbation. Patient's weight up 4lbs at home (although improved after she took additional torsemide at home), BNP 2776. Per note from Dr. ___ in ED, patient's cardiomyopathy is end stage and she refuses LVAD. No response to milrinone previously. During her hospitalization her I/Os and weights were measuerd daily, and she was monitored on telemetry for arrhythmias (negative for arrhythmia). Her electrolytes were repleted as necessary, and her home medications were continued for CHF. The patient as noted above is not interested in surgical assistance for her very poor EF, but is interested in learning more about palliative care. They were consulted during the hospitalization and recommended that Ms. ___ call them as an outpatient at ___ or ___. On discharge, the patient was euvolemic and at her dry weight of 111 lbs. # Hyponatremia: likely secondary to increased free water intake in setting of CHF. Stable on discharge (up to 136 from 122). #COPD: Stable, continued home medications. DISCHARGE WEIGHT: 111 lbs, her dry weight. OTHER TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: - Needs PCP and ___ follow up - Palliative care may be able to offer a non-surgical manner in which to improve quality of life. Recommend consultation as o/p.
summary: You came to ___ because you were having increasing weight gain, shortness of breath when you exerted yourself, and some abdominal pain that you get when you are having a heart failure exacerbation. During your hospitalization we provided you with IV lasix, and limited the fluid intake in your diet and the salt intake in your diet. By the time of discharge you weighed 49.6kg or 109 lbs, which is 6 pounds less than when you had a heart failure exacerbation in ___. We have not made any changes to your medications on this admission. We spoke with you regarding long term treatments for your heart failure including the implantation of devices such as a "left ventricular assist device" which you were not interested. We also encouraged you to obtain a colonoscopy for routine cancer screening due to your weight loss. Also, we discussed the bacteria in your urine, and the fact that you were having no symptoms and thus required no treatment at this time.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ year old ___ female with a history of afib and torsades, chronic diastolic CHF, HTN, DM2, COPD, and ILD who presented with confusion, anorexia after recent discharge 1 week ago. . # Altered mental status - per son, improved after stopping levofloxacin from previous discharge. On admission, she was AOx2 and per son this was her baseline. Poor appetite was likely due to PO flagyl, when this was changed to PO vanco her appetite improved. Prior to discharge, she was mentating at baseline per son. . # Acute on chronic renal failure - baseline 1.9, bumped to >3 on previous admission while diuresed with lasix drip, now persistently elevated. On admission, UOP was noted to be low, bladder scan showed 1000ml so pt was retaining, Foley was placed for decompression. Cr had been stable at 2.8 during active diuresis for the rest of the hospital stay. Given her volume overload clinically, we diuresed her with lasix and metolazone with net goal output of 1L negative per day. Renal artery stenosis was initially consideration given HTN progressing over past year but after further consideration by renal team, she was not scanned because would not be candidate for stenting regardless. Foley was kept in place, had successful voiding trial before discharge, post-void residual scan on day of discharge showed 200cc. She was discharged on metolazone and home bumetanide dose, will have close follow up with PCP and renal. . # Presumptive CDiff colitis: Started on PO flagyl on previous admission given clinical suspicion, C diff assay was negative. Pt reports that she is not having diarrhea and that her last BM was well formed and day prior to admission. She was changed to PO vanco and appetite/nausea improved. She completed 14-day of flagyl/vanco. PO intake, diarrhea, and abdominal pain were improved at time of discharge. C diff toxin assay negative x2. PCR was not sent because she completed her full course and would not have changed management given that she only received 2 doses of CTX for presumed UTI on admission. . #.Diastolic HF - last echo on previous admission, EF>55% with diastolic dysfunction; pt is on bumetanide at home, ACEI was held on previous discharge. Not on beta blocker given bradycardia. As above, she was clinically volume overloaded and actively diuresed with metolazone and IV lasix during hospital stay. Her BP improved to SBP 140s and her volume status improved. She was discharged on metolazone and bumex with compression stockings, will follow up with cardiology, PCP, and renal as outpatient. . #. Atrial fibrillation - baseline bradycardic in ___. INR therapeutic at 2.2 (goal INR 2.0-3.0) on admission. Digoxin was discontinued on previous admission given slow HR. Coumadin 3mg daily was continued and INR therapeutic throughout hospital admission. No events on telemetry. . #. HTN: have been elevated to SBP 170s on admission, but improved to SBP 140s with increased dose of hydralazine (75mg QID) and Imdur (90mg daily). Home clonidine continued. As above, initial concern for renal artery stenosis but scan not done because would not be stending candidate. She was discharged on increased dose of Imdur but home dose of hydralazine. . #. DM2: At home on glimeprimide, on qachs fingersticks and sliding scale during hospitalization. . #. Hypothyroidism: Continued levothyroxine
summary: You were admitted to the hospital with diarrhea, poor appetite, and volume overload. You finished a course of antibiotics for what was though to be an infection in your stool on the previous admission. Your appetite improved while you were in the hospital. When you were admitted, we found that you were retaining urine and a Foley catheter was placed. We gave you diuretics (water pills) to remove the fluid from your lungs and legs and monitored your urine output closely. We removed the catheter and you were able to urinate. We were able to remove a good amount of fluid from your body and are discharging you on medications that will continue to remove fluid.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ year woman with a history of complete heart block with biventricular pacemaker placement, severe systolic congestive heart failure with EF of 15% on last TTE (___) who presents with increasing edema and confusion concerning primarily for CHF exacerbation. ACUTE ISSUES: # CHF exacerbation: Pt presented with lethargy and worsening edema. Last EF 15% in ___. Appeared volume overloaded on exam with bi-basilar crackles, pitting edema, and JVD distension. She was initially admitted to the MICU. Placed on BiPAP, which she did not tolerate. Diuresis was largely unsuccessful despite furosemide gtt and metolazone. In addition, she was empirically treated for CAP with Levaquin. She became nearly anuric, with a rising potassium, 6.8 at last draw. She is currently on minimal oxygen by nasal cannula. After a discussion with the patient's family on ___, patient was made CMO. Her ICD was turned off with pacing continued. She was transferred to the floor, with all of her medications discontinued. On the floor, there was a second discussion with the family, and CMO status was changed to periodic vital checks, and daily labs. She was treated with morphine PRN air hunger and pain and olanzapine PRN agitation. Labs demonstrated hypernatremia, and she was treatd with IV D5W on a continuous gtt at 100-125cc/hour with improvement in her lab values. In addition, her INR was found to supratherapeutic to 7.6 and she was treated with vitamin K. INR dropped to 1.7. On discharge, she was restarted on Carvedilol 3.125mg PO BID and warfarin. Lisinopril and Lasix were held, however should be restarted in the case of hypertension and volume retention, respectively. # ___ - Likely a result of CHF exacerbation with poor renal perfusion. Patient's Cr peaked at 2.2 and BUN at 129 prior to trending down with IVFs. # Hyperkalemia: potassium was 6.8 and uptrending on transfer to floor with worsening UOP, however improved on the floor with no specific interventions and improved urine output. # AMS with periodic agitation: likely secondary to hypercarbia and worsening uremia. She received olanzapine for agitation. CHRONIC ISSUES: #AFib with mechanical mitral valve replacement and tricuspid annuloplasty - Patient was therapeutic for much of her course, however became supratherapeutic, as discussed above, with corrected to subtherapeutic levels. Warfarin had been discontinued on initial transfer to floor, and was restarted on discharge. #Hypertension - Carvedilol and Lisinopril were held on transfer to the floor with SBPs in the 100-110's. On discharge carvedilol was continued. Please restart lisinopril if patient becomes hypertensive. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: We are restarting Carvedilol 6.25mg PO BID and Warfarin on discharge Patient is being bridged with Lovenox while subtherapeutic Please restart Lisinopril 5mg PO daily if hypertensive Please restart Lasix 10Mg and 20mg on alternating days if volume retaining
summary: You initially presented with worsening swelling and fatigue due to an exacerbation of your congestive heart failure. You were treated with diuretics, which were largely unhelpful. You were transferred to the medicine floor, where you received IVFs for an elevated sodium level, as well as morphine and olanzapine for pain and agitation, respectively. On discharge, we restarted your Carvedilol, Acetazolamide and Warfarin, and instructed the facility to which you were transferred to restart your lisinopril and lasix as necessary. You may also benefit from digoxin in the future as well. You will need to take Lovenox (enoxaparin) until your INR is between 2.5-3.5.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Brief Hospital Course: ___ with h/o CAD, chronic systolic heart failure (EF 35%) with recent exacerbation 1 month ago, CKD baseline Cr 2.5, h/o HIT, COPD, who presents with worsening dyspnea. . # Dyspnea: On presentation, the patient described PND, and a CXR was consistent with flash pulmonary edema. His BNP elevated at 40K. He had no fever, cough, or leukocytosis to support a diagnosis of PNA. He had LENIs two weeks ago which were negative, making PE less likely. Our differential diagnosis for his pulmonary edema included CHF exacerbation (he was not taking lasix prior to admission vs possibly increased sodium intake) vs ischemia/angina vs bilateral renal artery stenosis. There was no wheezing on initial exam. His EKG was without ischemic changes, trops mildly elevated (but within his recent baseline). He was treated with BIPAP briefly in the ED, but was already on nasal cannula by the time he arrived in the ICU and was treated with IV lasix and symptomatically improved overnight. He was transferred to the general floor less than 24 hours later. His total diuresis was an average of 1L/day. We switched him from IV lasix to 60mg po lasix on ___ and then to 40mg po lasix on ___, which he tolerated well and can continue upon discharge. His Chest X-ray showed increasing pulmonary edema on ___, followed by resolving edema on ___. Transient episodes of dyspnea were also alleviated by his COPD nebulizers. No fevers or cough to suggest infection. -Given his recent admission less than 1 month ago for CHF exacerbation, if he returns with another exacerbation in the short term, we would recommend renal doppler to rule out an additional/potential etiology. . #. Epigastric/chest pain: Had multiple episodes of transient epigastric/chest pain with nausea and diaphoresis during his admission; he said these were similar to his presenting symptoms. On the general medical service, the main episode was associated with eating dinner on ___. Differential diagnosis includes angina vs possible biliary colic given presence of gallstones on CT. He has a fixed defect involving the PDA territory on P-MIBI in ___. CP resolved a few hours later on the evening of ___. The episode was alleviated by 2x sublingual nitroglycerin and oxygen. He experienced dyspnea after the main episode, which was alleviated by albuterol neb. EKG was within normal limits (old LBBB). Trops were elevated but still at his baseline. Negative stress test makes angina less likely and could be biliary colic however patient would be poor surgical candidate for gallbladder removal. - Started Imdur to treat possible angina. . #Tachycardia/HTN: He experienced persistent tachycardia in the 100-110 range for the first 36 hours of his admission. We felt that this could be secondary to his albuterol nebs, possible reflex tachycardia from beta-blocker withdrawal, vs intravascular depletion during diuresis (but he did not look dry on exam). We added metoprolol 12.5mg three times a day on ___, and his bpm range on ___ was much improved (75-88). We added Hydralazine 10mg qid and Isosorbide Mononitrate 10 mg PO BID for combined afterload and preload reduction to control his blood pressure, which had been elevated. We started the hydralazine on ___ and discontinued it on ___ because SBPs just below 120. We recommend that he continues Isosorbide Mononitrate Extended Release 30 mg PO qd upon discharge. . # UTI: His initial UA was positive, and his UCx returned as coag-negative staph. He was treated empirically with levofloxacin before the UCx returned. We discontinued the levofloxacin and the foley when the UCx returned on ___. His final UA still had evidence of a 18 WBC and leukocytes, but he continued to be asymptomatic and afebrile, so we opted not to re-treat him.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital with an episode of shortness of breath, nausea, and sweating. We thought this might be related to a brief exacerbation of your congestive heart failure. We gave you oxygen, nebulizers, and made some changes to your medications. We performed a few tests including Chest X-rays, EKGs of your heart, and blood tests. The Chest X-rays showed fluid in your lungs that improved during your hospital stay. The EKGs showed a fast heart rate but no other changes. The blood tests showed that your cardiac enzymes were a bit elevated from your heart working hard, but these numbers did not increase significantly over time, so we were reassured. When you had one of these episodes (shortness of breath, nausea, and sweating) in the hospital on ___, we re-checked all of these tests, and they were okay.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ lady with severe symptomatic aortic stenosis in the setting of treatment complications of high dose mantle field radiation and chemo therapy secondary to Hodgkins ___ including chest wall deformity, radiation pulmonary fibrosis, premature CAD, and autonomic dysfunction. She was admitted for TAVR ___ valve), which was done on ___. Her course was complicated by iliac & femoral artery injury s/p repair by Vascular Surgery. She was stabilized and was discharged to home. ACTIVE ISSUES: #. Aortic stenosis/CHF: now s/p TAVR. At recent baseline had been NYHA class III. Not an operable candidate so underwent TAVR. Good result with subsequent increase in valve area from 0.8 cm2 to 1.2-1.9cm2, EF up from 50% to >55%. Overall, she appeared quite volume overloaded so she was diuresed with IV Lasix. She continues on ASA, Plavix. Not on a beta blocker as CHF was mostly due to AS and will likely improve after TAVR, but this could be considered in the future. She will f/u with Dr. ___ after discharge. #. Vascular complication: During removal of the ___ Fr sheath of the left iliac artery, dye extravasation was noted with presumed iliac rupture. Hemostasis was achieved quickly with balloon tamponade. Vascular Surgery performed covered stenting of left internal iliac artery, femoral cutdown/thrombectomy, and primary repair femoral artery. Pt also with resultant RP bleed and Hct drop from 28 to 24, but this remained stable at 32 after 5u pRBC. CTA showed no active contrast extravasation. Good pulses bilaterally. She will follow up with Vascular Surgery after discharge. #. Surgical site infection: treating empirically, though low suspicion. She had mild erythema of left groin surgical site and one fever to 101.3 on POD1, with WBC up to 18. Vancomycin was started, but this was switched to clindamycin after the site appeared to improve drastically. She will complete a 7 day course of antibiotics. #. CAD: has LM&LAD stent. She was continued on ASA, Plavix, and statin throughout hospitalization. #. ?Plavix resistance: unlikely. As an outpatient, she has been put on high-dose Plavix due to concerns for possible Plavix resistance. Hematology was consulted regarding whether or not she could have Plavix resistance. They felt that there was minimal risk for her to continue the higer dose plavix and given that her outpt provider desired the higher dose, this was continued. #. ___: Baseline Cr 0.8, has been up to 1.1 but now back down to 0.8. Was likely due to hypotension from blood loss and resolved. #. Gout: left MTP, occurred this admission. This has happened in the past in the setting of diuresis. She was started on prednisone; pt refused colchicine. #. Sacral/coccyx wounds: related to radiation and scleroderma. Wound care was consulted and she had Mepilex to wounds, pressure redistribution chair cushion when OOB to chair. Could consider outpatient plastic surgery consultation. # BRBPR: Patient had several bloody BMs during her admission. On exam, she had clear hemorrhoids. She remained hemodynamically stable with no changes in hematocrit and no need for any transfusions. INACTIVE ISSUES: #. Hodgkins lymphoma s/p mantle radiation: Anesthesia evaluated the patient for ease of intubation given history of mediastinal radiation. The esophagus is narrowed making TEE difficult, therefore TTE was arranged during the procedure. #. Pulmonary fibrosis: The patient was continued on pulmicort with xopenex rescue as needed. #. Hypothyroidism: stable. She continued on Levothyroxine. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: -outpt ECHO -outpt GI followup for BRBPR
summary: You were admitted with worsening shortness of breath and unterwent a transcutaneous aortic valve replacement. The procedure went well but you had a left iliac rupture that needed to be surgically repaired. You have sutures in place that will be removed by Dr. ___ on ___. Please keep the site clean and dry with a dry guaze dressing, change daily. Your gout flared while you were here and you were started on a tapering dose of prednisone. Finally you had some blood in your stools that we think is because of the plavix and hemmorhoids. You need to make an appt for a colonoscopy as soon as you feel up to it.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ with a medical history of systolic heart failure (EF 30%), complete heart block s/sp PPM, chronic renal insufficiency (basline cr 1.5-1.8) presents with acute on chronic systolic heart failure and acute on chronic renal failure. # ACUTE ON CHRONIC SYSTOLIC HEART FAILURE: The patient was admitted to the MICU (given need for BiPAP in ED) with clinical, lab (elevated BNP compared to prior), and radiographic evidence of volume overload consistent with decompensated heart failure. He diuresed well with IV lasix boluses and was transferred to the floor given improved respiratory and mental status. His lasix was increased to 120mg PO BID. Of note he can be given IV lasix if he refuses his PO lasix while his acute CHF is continuing to resolve. Please note that we would recommend starting a low dose of carvedilol (suggest 6.25mg daily) once his current episode of acute CHF resolves. # ALTERED MENTAL STATUS: waxing and waning mental status with reduced orientation and inattention consistent with delirium. Brief episodes of mild agitation including concerns that hospital staff were not acting in his best interests and holding him against his will. Given small doses of prn haldol and olanzapine. Possible etiologies include renal failure, decomensated CHF, or hypercarbia (although blood gas not significantly different from prior). Impaired sleep wake cycle may have contributed as the patient had not been sleeping well the past several nights due to respiratory distress. Possible contribution from potential UTI although cultures. # LEUKOCYTOSIS: Elevated WBC without fevers spiking to WBC ~34 in the three days after pt ripped out his foley. Unclear if inflammatory response vs transient infection but patient remained afebrile with stable hemodynamics and no diarrhea. Started on vanc/cefepime which were stopped prior to discharge. Leukocytosis resolved by time of discharge and patient remained afebrile. # ACUTE ON CHRONIC RENAL FAILURE: Patient with baseline creatinine of 1.5 to 1.8 with creatinine of 2.5 on admission. Urine with hyaline casts suggesting pre-renal failure likely secondary to decompensated CHF. He was diuresed with lasix and his renal function improved. # DIABETES: Held oral hypoglycemic was held on admission and his sugars were well controlled on an ISS. His oral hypoglycemic was restarted on discharge. # Hypernatremia: Briefly hypernatremic to 149 likely ___ free water deficit. Resolved. #Code: Please note that this patient was made DNR/DNI while in house.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital because of increasing shortness of breath, leg swelling, and confusion. While in the hospital, you initially managed in the ICU with diuretics. You did well there, and were subsequently sent to the general medical floor. Your breathing continued to improve. We had a urinary catheter in your bladder to measure your urine output, but you pulled this out one of the nights. This caused some trauma to your urethra and some blood loss. It was subsequently removed and you did not have any urinary issues after. Finally, you had a bit of agitation which we managed with agitation medications. You seemed to improve from this as well.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Mrs. ___ is an ___ year old female with severe aortic stenosis, congestive heart failure, and atrial fibrillation who presents with worsening shortness of breath across several months. # DYSPNEA/AORTIC STENOSIS/CHF: Likely due to severe aortic stenosis and resultant pulmonary edema and pleural effusion as supported by CXR and elevated BNP. Echo confirmed severe AS as well as mild AR, mild-to-moderate MR, and mod-to-severe TR. Valvuloplasty was considered but decided against in light of her aortic regurgitation. A therapeutic thoracentesis was considered but felt to be unsafe at this time with concern for hypotension given her potential fluid shifts during diuresis. It might be considered in the future. She was started on spironalactone and her lasix dose was adjusted. Her weight decreased and her symtoms moderately improved during her hospitalization. Her metoprolol was reduced in order to give blood pressure room for diuresis and to avoid excessive bradycardia given her aortic regurgitation while still maintaining rate control given her periods of exertional tachycardia. The possibility of a future valvuloplasty or a pacemaker was discussed with the daughters prior to discharge, who felt that this was something they might consider if it became clinical necessary. # ATRIAL FIBRILATION: Patient was admitted with long-standing atrial fibrillation not on anti-coagulation. She was not tachycardic during most of this admission but had periods of tachycardia to the 140s after exerting herself. Her metoprolol was reduced as discussed above. According to the records anti-coagulation was decided against in ___ due to the patient's perceived fall risk. During this admission the decision about anti-coagulation was deferred to outpatient follow-up. She was started on a 325mg aspirin for chest pain which was continued on discharge for her atrial fibrilation pending further discussion of full anti-coagulation. # CHEST PAIN: Suspect again secondary to severe AS with limited coronary filling during diastole. Troponin 0.___K. EKG showed no ST segment changes. She was started on a 325mg aspirin. # UTI: Positive U/A, afebrile, patient not reporting dysuria but per family having worsening nocturia. Past h/o UTIs. Treated with a 3-day course of Cipro (started ___. Urine Cultures with >100,000 e coli (presumptive). # DEMENTIA: ongoing for ___ years, now moderate to severe, oriented to person and to her daughters but completely disoriented to place and time. Given haldol for mild agitation. Olanzapine held due to concern of daughters for adverse reactions. # SOCIAL: The patient's daughters have been staying with her ___ (alternating 12 hour shifts) and appeared quite exhausted. They explained that they would consider having help but that they know that their mother will "not do well with strangers." Apparently she gets agitated when her daughters are not around. Social work was consulted to help determine if there were services available which might be able to help the daughters with the care of their mother.
summary: You were admitted for increasing shortness of breath. While you were here you were treated with diuretics. We also performed some tests to assess the function of your heart. Our tests suggest that your shortness of breath was likely due to a combination of your aortic stenosis as well as some fluid that has collected around your lungs. You also had a urinary tract infection which we have treated with antibiotics. We have changed some of your medications while you were here. Please START taking Spironalactone 25mg daily. This is to control the amount of fluid in your body. Please REDUCE your dose of furosemide (Lasix) to 60mg daily. This is 3 of the 20mg tablets. This is to control the fluid in your body. Please REDUCE your dose of metoprolol XL (toprol) to 50mg daily. We are giving you a prescription for this. You can also just take ___ tablets of your 100mg Toprol. This medication is to control your heart rate. Please START taking Ciprofloxacin twice a day for the next day. You can stop after 2 more doses. This is to treat a urinary tract infection that you had. Please START applying Miconazole Powder to your rash twice a day for the next 5 days. You can then continue to use it as needed if your rash recurs. Please START taking a 325mg aspirin daily. This was started to reduce the risk of stroke given your atrial fibrilation.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ female with a hx of sCHF EF 30%, afib, DM II (last A1c 7.4 ___, obesity, hyperlipidemia who presented with increasing b/l lower extremity edema and symptoms consistent with PND #Decompensated Systolic Heart Failure, EF 25%: Presentation consistent with decompensated disease; most likely secondary to med and diet non-compliance. Etiology of systolic heart failure remains unclear. Less likely to be ischemic in setting of clean cath on ___ be relate to tachycardia induced cardiomyopathy or idiopathic. Rate controlled was optimized with metoprolol succinate 130. She initially diuresed with IV lasix and transitioned to torsemide 20mg daily. Home losartan was increased to 100mg daily. She was continued on home spironolactone and digoxin. She will need further consideration for an ICD going forth. #A Fib: Pt was in afib this admission. Afib was rate controlled with metoprolol succinate 150mg daily and anticoagulated with warfarin. Rate currently well controlled and hemodynamically stable. Pt was successfully cardioverted this admission. Chronic Issues Managed: #DM II:Diabetes has been poorly controlled on oral antihyperglycemics with last A1c 7.4 this past month. Glimepiride was held in favor of HISS. Pt should follow up with PCP for further management of diabetes. She may benefit from insulin going forth but medication and diet compliance are questionable. #Hyperlipidemia: continued with home atorvastatin. #Hypertension: Increased losartan to 100mg daily. Transitions of Care: #Consider ICD placement in future once heart failure stable #Pt will need close monitoring of blood sugars and consider transitioning to insulin for better glycemic control. #Full code
summary: You came in due to difficulty breathing at night and increasing swelling of your legs. We believe these symptoms are due to your previously diagnosed heart failure. We increased your dose of diuretics (water pills) with improvement in your symptoms. We also performed a cardioversion for your atrial fibrillation which was successful. We hope this cardioversion allows your heart to pump better and reduces your symptoms. You have been started on a medication called amiodarone which decreases the chance of your heart being in atrial fibrillation. This medication will effect your INR and you will require more frequent checks over the next several weeks. The ___ ___ clinic is aware of these changes.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ was admitted to the ___ on ___ for HFpEF exacerbation as well as hematochezia. ACUTE ISSUES ============= #Acute on chronic HFpEF exacerbation: Peripheral and edema at rehabilitation likely due to inadequate outpatient diureses. CXR at admission was consistent with pulmonary vascular congestion and mild pulmonary edema. Patient was started on Lasix gtt at 20mg/hr with a goal of net negative ___. Strict I/Os were monitored with a Foley catheter. Daily standing weights were taken. Twice-a-day electrolytes were monitored for electrolyte abnormalities and repleted as needed. Lasix was held as needed for symptomatic hypotension on HD1. Hypotension resolved on HD2. Spironolactone 25mg BID was re-started on HD4 after resolution of hypotension. Metolazone 5mg PO daily was added on HD8 to improve diuresis for 1 day. The patient was transitioned to oral torsemide 80mg QD and spirolactone 25mg BID prior to discharge, which she tolerated well without signs of fluid accumulation. #Hematochezia: Most likely secondary to history of hemorrhoids and diverticulosis. Last outpatient colonoscopy in ___. Hb stable during admission at ___ from baseline of ___ in ___. GI was consulted and performed flexible sigmoidoscopy on ___ after resolution of HFpEF exacerbation. BID CBCs were monitored for active bleeding. Patient was given Colace and senna as needed to prevent constipation. Patient was given witch ___ pad for hemorrhoids. #Atrial fibrillation: Patient is s/p multiple cardioversions, AVJ ablation, and BiV pacer (___). Patient's biventricular pacing was adjusted by her home cardiologist (Dr. ___ as an inpatient. Home rivaroxaban was continued. #Complicated, catheter-associated urinary tract infection: Patient developed night sweats/chills on ___ and was found to have a likely urinary tract infection on urinalysis. Indwelling Foley catheter was removed. IV cefepime was started (day 1 = ___ for empiric Pseudomonas coverage. Urine cultures grew pan-sensitive E. coli, so antibiotics were switched to PO Ciprofloxacin 250mg q12hrs prior to discharge, with plans for a 10 day total course (last day ___ for catheter associated UTI. CHRONIC ISSUES =============== #IDDM: Home oral glipizide and sitagliptin were held. Glargine 28U was continued. Insulin sliding scale with TID FSBGs were used. #Chronic back pain: Continued home gabapentin, APAP, and lidocaine patch while inpatient. Her home APAP was uptitrated to 1000mg TID due to poor pain control, resulting in significant improvement in her pain. #GERD: Continued home ranitidine #Anxiety/depression: Continued home sertraline ==================== TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: ==================== NEW MEDICATIONS: [ ] Torsemide 80mg PO DAILY [ ] Add Torsemide 20mg PO QPM PRN: Weight gain > 2 lb, increased edema, or dyspnea [ ] Spironolactone 25mg PO BID FOLLOW-UP ITEMS: [ ] Complete 10-day course of ciprofloxacin for catheter-associated UTI (___) [ ] Please record daily standing weights and provide close outpatient monitoring of PO diuretic regimen. If weight increases > 2 lb, please give additional PRN torsemide dose of 20mg (in addition to standing torsemide 80mg; total of 100mg). [ ] Please call Dr. ___ weights increase > 2lb lb for further recommendations on diuretic regimen. [ ] Please continue 2L daily fluid restriction and 3g sodium restriction. [ ] ___ consider outpatient colonoscopy for further diagnostic work-up if continues to have hematochezia
summary: You came to the hospital because of worsening shortness of breath, leg swelling, and weight gain all related to worsening of your congestive heart failure. You were given IV furosemide (Lasix), spironolactone, and metolazone to help remove extra fluid that caused your shortness of breath, leg swelling, and weight gain. Your pacemaker rate was adjusted by your home cardiologist. You were seen by our inpatient gastroenterology service for bright red blood in your stool. They performed a flexible sigmoidoscopy which showed hemorrhoids, however no evidence of active bleeding. You were diagnosed with a urinary tract infection. You were given IV antibiotics to treat this infection, which were switched to oral antibiotics before you went home. You received a nuclear medicine study ("stress test") to assess the blood flow to your heart. This test was normal.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ year old male with past history of CAD s/p stent at ___ E's ___, NIDDM, hypertension, hyperlipidemia who presents with several weeks of intermittent chest pain and a week of exertional dyspnea concerning for fluid overload and heart failure. # Coronaries: 100% chronic total occlusion of proximal total R PDA, s/p PCI to midLAD and Lcx, stent in midLAD, unknown type. (per ___ records) # Pump: normal EF # Rhythm: sinus #HFpEF exacerbation: Patient had progressive swelling of lower extremities with DOE and PND concerning for heart failure exacerbation. Trop neg x1. EKG had T wave flattening but no specific ST changes concerning for acute MI. Patient was started on IV Lasix for diuresis and transitioned to PO regimen of 40 mg Lasix. He was started on lisinopril 10 mg and carvedilol 12.5 mg PO BID. Echo records from outside hospital showed normal EF in ___. He also had records from ___ which showed chronic total occlusion of RCA and stent placed to mid-LAD. Stress echocardiogram was done which showed no inducible ischemia and stress ECG was without ischemic changes. He was discharged on atorvastatin 40mg, carvedilol 12.5mg BID, furosemide 40mg BID and lisinopril 20mg. He will follow up with an outpatient cardiologist, Dr. ___, for further management. Discharge weight: 130.8kg #Hypertensive urgency: Patient non-compliant with HTN meds at home (takes HCTZ intermittently) and found to have elevated SBP 180s on floor, asymptomatic. Home amlodipine, HCTZ, and verapamil were d/c'ed and patient was transitioned to lisinopril 20mg and carvedilol 12.5mg for both HTN and CAD/HF management. BP should be monitored and medications titrated as needed. #Low back pain - patient uses gabapentin, tramadol, and naproxen at home. Naproxen was discontinued given concern for ___ and cardiovascular risk. #Diabetes: Insulin sliding scale while inpatient. Restarted metformin on discharge. #Asthma: Controlled with Albuterol inhaler prn #OSA: non-compliant with CPAP. Encourage use as outpatient. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: - patient to establish care with cardiologist for further outpatient f/u. Appointment schedule with Dr. ___. - continued titration of diuretic dose based on volume status and creatinine - monitor BP and titrate anti-hypertensives as needed - patient reports poor compliance with CPAP for OSA- please continue to encourage use Discharge weight: 130.8kg
summary: You were admitted to ___ due to lower extremity swelling and some new shortness of breath with walking. You were found to have signs of fluid overload and heart failure on exam and were treated with a medication called Lasix. You will need to continue this medication at home. You were also found to have very high blood pressure. You were started on carvedilol, a medication that helps treat your heart disease and high blood pressure. You were also started on Lisinopril for blood pressure control. You were also started on Lipitor for your heart disease. A stress echocardiogram was done to evaluate your heart for worsening heart disease and for heart pumping function. This showed no major abnormalities. We have set up an appointment for you with ___ cardiology (see below). You need to take these medications every day.

text: Brief Hospital Course: REASON FOR HOSPITALIZATION: ___ female with history of HTN, HDL, MI ___ with DES to RCA ___ in the context of chest pressure & troponin elevation presents with DOE & self-remitting, atypical, intermittent chest pain at rest for 2 weeks. ACTIVE DIAGNOSES: # Atypical Chest Pain: Ms. ___ was ruled out for an acute myocardial infarction with two sets of negative cardiac biomarkers and an EKG that did not show any signs of ischemia. Given her unremarklable perfusion study in ___ and her negative adenosine stress test ___, she was not felt to be in need of further cardiac testing. Her pain was intermittent, seems to have been noticed mainly in a reposed position, and was not associated with any other symptoms. As such, it is possible that her chest discomfort is due to worsening gastric reflux in the setting of recent weight gain. # Dyspnea on Exertion: At baseline the patient is able to walk approximately 25 feet before she begins to feel short of breath. Recently, her functional capacity has diminished somewhat (she is now limited to ___ blocsk) secondary to worsening dyspnea on exertion in the absence of chest discomfort. On admission she had a chest xray that was not concerning for an acute cardiopulmonary process. Her shortness of breath was felt to be multifactorial (obesity, deconditioning, COPD, CHF). She was recently seen an an outpatient basis for lower extremity edema & DOE and her morning home lasix dose was increased from 80 mg to 160 mg (in addition to the 80 mg she takes QPM). She did not endorse any dietary indiscretions but does state that given the sweltering heat last week, she did consume more fluid than usual. She was instructed to keep a journal of her daily weights and call her PCP if her weight increased greater than or equal to two pounds from day to day. CHRONIC DIAGNOSES: # Coronary Artery Disease: Ms. ___ was continued on her home doses of aspirin, isosorbide dinitrate, nitroglycerin tablets, plavix, and simvastatin. # Congestive Heart Failure: She was also continued on her home doses of lasix (which was recently increased as above) and metoprolol. # Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease: The patient was continued on her home inhalers. We recommended that she visit an outpatient pulmonologist for pulmonary function tests and a possible sleep study (to evaluate for Obstructive Sleep Apnea and Obesity Hypoventilation Syndrome). # Hypertension: Ms. ___ was continued on her home doses of diovan, metoprolol, & amlodipine. # Diverticulosis: She was given a bowel regimen while in house. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: # Follow up: The patient was instructed to follow up with Dr. ___ on ___. She was also encouraged to ask Dr. ___ ___ a referral to a pulmonologist to further evaluate her COPD and assess her for possible Obstructive Sleep Apnea. # Risk for readmission: During her brief admission, the patient was encouraged to weigh herself daily (and call her doctor if she gainedand adhere to a low-salt, fluid-restricted diet. She is at risk for readmission for volume overload if she does not adhere to her dietary restrictions.
summary: You came to the hospital because you had been experience intermittent chest pain at rest along with shortness of breath while walking. There are many causes of chest pain, and not all of these causes are related to the heart. While you were in the hospital we determined that your chest pain was extremely unlikely to have been caused by a heart problems. Your EKG (which is an electrical picture of your heart) and bloodwork were not concerning for a blockage in one of the arteries in your heart. Since you had a recent stress test (which was negative), we did not feel that you needed to undergo any additional testing. Given the fact that your chest pain lasted only several minutes and was not associated with any other symptoms, it is possible that it could be related to gastric reflux. Regarding your shortness of breath, it is likely that this is due to a variety of different factors. The fact that you have been somewhat inactive over the past year and you have gained weight can all contribute to shorness of breath and decreased exercise tolerance. You also have COPD, which can also cause intermittent shortness of breath. You had a chest xray that did not show anything that could explain your sensation of breathlessness. It is possible that some of your shortness of breath was due to excess fluid in yoru body (which is why your lasix dose was recently increased at clinic). You should continue to take the increased dose of lasix. It is important that you weigh yourself each day to determine if you are rapidly gaining weight (greater than 2 pounds of weight gain from one day to the next usually indicates excess fluid and you may need to increase your lasix dose).

text: Brief Hospital Course: Patient is a ___ with COPD, tracheobronchomalacia, admitted to the general medicine service with respiratory distress; BiPAP requirement, tachycardia, and elevated lactate. # Respiratory distress due to tracheobronchomalacia. Patient with baseline significant TBM and possible additional type of COPD, though patient denies emphysema/asthma (unclear etiology and time course of TBM.) Also of note is her recent steroid use for TBM. The initial differential diagnosis included COPD exacerbation (if reactive airways truly present), pneumonia, PE and volume overload. There was no definitive positive culture data. Also metabolic acidosis from lactate as below likely to be contributing, as patient with poor respiratory reserve and unlikely to be able to satisfactorilly compensate from respiratory standpoint. Pt initially required BIPAP, but this was discontinued on hospital day 3, and only required PRN overnight. Pt was placed empirically on heparin gtt pending further w/u for PE. LENIs were found to be negative for DVT and V/Q scan did not show any abnormalities. Therefore heparin gtt was discontinued. Pt continued to be unable to produce sputum for cultures. Viral swabs were performed which were negative for viral antigens. Urine legionella antigen was also negative. Pt was initially treated empirically with vancomycin, ceftriaxone and azithromycin (day ___ and a PICC line was placed on HD3. Vancomycin and ceftriaxone were discontinued on ___, and she was continued only on azithromycin (___ompleted ___ as she had no white count and was afebrile. Patient was also treated for fluid overload with boluses of IV Lasix with improvement in her respiratory function. TTE was also performed to evaluate cardiac function which demonstrated a normal LVEF with LVH suggestive of diastolic failure. Anxiety is likely a large component in her respiratory distress, and she received ativan as needed for anxiety/shortness of breath (and this should be considered before immediately resorting to nebs). PPI was increased for GERD as possible etiology of TBM and/or cough. She was on steroids, which were tapered rapidly. She was on 10mg Prednisone in-house. On the floor, patient's respiratory status remained stable with continued cough. # Obstructive Sleep Apnea: Patient had a bedside limited sleep study which showed findings consistent with obstructive sleep apnea. Sleep disorder team arranged for patient to receive a CPAP machine at home on discharge, and arranged for supplemental nighttime oxygen to be delivered to the patient's house for use until CPAP machine delivery. # Acute on Chronic Diastolic CHF: New diagnosis on TTE. On beta-blocker. ___ not started (in light of allergy to lisinopril). Lipid panel showed elevated lipids and Zocor was increased in dose prior to discharge. Lasix 40mg daily was given in-house but patient was not discharged with Lasix as she was felt to be euvolemic. She was instructed to follow up as outpatient for possible initiation of Lasix as needed. # Lactic Acidosis Elevated lactate of 3.1. Pt was not hypotensive, though tachycardic. No evidence of bowel ischemia/abd benign. This was tracked and trended down throughout admission and was felt to be diaphragmatic. # Metabolic acidosis. Likely lactic acidosis with very elevated lactate as above. Also with small ketones in urine. This resolved throughout the course of hospitalization.
summary: You presented to the hospital for shortness of breath and a cough, and were found to have a low oxygen level. You were placed on a bipap breathing machine, and was sent to the intensive care unit until your breathing improved. You were started on antibiotics for a pneumonia, and given steroids and breathing treatments and a water pill with improvement of your symptoms. You were on a blood thinner briefly until a test proved that you did not have a blood clot in your lungs causing your shortness of breath. An ultrasound of your heart showed possible decreased heart function. Because of your new diagnosis of decreased heart function (Diastolic heart failure) you should see your primary care doctor within ___ weeks of leaving the hospital to discuss the possibility of starting Lasix on a daily basis to help prevent fluid from building up in your lungs.

text: Brief Hospital Course: SUMMARY ======== Mr. ___ is a ___ year-old man with a history of multipmyeloma, hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (possible amyloid cardiomyopathy), CKD stage IV, mild cognitive impairment, and prostate cancer who is admitted with dyspnea concerning for decompensated heart failure ACUTE ISSUES: ============= #Acute on Chronic Heart Failure with Preserved Ejection Fraction (HFpEF) #Bilateral pleural effusions #Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy Patient came on ___ with one day history of shortness of breath, PND and orthopnea with associated bilateral lower extremity edema. CXR from ___ showed evidence of pulmonary edema, bilateral pleural effusions. proBNP was elevated to 8725, concerning for decompensated HF. Unclear precipitant of decompensation ___ be secondary to medication non-adherence at rehab facility). Given leukocytosis and possible evidence of pneumonia on CXR, patient was treated for community acquired pneumonia (see below). Negative troponins and no obvious EKG changes compared to priors argues against ischemia. Patient was suspected to have amyloid cardiomyopathy due to TTE findings of uncontrolled hypertension and low ECG voltage criteria of LVH; however, he was investigated by fat pad biopsy and cardiac MRI that was not concerning for amyloidosis. Patient diuresed well with Lasix, and was transitioned to PO torsemide 80mg daily to net even fluid balance. He was also managed with metoprolol, spironolactone and ASA. #Community acquired pneumonia Patient with CXR showing possible evidence of pneumonia although without frank consolidation. Patient was afebrile but did have a mild leukocytosis and lactic acidosis. He was treated empirically with a 5 day course of azithromycin and ceftriaxone. CHRONIC ISSUES: =============== #CKD Patient's baseline Cr 1.9-2.1, Cr at baseline on admission #Neuropathy ___ chemotherapy Continued renally-dosed gabapentin #Multiple Myeloma Was supposed to restart chemotherapy on day of admission. Patient will follow with Oncologist Dr. ___. Continued acyclovir TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: ==================== - New Meds: Spironolactone 12.5 daily - Stopped/Held Meds: None - Changed Meds: Metoprolol succinate decreased from 50 mg to 25mg daily, Torsemide increased from 20 mg to 80mg daily - Discharge weight: 79.2 kg (174.6 lb) - Discharge creatinine: 1.7 #HFpEF [] Measure weights daily [] If weight goes up by ___ pounds in one to two days, consider increasing torsemide dose to 100mg.
