[] If weight goes down by pounds in ___ or if patient becomes dehydrated/dizzy, consider decreasing torsemide dose to 60mg daily, and schedule PCP ___. [] Continue torsemide 80mg daily [] Continue metroprolol succinate XL 25mg daily [] Continue spironolactone 12.5mg daily #Multiple myeloma [] Continue acyclovir 200mg BiD [] Continue dexamethasone 20mg on ___ weekly [] Patient was supposed to restart chemotherapy for multiple myeloma on the day of his admission. He will follow up with Dr. ___. Major Surgical or Invasive procedures:None
summary: You were admitted because of shortness of breath You were admitted to the hospital because you had been feeling short of breath and you were found to have fluid on your lungs. This was felt to be due to a condition called heart failure, where your heart does not pump hard enough and fluid backs up into your lungs. You were given a diuretic medication through the IV to help get the fluid out. You improved considerably and were ready to leave the hospital. Your weight at discharge is 79.2 kg (174.6 lb). Please weigh yourself today at home and use this as your new baseline. Please weigh yourself every day in the morning.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ yo M PMHx Metastatic Lung Cancer presenting with shortness of breath and hypotension (which resolved prior to admission), was briefly admitted to ICU for monitoring, but was discharged home after goals of care discussion decided to pursue home hospice. # Hypotension/Tachycardia: Patient was referred to ___ for BP 70/56 sitting and 58/40 standing in the setting of dyspnea and poor PO intake. On arrival to ___, SBP 110s without IVF/pressors. Pt was given gentle IVF and allowed to eat, and remained normotensive thereafter. After discussion with the ICU team, patient indicated that his treatment preference was to be discharge home with hospice. In addition, his goals of care transitioned to specifically discontinue non-symptom-focused interventions and, to facilitate this, he was set up with home hospice. Of note, lasix, which he had been taking prior to admission, was stopped on discharge. # Dyspnea: Patient had had worsening dyspnea for the past several days prior to admission; nebulizer was unhelpful and pleural fluid drainage was only mildly helpful. CTA chest was remarkable for new ground glass opacity concerning for pneumonia but pt was without fever, or sputum production c/f pneumonia. After one night in ICU, pt felt that respiratory status improved and returned to baseline. Accordingly, likely experienced chronic process from ongoing pleural effusions. # Metastatic NSCLC: Not currently undergoing directed anticancer therapy with treatment focused on symptoms (pleural drainage catheter for recurrent pleural effusions). CTA chest this hospitalization concerning for disease progression. PleurX Catheter was drained. Pt has outpt heme/onc f/u appt, and was discharged on hospice as above # Acute Cholecystitis status-post Percutaneous Biliary Drainage: Status-post discharge on ___, was started on amox-clavulanate for a 10 day total course (___). Was continued during hospitalization and pt should finish remaining doses upon discharge # Chronic Systolic Heart Failure / Cardiomyopathy: Furosemide held in setting of hypotension and hypovolemia on examination ## TRANSITIONAL ISSUES - Atorvastatin d/c to avoid polypharmacy in the setting of goals of care. - Home lasix was stopped. - Evaluated and assessed to require home Oxygen. - Home w/ hospice. Would discuss need/goal of re-hospitalization w/ PCP and ___ provider # Communication: Patient, ___ ___ ___ # Code: DNR/DNI confirmed
summary: You were admitted because you had an episode of low blood pressure. Your blood pressure has remained in the normal range since you have been here. You were likely just dehydrated, and thats why we gave you fluid in the hospital. We spoke about the plan to focus more on treatments that will help you feel better and avoid admissions to the hospital. We have arranged the hospice team to meet with you and arrange this plan. It is important that you finish your course of Augmentin for the gallbladder infection. You will also be sent home with oxgyen to help you breathe easier when you walk. Furosemide (Lasix) and atorvastatin (Lipitor) are no longer needed.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ year old man s/p radiation with constrictive pericarditis, now s/p pericardotomy, radiation lung disease, and depressed LVEF with mixed pre-post capillary pulmonary hypertension and severe right heart dysfunction, here with acute systolic CHF exacerbation #Acute Decompensated Systolic Heart Failure: Patient was recorded as 15 lbs over dry weight. Started on bolus IV lasix with a lasix gtt, which was increased subsequently with good results. Patient was administered metolazone and patient diuresed well to dry weight. Had contraction alkalosis and was given diamox with improvement. Transitioned to PO torsemide with good results. Enalapril and carvedilol were held given hypotension throughout admission and on discharge. PAtient was referred to ___ for heart/lung transplant workup as an outpatient. Patient ambulated without shortness of breath prior to discharge. #Acute kidney injury: Creatinine was 4.0 on admission above baseline of 1.5. Likely due to fluid overload. Daily diuresis improved creatinine to 2.2 on discharge. Renal function should be followed as outpatient. #Pulmonary Hypertension: Patient was given sildenafil while inpatient. He was hypoxic at night time and was maintained on supplemental O2. A CT showed plerual effusions and some inflammation with stable adenopathy. #Anemia: Found to be profoundly iron deficient in presence of microcytic anemia. Was started on Fe supplementation TID with recommendation for outpatient iron infusions. Transitional Issues -please ensure follow given weekend discharge without scheduled outpatient appointments -should receive Iron infusion as outpatient -please closely monitor HR and BP, restart carvedilol and enalapril at low dose when able -outstanding heart/lung transplant workup, ___ to contact regarding setup via social worker -renal function not at baseline, please follow -should be setup for home O2 given night time hypoxia, please follow up
summary: You were admitted for shortness of breath. You were found to be in an acute heart failure exacerbation with injury to your kidneys. You were diuresed with IV lasix and responded well. You were converted to torsemide and should continue this as an outpatient. We consulted heart/lung transplant surgeons at ___ ___ who are reviewing your information and will likely see you as an outpatient. Your kidneys improved and are on their way towards your baseline kidney function. Due to hypotension, we could not administer your carvedilol or enalapril, which are being held at discharge. You are continuing on your sildenafil for your pulmonary hypertension. You are starting on torsemide for your diuresis. Please take this daily.

text: Brief Hospital Course: # Angioedema with lisinopril # HBsAb negative # Prediabetes identified: A1C 6.4% # Will follow-up with Dr. ___ failure) after discharge # Recommend sleep study to confirm a suspected diagnosis of OSA # Subclinical hypothyroidism, consider repeat in ___ weeks # Obtained Mass Health insurance for medication access # EtOH cessation recommended given cardiomyopathy # Tobacco counseling # Started on calcitriol: for Vit D deficiency. by labs has secondary hyperpara - elevated PTH, normal calcium levels, and low vitamin D. Suspect due to CKD. Will need close follow up of kidney function. # Mycoplasma IgM/IgG sent (subclinical infiltrate on CXR, rash, and cardiomyopathy). Fourfold rise or fall in titers is the gold standard to confirm acute infection, recommend follow up titers if positive to confirm diagnosis. # Outpatient heme onc follow up scheduled for persistent leukocytosis of unknown etiology # Discharge weight: 114.5kg Mr. ___ is a ___ year old male with a history of hypertension (not taking medications given loss of health insurance) and heavy daily EtOH use ___ pints per day x ___ years) who presented with severe hypertension, renal failure, hepatic dysfunction, and new acute heart failure. # Heart failure with reduced ejection fraction: Mr ___ presented with weight gain, fatigue, shortness of breath, abdominal distention, and pedal edema. Labs with elevated BNP, negative troponins, liver and renal dysfunction. He was diuresed around 50 pounds with intravenous furosemide before converting to oral torsemide, liver and kidney function improved. Initial ECHO showed EF 15% with global dysfunction. Once euvolemic, nuclear stress test was non-ischemic and repeat ECHO showed improvement in EF to 49%. Kappa/lamda ratio was slightly elevated but in consultation with hematology there was a low suspicion for amyloid. Alcohol is thought to be contributory and an infectious process may be responsible given prodrome of cold symptoms, rash, and subclinical infiltrate on chest x ray consistent with pneumonia. Of note, patient developed unilateral lip swelling shortly after a trial of lisinopril, thought to be atypical presentation of angioedema. No airways symptoms. Swelling resolved with removal of drug. - Preload: Torsemide 20mg daily - Neurohormonal blockade: carvedilol 12.5mg BID - Afterload: Valsartan 80mg BID, Hydralazine 50mg q8hrs - follow up with cardiology - discharge weight: 114.5 # Hypertension: Patient presented with hypertensive urgency and difficult to control pressures requiring multiple medications including nitroglycerin drip. Secondary hypertension workup suggests sleep apnea, all other labs negative (renal artery ultrasound, ___ ratio, calcium, metanephrines). BP medication regimen as above. # Renal failure: Improved with diuresis but baseline is not known. Vitamin D deficency, elevated PTH, and normal calcium on labs. Protein/Creatinine ratio 2.1. Renal ultrasound showed that the renal cortex is increased in echogenicity bilaterally, consistent with diffuse parenchymal disease. - Calcitriol - may require nephrology follow-up # EtOH and tobacco use: social work saw patient, counseling provided. #Leukocytosis: Remained elevated during hospitalization, chest x ray showed infiltrate consistent with pneumonia but given clinically well appearing we did not treat. - Heme onc consult as outpatient
summary: You came to the hospital with shortness of breath, weight gain, and swelling in your legs. An ultrasound of your heart showed it was not pumping properly. This is known as cardiomyopathy. With diuretic medication we removed around 50 pounds of fluid from your body and your heart pumping function improved nicely but is still not completely normal. A stress test showed the blood flow to your heart was normal. Your blood pressure was also very high. The same medications we used to treat your cardiomyopathy will help control your blood pressure. You will also need a sleep study to evaluate for a breathing problem during sleep called "sleep apnea." You can arrange this through your primary care doctor's office.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ is a ___ year old woman with hypertension, type 2 diabetes on insulin and obesity who presents with acute hypoxic respiratory failure in the setting of hypertensive emergency. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES =================== [] Weight at discharge: 112.7 kg [] HTN regimen at discharge: Losartan 100 mg daily, Diltiazem XL 240 mg Daily, Furosemide 20 mg [] Patient found to be tachycardic. On discharge HRs in the 100s, started on diltiazem in house [] A1C 12.7 -> patient will require follow-up with PCP or diabetes specialist regarding her diabetes management [] Patient will require renal US to investigate for renal artery stenosis. ___ need MRI due to body habitus [] ECHO ___: Normal LV wall thickness, cavity size, and global systolic function (LVEF>55%). No significant valvular regurgitation. [] Patient requires repeat echo when HR are better controlled (the one from ___ was performed at HR of 140s) [] Consider workup for pheochromocytoma as an outpatient if blood pressures continue to be difficult to control [] CTA showing extensive bilateral ground-glass opacities likely represent pulmonary edema. Could consider repeat imaging when pulmonary edema resolves [] Consider sleep study for OSA [] ASCVD risk prevention: chol found to be elevated. Patient should be started on a statin in the outpt setting. #Acute hypoxic respiratory failure #Acute pulmonary edema in the setting of hypertensive emergency Patient presented with acute hypoxic respiratory failure and shortness of breath, in the setting of hypertensive emergency. Initially hypoxic to the ___ and hypertensive to the 220s, requiring nonrebreather, then BiPAP, then placed on a nitro drip. The hypoxia improved quickly after being on BiPAP and then she quickly transitioned to being stable on RA. Imaging suggests pneumonia vs edema, but given the acute onset of symptoms and clinical picture, pulmonary edema in the setting of acute diastolic heart failure triggered by HTN is more likely. CTA showed no PE. Patient received 20 IV Lasix in the emergency department, and 20mg PO Lasix the following day. Blood cultures were negative by the time of discharge. Could consider repeat imaging when pulmonary edema resolves. #Hypertensive emergency #Hypertension Patient presented with HTN emergency in the setting of intercourse and potentially not well controlled blood pressure as an outpatient. Initially hypoxic to the ___ and hypertensive to the 220s, requiring nonrebreather the BiPAP then placed on a nitro drip. She had a similar episode, though much milder nature several months ago also during intercourse. Her blood pressure have improved on nitro drip, however she remains tachycardic. She had no concerning symptoms of angina on her usual activity level, and reports can go up a couple flights of stairs without any symptoms. Troponin was minimally elevated likely secondary to demand. Her echo was normal without valvular abnormalities or significant LVH. She should be evaluated for renal artery stenosis given the acute nature of this presentation, however given it occurred with exertion rather than at rest this is not urgent and can be performed as an outpatient. Patient was optimized on Losartan 100 mg daily, furosemide 20 mg PO daily, with plans to start diltiazem XL 240 mg daily on ___ after discharge due to HRs in the 100s-110s (she was on amlodiine 5 mg daily in-house). She will require close follow-up with PCP and cardiologist regarding the control of her blood pressure. #Insulin resistance type 2 diabetes. A1C is 12.7. Continue home lantus, but will reduce to 60 mg qhs until ensure safe dosing at higher dose. Discharged on home dose (Lantus 80) but will requires close follow-up for the management of her diabetes given the high A1C. #ASCVD risk prevention: chol found to be elevated. Patient should be started on a statin in the outpt setting.
summary: You came to the hospital because you felt very short of breath while having intercourse due to your blood pressure becoming too high You needed assistance with your breathing for a short amount of time with a special mask. We gave you a water pill to remove the fluid from your lungs. We controlled your blood pressure to keep it within normal range. We started you on Diltiazem XL 240 mg Daily and a water pill, Lasix, 20 mg daily to be taken instead of hydrochlorothiazide. Please start Diltiazem XL 240 mg, 1 pill on the morning of ___ and take daily. Take Losartan 100 mg every morning, starting on ___ (you received a dose on ___. Take Lasix 20 mg every morning, starting on ___ (you received a dose on ___.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ male with history of diastolic CHF, PAF on Coumadin, CAD s/p CABG and DES, COPD, and colon cancer s/p recent resection & chemotherapy who presented with progressive dyspnea and bradycardia in the ___. Diuresed with Lasix ___ mg IV for acute CHF exacerbation. Felt significant relief of dyspnea with diuresis. Patient then restarted on home Lasix 20mg daily. Discharge weight=91.8 kg. EKGs showed high-grade AV block with intermittent AV dissociation and ventricular escape rhythm, which was corroborated on telemetry. Home atenolol was discontinued at admission and not restarted. Electrophysiology consulted and placed a ___ Accolade permanent pacemaker without complications. He was discharged with a CareLink transmitter, which he is to press a button if he has any palpitations before his follow up with the EP Device clinic a week after discharge. His hospital course was complicated only by mild hypertension when off of his home beta blocker. He was discharged on lisinopril 10mg for hypertension and cardiac protection in the setting of CAD. # CORONARIES: -s/p LIMA to LAD, SVG to OM1 in ___ -s/p DES to RCA in ___ # PUMP: dCHF (EF >55%) # RHYTHM: V-Paced; Hx of PAF on Coumadin ============== ACTIVE ISSUES ============== # Acute Diastolic Congestive Heart Failure: Volume overload causing dyspnea in the setting of bradycardia with high-grade AV block. Echo confirmed preserved EF without focal wall motion abnormalities. Discharged on home Lasix 20mg daily. # Bradycardia with high-grade AV block s/p PPM placement: PPM placed ___ and patient is A-sensed, V-paced in the ___. Patient presented with documented low heart rate and dyspnea. EKG with high-grade AV block and documented intermittent AV dissociation with ventricular escape rhythm, at times symptomatic with lightheadedness. Home atenolol was held and not restarted at discharge. Patient to follow up with device clinic. # Acute on Chronic Kidney Injury: Likely cardiorenal, given concurrent CHF exacerbation with bradycardia likely causing poor perfusion. Creatinine downtrending after PPM. Discharge creatinine 1.3. Allopurinol held while inpatient and restarted at discharge. Started lisinopril at discharge for hypertension. # Troponinemia: Stable at multiple timepoints over first 24 hours of admission. Likely due to renal dysfunction. # Atrial fibrillation: Currently in NSR. Per previous notes, appears to be Paroxysmal AF. Continued home warfarin. # Hypertension: Patient reported "allergy" to metoprolol, states it caused him lightheadedness in the past. Discharged on lisinopril 10 mg daily. =============== CHRONIC ISSUES =============== # CAD s/p CABG and DES: Continued home atorvastatin. Started on lisinopril 10 mg prior to discharge. # Gout: Held home allopurinol as above; restarted at discharge. ===================== TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: ===================== [ ] Patient discharged with one day of clindamycin to be taken on ___ to complete a total 3-day course of antibiotics [ ] Patient discharged on lisinopril 10mg daily for hypertension [ ] Home atenolol discontinued and not restarted at discharge [ ] Patient's primary cardiologist may consider adding a beta blocker after discharge [ ] Patient stated he had an allergy to metoprolol, which had caused "lightheadedness" in the past; this was NOT documented in the chart. Suggest follow-up and further characterization of this medication reaction by his outpatient providers.
summary: You were admitted with shortness of breath and a slow heart rate (called "bradycardia"). The shortness of breath was due to fluid overload caused by diastolic congestive heart failure. Your slow heart rate, due to a rhythm problem in your heart, most likely contributed to the fluid overload. The fluid was removed by giving you extra Lasix (furosemide), which makes you urinate. We brought you done to the regular amount of fluid for your body (your discharge weight was 91.8 kilograms or 202 lbs). You will be discharged on your regular dose of Lasix. Your slow heart rate was corrected with the implantation of a permanent pacemaker. You must be sure to follow up with the electrophysiology device clinic as well as with your primary cardiologist.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Mr. ___ is a ___ year old male with PMH significant for ___ dilated cardiomyopathy (EF 19%, severe MR), HIV ___ with ARV meds, last CD4 471 in ___, chronic tachyarrhythmia, schizophrenia, bipolar disorder, depression/anxiety, and methamphetamine use who presented with chief complaint of dyspnea and scrotal swelling due to HFrEF exacerbation. ___ was diuresed with IV Lasix 160mg BID with good urine output, though ultimately still appeared volume overloaded (elevated JVP to mid neck at 90degrees) when he decided to leave AMA. CHF exacerbation thought to be due to medication ___ and also possibly amphetamine use (Utox POS on admission, ___ denied use). Otherwise during this hospitalization, ___ underwent ETT which was ___ (only exercised for .75min, reaching ___ METS). HIV viral load returned elevated, Tcell lymphocyte subset pending at time of discharge. Labs were notable for hypervolemic hyponatremia. Transaminitis and hyperbilireubinemia (thought to be related to underlying HIV with additional congestive hepatopathy) improved with diuresis. Cr rose to 1.6 from 1.3 on admission (baseline 1.1) with ongoing diuresis. ___ had a short run of NSVT (polymorphic) AM of discharge, did receive some IV magnesium. Unfortunately, ___ decided to leave AGAINST MEDICAL ADVICE. He was assessed for capacity and was able to explain the risks of leaving the hospital including worsening heart failure and death from cardiogenic shock. He stated that he wanted to transfer his care to another hospital. He refused to have ___ appointments scheduled. ___ was provided with prescriptions for his medications including entresto and torsemide. ___ was not given HAART given prior plan to start as an outpatient given ___. CHRONIC ISSUES: ============== # schizophrenia # depression # bipolar disorder # anxiety -Continued home abilify -Continued home buproprion TRANSITIONAL ISSUES =================== **Discharge weight: 59.2kg **Discharge Cr: 1.6 **Discharge diuretic regimen: Torsemide 80mg BID x3days, then 60mg BID (increased from 40mg BID) - Of note, ___ again refused discussion re ICD placement during this admission - Consider repeat TTE and pMIBI if evaluated when euvolemic - Cr increasing to 1.6 with ongoing diuresis and possible hypotension during ETT, should have repeat labs drawn with outpatient provider - ___ LFTs this admission, should have repeat labs drawn with outpatient provider - ___ cell lymphocyte revealed CD4 187, HIV viral load 4.9, should discuss ___ of HAART with outpatient provider - ___ complained of some L ear pain this admission, should have evaluation with outpatient provider -___ palliative care ___ as outpatient
summary: You were admitted to the hospital because you had scrotal swelling and were feeling short of breath. Please see below for more information on your hospitalization. You were given IV diuretics to help remove excess fluid in your body caused by worsening heart failure. You had a scrotal US which showed some swelling due to the excess fluid. You had a stress test which showed that you have significantly reduced exercise tolerance. Unfortunately, you decided that you wanted to leave the hospital AGAINST MEDICAL ADVICE before your medication regimen could be optimized. You were able to explain that you risk worsening heart function and possibly death by leaving the hospital too early.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ASSESSMENT & PLAN: Ms. ___ is a ___ past medical history significant for CAD, atrial fibrillation, congestive heart failure, hypertension, and a past CVA who presents from her Geriatric appointment urgently today with SOB, abd pain, weakness, leg ulcers - tthe majority of these appear to be chronic issues and this acute exacerbation is likely due to ___, hyperK, and extravascular volume overload. She has been improving since starting tx in the ED and has been diuresed well on the floor. She had a turbulent hospital course as her volume and respiratory status waxed and waned, but she did stabilize following a transfusion and was more alert toward the time of her discharge. She will be sent home with ___ to assist her helpful son. . # Abdominal pain: DDx includes constipation vs. SBO. Improved distension this morning, patient notes decreased pain - no abdominal pain x several days . # Shortness of breath: DDx includes acute on chronic CHF vs. COPD exacerbation vs. PNA, worsening pulm edema on CXR - continue nebs prn - continue home lasix dose - will add additional lasix if pt tolerates . # Hyperkalemia: likely ___ acute renal failure. Other possibilities to consider are loss from cells such as myositis. CK unremarkable at 72. - trend lytes - monitor on telemetry . # Acute renal failure: Cr 1.6 on admission, up from baseline 1.0. Possibly pre-renal ___ poor po intake, pain. - Cr stable at 1.6 - monitor lytes - small fluid challenge already given in ED . # Elevated INR: up to 4.4 on admission. On coumadin for afib. - monitor INR - now 2.7 - monitor for s/s bleeding - daily INR checks - decrease coumadin to 1mg daily . # CAD: Pt without chest pain, ECG unchanged from prior. Trop 0.04 on admission, but possibly elevated in the setting of acute renal failure. - continue ASA 81mg, statin . # Afib on Coumadin: currently, rate controlled. On coumadin with supratherapeutic INR. - continue Sotalol - consider decreasing dose if renal fx still down - trend INR 2.9-->2.7 today, dec dose yesterday to 1mg/day down from 2 . # Acute on chronic systolic heart failure: Last TTE ___ showed EF 40-45%. Pt has aortic valve bioprosthesis. TTE: improved LV fx overall, but worsening pulm HTN and tricuspid regurgitation - lasix as above . # Pain control: - continue home pain meds . # HTN: currently BP 140/43 - continue Amlodipine, lasix, sotalol . # CODE: DNR (verified with pt, son) # CONTACT: Son ___ ___ # PENDING: Nothing
summary: You were admitted for weakness, and found to have acute renal failure, increased levels of potassium in your blood, and anemia (decreased blood level). During your hospital stay the staff worked to decrease the amount of fluid in your body which was causing some of your symptoms and increasing the amount of blood that was circulating using a transfusion. Your elevated potassium was treated both in the ER and on the hospital ward. You were found to have elevated levels of your blood thinner (your blood was too thin) and that was corrected as well. Your Coumadin dose will need to be adjusted as an outpatient.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Mr. ___ is a ___ with history of hepatitis C cirrhosis complicated by ascites, hepatic encephalopathy, esophageal varices, and portal vein thrombus who presented with altered mental status consistent with hepatic encephalopathy #Altered mental status. Likely hepatic encephalopathy as the patient was here for similar presentation 10 days ago. At that time he had no infectious cause identified and cleared with rifaxim and aggressive lactulose regimen. On ultrasound he has no tapable pocket of fluid. Negative infectious work up with negative U/A, CXR, and blood culture are NGTC. Has improved with lactulose regimen. He will need to continue an agressive lactulose regimen as an outpatient. # Portal vein thrombosis: Portal vein thrombosis was identified on CTA in ___, with presence of stable nonocclusive thrombus within the main and right portal veins confirmed on abdominal ultrasound with Doppler on admission from ___. He was started on lovenox to coumadin bridge at that time. INR 2.2 on admission. He was continued on 4mg of coumadin while he was in the hospital and with 2 days at that dose his INR was 3.8. He was asked to hold his coumadin and restart at 2mg with outpatient INR follow-up. # Hepatitis C cirrhosis: Cirrhosis is decompensated on this admission with encephalopathy and trace ascites, and esophageal varices, grade not specified, were identified on EGD in ___. MELD score is 17 at the time of admission. His liver function tests are stable and consistent at his baseline. He was maintained on rifaximin and lactulose, with an up-titration of the lactulose inorder to help clear as above. In addition he was maintained on his home dose of furosemide, spironolactone, and nadolol. He will continue work-up for a transplant as an outpatient. #Thrombocytopenia: 65k on admission. Consistent with baseline. Likely due to cirrhosis. He remained stable during this hospitalization. # Macrocytic anemia: Stable with baseline at time of admission with an MCV of 99. In the setting of therapeutic vitamin B12 level, macrocytic anemia likely reflected underlying liver dysfunction. # Chronic diastolic heart failure: He was last evaluated by an ___ cardiologist in ___ and was at that time felt to have some evidence of diastolic heart failure on the basis of clinical overload and suggestion of left ventricular diastolic dysfunction on TTE (LVEF of 65% in ___. He appeared mildly volume overloaded, including mild pulmonary edema on CXR. He was maintained on his home dose of furosemide, and spironolactone. TRANSITIONAL: His INR was elevated at time of discharge. He was asked to cut his dosage to 2mg and hold his dose for 1 day. He will follow up with repeat INR 2 days following discharge.
summary: You were admitted with altered mental status due to your liver dysfunction. You were investigated for your infection and one was not found. It is likely that the cause of your change in mental status was that you were not taking enough lactulose. You were given more lactulose and your mental status cleared. You were able to be discharged home with outpatient follow-up. You should continue to take your lactulose as an outpatient. You should have ___ bowel movements per day and depending on your mental status, you may need to have more. Please remember to stay hydrated. You may also benefit starting a low protein/vegan diet as well.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is an ___ female with history of metastatic lung cancer to the brain s/p CyberKnife SBRT, atrial fibrillation (not on anticoagulation), diastolic heart failure, and nonconvulsive status epilepticus on AEDs who presents with altered mental status, headache, hypotension, and bradycardia. Hypotension and bradycardia resolved. # Left frontal headache # Altered Mental Status: patient presented initially to ___ clinic minimally resposive to questions and weeping, pointing to her right eye/ forehead indicating pain. Concerning for progressing intracranial metastatic disease especially with progression on last MRI 1 month prior. Patient underwent head CT in ED without new findings and patient quickly returned to baseline mental status before arrival to floor. Underwent MRI brain and orbit to evaluate for worsening metastatic disease which showed no change. Given patient also hypotensive and bradycardic, concern that patient may have also have had an unwitnessed seizure so underwent video EEG that did not show any epileptiform activity. This was not completed due to patient refusal. Upon further evaluation of headache, patient pointed to left frontal area and states the pain waxes and wanes and no palpable vessel to suggest TA, also fake eye on left and reports pain w/it historically. Following collateral from grandnieces who were visiting for an extended time and spoke her ___ kriole native to her village in ___ she has complained of LEFT sided headache and above eye prosthesis which she says happens when it is cleaned infrequently. Mentally, she was a clear as she has ever been to staff on the floor who are familiar with the patient. # Hypotension: # Bradycardia: Both resolved on arrival to ED. Per EMS report, patient hypotensive to ___ and HR in ___. VS in ED were normal w/BPs 120s and HRs ___. While monitored inpatient she had no VS abnl. Lacosamide has been reported to cause hypotension in rare cases but unlikely. Thought could have been due to a seizure and post-ictal state but EEG unremarkable. No bradyarrhythmias on telemetry. Patient appears euvolemic to hyppervolemic and would expect tachycardic response if hypovolemic. She will have cardiology follow up for her heart failure. # Seizure Disorder Continued keppra and lacosamide doses at home doses of 1500mg keppra and 150mg vimpat given no signs of seizure activity on EEG. -levels pending at d/c # Abdominal Pain: # Nausea/Vomiting: Chronic issues. Appears improved and did not require tretment # Metastatic Lung Cancer: Metastatic to the brain. Last MRI brain was with progressive intracranial metastatic disease in setting of holding crizotinib. Repeat MRI here as above. Will need goals of care discussion as outpatient given poor performance status and recurrent admissions. # Dysphagia/Nutrition Continued ground solids and thin liquids, aspiration precautions maintained. no witnessed events # Atrial Fibrillation: Not on anticoagulation (likely secondary to fall risk and brain metastasis). # Chronic Diastolic Heart Failure # Lower extremity edema Patient with problems with volume overload on previous admissions but appears euvolemic on admission. Torsemide initially held due to hypotension and hypokalemia but later restarted. Continued aspirin and atorvastatin # GERD Continued omeprazole and ranitidine # Gout Continued allopurinol # Hypothyroidism: Continued levothyroxine ==================== TRANSITIONAL ISSUES ==================== [] Seuizures: Patient continued on home doses of lacosamide and keppra. Has scheduled follow-up with neurology [] Follow up levels of keppra and lacosamide [] being evaluated for ?bradycardia and heart failure with new appointment with cardiology. Discharged on home 80mg torsemide. [] d/c bun/cr ___, weight (refused) Name of health care proxy: ___ Relationship: son Phone number: ___ Code: on this admission pt full code. Always discuss as clinically indicated.
summary: You were in the hospital because you had low blooc pressure, a slow heart rate, and were not acting like yourself. You had a test that looked at your brain activity to make sure you were not having a seizure. This test did not show any seizures, thought it was limited because you removed the wires and refused to keep them on. It was explained to you by your grandnieces why the monitoring was necessary, but you were still adamant about not wearing them. Your heart activity was monitored and your heart did not have any abnormal activity, though this was limited because you removed the wires and refused to keep them on. It was explained to you by your grandnieces why the monitoring was necessary, but you were still adamant about not wearing them. You had an MRI of your brain which showed no changes, particularly nothing wrong with your right eye and nothing wrong with the orbit associated with your fake eye.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is an ___ with a history of Afib, HTN, chronic LBP, AAA s/p endovascular repair and recent 2.5 week admission at ___ for orthostatic hypotension who was transferred from ___ after a fall, found to have a Trop of 0.4, now transferred to CCU w/decompensated CHF. #NSTEMI: The patient initially presented to ___ s/p fall with EKG demonstrating V4-V5 ST depression and troponin 0.04. Denied CP, SOB, nausea or diaphoresis. TTE demonstrated severe hypokinesis of inferior, lateral, and posterior walls. In the setting of tropeniemia and concern for ischemia given echo findings, the patient was transferred to ___. She was taken to the cath lab on ___ where she was found to have 85% occlusion of the left circumflex and chronic RCA occlusion. A BMS was placed without complications and the patient was started aspirin 81, metoprolol 12.5mg BID, Plavix 75mg daily, and lisinopril 5mg daily. Home atorvastatin 10 mg was increased to 80 mg. She will continue the Plavix for a total of 30 days. #New Acute Decompensated Congestive Heart Failure: No prior history of CHF, now w/ LVEF 35% likely ___ recent ischemic event(s). Also noted to have at least moderate eccentric mitral regurgitation (likley 3+) with CXR asymmetric pulmonary edema likely secondary to eccentric mitral jet. Precipitating factors for acute decompensation aside from ischemic event could include IVF at OSH and recent starting fluorine for orthostatic hypotension. The patient was inititated on diuretic therapy, however, given her underlying orthostasis, she will takwe changed the prescription to Lasix 20mg as needed if she has >3 lbs weight gain and florinef was held. Midodrine was started with breakfast and lunch for orthostasis and a little fluid (___) was given back in the 24 hours prior to discharge. Started on Metoprolol (in am), lisinopril (at night), aspirin and atorvastatin for underlying ischemic heart disease and recent NSTEMI. Monitor daily weight and I/O's. Discharge weight 51.8 kg. #MR: Ischemic in origin. Eccentric jet from new ischemic insult (likely posteromedial chordae ___ RCA ischemia) likely explains the abnormal CXR findings and her hypoxia. Treated with afterload reduction as above. # S/p fall: Unclear etiology, doesn't know if she lost consciousness. Mechanical vs syncopal (given history and orthostatic) vs cardiac. Concern for arrhythmia given extensive myocardial damage on TTE. # T2 fracture: Unclear acuity per ortho. Logroll precautions. Wore brace and ambulated with ___. Pain treated with Tylenol and prn tramadol. # Orthostatic hypotension: Noted two days prior to discharge. Dizzy while walking with ___. Gave a small amount of fluids (2 250 cc boluses) and started midodrine 2.5 mg BID with improvement in pressures and symptoms. Held florinef given concerns with volume status as discussed above. # Afib: CHADs 4. Continued home amiodarone. Initially held apixaban and started heparin gtt, then restarted apixaban. # Hypothyroidism: Continued levothyroxine.
summary: You were admitted to ___ because you'd had a fall. You were found to have small fractures in your spine. You were seen by the orthopedic surgeons who recommended no intervention for your back. You had some EKG changes and lab values that were concerning for having a heart attack. You also had shortness of breath due to having fluid in your lungs. You had a cardiac catheterization that showed a blockage of one of the arteries that supplies the heart. A stent was placed to relieve the blockage. Your shortness of breath improved with medicines to help take the extra fluid out of your lungs. You also had some low blood pressures but this improved with starting a new medication called midodrine and giving a small amount of fluids.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ with PMH of extensive ischemic CM and CAD and goiter presents with progressive shortness of breath over the last week. PMH suggests that this may be a CHF exacerbation or worsening CAD, but lab data and HPI/CXR/PE suggest that perhaps an element of upper airway obstruction may be contributing to his symptoms. # CORONARIES: S/P CABG in ___ and multiple MI, last cath in ___ showed 3VD. - Worsening shortness of breath with exertion and partial relief by nitro suggestive of angina - EKG with no ST segment changes - Less concern for active coronary disease given neg biomarkers and no EKG changes - Deferred coronary imaging or stress at this time - Continued home plavix and atorvastatin . # PUMP: Last ECHO showed EF 25% with global diastolic dysfxn. History of worsening shortness of breath concerning for CHF exacerbation. - Pt with stable ___ edema, lungs without rales and only mild edema on CXR - Cont home BB, ___, nitrate - Restarted torsemide 20mg QD . # ___: Cr 1.7 on admission, decreased to 1.4 on DOD - Likely due to renal hypoperfusion in setting of volume overload and HF - Improvement to 1.4 in setting of negative fluid balance argues for this hypothesis . # Goiter - Pt has large goiter on exam and evidence of tracheal shift to the R with possible early narrowing on CXR from prior admission - This was thought to be another possible reason for his shortness of breath - CT neck and chest performed, appears that pt has a large substernal goiter that is not causing significant compression of the trachea - ENT consulted and they determined that the patient did not have significant airway obstruction above the vocal cords with fiberoptic laryngoscopy - Last TSH 0.85 on prior admission, had negative FNA in ___ - His goiter might be be functionally significant at this point in time from an endocrinologic standpoint, but may cause obstructive or mass effect problems in the future - Further workup will involve his PCP ___ . # HTN - BP within low 100/70's during hospital stay - Continued home ___ and Imdur at their usual doses . # Hyperlipidemia - Continued home statin . # Epilepsy - Continued home keppra . # GERD/PUD - Continued home Ranitidine . # DM2 - ___ normal 70/30 dose with ISS while in hospital, restarted home doses as outpatient . # Asthma - Continued home advair with PRN albuterol . # AICD - interrogated by the EP staff, no evidence of any tachyarrhythmic activity leading up to his hospitalization . # Nasal congestive symptoms - Flonase and astelin given, seemed to alleviate nasal congestive symptoms
summary: D@ ___, You were admitted to ___ for an evaluation of your shortness of breath. Our labs studies and EKG showed that you are not likely to be suffering from any further blockage of your coronary arteries. You may be more short of breath because you have not been able to take your home torsemide, which we restarted while you were here in the hospital. Also, we gave you some nasal inhalers to help with your congestion symptoms. The EP doctors checked your ___ and determined that you did not have any abnormal heart rhythms. We performed a CT scan to evaluate your goiter. It showed that your goiter is quite large and extends into your neck. We had an Ear Nose and Throat team evaluate you, and they think that although your goiter is very large it doesn't require any intervention at this point in time.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ w/ hx of T2DM, PAD s/p fem-pop bypass, HTN, HLD, CAD s/p CABG and PCI, prostate cancer currently undergoing external beam radiation therapy, and ESRD s/p renal tx in ___, who presents with shortness of breath, found to have PNA and acute on chronic systolic and diastolic heart failure. BNP >18k with increased ___ edema, shortness of breath, crackles on exam, and CXR concerning for pulmonary edema and/or pneumonia. Troponin was elevated (peaked at 1.02), and EKG showed RBBB and left anterior fascicular block (not new). The patient was initially treated for NSTEMI with IV heparin, but the heparin was stopped due to lack of new EKG findings and no symptoms of chest pain/pressure. Echo showed EF 30% compared to EF of 50-55% in ___. Because there was still some concern that ischemia might be contributing to heart failure decompensation, patient underwent a cardiac catheterization, which showed three vessel coronary artery disease, a patent LIMA to the LAD, patent but diseased SVG-diagonal branch, patent but diseased SVG-posterolateral branch, occluded SVG-Ramus, and occluded SVG-PDA. The total occlusion of the RCA stent and the narrowing of the SVG-diag and SVG-PL branch were new findings compared to last cath in ___ (there was 90% occlusion of RCA in ___. However, because a nuclear stress test in ___ showed abnormality in the RCA territory, it may be that the RCA has been down for some time. Here at ___, nuclear stress test showed no reversible perfusion defects. Therefore, the risk of stenting the patent SVG-diag and SVG-PL branch grafts was thought to be greater than the benefit, and no intervention was performed. The patient was diuresed with ___ IV Lasix ___ times/day, and he was net negative ___ L daily in terms of fluid balance. Admission weight was 93.9 kg. Discharge weight was 82.9 kg. He is being discharged on torsemide 20mg daily. Lisinopril 2.5mg was also added. ***He should have labs drawn prior to PCP visit on ___. The patient spiked fevers as high as 102.8 during the first 2 days of admission. He had been flu negative x2 and had failed levofloxacin x7d in the outpatient setting. He is immunosuppresed (on prednisone, mycophenolate, sirolimus), so we decided to treat broadly with vancomycin and cefepime. The patient was on Bactrim for PCP PPX, and we sent an induced sputum for PCP; it was negative. The patient improved on vancomycin and cefepime and completed an 8-day course ending on ___. The patient was continued on amlodipine, metoprolol, 325mg aspirin, atorvastatin (dose was increased), lisinopril, and Plavix. ***He should have LFTs and lipids rechecked as an outpatient. He has high-risk prostate cancer and continued to get radiation therapy on the ___ during his hospital stay. He was continued on doxazosin and tamsulosin. Pt had dysuria likely related to XRT (his U/A was not c/w UTI). For his ESRD s/p transplant, he was continued on his immunosuppressants, and his creatinine remained at baseline (~1.8-2.2). His diabetes was managed with insulin sliding scale. Patient was full code during this admission.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital with heart failure and pneumonia. Your pneumonia was treated with antibiotics (vancomycin and cefepime), and your heart failure was treated with diuretics (IV Lasix and than oral torsemide). We thought that it was possible that you had heart attack that led to worsened heart failure. Therefore, you had a cardiac catheterization, which showed a new blockage of an artery and more narrowing of the vessels in your heart since your last cardiac catheterization. However, based on the results of a stress test, we do not think that these narrowed arteries are necessarily causing worsening heart function.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ with PMH significant for HFrEF (EF 35%), mitral stenosis (MVA 1.0-1.5cm2), COPD who presented with progressive shortness of breath as a result of heart failure exacerbation due to hypervolemia. #Decompensated Heart Failure with reduced EF (35%) with hypervolemia. Sudden onset of symptoms overnight are possibly due to flash pulmonary edema supported by xray findings and high BP 171/63 on admission. Had minimally elevated JVP, crackles on lung exam, trace pedal edema up to mid shin. In ED started on Nitro gtt, 1 dose of 40mg lasix in ED. pBNP 4493. In ___ 3239. Trop negative. Patient received lasix IV BID then transitioned to oral torsemide 20mg. She was on lasix 20mg at home but it was felt that torsemide would be more effect. She continued to diurese and was euvolemic on discharge with a resolution of dyspnea. Her ambulatory O2 Sats were 93%. On echocardiogram review it was found that patient has dysynchronous ventricular contrcaction and that she might benefit from cardiac resynchronization therapy. She should follow up with EP in addition to her cardiologist for further work up. Discharge weight: 60 kg. She had a slight increase in Cr and has an order for follow up lab work on ___. She might need torsemide adjusted as necessary based on renal function. She has f/u in place with PCP and will be scheduled with a f/u with her cardiolgist. #HTN: On presentation patient's BP was 171/63 and she was started on a nitro drip and was discontinued on the second day. Her home medications of lisinopril 10mg and metoprolol were started. Blood pressures continued to be elevated above 150 systolic. Metoprolol was increased and patient's BPs improved. She was discharged with Metoprolol succinate 100 mg QD in addition to the home dose of lisinopril. She should be assessed at f/u for tolerance and to inquire about any symptoms of hypotension. #Paroxysmal atrial fibrillation on amiodarone. Tatient remained in sinus rhythm. INR was therapeutic (2.6) at discharge on 5mg warfarin. #COPD controlled with spiriva. Patient denied exacerbated cough or wheezing or any recent illnesses. #CKD: baseline Cr 1.2-1.8. Stable during admission with a peak at 1.9. On discharge Creatinine was 1.8. Patient has orders to to recheck on ___. #Diabetes - controlled on metformin at home. Didn't required any SSI in hospital.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital after developing shortness of breath. In the hospital it was found that you had excess fluids in your body from you heart failure, a condition that is called a Heart Failure Exacerbation. To treat you we used diuretics which are medicines to help you excrete water from your body. At home you take a diuretic which is called lasix (furosemide). However, while in the hospital we switched you from it to a newer medication called torsemide which is more effective. Your kidney function needs to be monitored and you will need to get blood work to check the kidneys on ___. We will print you a lab slip to take with you. We also discovered by reviewing your echocardiogram (heart ultrasound) that your left ventricle doesn't contract in a synchronous manner. This could be contributing to your heart failure.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ with hx hypertension, BPH but no prior cardiac history, recently diagnosed CHF, transferred after resolution of CHF exacerbation for evaluation of chest pressure and possibly reversible ischemic cardiomyopathy, found with significant L main disease. # Chest pressure: Likely progression of stable angina. Troponins were mildly elevated 0.06 -> 0.07 -> 0.06 per report at ___ ___, representing some demand ischemia. Upon transfer, pt was chest pain free. Catheterization revealed significant L main coronary artery disease. Given L main disease, indication for cardiac surgery. Cardiac surgery consulted and saw patient. However after extensive explanation of risks and benefits of CABG vs. PCI, pt opted for PCI. He had a drug-eluting stent placed on ___ without complication. - ASA 325 mg for at least one month - plavix 75mg for minimum one year - atorvastatin 80mg - Continue Metoprolol Succinate XL 25 mg PO DAILY - lisinopril 5mg # CHF, s/p recent exacerbation - ECHO ___ demonstrated EF 40-45%. Pt was admitted to ___ with elevated BNP and CXR evidence of pulmonary edema as well as bilateral lower extremity edema. He was diuresed with 80mg IV lasix BID with loss of 8 lbs and improvement in his dyspnea and chest pressure. He was essentially euvolemic upon transfer, and was continued on his home dose of furosemide 60mg PO BID. However, he continued to maintain a net negative fluid status throughout his hospital stay. For this reason, his furosemide was decreased to 40mg BID. Dry weight 182lbs. - furosemide 40mg PO BID - ACEI, betablockade as above # BPH - Cont tamsulosin # Lumbar spinal stenosis - Cont gabapentin TRANSITIONAL ISSUES - Cardiologist Dr. ___ will call to set up follow up appointment for possible repeat echo, management of lasix, aspirin, plavix, and possible referral to cardiac rehabilitation.
summary: You were transferred to us after an exacerbation of your congestive heart failure (CHF). You initially had trouble breathing when lying flat, because you had too much fluid in your lungs. At ___, they diuresed you with IV lasix. When you came to ___, you were essentially at a good fluid balance, and at an appropriate "dry weight" of 185lbs. You were complaining of some chest pressure at ___, so they wanted you to come to ___ to have a catheterization to see if there were obstructions in your coronary arteries. We did a catherization and found a significant obstruction of the one of the main coronary arteries. The kind of obstruction you had is usually intervened upon surgically, however when we presented the risks and benefits of bypass surgery vs. stent, you picked the stent. You had a drug-eluting stent placed on ___ in your left main coronary artery. There is a risk of obstruction of the stent, but to minimize this risk you must take plavix 75mg every day for one year. You must also take aspirin 325mg unless your cardiologist lowers this dosage. For you heart failure, the most important things are to weigh yourself daily to make sure you are 185lbs.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ male with recent renal transplant in ___, with nonischemic cardiomyopathy, sCHF (LVEF 35%), asymptomatic atrial flutter with controlled ventricular rate not on any nodal agents and, recently hospitalized for CHF/PNA at ___ on ___, who presents with atypical chest pain and diarrhea for three days. C. difficile testing negative as well as stool O&P. Patient was seen by transplant nephrology while inpatient and tacrolimus dose was adjusted. Patients diarrhea improving at time of discharge, with pending CMV VL, and plans for close f/u by renal transplant team. #Chest pain: EKG unchanged and troponin unchanged compared to prior at 0.03 in setting of renal insufficiency. On review of telemetry, chest pain appears to coincide with episodes of relative tachycardia in atrial flutter, with rates ___. Recent nuclear stress test showed no perfusion defects, making ACS unlikely. Patient given loading dose of ASA and continued on home aspirin 81 , atorvastatin 80 mg daily, Hydralazine and isosorbide. Initially added low dose BB, although pt developed bradycardia. Discussed without outpatient cardiologist; plan to hold BB and consider PPM placement as outpatient to allow for adequate rate control. Frequency of chest pain episodes improved during hospitalization, with infrequent episodes of relative tachycardia by telemetry. # Nonischemic cardiomyopathy, sCHF (LVEF 35%): Admission CXR read as mild congestion, and patient was found to have increased lower extremity edema around ankles with complaint of dry cough for the past several days PTA. Lasix was titrated during admission to achieve euvolemia. increased from 20 mg daily to 20 mg BID. #Diarrhea - concern for C. diff as patient had recent exposure to antibiotics for HCAP during last hospitalization (dc ___ c. diff testing was negative as well as stool O&P. A problem with initial lab sample resulted in delayed CMV viral load testing; after discussion with renal transplant team, and given improving diarrhea, plan for f/u CMV viral load as outpatient. # Atrial flutter: With known atrial flutter with rates in the 60, although as above intermittently up to ___. On admission, pt not on nodal blockers. Patient was trialed on a low dose of metoprolol but was not able to tolerate it secondary to HRs in the ___. Discussed with outpatient cardiologist as above, plan to consider PPM placement as outpatient. Discharged off of BB. Coumadin continued as above with goal INR ___. # Supra therapeutic INR. INR found to be 3.8 on admission. Coumadin held and restarted at home dose once INR< 3.0 # ESRD s/p ECD Renal Txp ___. On admission patients Cr at baseline. Tacrolimus found to be therapeutic. Renal transplant following and adjusted tacrolimus level to 6mg BID. Patient continued on home immunosuppression: CellCept, tacrolimus, and prednisone w/atovaquone and vit D, sodium bicarb # HTN: stable. Patient managed on home regimen Hydralazine 25 TID and Isosorbide Dinitrate 30 TID. # HL: Cont atorvastatin # IDDM: Home NPH and HISS # Gout: stable. Continued home allopurinol # Code: full (confirmed) # Emergency Contact: ___ ___ ******TRANSITIONAL ISSUES:********* #Patient may benefit from non-emergent EGD to continue to workup the chest pain #Lasix increased to 20mg BID due to increased ___ swelling, congestion on CXR. # Attempted to start patient on metoprolol 6.25 BID for atrial flutter and CHF with EF <35%. However he was persistently bradycardic to the ___ on telemetry, therefore metoprolol was discontinued. # Consider ICD placement in addition to pacemaker placement given the severity of his heart failure and to optimize his heart failure and rate management #Transplant nephrology followed patient while inpatient. Tacrolimus dosing reduced to 6mg BID on discharge.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital after you had several days of chest pain at home. We were concerned about a heart attack but after checking the heart enzymes in your blood it does not seem like you had a heart attack. Your chest pain may be due to a stomach ulcer or irritation, or occur when your heart is beating fast. You should ask your primary care doctor about getting a endoscopy which is a procedure where a camera is placed down your throat to evaluate your stomach. Additionally, while admitted you complained of a dry cough, some increased swelling around your ankles and the chest x ray showed some fluid buildup in your lungs. For this reason, we changed your lasix dosing to 20mg twice a day. We attempted to start you on a medicine to better control your heart rate and treat your heart failure called metoprolol, however due to your low heart rate this was discontinued.

text: Brief Hospital Course: 1. Hypoxic Respiratory Failure The patient recently recovered from multifocal PNA requiring long intubation and trach placement. He had been doing well at home s/p trach removal. Now presenting with acute onset of respiratory distress. No witnessed aspiration event. CXR with multifocal opacities concerning for edema vs infiltrate. While on invasive ventilation had versed and fentanyl for sedation. A diagnostic bronchoscopy was preformed to evaluate and to obtain sputum culture. Pt chronically immunosuppressed given kidney transplant, has been on PCP ___. Pt with evidence of volume overload on exam, diuresis was attempted with ethacrynic acid as the pt has history of lasix allergy. A TTE was performed to evaluate for worsening AR/MR or new focal wall motion abnormality as cause for CHF. The TTE was a limited study, and MR could be exacerbating current problem, however, no evidence of worsening by Echo. There was no evidence of infection by sputum, fever, or WBC and bronch appearance was consistent with pulmonary edema. The patient was extubated without complication and was breathing comfortably on room air. The pt was periodically agitated despite seroquel 75mg. His wife stated patient has had paradoxical response to haldol in the past, given zyprexa SL with good result. Upon arrival to the floor, the patient had one episode where he felt subjectively short of breath with no desaturation that was resolved with positioning in a seated position, and another episode which was related to aspiration of his breakfast which resolved with suctioning, sitting, and O2 cannula therapy. The patient has been saturating in the high 90's on room air since. In addition, the patient has been given ethacrynic acid IV to maintain fluid negative status. On discharge, pt's oxygen saturation was 95-98% on room air. He was breathing comfortably without the sensation of shortness of breath. He was no longer requiring diuretics and Is/Os were kept even. 2. CKD s/p cadaveric renal transplant: Pt was continued on his current regimen of tacrolimus, prednisone and cellcept. His Cr remained stable at 1.0 and 1.1 during his course. Renal was following and tacrolimus dose was increased to 4mg BID. Tacrolimus levels were followed and pt was discharged on Tacrolimus 2mg PO BID. Creatinine remained stable throughout admission and but increased to 1.4 on day of discharge. Patient was instructed to have labs draws as outpatient with results faxed to primary care physician and nephrologist. 3. Hypertension: BP meds were held initially given recent hypotension with intubation. HCTZ was started for gentile diuresis. Patient was started on half of home dose of metoprolol. Lisinopril was not restarted due to concerns for worsening creatinine on day of discharge. 4. DM1: Patient's home regimen of NPH and insulin sliding scale. Blood sugars initially ranged into the high 200's low 300's after transfer to the floor; however, the patient was placed on bedtime lantus with HISS, and morning NPH. ___ down into mid to high 100's. Patient will be discharged on home regimen of NPH BID with lispro Sliding Scale. 5. CAD s/p CABG: The patient has extensive cardiac disease, as he is s/p NSTEMI and V-fib arrest in ___ and CABG ___. No evidence of ischemia on EKG. CE enzymes were cycled and negative.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital due to shortness of breath and hypoxic respiratory failure. Because your hypoxia was so severe, you were intubated (a tube was placed down into your lungs to help you breath) and moved to the intensive care unit. During this time you also developed some transient lowering of blood pressure which resolved. After looking at your chest X-ray, it was decided that the reason that you had become short of breath and had the respiratory failure was because of increased fluid in your lungs. You were given a drug called ethacrynic acid, which caused your body to remove the fluid from your lungs, and you began to breath better. Given that many times, it is the heart that is the cause of this sort of respiratory failure, you had a study done of your heart called an echocardiogram which showed that your heart was working fairly well. It may have been a temporary increase in your heart rate that may have caused it to be unable to pump blood outwards causing fluid to back up in your lungs. In addition, given that you have a history of aspirating on your food, it is important that you eat only ground up solids and pureed forms of food with crushed tablets.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ woman with severe CAD and prior stents (s/p cath ___ with multi-vessel disease), DM2, HTN who presented with chest pressure at rest and a syncopal episode now with Cr to 1.3, Lasix held. #Angina: Ms. ___ presented to the hospital with angina. Given her extensive cardiac history, this was thought to be secondary to her known coronary artery disease. She was medically treated with Aspirin, Crestor 40mg, and increase in Carvedilol to 75mg BID, Imdur to 150mg BID, and Hydralazine 60mg TID. We also consulted Cardiac surgery, who recommended bypass surgery, to be scheduled by her. She was initially started on Ranolazine to decrease her angina symptoms; however, a routine pre-op check up revealed slightly elevated LFTs, but she did not have any nausea, vomiting, or RUQ tenderness to palpation. At discharge, her physical exam was benign. She was instructed to schedule a right upper quadrant ultra sound for ___. # Syncope: Ms. ___ presented to the hospital after a presyncopal/syncopal event at church. She has some nausea associated with the event, but no diaphoresis. This was thought to be arrhythmia vs. vasovagal. # Chronic Heart Failure (EF 35%): She appeared fairly euvolemic, with minimal volume overload with physical exam notable for subtle lower extremity swelling. She was also on Quinapril. # Hypertension: Mrs. ___ was hypertensive on admission, but during the length of stay, her blood pressures remained between 100s-140s. She was discharged on Quinapril 80mg, Imdur 150mg BID, Hydralazine 60mg Q8H and Amlodipine 10mg. # Pre-Syncope: She had an episode of pre-syncope while standing up in church the evening prior to presentation. Did not appear to have a prodrome preceding the event, which made it concerning for possible arrhythmia. No seizure-like activity and no post-event weakness/focal neurological deficits. A V/Q scan was completed to ensure that there was no PE. Given her angina and ischemia, it was thought that she experienced an ischemic arrhythmia. She was seen by Electrophysiology, who recommended revascularization and emphasize that there was no role for ICD at this point. She did not have any syncopal episodes in the hospital. # Chronic systolic CHF: She was slightly volume overloaded while she was in the hospital, with slight elevation in JVP to 11. She was discharged on Lasix 40mg daily, as well as Coreg and Quinapril. # ___: During the hospitalization, she developed pre-renal ___, likely because of over-diuresis. At discharge, her Cr was 1.3, from her baseline of 1.0. Transitional Issues: ==================== # BMP to check renal function to be followed up on ___ with ___ at Cardiology Clinic. # Please consider LFTs on ___ to ensure that her LFTs are downtrending. # Patient was ordered for outpatient liver US to work-up her new transaminitis. Please ensure patient gets this study. # Medication changes: Coreg at 75mg BID. Imdur at 150mg BID. Hydralazine at 60mg TID. # Cardiac surgery saw patient and recommended CABG. This will be scheduled by the patient. The patient understands her risk of not having the CABG done while she is in-house. # CODE: FULL # CONTACT: ___ (daughter) Phone number: ___ ___ on Admission: The Preadmission Medication list is accurate and complete. 1. amLODIPine 10 mg PO DAILY 2. Carvedilol 50 mg PO BID 3. Ezetimibe 10 mg PO DAILY 4. Furosemide 40 mg PO DAILY 5. glyBURIDE-metformin 2.5-500 mg oral BID 6. HydrALAZINE 40 mg PO Q8H 7. Isosorbide Mononitrate (Extended Release) 120 mg PO BID 8. Quinapril 80 mg PO DAILY 9. Rosuvastatin Calcium 40 mg PO QPM 10. Zolpidem Tartrate 5 mg PO QHS:PRN insomnia 11. Aspirin 81 mg PO DAILY 12. Vitamin D ___ UNIT PO DAILY 13. Ranitidine 300 mg PO DAILY
summary: You came in after having chest pressure and an episode where you almost passed out. We believe that this episode may have been related to the known blockages in your heart. We have made some changes to your home medication to prevent your symptoms of angina. However, we believe that definitive treatment would be surgery. You spoke with Dr. ___ cardiologist, and agreed to discuss the surgery and your options. We asked the cardiac surgery team to speak with you about a coronary bypass surgery. They recommended Coronary Artery Bypass surgery to be scheduled by you. You were also started on Ranolazine, but because your liver tests were elevated in the hospital, we discontinued the medication at discharge. We recommended that you get an ultra sound test to further evaluate the your liver. The test has been ordered, but you will need to schedule it by calling ___. You also had a mild worsening of your kidney function and we recommend that you go to ___ on ___ to get blood drawn to check it. We have provided you with a copy of the order for blood test. You should continue on your regular Lasix dose unless instructed by Dr. ___ someone from his office.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Mr. ___ is a ___ yrs. old male with a h/o htn, AI, MR, and mild cardiomyopathy (EF 40-45%) who presented to outside clinic with dyspnea on exertion. . Active Issues: ================================= # Acute on chronic diastolic CHF: Patient has cardiomyopathy with EF (40-45%). The differential diagnosis for his acute decompensated heart failure include atrial fibrillation, dietary indiscretion, medication noncompliace, infections, cardiac ischemia. As per outpatient cardiologist, the atrial fibrillation is new and is most likely culprit for his decompensation. EKG did not show ischemic changes, troponin 0.01 making ischemia unlikely. Cath on ___ revealed clean coronaries with elevated wedge pressure. ___ Echo revealed Mild-Moderate regional and global left ventricular systolic dysfunction c/w CAD. Moderate functional mitral regurgitation. Moderate-severe tricuspid regurgitation with at least mild-moderate pulmonary artery systolic hypertension. He was placed on lasix IV and diuresed approximately 10L. Admission weight was 74.2 kg discharge weight 62.5 kg. He was transitioned to po furosemide. . # Atrial Fibrillation: Currently is being rate controlled with atenolol, which is a home medication. CHADS2 score 3 merits anticoagulation. He was started on heparin while be bridged to coumadin. Patient had elevated TSH with normal T4 suggesting subclinical hypothyroidism. During admission, he was having multiple 2 sec pauses when his HR was in the high ___. His atenolol was decreased no more pauses were noted on telemetry. . Inactive Issues ================================= # HTN: Patient was normotensive. During diuresis, patient was slightly hypotensive with to SBPs ___ and amlodipine was discontinued and lisinopril was decreased while he was being actively diuresed. . Transitional Issues ================================== -Subclinical hypothyroidism - patient had elevated TSH with normal T4.
summary: You came into the hospital because you had gained a lot of weight and were having some difficulty breathing. While here we had you on a diuretic called furosemide (aka lasix) to help you urinate all of the water weight that you were retaining. We also saw that you had a rapid irregular heart beat called atrial fibrillation (aka a.fib). This condition puts you at risk for stroke. To help reduce that risk we started you on a blood thinning medication called coumadin. While on coumadin, you will be required to get your blood checked to see how thin your blood is. During your admission, you had a cardiac catheterization that did not show any blockages in your coronary arteries. PLEASE weigh yourself daily. If your weight increases by more than 3lbs please contact your MD.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Patient Summary Statement for Admission: ========================================= ___ is a ___ year-old male with a history of cocaine abuse, HTN, ESRD on HD TTS, HFrEF (EF43%), and asthma who presented after being jumped and missing scheduled outpatient HD. Patient underwent extensive imaging including CT C Spine, CT Head and CT sinuses. The CT Head was notable for a small focus of subarachnoid hemorrhage seen inferior to the right tentorium. Patient was evaluated by Neurosurgery who recommended CTA head for further characterization which demonstrated patent vasculature with no evidence of aneurysm, and neurologic exam remained normal. Patient also underwent dialysis ___ based on his usual TuThSat schedule. Unfortunately, he got dialysis prior to planned discharge on ___ and was unable to initiate outpatient HD at ___ in ___. He was asked to stay in-house until his ___ HD session and will establish outpatient HD at ___ Dialysis ___ in ___ on ___. ACUTE Issues Addressed: ======================= # Head Injuy # Subarachnoid Hemorrhage: Patient presented following a kick to the head. He had CT C-Spine and CT Sinus completed in the emergency department which did not demonstrate acute abnormality. CT head was notable for a small focus of subarachnoid hemorrhage seen inferior to the right tentorium. Neurosurgery evaluated the patient and recommended a CTA to further characterize this finding. CTA was limited due to contrast distribution, however the study revealed patent vasculature and no evidence of large aneurysm. Patient remained hemodynamically stable during admission with no neurologic deficits on exam. He is asked to follow up in concussion clinic. # ESRD on HD ___ # Hyperkalemia Mr ___ underwent dialysis ___ in the emergency department based on his usual dialysis schedule. He was followed by the Renal team while inpatient and maintained on a low potassium diet. Patient's potassium was stably elevated during admission. Patient continued Sevelemer and Nephrocaps. Following discharge, patient will resume dialysis at ___ in ___ on ___ at 3:30 pm. CHRONIC Issues Addressed: ========================= # Chronic systolic heart failure: # HTN Patient's last known ejection fraction was 43% as of ___ suspected to be cocaine cardiomyopathy. Patient continued Torsemide 200mg BID for volume management (although he is almost anuric), Carvedilol, and Imdur/Hydral for afterload reducation. # Anemia: Patient's hemoglobin was 7.8 on admission and remained stable during admission. Patient continued EPO and Venofer at dialysis per nephrology. # Mood Disorder # Substance Abuse: Patient continued Sertraline prescribed during previous admission. He had previously expressed interest in treatment for cocaine abuse and was provided information regarding treatment programs at discharge. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: ==================== []Medications with change in doses: -Hydralazine 150mg BID -Imdur 60mg []Medications Stopped: -none []Dialysis follow up scheduled for ___ at 3:30pm at ___ Dialysis ___ Phone: ___ Nephrologist: TBA []Continue low potassium, low phosphate diet. []No PCP at present, thus PCP appointment was set up at ___ for ___ at 2:30 pm []Concern for OSA, patient should be referred for a sleep study after establishing with a PCP []During the hospitalization, patient expressed interest in getting set up with resources outpatient to help with his cocaine use. Intake appointment at AD Care IOP can be made at: AD Care IOP ___, ___ M-F, 4:30 - 8 p.m. []Patient with hematuria in previous admission. Given smoking history, follow up with ___ is recommended. Appointment can be made at ___ []Patient will follow up with ___ clinic on ___ at 10:00 AM Code Status: Full Code HCP: ___ (brother) ___
summary: You were admitted to the hospital because you were hit in the head We took an image of your head that showed a small amount of blood. Because of the unusual location of the bleed, we wanted to make sure that you do not have any underlying abnormal vessels in your brain. We took a second image of your head which did not show any other concerning findings. Our Neurosurgeons evaluated you and did not think you needed surgery. You got dialysis while in the hospital. We monitored your blood pressure and blood counts which were stable You should try to eat a low potassium and low phosphate diet. You should try to drink less than 1.5L of water a day. Please attend your dialysis sessions three times per week (___). Your next session is ___. 3:30 pm @ ___ Phone: ___ Nephrologist: TBA.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Patient is a ___ with history of HFpEF, atrial fibrillation (chads2 9) on warfarin, prior CVA, bradyarrhythmia s/p pacemaker placement, PVD, mild cognitive impairment, known uterine mass, and HTN/HLD/DM2 who presents with shortness of breath, concerning for acute on chronic diastolic heart failure exacerbation likely due to hypertensive urgency and dietary indiscretion. Her antihypertensive regimen was modified and she was given an increased dose of furosemide; her blood pressures improved but were still elevated at 160s, so spironolactone was added prior to discharge; she returned to and was stable at her dry weight on an increased dose of furosemide x 3 days prior to dsicharge. ACTIVE ISSUES ADDRESSED # Acute on chronic diastolic heart failure exacerbation # Hypoxemia - Patient presented with new O2 requirement and orthopnea associated with abdominal bloating. Fluid overload suspected given: weight up 15 pounds from d/c in ___, pulmonary edema and effusions on CXR, BNP elevated >1000. Most likely precipitant for HFpEF exacerbation is uncontrolled HTN (SBPs 180-200s recently) likely due to increased salt intake (pt was not missing medicine). Ischemia r/o with trop NEG x2 and neg ECG, TTE unchanged compared to prior. Patient's SOB resolved s/p diuresis and blood pressure control. Discharged on 80mg PO furosemide daily, up from 40mg daily. # Hypertension - SBPs >180 on admission, most likely contributing to pulmonary edema/HFpEF exacerbation. Replaced home metoprolol with carvedilol, which was uptitrated to max dose. Amlodipine reschedule for nighttime given early-morning hypertension. Lisinopril and isosorbide continued. Spironolactone 12.5mg added prior to discharge. # UTI - Symptomatic with positive UA. Cultures grew E.Coli; given ceftriaxone for 3 day course (___). Final culture was sensitive to CTX. # L eye conjunctivitis: pt developed L eye conjunctivitis. Started on Erythromycin ointment QID with good response. Last dose ___. # Normocytic anemia - Hb 9.7 from recent baseline of ___. Suspect hemodilution iso CHF exacerbation, supported by Hb is improving w/ increased diuresis. Anemia labs show normal TIBC, ferritin, transferrin, low ___ Fe, and high 309 LD. Consider outpt supplementation of low Fe stores. CHRONIC ISSUES ============== # DM2 - HbA1C 9 % (___): Decreased ___ lantus from 4U to 2U in the setting of low AM glucose. Continued home lantus 36U qAM. ISS. # Atrial fibrillation (chads2 9): persistent. Rates well controlled. Continued home warfarin. # Dyslipidemia: home Pravastatin # Peripheral vascular disease: home ASA # Mild cognitive impairment: pt oriented and answers questions appropriately. Continued home memantine, risperidone 0.125 mg PO QHS for sleep. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES New medication: - Erythromycin ointment QID for conjunctivitis to L eye for 5 days (last dose ___. - Carvedilol 25mg BID - Spironolactone 12.5mg daily Changes to medications: - ___ Lantus decreased to 2U (from 4U QHS) - Furosemide increased to 80mg PO daily (from 40mg) Discontinued meds: - Metoprolol 200 mg PO QD Follow-ups: [] Continue to monitor blood pressure and titrate medications as needed. [] The patient should have a chem panel (for K, Cr, Mg) within 1 week given furosemide increase and spironolactone. [] She should have an INR check as per her outpatient ___ clinic schedule. [] Follow up left eye conjunctivitis; redness/discharge had resolved within a day or so of starting erythromycin ointment. [] Consider outpatient iron supplement and standing bowel regimen for iron-deficiency anemia. [] Pt has abdominal fullness in the setting of reported prior uterine mass in Atrius records. Consider additional workup as indicated. Discharge (dry) weight: 72.9kg (160.71 lb). Discharge creatinine: 0.8
summary: You were admitted to the hospital for shortness of breath. You appears to have too much fluid in your body, likely due to high blood pressures and too much salt. We gave you medications to help you pass out the extra fluid, and now you are at your dry weight of 160 pounds. We changed your blood pressure medicine to try and get it under better control. We did a test to look at your heart function, which found that your heart function has not changed. This is good news!. You were found to have an infection in your bladder and were given an IV antibiotic to treat it. You were found to have an infection of your left eye, which improved with a antibiotic medicine applied to the eye.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Mr. ___ is a ___ year old male with history of CAD ___ MI with ___ 3 to RCA in ___ at ___ and CABG x 1 (SVG->OM) with MR ___ MVR (30 mm Physio II annuloplasty ring) both in ___, HFrEF with EF 40-45% now recently worsened postoperatively to 20% ___ Biventricular ICD, Percent BIV pacing: 0%), atrial tachycardia ___ ablation and ICD placement ___. The patient presented with intermittent SOB and chest pressure on exertion. He underwent cardiac catheterization which showed no significant residual CAD with patent vein to OM and patent stents in RCA. Troponins were negative. No evidence of COPD or pneumonia. Cardiac monitoring with no substantial events. He appeared euvolemic. He was continued on aspirin, atorvastatin, clopidogril. ECHO showed stable cardiac function with LVEF ___, biventricular cavity dilation with severe global biventricular systolic dysfunction. Mild aortic regurgitation. Mild mitral regurgitation. Moderate tricuspid regurgitation. Moderate pulmonary hypertension. The underlying etiology of his systolic heart failure remains unclear with no clear cause revealed by workup to date. He was continued on metoprolol, spironolactone, torsemide, and lisinopril. TRANSITION ISSUES: - He will need continued followup for management of heart failure with reduced ejection fraction.
summary: You were admitted because of increasing shortness of breath and chest pressure while walking. During your stay you underwent imaging of the heart which showed that your pumping function of the heart was still low but stable from prior. Also during this admission you underwent a coronary catheterization which looks at your heart blood vessel. The procedure found that you have some narrowing in your coronary arteries and bypass vessel. However, none of them were blocked enough to cause your symptoms. Therefore the cause of you presenting symptoms is unclear and less likely to be originating from the heart.
