text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ with complex medical hx notable for morbid obesity, dCHF, recent admission for temporal arteritis (completed steroid taper 3 weeks ago, did not take any proph abx), DM, HTN, HLD, OSA who presented to outpatient cardiology clinic today with dyspnea on exertion and profound orthopnea. # Dyspnea/___ Exacerbation - patient with hx of dCHF and volume overload. Trigger likely combination of obesity, HTN, diabetes, recent prednisone. Patient diuresed with lasix gtt (15mg/hr) with occasional metolazone dosing. Monitored daily weights, electrolytes. Continued home carvedilol 25mg BID. Initated patient on Hydralazine and Isordil for afterload reduction in setting of acute CHF. Patient diuresed and transitioned to PO diuresis prior to discharge. Volume status was somewhat difficult to assess given her obesity, and she underwent right eheart cath which showed elevated right sided filling pressures and pulmonary hypertension. She was evaluated by pulmonology who felt that her current presentation was more likely due to pulmonary hypertension and OSA and recommended pm bipap. This was started in house with dramatic improvement in subjectieve dyspnea. She was discharged on PO diuresis and Bipap. Weight on Discharge: 100.7kg. Diuretic Regimen: Lasic 120mg PO daily. ___ consider addition of ACE-I if renal function improves given DM. # ___ - Cr 2.4 on admission with baseline ___ in ___. Continues to slowly trend up. DDx cardiorenal vs worsening renal function given severe DM. Also concern that potentially reaching limit with diuresis, though Na stable, K not trending up. Bicarb not interpretable as patient with likely chronic respiratory acidosis with compensation and bicarb 35 on admission. Trended creatinine this admission and was 3.2 on discharge. Cr varied with diuresis (see discussion above). She seemed to level off at a value that was above eher baseline, possibly representing establishment of a new baseline. Nephrotoxix medications were avoided. She was discharged with close laboratory and renal followup. # Anemia: component of anemia of chronic disease and decreased Epo from CKD. She is on chronic epo injections as outpatient and was do for one. Given her dyspnea and her downtrending hgb this admission (slowly downtrnding) she was transfused one unit PRBCs with approproate bump. She also felt subjective improvement in SOB after transfusion. She should see her hematologist as she is due for Epo. CHRONIC ISSUES: # DM - patient with massive insulin requirement. Currently on 60U glargine with high dose sliding scale. ___ be candidate for U500 dosing (per ___ better to make transitional issue). ___ followed patient this admission and adjusted regimen PRN. # Insomnia - continued home ___ Xanex, though benzos potentially contraindicated in this elderly pateint # Depression - continued home Sertraline 25mg ___ # HLD - Continued home rosuvasatin 40mg qHS, Ezetimibe 10mg ___, ___ 81mg # Neuropathy - continued gabapentin 100mg TID, 5mg oxycodone qHS and PRN for leg pain (home med) # OSA - CPAP ordered, encourage use # GERD - cont omeprazole 20mg ___ # Def - continued home calcium carbonate, vitamin D TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: ***Transitional Issues:*** - Weight on Discharge: 100.7kg - Diuretic Regimen: Lasic 120mg PO daily - Consider addition of ACE-I if renal function improves given DM - consider U500 insulin with outpatient endocrinlogist given large insulin requirement - hypersensitivity panel was negative - Patient will need close renal follow-up - Patient will need formal sleep study for final OSA workup
summary: You were admitted for shortness of breath when you lie down and increased lower extremity edema. We initially felt this was likely due to fluid overload from heart failure. We gave you IV medications to make you urinate off the fluid and then switched back to oral medications, yet you were still short of breath. You also had a procedure called a right heart catheterization while you were here, which showed that you had some mild pulmonary hypertension. Our lung doctors also saw ___ while you were here and were concerned that you may have obesity hypoventilation syndrome, and recommended a Bipap machine to help you breathe better at night.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ M with PMH of CAD s/p MI x2 with stent placement in ___ and ___ (LAD), ischemic cardiomyopathy s/p ___ ICD EF 20%, IDDM, HTN and morbid obesity presenting with CHF exacerbation. Active Issues: # Acute on Chronic Systolic ___ Failure: Patient with history of sCHF with EF 20%, admitted due to an exacerbation of ___ failure. He was diuresed with lasix IV boluses (120mg IV) and drip (20cc/hr) successfully, returning to his dry weight of 118.4. The course was complicated by an uptrend in creatinine that required suspension of diuretics for a day. He was later transitioned back to oral diuretics, torsemide 40mg. He was continued on home metoprolol, valsartan, and spironolactone. # Urinary Tract Infection: Patient found to have a positive urinalysis, hematuria, and suprapubic pain. Patient was treated with ceftriaxone for 7 days. # Right lung apex lesion: Chest CT was performed for further evalution of dyspnea. It showed a Stellate subpleural density at the right lung apex likely corresponding to an infectious or inflammatory process. Repeat chest CT is recommended in 3 months. Pulmonology was consulted for furtehr evaluation and did not have any recommendations but repeating chest CT. # Thrombocytosis: Pt had uptreding plt count. On discharge, it was 691. Exact etiology unclear but most likely reactive. Inflammatory markers including ESR and CRP were checked and they were elevated to 130 and 113. We recommend further evaluation as outpatient. Chronic Issues: # CAD s/p MI x2 and PCTA x2 with stent placement in ___, LAD stent in ___, with 2 recent BMS in ___, and recent admission to CCU ___ for STEMI s/p 2 DES to RCA. Patient was continued on home prasugrel, aspirin, atorvastatin, metoprolol, and valsartan. # Atrial fibrillation: Patient was receiving 1.5 mg of warfarin, however he showed a downtrend of INRs throughout hospital course. He will be discharged with a higher dose of warfarin (2mg) Continued on home amiodarone and metoprolol. # HTN: - Continue metoprolol in q6 equivalent (on 100mg XL BID at home) - Continue home valsartan but change dose to 40mg BID # Leukocytosis: Appears to be chronic. Was normalized after treatment of UTI. - Continue to monitor # IDDM: - Fingersticks qid - ISS - Continue home lantus # Hx of chronic bilateral first toe ulcers: Followed by podiatry outpatient. - Continue to monitor - Continue strict glucose control - Increase lantus to 58 Units Breakfast 54 Units Bedtime # Hyperlipidemia: - Continue atorvastatin 80mg daily # GERD: - Continue home Protonix. # Gout: - Continue Allopurinol ___ PO daily. # Sleep apnea: - Continue home CPAP. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: -monitor electrolytes and kidney function closely (Cr on discharge 1.9) and adjust medications as needed -Home torsemide has been decreased from 60 mg to 40mg because of rising reatinine. Please adjust diuretics dose as needed. -Home warfarin has been increased from 1.5mg to 2 mg given INR on discharge 1.8. Pt to check INR on ___. -Home glargine has been increased: New dose: 58 Units Breakfast 54 Units Bedtime. Please recheck if doses are adequate. -repeat chest CT for stellate subpleural density in right lung apex in 3 months (should be repeated in ___. -recommend outpatient workup for thrombocytosis -recommend outpatient workup for elevated inflammatory markers (ESR 130 and CPR 111).
summary: You were admitted to the hospital for shortness of breath. Your symptoms were due to an exacerbation of your ___ failure. You received IV diuretics to diminish fluid retention and clear your lungs. Later this was transitioned back to oral diuretic medication. At admission we also detected a urinary tract infection. This was treated with antibiotics. We have changed the dose of three of your medications. You will now take a lower dose of torsemide (40mg PO daily), and a higher dose of coumadin (2 mg daily). You will increase the units of insulin you receive to 58 units in the morning and 54 at night.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ G1P1 POD# ___ s/p primary LTCS at 36w4d for PPROM/chorio who p/w chest tightness and SOB . 1. Respiratory distress: Given the evidence of pulmonary edema seen on CXR, this is likely CHF, possibly ___ post-partum cardiomyopathy. However, there was concern as well for PE given her post-op state as a risk factor. Significant effort was undertaken to get an IV and draw labs (see below) but the patient left AMA before this could be done. She received Lasix 20mg po qd with good urine output. She left AMA before a followup CXR could be done. She was advised to get a TTE as an outpatient. Given her anxiety, there may be a psychiatric component to this as well. 2. Chest pain: This was likely ___ CHF as above. ECG showed non-specific ST/T changes. Attempts were made to draw labs to rule out MI (see below) but she left AMA before this could be done. She was monitored on telemetry and had one five-beat run of NSVT but otherwise had no events. 3. s/p C-section: OB team followed her while in-house. They recommended labs including LFTs, CBC, uric acid, Cr, but these could not be drawn before she left AMA. Incisional pain control was achieved with Dilaudid. She received ativan and xanax on ___ in an attempt to place an IV (see below), so she was advised not to breast feed for 24 hours. # anxiety: The patient has significant needle phobia and refused multiple attempts at blood draws and IV placement. A significant amount of time was spent trying to convince her to allow IV placement. She received a total of 0.5mg Xanax and 3mg po Ativan ___ throughout the day, and by the end of the day she allowed 2 attempts at IV placement and lab draws, which both failed. She agreed to stay overnight to be monitored on telemetry, and the following morning after Dilaudid for pain control, she agreed to another IV placement attempt. However, she changed her mind again and demanded to leave AMA. She voiced understanding of the risks of leaving, including death, without ruling out conditions such as PE or MI. She was strongly advised to follow up with her PCP as soon as possible and to get an echocardiogram.
summary: You were admitted with symptoms of shortness of breath and chest pressure. This may be due to fluid overload, and you were given two doses of Lasix. Your anxiety with regards to needles required a significant amount of time and medications including benzodiazepines to enable you to allow us to attempt IV placement and blood draws. Despite this, two attempts were unsuccessful. Because of your refusal to allow further attempts at IV placement or phlebotomy, we have been unable to rule out life-threatening conditions such as a pulmonary embolus or heart attack. You have decided to leave against medical advice. You should not breast feed for 24 hours due to the medications (benzodiazepines) that you received yesterday.

text: Brief Hospital Course: # SHORTNESS OF BREATH: Patient with hx of ___ who reports increasing DOE and swelling of lower extremities. Elevated JVD and elevated BNP. XR with pulmonary edema, though improved from previous. Given i.v. lasix in ED. Most likely exacerbation of CHF in setting of dietary indiscretions. No chest pain and initial troponin neg and no ST or T wave changes on EKG. Trending trops neg X3. No signs of infection on CXR. Given degree of MR/TR in setting of CHF, worked to optimize BP meds to reduce afterload. Started on diuresis with i.v. Lasix at 20 MG BID. Initial diuresis was suboptimal, so lasix dose increased on HD2 to 30 MG BID and then up to 80 MG i.v. BID eventually. Switched to 80 MG PO BID lasix on ___. Improved sympomatically with diuresis. Weight upon admission was around 111 lbs. Was at 106.8 lbs by ___ upon discharge. Was discharged on 40 MG PO daily. . # MDS: Usually with anemia and reactive thrombocytosis. H/H above previous here. Elevated Plts. Patient developed Hct as low as 26.8 on ___ and was given a transfusion of 2 units pRBC. Patient was complaining of fatigue prior to transfusion but improved post-transfusion. Continued to monitor CBC during hospitalization. Hct was 27.6 upon discharge. To be followed by hematology for pro-crit and further management. # Moderate TR & MR, Mild AR: stable on exam, all likely contributing to CHF, treated as above, afterload reduce with amlodipine
summary: You were cared for in our hospital for shortness of breath. We believe your symptoms are consistent with an acute flare of your congestive heart failure, presumabably from dietary indiscretion. Eating foods with high salt content, such as ham and frozen foods, can often trigger a worsening of your heart failure. Your symptoms improved with diuresis using i.v. Lasix. You were also found to have worsening weakness since admission. Your blood tests every day showed decreasing hematocrit levels suggestive anemia. This is consistent with your previous history of Myleodysplastic Syndrome (MDS). We transfused you with blood to help with your lowering blood counts. You tolerated this well.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ hx of CHF, recurrent PE on coumadin (most recent PE ___, macrocytotic anemia, GI bleed, dementia who presented w/ exacerbation of chronic chest pain, abdominal discomfort and anemia. Active Diagnoses: ================== # Anemia: Anemia likely due to combination of anemia of chronic disease (?MDS), and intermittent diverticulosis. Could also have component of iron deficiency based on high RDW (no current iron studies and normocytic). Pt's history concerning for infection of GI source; however guiac neg stools reassuring for no frank GI bleed. CT neg for retroperitoneal or intraluminal bleed, inflammation. Pt responded well to 1 unit pRBC, with Hct rising appropriately to 26. Pt will need to be connected to outpatient hematologist as transfusion-dependent and may need workup for exact etiology. # UTI: Pt had positive UA, and given advanced age, treated this as complicated UTI (10 day antibiotic course). Given systemic symptoms of diarrhea, some concern for pyelonephritis. Treated with Ceftriaxone initially then transitioned to PO Bactrim on day 2 of admission. Will need follow-up of urine culture to ensure sensitive to bactrim after discharge. Coumadin dosing adjusted as below given interaction of bactrim with warfarin. # Chest pain, posterior neck pain: Troponins neg with EKG unchanged from prior, so likely not cardiac pain. This is likely exacerbation of costochondritis with perhaps some component of radiculopathy. On discharge, patient feels improved from admission, and can follow up with her PCP/orthopedics for repeat Marcaine and Kenalog injection if needed. ___ consider neck imaging and gabapentin for possible neuropathic pain as outpatient. # Leukopenia: Has history of mild leukopenia in the past now with WBC count of 2.6. Could be related to mild dilutional effect vs viral infection (with diarrhea) vs MDS. ___: # Recurrent PEs: Patient chronically on warfarin for recurrent PEs. Coumadin dosing decreased to alternating 1mg and 2mg every other day due to bactrim interaction. Will have repeat INR on ___. # Diastolic CHF: Mild pulmonary edema on CXR without active s/s of overload on exam. Torsemide held due to concern for GI bleed, can restart on discharge. Transitional Issues: ====================== - Pt is on PO Bactrim for total of 10 day course (___) - Please follow up Urine culture to ensure that the organism is susceptible to bactrim - Please check INR on ___ and ensure that pt is alternating her Coumadin dose from 1mg to 2mg until ___. - Pt will need to be connected with outpatient heme as she is transfusion-dependent with unspecified anemia - Pt has chronic costchondral joint pain in left anterior chest and posterior neck, for which she has had successful Kenalog and Marcaine injections with orthopedics before. This pain was not addressed in the hospital, and she needs to follow up with either PCP or orthopedics for intramuscular or intraarticular pain relief. - Would consider work-up for radicular pain from cervical spine given ongoing neck and anterior chest pain. Could consider gabapentin for radicular pain in future. - HCP is son, ___ ___ ___ on Admission: The Preadmission Medication list is accurate and complete. 1. Acetaminophen w/Codeine ___ TAB PO Q4H:PRN pain 2. Metoprolol Succinate XL 25 mg PO DAILY 3. Omeprazole 20 mg PO BID 4. Torsemide 40 mg PO DAILY 5. Warfarin 1 mg PO DAILY16 6. Vitamin D 1000 UNIT PO DAILY 7. Ferrous Sulfate 325 mg PO BID
summary: You were admitted with chest pain, abdominal pain and were discovered to be very anemic (have low blood counts) and to have a urinary tract infection. We gave you one unit of blood in the emergency department, and after that, you did not require any more transfusions. We believe that your abdominal pain may have been a reaction to your urinary tract infection, which we are treating with antibiotics. The antibiotics will need to be taken for 8 more days after discharge. Due to interaction between bactrim (antibiotic) and warfarin, please decrease your warfarin dosing to alternating between 1mg and 2mg daily as follows: Warfarin: Take 2mg ___ Take 1mg ___ Take 2mg ___ Take 1mg ___ Take 2mg ___ Take 1mg ___ and will have INR drawn We believe that your chest pain does not represent a heart attack, but rather is joint pain in your ribs and muscles possibly affected by your neck joints that is going to be best treated with your primary care doctor and orthopedic surgeon.

text: Brief Hospital Course: This is a ___ y/o female with history of DMII, CKD and hypertension who presents 1 week after a fall, found to have a urinary tract infection and acute on chronic renal failure, with course complicated by NSTEMI, medically managed. ACTIVE ISSUES: # Urinary Tract Infection: Patient was febrile in the ED with positive urinalysis. While she was given vancomycin, cefepime and azithromycin in the ED, there was no clear sign of pneumonia on imaging or exam. She was transitioned to CTX for UTI, and UCx ultimately grew e.coli sensitive to CTX. With this treatment, mental status and renal function improved. # Acute on Chronic Renal Insufficiency: Likely related to urinary tract infection and subsequent poor PO intake, improved with abx and gentle IVFs. Her lasix and lisinopril were initially held, though restarted prior to discharge. # NSTEMI: Pt developed chest pain on HD4 with subsequent troponin leak. Cardiology was consulted, and she was started on a heparin gtt. However, Hct dropped 10 points the following day, and heparin gtt was held. TTE was performed and showed EF 60% with no focal wall motion abnormalities. She was given 1 unit of pRBCs. Given crackles on exam and significantly elevated BNP, she was given IV lasix as well. She was ultimately transferred to the cardiology service. While on the cardiology service, heparin drip continued to be held. She remained chest pain free. Her medications were optimized including starting of atorvastatin and plavix. The family opted for medical management and deferred catheterization. She was chest pain free at time of discharge. # anemia: Pt was admitted with baseline hct at 34. She remained stable for many days, however in setting of starting heparin drip for ACS, pt developed 9 point hct drop, confirmed on repeat. Her heparin drip was discontinued and pt recieved 1 unit PRBC with return of her hct to 30. No source of bleeding was identified and pt was guaiac negative. Her hct remained stable for the remainder of her hospital course. # acute on chronic CHF exacerbation: On HD4, pt also developed worsening shortness of breath with associated crackles on exam. CXR with mild fluid overload. This was in the setting of her lasix having been held for acute on chronic renal failure. She was started back on her PO lasix regimen and also received extra IV doses with blood tranfusions. Pt appeared euvolemic at time of discharge. # Hip pain: Pt with recent fall; however, hip films were reported negative at nursing home. Repeat films here were also negative for fracture. Pain was managed with standing tylenol. CHRONIC ISSUES # Hypertension, benign: BP elevated here. Given ___, held lisinopril and lasix initially. Continued metoprolol and felodipine. Lasix and lisinopril were ultimately restarted as above. #Diabetes II, controlled, with complications (CKD) Will continue lantus but at lower dose given ___ and unclear PO intake, sliding scale #Hyperlipidemia: changed from simvastatin to atorvastatin. #Gout: colchicine was held during hospitalization due to poor renal function. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: Pt remained full code during this admission, though family had expressed a desire to avoid invasive procedures such as cardiac catheterization. This should continue to be addressed as an outpatient.
summary: You were admitted after your facility found that your kidneys were damaged and you were more fatigued. You were found to have a urinary tract infection, which was treated with improvement in your energy and kidney function. However, while at the hospital, you developed chest pain and we found that you were having a heart attack. We started you on a blood thinner briefly, but there was concern for bleeding so this was stopped. You received 1 blood transfusion and your blood counts remained stable for the rest of your hospitalization. Because of the heart attack, we made a few changes to your medications. Your facility will help make sure you get the correct medications.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Patient is a ___ year-old male with a history of CABG, Hypertension, Diabetes, and Hyperlipidemia who presented with abdominal pain and mild respiratory distress. 1.) Heart Failure: Patient with known systolic dysfunction consistent with coronary artery disease. EF 40-45%. Crackles heard on original exam and BNP elevated on admission. Negative ECG and negative cardiac enzymes suggested no acute coronary syndrome. Echo showed no worsening systolic dysfunction. Breathing and abdominal pain improved with diuresis. No crackles on discharge. Patient maintained on B-blocker throughout hospitalization. ACE-I held due to worsened renal function. Additionally, as Cr rose furosemide also held with stable volume status. These medications will be restarted by PCP who will see Mr. ___ before the end of the week at ___ ___. 2.) CAD: Pt has known CAD and hx of CABG. Pt. never had chest pain during hospitalization and cardiac enzymes negative with unchanged ECG. No suspicion of acute coronary syndrome precipitating CHF exacerbation. Patient maintained on B-blocker and ASA during hospitalization. Patient also monitored on telemetry throughout hospitalization. 3.)Abdominal Pain: Cause unclear but negative CT scan, negative renal U/S, and rule out of MI suggests no major serious etiology. Patient had bacteruria w/o pyuria or other signs of infection. Patient treated for UTI and received anti-cholinergic medications and bowel regimen. Pain resolved as of ___. 4.) Acute on chronic renal failure: Cr 2.6 at admission up from baseline Cr of ~1.5 so ACE-I held. Acute renal failure most likely pre-renal in setting of volume overload and poor pump function. Cr increased with diuresis and when it hit max of 3.0 furosemide was held. Renal US negative. Patient found to have bacteriuria w/o pyuria and received one dose of bactrim but no urine eos so AIN not likely. Possible UTI treated and diuretics held and over last few days of hospitalization Cr decreased back to 2.4 on day of discharge. Will continue to hold diuretics and ACE-I pending further labs and continued improvement. Spoke w/ Dr. ___ or his NP will follow up ordered ___ labs (prescription in D/C planning) and will restart medications when renal function has improved adequately. 5.)Hyperkalemia: Patient had hyperkalemia up to value of 6 on ___. No EKG changes or symptoms. Patient was treated with two doses of 30 mg Kayexalate PO and reduced to 5. Will be rechecked on ___. ___ House will draw blood and Dr. ___ will follow up results. 6.)Cough: Patient was afebrile throughout hospitalization and chest radiograph not suspicious for pneumonia. Patient treated with symptomatic remedies with some improvement. 7.) Anemia: Hematocrit was 25.0 with a baseline of ___. A prior workup in ___ including colonoscopy, EGD revealed gastritis and attributed decreased crit to chronic kidney disease. Hematocrit monitored throughout hospitalization with no significant increase in degree of anemia. 8.) Bacteruria: patient has history of klebsiella urinary tract infection and had pan-sensitive klebsiella on ED UA w/o pyuria. Possibly just colonization, but given abdominal symptoms treated for presumed UTI considered complicated in this male patient. Two days of treatment to be completed after discharge. 9.)History of urinary retention: Patient initially had foley and continued on flomax throughout hospitalization. No problems with urinary retention after foley removal. 10.)IDDM: Patient maintained on outpatient sliding scale and diabetic diet with well controlled blood glucose values. 11.) Prophylaxis: Patient maintained on SC heparin. 12.) Code status: Patient was full code throughout hospitalization.
summary: You were admitted for abdominal pain and difficulty breathing. While you were here, you were found to have an extra amount of fluid in your body. You were given the medication LASIX to help you get rid of this fluid. A chest x-ray did not show any infection. You were found to have a urinary tract infection for which you are being treated with ciprofloxacin. Please make sure to complete the full antibiotic course. Your kidney function declined in the hospital due to the vigorous diuresis so your lasix was eventually held. Your METOPROLOL was increased to better control your blood pressure. You were started on aspirin to help protect your heart. You were also started on Ciprofloxacin for a UTI. You will take two more days of that medication. We are currently holding your LISINOPRIL and your LASIX as your kidney function worsened in the hospital. Dr. ___ will give you instructions about when to restart these medications.

text: Brief Hospital Course: SUMMARY ======== Mrs. ___ is a ___ y/o F w/ PMH significant for pAF (on dabigitran and now off flecainide), HTN, HLD, obesity, CAD with 80% D1 lesion on ___ cath, presents with palpitations, dyspnea, and chest pain, found to have AF with RVR, evidence of volume overload, and an NSTEMI with peak troponin of 1.04. ACTIVE ISSUES ============== #Type 1 NSTEMI #CAD Per patient's history, likely a Type 1 NSTEMI leading to acute on chronic HFrEF exacerbation. Atrial fibrillation was likely an exacerbator but less likely the cause as patient stated she had been in atrial fibrillation all summer. Patient was given aspirin, heparin gtt, metoprolol tartrate, rosuvastatin, isordil. Patient underwent a left heart catheterization on ___, which showed 100% proximal LAD occlusion, which was new compared to her prior cath in ___. She then underwent a TTE on ___, which showed regional systolic dysfunction in LAD territory and an EF of 35%. It was decided to do a CTO PCI; she underwent this on ___ and received a DES to LAD and another DES to D1 with no complications. She was placed on ticagrelor and her home dabigatran, and her aspirin was stopped. She will continue the ticagrelor 90 mg BID and dabigatran (at a reduced dose of 110 mg BID) for a year, then she will switch to aspirin and dabigatran indefinitely. #Acute on chronic HFrEF (EF 35%) Etiology thought likely due to NSTEMI. She was given her home PO Lasix 40 mg daily. On ___, she appeared slightly volume overloaded, so she was given 40 mg IV Lasix in addition to her PO Lasix 40 mg. She put out very well to this, and she was discharged on: DIURESIS: Lasix 40 mg PO QD PRELOAD: Imdur 60 mg PO QD AFTERLOAD: None NHBK: Metoprolol succinate XL 200 mg QD #AF with RVR (rates ~130s) Patient with history of persistent AF that has been resistant to DCCV while on Flecainide. Given her CAD, she is limited in what AADs she can take. Initially, patient's HRs were 110s-120s with spikes to 130s-140s, and she did not feel well with these heart rates. She was continued on her home metoprolol dose fractionated. She was eventually loaded with digoxin and continued on digoxin 0.25 mg QD; her HRs then were ___ with very occasional spikes to 100s (still in a-fib). For anticoagulation, her dabigatran was held while on the heparin gtt, then it was resumed after her stents were placed. CHRONIC ISSUES: ================ #GERD Esomeprazole not on formulary - Switched to omeprazole 40 mg QD while in house #Depression - Continued fluoxetine 20 mg daily #Hypothyroidism - Continued levothyroxine 100 mcg daily TRANSITIONAL ISSUES =================== [ ]NSTEMI s/p ___ 2 - Please ensure patient continues on ticagrelor 90 mg BID and dabigatran 110 mg BID for ___ year; she will switch to aspirin 81 mg QD and dabigatran indefinitely after that. [ ]HFrEF - Please ensure patient's furosemide 40 mg QD is the appropriate dose for her. Weight on afternoon of discharge was 123.7kg. [ ]HFrEF - Patient's TTE here showed reduced EF; patient should be started on ___ (allergic to lisinopril) and spironolactone/eplerenone. [ ]A-fib - Patient started on digoxin here to control her rates; please titrate as needed. ================== CORE MEASURES: ================== # CODE STATUS: Full code # CONTACT ___ (husband) ___, ___ (Son) ___
summary: You were admitted because you were having chest pain, and tests showed us that you were having a heart attack. You were given medications to treat your heart attack. You underwent a procedure called a left heart catheterization, which looked at the arteries of your heart; it showed that you had some blockages in your heart arteries. You underwent a procedure in which two stents were placed in these blocked heart arteries to open them back up. You were given a new medication for your atrial fibrillation called digoxin, which will help control the rates better. You were given a new medication called ticagrelor, or Brillanta, to prevent blockage of your new stents. It was noticed that you had some extra fluid in your body; we gave you medicine to help get rid of that fluid.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ yo woman with history of afib, sick sinus syndrome s/p pacemaker placement, 3-vessel CAD, systolic CHF, and severe peripheral vascular disease s/p femoral and popliteal stenting with necrotic right ___ toe admitted for AFib with RVR and CHF exacerbation, found to have rethrombosis of femoral artery stent. # Severe PVD with necrotic right toe ulcer: Patient has a nonhealing gangrenous ulcer ___ her right foot, which started over one year ago, with chronic polymicrobial osteomyelitis (growing MRSA and Cornyebacterium spp.) confirmed on foot x-ray, and for which she has been treated with Vanco/Moxi via PICC as outpatient. The cause of this ulcer is severe peripheral vascular disease, for which she underwent popliteal artery catheterization with placement of 3 stents on last hospitalization (discharged on Plavix but not coumadin). She underwent repeat arterial study during this hospitalization which showed significant decrase ___ pulse amplitudes at right popliteal and posterior tibial arteries. She therefore went for repeat femoral artery catheterization, where she was found to have thrombosis of the distal R SFA-popliteal stent. She underwent successful thrombectomy and PTCA of the thrombosed stent, and successful PTCA and stenting of the R ___ with a DES. Repeat arterial studies showed patent R SFA to popliteal artery stent and improved PVRs at level of ankles and metatarsals; pulses were dopplerable all the way to distal extremity. However, right ___ toe was found to be chronically necrotic and nonhealing, ultimately requiring amputation under local anesthesia. Antibiotics during hospitalization were IV Vanco/Flagyl and PO Cipro (no Moxi available ___ formulary). Also, because stent had rethrombosed, patient was on Heparin drip throughout hospitalization and was started on Coumadin afterward ___ order to prevent rethrombosis of her stents. Discharged on Vancomycin and Moxifloxacin, which will continue until DC date deterimed on followup with infectious diseases on ___. # AFib: Patient was ___ AFib with RVR on admission. This was rapidly corrected via uptitration of her Metoprolol succinate from 200 to 300mg daily and addition of Diltiazem ER 240mg. Her rates were well-controlled ___ the 60-70s throughout hospitalization once med changes had been made. She was initially on heparin drip prior to toe amputation and was placed on coumadin post surgery. Initially on 2mg daily, INR 1.9, so increased to 3mg daily. Please follow INR next on ___. # Acute on Chronic Systolic Heart Failure: Patient's weight on last discharge was 56.9 kilograms. Her weight on admission was 54.3 kg but she had lost weight secondary to anorexia caused by her outpatient antibiotics and she appeared clinically volume overloaded with elevated BNP. She was diuresed with IV Lasix, then transitioned back to home dose PO Lasix 20mg daily. CHRONIC ISSUES: # CAD: patient has severe 3 vessel CAD confirmed on cath during last hospitalization. Medical management with Aspirin and Plavix. # DM: diabetes treated with ISS during hospitalization. Resume home regimen on discharge. TRANSITION OF CARE: - Antibiotics: patient has appointment with ID specialist ___ ___ on ___. Unless directed otherwise, DO NOT discontinue PICC line until then as she will need to continue with Vanco/Moxi. - Need to monitor INR as newly started on Coumadin. Goal ___. ___ INR=1.9, increased from 2mg daily to 3mg daily on ___. ___ need to increase once off antibiotics.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital for fluid overload caused by your heart failure and atrial fibrillation (rapid heartbeat). You were treated with diuretics to take off the extra fluid. We also gave you medications to slow your heart rate. Additionally, it was found that the stents ___ the arteries ___ your right leg had become blocked (rethrombosed) after your last surgery, causing your to have poor blood flow to your right foot. This was corrected via restenting of the artery and removal of blood clots that had been formed. However, your right fourth toe was chronically infected without potential to heal, so it had to be amputated.

text: Brief Hospital Course: In summary, this is a ___ old female with a history of hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, SVT, hypertension, COPD s/p multiple PEA arrests who is admitted with CHF exacerbation and work up for constrictive pericarditis versus restrictive cardiomyopathy. She is transferred to the CCU due to runs of atrial tachycardia to the 160s and associated hypotension. .. # RHYTHM: She has a history of multiple SVTs including atrial fibrillation, atrial tachycardia, MAT and AVNRT. She was transferred to the CCU after bursts of what appeared to be MAT. Due to her hemodynamic compromise in this rhythm, she was reloaded with amiodorone at a dose of 400 mg BID. She was then loaded with digoxin and had a subsequent decrease in her ventricular rate to ___ with ___ atrioventricular conduction. Her metoprolol was uptitrated as blood pressure would tolerate. . Plan for AV nodal ablation and permanent pacemaker placement was delayed by symptomatic candidal UTI being tx with 2 week course. However, at time of discharge her rate was well controlled on the combination of amiodorone, digoxin, and metoprolol. Pt will return for elective procedure in 1 to 2 weeks after repeat UA is clear. .. # PUMP: She has a history of hypertrophic cardiomyopathy with preserved EF and moderate symmetric LVH. Right heart catheterization at ___ prior to transfer showed equalization of pressures concerning for constrictive pericarditis. However, right and left heart catheterization at ___ showed prominent x and y descents and concordance of left and right ventricular pressures with respiration, all consistent with a restrictive cardiomyopathy. Endomyocardial biopsy was performed and pathology was consistent with hypertrophic cardiomyopathy. Cardiac MR was done with full report above. . On physical exam and chest x-ray, it was clear that she was volume-overloaded and her symptoms of shortness of breath, fatigue were consistent with her usual heart failure exacerbations. She was therefore started on a Lasix drip with goal diuresis 1 to 1.5 L per day. At time of discharge, her net diuresis is negative. Her oxygen requirements are 2L. . # CAD: There was no evidence of coronary disease on cardiac catheterization during this or previous hospitalizations. Blood pressure and heart rate control were achieved with metoprolol, amio, and digoxin for primary prevention. . # URINARY TRACT INFECTION: During the hospital course, she started complaining of burning with urination. A urine culture grew out yeast, 100,000 colonies, and because she was symptomatic we decided to treat with a 14 day course of fluconazole. A renal ultrasound was performed to look for perinephric abscess; this showed no hydronephrosis or perinephric abcess, it did show a small left pleural effusion. Her symptoms resolved on fluconazole and is to have a repeat Ua in ___ weeks. Patient can have ablation once UTI resolves. . # COPD: We continued her outpatient regimen of Xopenex, Montelukast, and ipratropium. . # DEPRESSION / ANXIETY: We continued her outpatient regimen of bupropion, clonazepam, sertraline. . She was maintained on a heparin drip due to her underlying atrial dysrrhythmia; this was switched to lovenox instead of coumadin at time of discharge because there was concern fo interactions with digoxin and amiodarone. She had a cardiac heart-healthy diet. There was no indication for GI prophylaxis.
summary: You had an episode of congestive heart failure with high pressures within your heart at ___. We increased the amount of fluid medicine (spironolactone) to take with the Furosemide. We also started you on Digoxin and increased your Metoprolol to control your heart rate. You have a urinary tract infection and we started you on fluconazole for a total of 14 days. You will need an AV node ablation and pacemaker after your urinary tract infection is cleared. Stop taking your warfarin for now, you will give yourself Lovenox twice daily until the nurse who arranges the pacemaker tells you to stop taking it. We started Lisinopril to control your blood pressure better and help your heart pump. We also started Spiriva to help with your breathing.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Hypertensive Urgency: Patient presented with SBP's> 180 in the ED with associated shortness of breath and chest heaviness. Has longstanding history of unmedicated hypertension per faimly, most likely essential hypertension. Required nitroglycerin drip in the emergency department to bring blood pressure down to 160's with resolution of symptoms. Evidence of longstanding hypertension on Chest XRay with severe cardiomegaly. The patient was started on captopril in house and transitioned to 40 mg PO lisinopril daily, as well as metoprolol succinate 25 mg qday. Blood pressures were lowered to the 150-160 mm Hg region for SBP's. Patient tolerated medications well. Urine protein/creatinine to check for other end organ damage of longstanding hypertension was negative. Goal BP 150-160mmHg as patient has lived at very high blood pressures for most of life. Also placed on 10 mg PO lasix daily for CHF symtpoms (see below) *Follow up CMP, Calcium, Magnesium, Phosphate *Blood pressure check *Fundoscopic exam/opthamology exam *Assess compliance with low salt diet CORONARIES: No prior laboratory data. Risks for CAD include longstanding hypertension, age. Serum lipids were normal, HbA1c pending, and urine glucose/ fastiing glucose within normal limits. Given chest heaviness symptoms, patient's in-house cardiologist Dr. ___ persantine ___ to evaluate coronary vasculature and assess necessity for intervention or possible statin. Scheduled in OMR, and patient's HCP supplied with number to schedule persantine ___ within the next month. *f/u if ___ done, contact lab at ___. *continued on aspirin 81 mg daily PUMP: No previous echo in records. Clinically appeared to be in mild fluid overload with elevated JVP and crackles. Had dyspnea on exertion. Gave 10 mg IV lasix once in house with good output. Discharged on 10 mg PO lasix daily for symptom management. As longstanding hypertension risk for developing diastolic and systolic heart failure, will need outpatient TTE to assess function. Patient's HCP supplied with number to schedule transthoracic echo within next few weeks. *f/u TTE results, contact lab at ___ *Re-evaluate heart failure medical regimen pending echo results and symptomatology. Normocytic Anemia: No baseline hematocrit. Anemia labs showed mild iron deficiency, but no evidence of hemolysis or B12/folate deficiency. Patient deferred guiac exam. No colonoscopy to date. Anemia is most likely combination of bone marrow suppression with advanced age, as well as possible ___ GIB. As patient endorses chronic pain medication use, including NSAIDs daily, should follow up CBC to assess for worsening anemia. Counseled on avoiding NSAIDs, and given PO iron for supplementation. *follow up CBC *make sure not using NSAIDs (besides baby asprin) *TSH/ SPEP/UPEP pending at time of discharge for complete workup of anemia. *Discuss colonoscopy with patient Chronic Pain: Has chronic osteoarthritis. Liver tests were within normal limits. Counseled on avoiding NSAIDs and suggested Tylenol ___ mg TID as needed, and counseled not to exceed recommended doses. Insomnia/Agitation: Was given Trazodone 25 mg qhs once in house with no adverse effects. General Care: Needs arrangement with a PCP for BP management and investigation of possible colonoscopy, as well as other screening age appropriate screening as not seen an MD in ___. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: Per above. Needs follow up of age appropriate screening. Will also need discussion of code status, as daughter seemed confused about the concept, but appeared she and her mom would prefer a DNR/DNI status. Dedicated outpatient discussion would be appropriate. PENDING LABS: TSH,SPEP,UPEP,HbA1c, outpatient TTE, ___
summary: You were admitted to the hospital with shortness of breath and chest heaviness. You were found to have very high blood pressure (known as "Hypertension"). You were started on several medications to help control your hypertension. In addition, it is important to reduce the amount of salt in your diet. Please limit your sodium intake to less than 2 grams per day. This can be determined by looking at the Nutrition Facts on the foods you eat, and seeing how much sodium is listed for each serving. Your total sodium intake per day should not exceed 2 grams. Additionally, you clinically appeared to be in a condition known as "Heart Failure". The best way to assess for heart failure is with a procedure knwon as an echocardiography. Unfortunately, because you were admitted over the weekend, and echocardiography was not available until the following week. You and your health care proxy decided you did not want to stay for this test, and would rather have it performed as an outpatient.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ male with advanced bladder cancer on home hospice, HTN, HLD, aflutter on coumadin, and complete heart block s/p pacemaker, and CHF ___ amyloid cardiomyopathy, who presented to the ED due to painful urination, frequency, and incontinence. The discomfort is felt to be due at least in part to the infiltration of his tumor into the penis and urethra. #DYSURIA The case was given extensive consideration by the urology and ___ services. Foley is not feasible given his penile tumor burden, and suprapubic catheter felt to be too risky because of extensive bladder tumor that would lead to bleeding and then likely clotting of the catheter. ___ feels that given the unimpressive evidence for obstruction, a nephrostomy tube would not divert enough urine to truly relieve his symptoms, and that the risks would therefore likely outweigh any potential benefit. Therefore the focus was shifted to medical management of the patient's symptoms. He was started on standing pyridium and Tylenol TID and given PRN low-dose morphine pills. Long-term potential toxicities of phenazopyridine were discussed with the HCP and she is aware this long-term use is an off-label compassionate use given her father's ___ goals of care. He was also started on Flomax for obstructive voiding symptoms, although it is unclear whether this will help or not. #CHRONIC DIASTOLIC/SYSTOLIC HEART FAILURE ___ The patient has cardiac amyloidosis; LVEF is ~45%. He appeared clinically dry and had ___ on arrival; ___ improved with holding torsemide for two doses. The patient was formerly on torsemide 20 mg daily, but his daughter tells me his PO intake has dropped off markedly. The torsemide dose was halved to 10 mg daily. His lisinopril was also held for ___ and ___ BP. He needs a volume exam and a Creatinine check within a week. #A-FLUTTER Of note the patient initially received vitamin K in anticipation of a procedure, so his INR will be expected to take several days to become therapeutic again on his home coumadin dose. His lisinopril was also held since his systolic blood pressures were mostly close to 100. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: ==================== - Follow CBC if pyridium is to be continued. - Follow up volume exam and Creatinine on his reduced torsemide dose. - restart lisinopril if tolerated (held for ___ and soft BP)
summary: You were admitted to the hospital because of pain with urination. We have prescribed a medication to help numb the bladder and urethra (PHENAZOPYRIDINE). This medication is usually only used for a few days (it can cause serious but reversible blood disorders with long-term use), but since the main goal of your care right now if make you as comfortable as possible, I suggest you just keep taking it regularly. This medicine will stain your urine an unusual bright orange color; this is a NORMAL side effect of this medicine. Please take one phenazopyridine (sold over the counter as AZO) and two acetaminophen tabs (extra strength Tylenol) three times daily. If you have pain despite these meds, take half a tab of MORPHINE. We also gave you TAMSULOSIN (Flomax), which may make it easier to urinate without any straining. You are likely to have worsening difficulty urinating as time goes on and may completely obstruct at some point.

text: Brief Hospital Course: PATIENT SUMMARY: ================ Mr. ___ is a ___ year old man with pAF c/b L frontal CVA (___), severe AS, CHB s/p PPM (___), new cardiomyopathy (EF 35%, suspect pacing-induced) presenting from cardiology clinic for hypotension and dizziness. Given IVF in the ED and hypotension and symptoms improved. ACUTE ISSUES: ============= #Lightheadedness: #Hypotension: The patient was found to have BP 87/58 in his cardiologist's office, associated with lightheadedness. This was thought to be multifactorial. He has severe AS (per below) and was started back on Lasix ___ weeks ago by his PCP for lower extremity swelling, and so in the setting of being pre-load dependent may have become symptomatic from AS with the reduced preload. In addition he has newly reduced EF, thought to most likely be pacing-induced cardiomyopathy (per below). He received 1.5L IVF in the ED, and all home antihypertensives were held. His hypotension improved with BP back into the 110-130s/50-80s. His lightheadedness improved significantly. CT head was negative for any acute process. #Severe AS: On ___ ___ <1, peak gradient 55, mean gradient 33. He has not had chest pain, syncope, or dyspnea at home but often feels lightheaded. During his hospitalization, he was worked up for a TAVR and underwent cath on ___. TAVR team was contacted and they will contact patient as an outpatient to continue the evaluation. #HFrEF: #?Pacing-induced cardiomyopathy: Noted on ___ last admission (for AFib and CVA) to have EF 35% (from previously normal in ___. Given dual-chamber PPM was placed ___, pacing-induced CMP is highest on differential. EP was consulted for the placement of an LV lead, and they reported that it is reasonable to consider CRT-P (not CRT-D given advanced age and need for additional implantation of an RV ICD lead which might cause issue due to extra leads) if CAD/TAVR evaluation unrevealing, and they will reassess after CAD/TAVR testing complete. He had coronary angiography on ___ that revealed non-obstruction CAD. Later in admission appeared a little volume overloaded on exam with mildly elevated PCWP of 17 on RHC ___, however given pre-load dependent with severe AS held off on any diuresis or titrating of heart failure medications, which should be done as an outpatient. CHRONIC ISSUES: =============== #pAF: CHADS2VASC 5 (CHF, Age=2, Stroke=2). He was continued on apixaban 5 BID. #HLD: He was continued on home atorva 40 daily. #Gout: He was continued on home allopurinol. #Normocytic anemia: Long-standing, baseline Hb ___. On admission Hb 11.1. He was continued on home iron. #Hypothyroidism: He was continued on home levothyroxine. #GERD: He was continued on home omeprazole. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: ==================== DISCHARGE WEIGHT: 70.22kg (154.8 lbs) DISCHARGE Cr: 1.4 [] TAVR Team will contact patient as an outpatient to discuss further evaluation for TAVR in setting of severe AS. [] Patient will need to restart/titrate HF medications with cardiology as outpatient. He is currently not on BB, ACE-I, diuretic, which should be restarted slowly as an outpatient [] Seen by EP inpatient who report that patient may be candidate for CRT-P if CAD/TAVR evaluation unrevealing. EP will need to reassess after CAD/TAVR testing complete. # CODE: DNR/DNI # CONTACT: HCP: Name of health care proxy: ___ Relationship: husband Phone number: ___ ___ on Admission: The Preadmission Medication list is accurate and complete. 1. Allopurinol ___ mg PO DAILY 2. Atorvastatin 40 mg PO QPM 3. Levothyroxine Sodium 50 mcg PO DAILY 4. Omeprazole 40 mg PO DAILY 5. Apixaban 5 mg PO BID 6. Ferrous Sulfate 325 mg PO DAILY
summary: You were admitted to the hospital because you were feeling lightheaded, worse with changing position, and you were found to have a blood pressure that was a little low. You had imaging of your head which did not reveal anything acute or concerning. You were seen by the electrophysiology doctors to ___ your pacemaker. You had a procedure called a cardiac catheterization to look at the function of your heart and to look at your coronary arteries. It showed that you have non-obstructive coronary artery disease, which means there is some disease in the blood vessels in your heart but the disease was not enough to need stents placed.

text: Brief Hospital Course: The patient was transferred from ___ for catheterization. Her CXR performed on ___ still showed acute on chronic systolic heart failure. She was diuresed with 20 mg IV Lasix on ___. She underwent cardiac catheterization on ___ and was found to have two vessel disease and no intervention was performed due to the high risk with triple therapy as the patient is on chronic Eliquis for atrial fibrillation (renally dosed at 2.5 mg twice daily). Her Heparin was stopped. She had no further shortness of breath. Her SBP dropped to the 70's after 40 mg IV Lasix was given and she was then given a total of 650 ml IVF with improvement in SBP. Her troponin peaked at 0.86. With regards to her CAD, her Atorvastatin was increased to 80 mg Daily from 40 mg, and an echo was performed on ___ showing severe pulmonary hypertension. A repeat CXR showed continued substantial cardiomegaly with a small to moderate right pleural effusion which had decreased from the prior study with interval improvement in interstitial pulmonary edema which was minimal. She continued with all of her other home medications having been switched to her oral Lasix. She currently has appointments scheduled with Dr. ___ Pulmonary in ___, including PFT testing, which is requested to be moved up sooner. She will have early follow up with Dr. ___ Cardiologist as well as her PCP. She had been treated for a UTI at ___ and was on levoquin although she was noted to have some confusion there. This antibiotic was subsequently changed and she completed treatment. Her family felt she remained still "out of sorts" and a repeat UA was completed here which was mildly positive, so she was started empirically on Nitrofurantoin 100 mg BID. Culture revealed ___ colony of yeast and the Nitrofurantoin was discontinued on ___. Her Ranitidine was held while at ___, and will be reassessed at outpatient appointment with PCP. She worked with Physical Therapy and was deemed appropriate for discharge on ___.
summary: You were admitted for cardiac catheterization for NSTEMI. ___ were having shortness of breath, which is your angina equivalentYou were found to have fluid overload while at ___ and were given medications to help take fluid off your body. Your catheterization revealed two vessel disease and no intervention was done due to the risk of requiring triple therapy to prevent thrombosis (Eliquis for AFIB + Aspirin + Plavix, which increases your risk of bleeding). It was decided instead to manage ___ with medications. If symptoms persist, there will be consideration of a staged intervention. Attend cardiac rehab once cleared by Dr. ___ ___ had an ultrasound of your heart (echocardiogram) that revealed pulmonary hypertension.

text: Brief Hospital Course: This is an ___ year old woman with a PMH of severe AS s/p ___ TAVR on ___, atrial fibrillation, tachycardia-bradycardia syndrome s/p PPM (___) and heart failure with a preserve ejection fraction who presented to ___ with acute shortness of breath at rehab. ACTIVE ISSUES # DYSPNEA. The patient presented with worsening effusion and pulmonary edema, consistent with fluid overload. She had no fevers, cough or increased sputum production, which was not consistent with COPD exacerbation or pneumonia. TTE demonstrated no misplacement or misalignment of her ___. Her heart rate was well controlled. She has been on a very protracted taper of prednisone; her dose was dropped from 11 mg qd to 10 mg qd 1 day prior to admission. Her BNP was elevated on admission, also consistent with fluid overload. She was gently diuresed with good response, and close monitoring of her chemistries. Her dyspnea was likely multifactorial, with anxiety and COPD playing a role, however, she appeared volume overloaded on exam as well on admission. On discharge her CXR revealed much improved pulmonary edema and pleural effusions with trace pedal edema. Her COPD and anxiety were managed, as below. # ACUTE KIDNEY INJURY. The patient's baseline creatinine is 0.7-0.8. Upon arrival at ___, it was elevated to 1.1-1.2 range - likely secondary to renal vascular congestion. Creatinine returned to baseline with careful monitoring of fluid status. # COPD. The patient is already on prednisone (protracted taper). She had intermittent mild wheezing, that was treated with levalbuterol and ipratropium nebulizers, however due to lack of fevers, cough or increased sputum production, and no antibiotics were added. Home Advair also continued. Prednisone was tapered and discontinued. #ANEMIA: patient was noted to have gradually declining hemoglobin (9.2 on admission, nadir 8.3). She received transfusion of 1U pRBCs. Hgb on day of discharge was 9.8. #UTI - patient was found to have leukocytosis, positive UA and endorsed dysuria. Urine culture was pending at the time of discharge. She was treated with ciprofloxacin, total course 7 days, to be completed as an outpatient on ___. INACTIVE ISSUES # ATRIAL FIBRILLATION. Her rate was under good control during her hospitalization, and was not attribued to her the etiology of her dyspnea. Metoprolol, diltiazem and warfarin were continued (INR remained therapeutic). Rate control was difficult to achieve, and so she was started on digoxin 0.125mg every other day, which was a medicine she had been on prior to TAVR but was discontinued on prior discharge. # ANXIETY. Gentle and monitored dosing of benzodiazepines were administered, as needed, for anxiety symptoms. # DIABETES MELLITUS. Her home oral antidiabetic medications were held while in house, in favor of ISS. *** TRANSITIONAL ISSUES *** -Ciprofloxacin for UTI day of antibiotics ___ -Should have chem7, INR, and digoxin level check ___ while at rehab. Titrate potassium chloride dosing as necessary as patient is taking torsemide -Torsemide started at 20mg PO daily. She had formerly been on torsemide 20mg PO BID. -Digoxin 0.125mg PO every other day started. Patient was taking this prior to hospitalization for TAVR procedure, and it was restarted since rate-control for A-fib was difficult even with diltiazem and metoprolol.
summary: You were readmitted here from your rehab facility after becoming very short of breath over two days. You were recently hospitalized because you developed an accumulation of fluid in your lungs after your aortic valve procedure which caused episodes of low blood pressure and a rapid heart rate. When you were released last week, your fluid status and breathing had improved significantly. We decreased your dose of diuretics because your risk of developing heart failure was lowered by having your valve replaced. Since you developed shortness of breath you had to come back to the ___ for more diuretic therapy to relieve your symptoms. Overall, you did well and we were able to reduce the fluid in your lungs. However, you did develop a urinary tract infection for which you will take one more day of antibiotics.Your weight upon discharge was 123 pounds. Your diuretic therapy will be continued at ___ 20 daily.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Mr. ___ is a ___ yo M LVAD as of ___, bridge to transplant, status 1B at ___, recently admitted last week came in with headache and vertigo, who presents today with worsening headache. # Headache: Chronic daily headache with migrainous features, likely with some comoponent of analgesic rebound. NCHCT again is reassuring, as it was on last admission. New L anisocoria noted, so vascular etiologies evaluated with CTA/CTV, which was unrevealing. Pt started on gabapentin for migraine prophylaxis, with fiorocet for breakthrough (no more than ___ doses per week). Magnesium was also helpful in managing his headache. He was continued on metoclopramide but meclizine was discontinued per neurology. He will follow up with Neurology as an outpatient. # Dyspnea: Patient had a mildly elevated white count, but denied cough or fevers/chills to suggest pneumonia. CXR non-revealing. His JVP was elevated with prominent V-waves, but this is chronic. He was given 2 doses of Lasix 80mg IV but his Cr increased from 1.1 to 1.5, so further diuresis was deferred. He was restarted on his home torsemide and spironolactone. Recent RHC demonstrated RA 13 with PCWP 9 and cardiac index 2.9 L/min/m2. That confirmed that his left heart filling pressures are not excessive when his CVP is modestly elevated. Moreover, an echocardiogram during his admission last week showed the interventricular septum in the neutral position, no mitral regurgitation, and closed aortic valve, suggesting there is minimal room for further increase in his LVAD speed. Ultimately, some of his exertional intolerance may be relative to chronic right heart failure while on LVAD support, but we are not at a stage where he requires inotropic support and there does not appear to be room to augment his diuresis or LVAD support. # Dilated ischemic cardiomyopathy: EF ___, ___ heartmateII LVAD implant for BTT, MVR, redo AVR (bioprosthetic). Patient is currently experiencing increased shortness of breath, and does have elevated proBNP (1800 from 1000 in ___. Also appearing volume up on exam. RAMP TTE on ___ revealed elevated filling pressures. Diursed with Lasix 80mg IV x1 on admission. INR goal 2.5-3.5. He was continued on amlodipine 10mg daily, Digoxin 0.125 mg daily, and hydralazine 100mg daily # Hypertension - On admission, noted to have MAP of 100. Goal MAP for patient is <85 as above. During last admission, hydralazine was increased to 100mg TID. Overnight has been within range on home medications. He was continued on home amlodipine 10mg daily and hydralazine 100mg daily # History of mechanical valve (aortic valve replaced age ___ and MVR at age ___: Warfarin dosing with goal INR 2.5-3.5. Currently supratherapeutic at 3.8. Warfarin was held on admission then restarted. # Hypothyroidism: Synthroid dose increased in ___. Continue Levothyroxine Sodium 175 mcg PO DAILY, found to have TSH of 14 # Chronic Hep B: On home entecavir, followed by ___ Liver Center. Given negative viral load and stage I fibrosis and grade 1 to 2 inflammation, he is not contraindicated for heart transplant. For now will continue home entecavir, however, post transplant he should have double coverage for the first six months with hepatitis B immunoglobulin in addition to the entecavir. He should have an outpatient RUQ ultrasound to screen for hepatocellular carcinoma. # History of Atrial flutter ___ ablation, also with afib: Continued home amiodarone 200mg daily. # Gout: continued allopurinol ___ mg PO/NG DAILY. Continued Colchicine 0.6 mg PO/NG BID. # Fe deficiency anemia Transitional issues: ==================== - Recommend vestibular ___ for dizziness as outpatient - Started on PRN Fioricet (no more than ___ and preventative gabapentin - Consider uptitration of levothyroixine - INR check ___ and titrate warfarin #DISCHARGE WEIGHT: 108kg
summary: You were admitted to the hospital because you had a severe headache, which was similar to the one that brought you into the hospital recently. We evaluated your headache thoroughly, and believe that it is a combination of a tension or migranous type headache, with some contribution of rebound headache from the medications you were taking to treat your headache. You had a CT Scan of your head which showed your old stroke but NO NEW CHANGES OR BLEEDING, and no evidence of blood clot, narrowing, or swelling that may have caused your symptoms. We started you on two new medicines, one (gabapentin) is to prevent headaches from occurring, and the other (fiorocet) is to treat any occasional breakthrough headaches. Please weigh yourself every morning and call if your weight increases by more than 2 pounds in one day or 5 pounds in 3 days.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ==================== PATIENT SUMMARY ==================== Mr. ___ is a ___ old who with non-ischemic cardiomyopathy first diagnosed in ___, AF s/p ICD implant ___ and VT ablation, chronic systolic heart failure (LVEF 25%), who presented after scheduled RHC in the setting of recent dyspnea on exertion - with RHC demonstrating moderately severe elevation of right heart pressures and low cardiac index, now improved s/p increased Losartan dose. ==================== ACUTE ISSUES ==================== #Acute on chronic systolic heart failure #HFrEF: Non-ischemic cardiomyopathy: NYHA II, warm/well perfused and with volume overload on exam. LVEF ___. Trigger likely dietary indiscretion vs. insufficient afterload control. Palpitations only rarely felt, no worsened angina. Patient arrived on ___ for scheduled RHC to determine filling pressures (RA 7, PA pressure 58/24, PA mean 35, Wedge 20, CI 1.9). His ___ was left in, where we trialed 50mg Losartan daily with subsequent improvement in CI and PA pressures(most recently RA 4, PA pressure 37/20, PA mean 26, Wedge 24, CI 2.5). As such, his ___ was removed without complications on ___, and he was discharged with 80mg PO Torsemide, 50mg PO Losartan, 100mg Metoprolol XL daily, and 20mg Epleronone PO daily. ==================== CHRONIC ISSUES ==================== #Atrial fibrillation: CHADS-Vasc 1. His Apixaban was held in the setting of scheduled RHC, and was restarted after his RHC on ___. # Depressive disorder # Anxiety: Continued home Lorazepam QHS PRN # GERD: Continued home Lansoprazole. Would recommend considering stopping Ranitidine in the future. # Celiac disease # IBS: Continued home Sucralfate QHS and Dicyclomine # HLD: Continued home Pravastatin # Cervical Spondylosis and Cervical Radiculitis # Myofascial pain syndrome: Continued home Gabapentin and Hydrocodone-Acetaminophen, # Transaminitis: Likely ___ hepatic steatosis. We monitored throughout his hospitalization. # HSV: Continued home Valacyclovir #Thrombocytopenia: Chronic, suspect ___ liver dysfunction. We monitored throughout his hospitalization. #Seasonal allergies: Continued home Loratadine, Fluticasone, and Azelastine ==================== TRANSITIONAL ISSUES ==================== [ ] DISCHARGE WEIGHT: 93.8 kg (206.79 lb) [ ] DISCHARGE DIURETIC: Torsemide 80mg PO daily [ ] DISCHARGE ANTICOAGULATION: Apixaban 5mg PO BID [ ] FOLLOW UP LABORATORY TESTING: BMP on ___ at follow up with Cardiology [ ] MEDICATION CHANGES: [ ] NEW: None [ ] STOPPED: None [ ] CHANGED: Losartan 37.5mg PO daily -> 50mg PO daily [ ]Discuss stopping Ranitidine with PCP [ ]Follow up with Cardiology on ___
summary: You were admitted to the hospital because you had been feeling short of breath and had abdominal swelling. This was felt to be due to a condition called heart failure, where your heart does not pump hard enough and fluid backs up into your lungs. A ___ catheter was placed in your heart, where we were able to monitor the blood pressures. We increased the dose of your home Losartan, and your pressures improved. We also used an IV diuretic to help get fluid out of your lungs and abdomen, and you were restarted on your home dose of Torsemide. You improved considerably and were ready to leave the hospital.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ with history of dilated cardiomyopathy and afib s/p recent ___ presenting with dyspnea and reproducible chest pressure. # Dyspnea: likely 2'/2 CHF exacerbation given elevated BNP, versus possible pneumonia given CXR possibly suggestive of pneumonia and patient's subjective fevers/chills. No fevers during hospitalization, no cough or sputum production. Dyspnea improved with diuresis and albuterol nebs. - needs a Creatinine and electolyte check on ___ given mild increase in Creatinine with diuresis to 1.7. # Chest pain: Unlikley ACS as troponin negative x 3, EKG nonspecific, although concern for ACS given hx of diabetes, HTN. Unlikely secondary pneumonia causing a pleuritic chest pain, though unlikely given unconcerning CXR, and the lack of fever or leukocytosis. Unlikely PE given supratherapeutic INR and negative D-dimer. Most likely secondary to musculoskeletal given the fact that it is reproducible and pleuritic. Pain improved with standing tylenol. # Afib: patient is s/p DCCV ___. Was in and out of afib in the ED. Given E to E ratio likely relies on atrial kick for a significant amount of EF. Continued amiodarone and metoprolol to maintain sinus rhythm. Patient remained in sinus though with frequent PACs. Warfarin was held given supratherapeutic INR, but restarted at - restarted warfarin on day of discharge. Will need a recheck on ___. # QTc prolongation: QTc between 480-490. Magnesium was within normal limits during hospitalization. Avoided QTc prolonging medications. # HTN: continued home lisinopril, metoprolol # HL: continued pravastatin # Diabetes: held glipizide, Blood glucose well controlled in hospital. SSI in house TRANSITIONAL: - needs a Creatinine and electolyte check on ___ given mild increase in Creatinine with diuresis to 1.7. - restarted warfarin on day of discharge. Will need a recheck on ___. - Bumetanide increased to BID from QD. # CODE: Full (confirmed) # CONTACT: ___ (Niece) ___
summary: You presented with symptoms of chest pressure and shortness of breath that we think was caused by an exacerbation of your heart failure. This might also have been exacerbated by episodes of atrial fibrillation, however, with metoprolol and amiodarone in the hospital your heart rate remained normal and regular. You also noted improvement in your breathing with albuterol and improvement in your chest pain with acetomenophen. Also you should continue to take albuterol and acetomenophen as needed for your breathing and chest pain. You responded very well to your home diuretic therapy (Bumetanide). You are now back to your "dry" weight of 222.46 pounds.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ASSESSMENT AND PLAN: ___ yo man w/ HTN, IDDM, ESRD on HD ___, severe PVD s/p BKA, CAD of unclear extent, diastolic CHF (may no longer be preserved EF), renal carcinoma s/p local radiofrequency ablation of tumor without nephrectomy who presented to ___ w/ severe SOB and hypoxia, transferred to ___ w/ c/f NSTEMI on heparin. # CORONARIES: unknown # PUMP: per ___ ECHO, preserved EF # RHYTHM: sinus # NSTEMI: Patient likely is having type 2 demand ischemia in the setting of acute heart failure exacerbation and having retention of his troponins in the setting of CKD. EKG unchanged from prior in ___. While his troponins are elevated, baseline troponin is unclear in the setting of his CKD. However, given CAD history and symptoms prior to admission cannot rule out ischemic disease. Troponin plateaued at .78. CKMB was negative. Treated with aspirin/heparin drip/atorvastatin/isosorbide dinitrate and metoprolol. Heparin drip stopped ___. Cardiac cath showed mid LAD narrowing, total RCA narrowing and proximal LCx narrowing. ECHO showed mild regional left ventricular systolic dysfunction with EF 40%, c/w CAD. Mild mitral regurgitation. After reviewing films with cardiac surgery and interventional cardiology plan is for CABG next week. # Pneumonia: He reported coughing three days prior to admission. He continued to have coughing dyspnea, crackles, and wheezing despite fluid removal with HD. ___ CT chest showed bilateral ___, centrilobular, ground-glass opacities, greatest at the left lower lobe, are most likely infectious. Mildly prominent mediastinal lymph nodes, likely reactive. He had no leukocytosis and remained afebrile. He was treated with a 5 day course of Levofloxacin 500 mg PO Q48H ending ___. # Acute diastolic and systolic CHF exacerbation He appeared volume overloaded on admission. New cough was though to be partially secondary to fluid overload in setting of CHF exacerbation. He has a history of HFpEF. As the patient was anuric he required HD for fluid removal as well as placed on a 1L fluid restriction. During multiple HD sessions her reported shortness of breath and chest discomfort. Troponins and ecg were stable from prior. ___ echo showed EF 40% and Mild regional left ventricular systolic dysfunction, c/w CAD. Mild mitral regurgitation. Hypoxia gradually improved since admission with HD fluid removal over many sessions and antibiotic course as above. # HTN: Decision was made to stop amlodipine given reduced EF and well as Lisinopril in case it was contributing to his cough. Pressure was controlled with fluid removal during HD as well as hydralazine 50mg TID, metoprolol 25 mg /d, and isosorbide 30 mg TID. # CODE: full TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: ------------------- []NSTEMI- treated medically. Plan for cardiac bypass surgery ___ due to multivessel disease. He will be discharged and return to the hospital day of the surgery. Simvastatin switched to atorvastatin. []CAD - Plan for bypass surgery as above. []Pneumonia- presented to hospital with cough. Infectious process seen on CT. Will complete course of Levofloxacin on ___. []HTN- discontinued lisinopril and amlodipine. Discharge on hydralazine, Lasix, labetalol and isosorbide mononitrate. []Mupirocin ointment to nares daily until ___ []Chlorhexidine bath ___ evening prior to surgery
summary: You were admitted because you had shortness of breath. The shortness of breath was likely due to a number of things including a pneumonia, Heart failure, and a potential heart attack. You received antibiotics to treat the infection. Hemodialysis was performed to remove fluid to help you breath better. In addition you had an ultrasound of your heart which showed that it has been weakened. You also had a procedure performed to shoot dye into the vessels of your heart which showed that many of the vessels are blocked. The cardiologist and cardiac surgeons have recommended you have this repaired with surgery because that is more effective than having stents placed. After you leave the hospital please attend your normal hemodialysis on ___ and ___. You should return to the hospital to have open heart surgery to repair your vessels next ___. Please be there by 6am. You will be contacted by the cardiac surgery team on ___ with more information prior to the surgery.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ y/o F with PMHx of TBM, bronchiectasis, h/o DVT/PE, PPM, sCHF, afib, HTN, hypothyroidism, prior d.___, who presented to the ED after calling EMS for progressive weakness at home, found to have evidence of PNA on CXR. # PNA, Dysphagia, Tracheobronchomalacia, Bronchiectasis: Given history of prior PNA in this area as well as pt report of dysphagia (see HPI), this raises concern for possible recurrent aspiration events at home. Pt also with significant underlying pulmonary disease, including tracheobronchomalacia and bronchiectasis, which may be contributing to her respiratory issues. Pt was placed on zosyn initially and then was broadened to meropenem as below. Swallow eval was ordered which showed concern for aspiration. However, video swallow did not reveal this finding and pt's diet was advanced to regular. Pt completed 10 days of antibiotic therapy during admission. ___ WILL NEED A REPEAT CXR IN 4 WEEKS TO DOCUMENT RESOLUTION OF OPACITIES AFTER TREATMENT FOR PNA. # UTI: UA is consistent with UTI. While pt attributes urinary frequency to her lasix, urinary infection could also be contributing. Of note, prior urine cx significant for resistant e.coli. She was initially placed on zosyn and then broadened to meropenem based off of prior sensitivities. Final ucx with MDR ecoli. Pt completed 10 days of antibiotic therapy during admission # chronic right hip pain, s/p ORIF: Significantly affecting QOL, reports pain is worse with movement; pain limits her mobility. Added long-acting MS ___ and continued ___ prn breakthrough pain with good results. On the day of discharge, pt reported uncontrolled right hip pain at rest. Physical exam c/w possible trochanteric bursisits, for which she was started on a short prednisone burst. Ongoing assessment per hospice team. . #goals of care-Pt has been discussing this for some time and appears to have some difficulty with deciding on course of action. She appears to vascillate between wanting hospice and more aggressive care. PACT team has been involved and palliative care was consulted. Pt worked extensively with SW, paliiative care and case management to decide on best course of actiion. Ultimately, she chose to go home with hospice and 24hr care. She was prescribed a Hoyer prior to discharge. . #acute DVT: Pt with h.o DVT/PE, found to have b/l DVT on ___. Stopped rivaroxaban and started lovenox BID # Chronic dCHF: Appears eu- to hypovolemic on exam (dry MM, acute infection). While BNP was elevated, this is fairly consistent with prior values in OMR. No significant crackles on exam, no significant pulmonary edema on CXR. Held lasix initially and then restarted home lasix as pt reported increase in dyspnea ___. Responded well to reinitiation of home dose lasix. She was given some 20mg IV lasix prn as well. Subsequently, she became mildly hypotensive, and her evening lasix dose was held. As she denied any change in her breathing, and she remained clinically stable on room air, the pm lasix dose was discontinued. Her volume status will continue to be monitored at home under hospice care, and if again volume overloaded, her evening dose lasix can be resumed. # AFib, SSS s/p PPM, HTN: Continued a/c with ASA, bb, statin and lasix as above. Rivaroxiban dc'd, lovenox started. B-blocker dose was decreased at time of discharge. # Prior DVT/PE: lovenox started in place of rivaroxiban # Depression: on sertraline # Hypothyroidism: on levothyroxine # DVT PROPHYLAXIS: []heparin sc []SCDs [x]systemic # CODE STATUS: DNR/DNI (confirmed with pt) . Transitional care 4 week f/u CXR
summary: You presented to the hospital with weakness and were found to have a pneumonia as well as a urinary tract infection. You were treated with antibiotics. You were seen by our speech and swallow specialists. Ultimately, your video swallow test did not show aspiration and you were given a regular diet. You were found to have blood clots in your leg (DVT) and your rivaroxiban was stopped and you were started on lovenox. You completed your antibiotic course for PNA and UTI during admission. You were also seen by our palliative care team to help you to define your goals of care. You decided to return home with hospice care and 24 hr assistance, and these services were arranged for you.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ woman with a history of poorly-controlled type 2 diabetes, hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and CAD s/p CABG and mitral valve repair on ___ with a subsequent hospitalization c/b cardiac arrest (PEA, sepsis) and left leg harvest site infection s/p angioplasty and debridement and wound vac who presents with shortness of breath, bilateral lower extremity swelling, and blisters over her right foot. # Acute on chronic systolic heart failure: She was discharged from rehab 2 weeks prior to presentation. Over that time she had sub-acute presentation of symptomatic heart failure. Of note, she was not on a home diuretic until the days before presentation. She was admitted and improved with diuresis. Her right foot blisters were thought to be ___ volume overload and wearing tight fitting slippers. She initially required supplementary oxygen, but her oxygen requirement improved with diuresis. # Right foot cellulitis: Patient presented with foot and toe blisters that were likely form volume overload. She developed a right foot cellulitis at the site of a burst blister. It improved with antibiotics. She will continue 1.5 more days of antibiotics after discharge to complete a 7 day course for cellulitis. # Cholecystitis: Ms. ___ had imaging that was suggestive of cholecystitis. This imaging with c/w prior images. She was seen by surgery and was thought not to be having any active symptoms of acute cholecystitis. They requested that she follow up with them in the outpatient setting for a cholecystectomy. Chronic issues: # CAD s/p CABG: - Continued ASA - Continued atorvastatin 80 mg daily # Diabetes: Held metformin while admitted. Patient was on ISS. Discharged on home regimen. # Orthostatic hypotension: Midodrine and fludrocortisone were started at rehab in the setting of severe orthostatic hypotension, which was limiting her ability to work with ___. Continued her midodrine but discontinued fludrocortisone in the setting of CHF exacerbation. # Hypothyroidism: TSH low on admission. Likely over dosed - Reduced dose synthroid # GERD: - Continued ranitidine Transitional Issues: Discharge weight: 53.3 kg - Please follow up chemistry panel which will be drawn by ___ on ___ - TSH low on admission. Decreased dose of levothyroxine. Please follow up TSH in 5 weeks (Last week of ___ - Pt being discharge to complete 1.5 more days of abx (cefpodoxime, metronidazole) for RLE cellulitis. - Pt started on 10mg of furosemide daily and 12.5 mg of spironolactone. - Not started on ACE-I in the setting of requiring midodrine to prevent orthostatic hypotension. - Seen by surgical team regarding imaging findings concerning for cholecystitis.
