High risk to develop acute cholecystitis. Please have pt follow with surgery as outpatient, once she has stabilized enough for surgery.
summary: You were admitted with worsening heart failure. We think that this caused your swelling and your blisters on your feet. While you were here, we gave you diuretics, which are medications to help you urinate. First, we did this through your IV and then we switched you to an oral regimen. At discharge, you weighed 53.3 kg or 117.5 lbs. Weigh yourself daily and notify your cardiology team if your weight increases more than 3lbs in one day. You developed an infection on your right foot. We think this is because you had a blister on that foot that burst before you came into the hospital. We treated you with antibiotics, and the infection improved. Please finish your course of antibiotics at home even if you are feeling better. Lastly, there was concern that your gallbladder might be inflamed. We had our surgery team evaluate you and they think that your issues have been happening for a long time. They recommended that you follow up with them when you are feeling better.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Mrs. ___ is a ___ y/o lady with PMH Graves disease, HTN, remote breast cancer, who presented to PCP with lower extremity edema, found to have acute diastolic CHF and new atrial fibrillation with RVR, admitted for management of heart failure and arrhythmia. # CORONARIES: no known CAD # PUMP: EF >55% ___ # RHYTHM: AF ACUTE ISSUES: =============== # Acute Diastolic CHF # Pulmonary HTN Pt had significant volume overload ___ edema, elevated JVD) on admission. EF was normal in ___ with moderate pulmonary HTN. CHF likely ___ long-standing hypertension with possible component of new AF. Pt was treated with diuresis with IV Lasix. She developed ___ with Torsemide 40mg that improved with holding diuretics. Discharged off diuretics as remained net negative off diuretics. # Atrial fibrillation Pt was also found to have new AF, with a rapid rate and unknown duration. She was started on metoprolol for rate control and apixaban given CHADS-VASC of 4. She received TEE on ___ that showed no clots and underwent DCCV on the same day which was unsuccessful. She then was started on amiodarone loading with plan to have repeat DCCV performed upon completion of amiodarone load. Started on metoprolol as well with rates controlled to <110 with activity on discharge. She was also discharged with ___ of hearts to assess for afib burden outpatient. CHRONIC ISSUES: ================= # Graves Disease: now on hormone replacement therapy, unsure what she had in terms of treatment. Continued home levothyroxine. TSH wnl # HTN: Home lisinopril was held initially and was continued at ___ # OSTEOPENIA: Continued home alendronate TRANSITIONAL ISSUES ======================= [] Started on amiodarone load with plan for 400mg BID through ___ and then 200mg daily [] Outpatient DCCV scheduled outpatient on ___ following completion of amiodarone load [] Monitor weights outpatient and start on Torsemide PRN for weight gain ___ with Torsemde 40mg, may consider torsemide 20) [] She should get a repeat TTE as outpatient to asses heart function once in normal sinus rhythm [] She will go with ___ of Hearts Monitor to assess for arrhythmia with strips going to Dr. ___ #NEW MEDS: Amiodarone, Apixiban, Metoprolol #CHANGED MEDS: Lisinopril held at discharge for hypotension Discharge weight: 64 kg (142 lb) Discharge BUN/creatinine: ___
summary: You were admitted because you had leg swelling and palpitations that was found to be due to heart failure and an arrhythmia of your heart called atrial fibrillation. You were admitted to the hospital because you had been feeling short of breath and you were found to have fluid on your lungs. This was felt to be due to a condition called heart failure, where your heart does not pump hard enough and fluid backs up into your lungs. You were given a diuretic medication through the IV to help get the fluid out. You improved considerably and were ready to leave the hospital. You received medication (Lasix) that helped removed some of the fluid from your legs. You initially were treated with a shock which was not successful in restoring the heart's normal rhythm. You then received a medication called amiodarone which helps the heart rhythm to normalize. However you went back into the irregular heart rhythm (Atrial fibrillation or AFib).

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is an ___ ___ speaking female with PMH of Hepatitis C, hepatic enchephalopathy, h/o LGIB, recently d/ced from ___ on coumadin for RIJ thrombus who initally presented from ___ on ___ with hypontension, transferred to MICU w/BRBPR and mental status changes. Now called out to floor, mental status improved following lactulose, being treated with ceftriaxone for UTI, HCT stable, EGD with no acute bleeding, plan for colonoscopy as an outpatient. . #Fever- Started on ___, most likely due to viral syndrome. She was evaluated with blood cultures, urine cx, stool culture and chest xray, none of which showed any evidence of acute bacterial infection. She was treated symptomatically with tylenol and oxycodone as needed for discomfort. . #wheezing, dyspnea - most likely due to combination of COPD exacerbation due to viral illness and mild degree of diastolic heart failure, CXR on ___ showed mild engorgement of bilateral hilar vessels and possible small effusions. She was started on a 3 day prednisone taper on ___ with plan for 30 -> 20 -> 10 then d/c. In addition she was treated with albuterol nebs q2hours and atrovent nebs q 6 hours. In addition, she was given an extra dose of IV lasix 60mg prior to discharge to help with her dyspnea. . #Acute renal failure -Baseline Creatinine likely around 1.1, currently with mild renal failure in the setting of likely overall volume overload with a possible element of diastolic heart failure. She was treated with usual lasix 60mg po daily and also given one time dose on lasix 60mg IV x1 prior to discharge. . # Mental status changes: On admission, she was encephalopathic, most likely ___ hepatic encephalopathy vs. UTI, currently back to baseline and stable. Urine culture with possible urinary tract infection, treated with ceftriaxone for a 7 day course, last dose given ___. She was back to her baseline mental status prior to discharge. She was continued on a bowel regimen and lactulose three times per day. . # GI bleed: Per medical records she had BRBPR on examination in the ED with Hct~22 ( baseline Hct is between ___, also developed gross melena - stat Hct check demonstrated Hct 27 --> 24.9. On transfer out of MICU, HCT relatively stable at 21 with no additional episodes of BRBRP or melena in ICU. EGD with grade I non-bleeding varices and non bleeding ulcers in gastric antrum. Transfused 4 units total during this admission. She was treated with IV and then oral protonix BID. A PICC line was placed for access. He daughter will be contacted by the gastroenterology fellow in the upcoming week to schedule an appointment for an outpatient colonoscopy. In the meantime, hematocrit should be checked at least several times per week to monitor hematocrit. . # RIJ thrombus: Patient presented with RUE edema during recent admission and was found to have a RIJ clot on CTA. She was started on a heparin gtt and then transitioned to Coumadin, INR~2.9 on admission. Coumadin was stopped during this admission given active bleeding. It was not restarted on discharge given likely slowly oozing from GI tract. . # Hepatitis C: prior complications of varices and encephalopathy. She was continued on lasix and nadolol, lactulose titrate to 4 BM/day. She was followed by the liver service during her hospitalization. . # Hypertension: continue outpatient regimen of amlodipine now that HCT/BP stable. Her isosorbide mononitrate was stopped in an effort to discontinue unnecesary medications. . # Access: very poor access, PICC line was left in place on discharge and can be discharged at the nursing facility once she is improved. . # Code: Full (verified with daughter) . #Communication: Daughter ___ H: ___ ___
summary: You were admitted to the hospital because you had low blood pressure and were also having blood in your stool. You were given blood transfusions to replace the blood you lost. In addition, you had an endocopy which showed that you have esophageal varices and some non-bleeding ulcers in your stomach. You were treated with an acid blocker pantoprazole for the ulcers. You were also treated with ceftriaxone for a urinary tract infection. You were seen by the liver doctors during your ___. They will contact you to schedule an outpatient colonoscopy to look for sources of bleeding. You also had confusion on admission, thought to be due to your liver disease. You were given lactulose and your symptoms improved. You also had fevers, body aches, sore throat and cough most likely due to a viral illness. You had blood cultures, urine cultures, stool cultures and a chest xray which did not show any evidence of bacterial infection.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Patient is a ___ yo female presents from OSH with CP and lateral ST depressions. Also associated acute on chronic diastolic heart failure. . #. CAD: Initially rest pain was concerning for unstable angina. She was begun on a heparin drip. She was ruled out for acute MI by 4 sets of negative cardiac biomarkers. Her medications were optimized to include a beta-blocker (Coreg), a high dose statin, a long acting nitrate, and an ACE. She was continued on her ASA/Plavix. On this regimen her HR and BP were better controlled. A persantine stress test showed only a moderate fixed inferior wall defect, not associated with any anginal symptoms. She remained chest pain free while in the hospital. After, evaluation by physical therapy, she was felt capable to return to her assisted living. This chest pain is likely a musculoskeletal strain that occured after she fell onto her left side a number of weeks ago. This information was not initially available due to her confusion from her UTI. However, as she cleared the story became more clear. She will follow up with her primary care provider. #. Acute on chronic diastolic heart failure: EF in ___ 40-45%. She presented to the hospital with signs of pulmonary edema on CXR and a new O2 requirement. This was likely from the fluid administration given at the OSH to correct some mild ARF. She was diuresed with boluses of 20mg IV lasix daily, making her approximately 3 liters negative for her current hospital stay. She was weened to room air and ambulating without desaturation. She will be discharged on her home dose of 20mg PO lasix daily. #. Rhythm: NSR on admit and throughout . #. UTI: Pan-sensitive e.coli diagnosed at the OSH. Continued on Cipro here with an anticipated 1 week total course. Her mental status improved throughout her hospital stay and on discharge was fully alert and oriented. . # DM1: Continued on her home dose Lantus with RISS to good effect. . # Kidney transplant: She was seen by the renal transplant service here and continued on her prednisone and tacrolimus with daily level checks. They agreed with our administration of a low dose ACE. She will follow up with her PCP and should have tacro levels checked at that time. . #Gastroparesis: Begun on Reglan PO with good effect. . #GERD: Continued on Pantoprazole with good effect. . # Depression: Continued on Celexa and Seroquel with good effect . #Seizure d/o: Continued on dilantin with good effect . #. FEN: Cardiac/Diabetic/Low sodium diet . #. Code: Full
summary: You were admitted for evaluation of your chest pain. You did not have a heart attack based on EKGs and lab tests. The stress test of your heart showed only some mild old heart disease but no new problems. This chest pain is likely related to the fall you had some time ago. We have added or altered some of your medications to better control your blood pressure and your heart rate. This will protect your heart in the future. You also had some increased fluid on your lungs. We gave you lasix to get this fluid off and your breathing improved. Please continue to take your home dose of Lasix. You were also diagnosed with a urinary tract infection. You were treated with antibiotics that you will continue to take for a few days at home.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ ___ female with ___ severe CAD s/p CABG (___), HFrEF (LVEF 13%), seropositive RA on plaquenil/sulfasalazine, and latent TB s/p RIPE therapy (completed ___ who presents with dyspnea and cough, likely ___ CHF exacerbation. #CORONARIES: 3VD s/p LIMA to LAD, SVGs to distal Lcx and distal RCA. #PUMP: EF 13% #RHYTHM: Atrial fibrillation with RVR ACUTE/ACTIVE PROBLEMS: # Acute on chronic HFrEF Presented with dyspnea, hypoxemia, elevated BNP and CXR showing pleural effusion concerning for CHF exacerbation. Likely due to under-diuresis. She underwent thoracentesis and IV diuresis with improvement in her dyspnea and hypoxemia. After she was clinically euvolemic, Torsemide 60mg daily was started which maintained euvolemia. She was continued on metoprolol. No afterload reduction was started while inpatient (not on ___ due to low blood pressures). # Pleural effusion. Thoracentesis on admission showed transudative effusion c/w volume overload. It improved with diuresis. # Atrial fibrillation New afib w/ RVR on last admission ___ weeks ago, thought to be a precipitating factor of her CHF exacerbation. Converted back to sinus after digoxin load. During this admission, she was in sinus rhythm with frequent PACs. Her metoprolol and Apixaban were continued. # Severe protein-calorie malnutrition # Dysphagia Patient endorses decreased appetite and weight loss over several months. Nutrition assessment was consistent with < 75% of estimated caloric needs > 1 month. In addition to her diet as ordered, she was recommended to take Ensure Enlive 4x per day, multivitamins and minerals, thiamine and folic acid. She was also assessed by ___ who recommended chin-tuck as patient is a high risk for aspiration (she had video swallow on previous admission confirming this assessment). She was started on Cholecalciferol 800u daily for borderline-low levels. The patient and family was counseled on chin tuck and aspiration precautions. She was also encouraged to take her medications with apple sauce. CHRONIC/STABLE PROBLEMS: # Leukopenia # Anemia: Evaluated by Heme/Onc in ___, thought to be ___ chronic disease and marrow suppression Hydroxychloroquine and/or sulfasalazine. Stable at recent baseline. # CAD: S/p CABG ___ with Left internal mammary artery to left anterior descending artery, saphenous vein grafts to distal circumflex and distal right coronary arteries. - Continued home atorvastatin 40 mg PO QHS - Continued home aspirin 81 mg PO daily # Rheumatoid arthritis: - Continued home hydroxychloroquine sulfate 200 mg PO every other day - Continued home sulfasalazine # Osteoporosis: . - started calcium carbonate 600 mg PO BID - Started vitamin D supplement ==================== TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: ==================== DISCHARGE CREATININE: 0.9 DISCHARGE WEIGHT: 35.5 kg (78.26 lb) Dry weight 78 - 80 lbs DISCHARGE HEART FAILURE MEDICATIONS: Metoprolol Succinate XL 12.5 mg PO DAILY Torsemide 60 mg PO/NG DAILY CHANGED MEDICATIONS: None NEW MEDICAIONS: Thiamine 100 mg PO/NG DAILY Duration: 5 Days Vitamin D 800 UNIT PO/NG DAILY FoLIC Acid 1 mg PO/NG DAILY Calcium Carbonate 500 mg PO/NG BID HELD/STOPPED MEDICATIONS: None [] Monitor PO intake and encourage Ensure Enlive 4X daily as recommended by nutrition [] Continue to counsel patient on chin tuck and aspiration precautions when eating and drinking [] Titrate Torsemide as needed [] Please repeat renal function, sodium, and electrolytes as patient has been slightly hypernatremic while inpatient due to poor free water intake. proxy chosen: Yes Name of health care proxy: ___ Relationship: Son Phone number: ___ ___ on Admission: The Preadmission Medication list is accurate and complete. 1. Apixaban 5 mg PO BID 2. Metoprolol Succinate XL 12.5 mg PO DAILY 3. Torsemide 60 mg PO DAILY 4. Atorvastatin 40 mg PO QPM 5. Hydroxychloroquine Sulfate 200 mg PO EVERY OTHER DAY 6. SulfaSALAzine ___ 1000 mg PO BID
summary: You came to the hospital because you were experiencing shortness of breath. You were found to have some fluid around your lungs. The fluid was sampled and did NOT show any signs of infection. You were given medicine to help remove the fluid from your body and your breathing began to improve. You were also seen by the nutritionist and the swallow therapy team. The nutritionist recommended vitamins and nutrition shakes which you will continue to take after you leave the hospital. The swallow therapy team was concerned that you were at risk to aspirate food and water into your lungs and recommended that you always swallow liquid or food with your chin tucked into your chest.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ year-old female with past medical history of FSGN status post living-unrelated renal transplant in ___, Coronary Artery Disease with NSTEMI, lacunar strokes, osteonecrosis of the long bones, and recent diagnosis of congestive heart failure ___ who presented with right flank (actually right lumbar/sacral) pain, right lower quadrant pain over transplanted kidney, nausea, and diarrhea over 5 days, as well as recent 15lb weight gain between ___ after stopping furosemide (though weight was downtrending on arrival having restarted furosemide). #FLANK/ABDOMINAL PAIN/DIARRHEA: Unclear etiology. Concerning for rejection. Her native kidneys are atrophic and are unlikely to have stones; further, no stones seen on CTU. No dysuria and UA unremarkable, reassuring for no UTI and culture with mixed flora (contaminant). Could be gastroenteritis in setting of diarrhea (patient has chronic diarrhea for ___ days and then no stool for ___ days, so 5 straight days of diarrhea atypical); however, diarrhea resolved by admission and no stool samples were thus able to be sent. Of note, she does have history of C.diff, but her picture was never concerning for evolution to toxic megacolon. Differential is broadened in immunosuppression; CMV and BK virus pending. CTU did not show diverticulitis. Biliary pathology less likely given normal LFTs; mildly elevated lipase in ED resolved by ___. Other etiologies include renal transplant rejection (see below). Pain controlled with standing 1g TID acetaminophen and PO dilaudid; patient takes vicodin at home. #ACUTE KIDNEY INJURY: Creatinine improved from 2.7 on admission to 1.8 (same as 3 days prior to admission) ___ apparently prerenal from volume losses (gastroenteritis, nausea), supported by UNa <20. Injury may have had component of restarting furosemide, having put on reported 15lb when she held it. Transplant rejection cannot be excluded especially given tenderness of transplant location. Somewhat reassuring that ultrasound is normal. Will be discharged on half normal dose of furosemide with plan to follow up within 14 days, and have repeat labs by ___, including Chem 7 and tacro/sirolimus. CHRONIC ISSUES ============== #CAD with h/o NSTEMI: Continued home metoprolol, ASA, clopidogrel, atorvastatin. #CHRONIC SYSTOLIC CHF: Patient w/new CHF last admission (TTE with ejection fraction 51% and wall motion abnormalities). Chest x-ray on admission without acute cardiopulmonary process. Furosemide held and restarted at half dose on discharge. #FSGN STATUS POST KIDNEY TRANSPLANT IN ___: Continued home sirolimus/tacrolimus and calcitriol. Kept on renal diet. Tacro level on ___ was 6.3. Rapamycin level 4.9. Doses not adjusted. #HISTORY DEEP VEIN THROMBOSIS, PULMONARY EMBOLISM: Coumadin until ___ (stopped ___ hemarthrosis). #HYPERTENSION: Continued amlodipine, metoprolol #GOUT: Continued Febuxostat #CODE: Full #CONTACT: ___ (Sister); Phone number: ___ ___ on Admission: The Preadmission Medication list is accurate and complete. 1. amLODIPine 5 mg PO DAILY 2. Aspirin 81 mg PO DAILY 3. Atorvastatin 80 mg PO QPM 4. Calcitriol 0.5 mcg PO DAILY 5. Citalopram 40 mg PO DAILY 6. Clopidogrel 75 mg PO DAILY 7. Febuxostat 120 mg PO DAILY 8. Famotidine 20 mg PO DAILY 9. Metoprolol Succinate XL 75 mg PO DAILY 10. Nitroglycerin SL 0.3 mg SL Q5MIN:PRN cp 11. Sirolimus 1 mg PO DAILY 12. Tacrolimus 2 mg PO Q12H 13. Zolpidem Tartrate 10 mg PO QHS:PRN insomnia 14. Furosemide 80 mg PO DAILY 15. HYDROcodone-Acetaminophen (5mg-325mg) 1 TAB PO Q4H:PRN Pain - Severe
summary: You were admitted to ___ for nausea and right lower quadrant/flank pain. The pain was right over your transplanted kidney. You also had evidence of worsened acute kidney injury (increased creatinine), which improved with fluids. We did not give you your furosemide, but, after we saw your kidney function improve it was decided you should restart it at one half your normal dose (40mg). You did not have any evidence of kidney stones or urinary tract infection. Some infectious tests, including CMV (cytomegalovirus, which you have had before) and BK virus are not back yet. You stopped having diarrhea on arrival, so we were not able to send stool samples to rule out an infection.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ with PMH of HTN, diabetes, Breast cancer s/p partial L mastectomy, CLL, hypersensitivity pneumonitis causing pulmonary fibrosis (diagnosed in ___, 50 pack year smoking history, emphysema on 4L baseline O2 who presents with shortness of breath and fatigue, found to have pre-capillary pulmonary arterial hypertension by RHC with no findings of systemic inflammatory disease or chronic PE superimposed on hypersensitivity pneumonitis causing cor pulmonale, clinically responsive to Sildenafil and diuresis. # Right heart failure- Pt found to have peripheral edema on ___ ECHO found to have right ventricular cavity is moderately dilated with moderate global free wall hypokinesis. There is abnormal septal motion/position consistent with right ventricular pressure/volume overload. No improvement with diuresis alone. Represents cor pulmonale in setting of PAH as seen on right heart cath. Diuresed well to 20mg IV furosemide BID and discharged on 40mg PO furosemide daily. Most likely worsening hypersensitivity pneumonitis and pulmonary fibrosis given right heart hypomotility worsening from last year and higher pulmonary pressures. With response to NO on right heart cath should have improved hypoxemia. Rheum panel negative and Nuclear lung scan did not show PE or regional compression of lung due to lymphadenopathy. Goals of oxygen are > 93%, discharged on 6LNC to achieve this goal at rest to prevent capillary constriction. Started sildenafil 20 mg TID for pulmonary hypertension and 40mg PO furosemide daily with intent for euvolemia. Presenting symptom of fatigue greatly improved with diuresis. # Chronic lymphocytic leukemia: Followed by observation only. Unclear if symptomatic from CLL, most likely symptomatic from respiratory issues. No infection while inpatient. Recommend outpatient f/u with oncologist. # Bilateral lower leg pain- Pt reporting shooting bilateral lower shin pain R>L. Improved with standing acetaminophen. # Hypertension: On clonidine and atenolol and HCTZ. Pt with vasovagal syncope when restarting atenolol, will hold on discharge. Held HCTZ given diuresis needs with furosemide. Continued home clonidine. #Syncope- Vasovagal in setting of bowel straining and pain from IV magnesium, diuresis. No PE on CTPA. # Diabetes: diet controlled #Panic Attacks- continued home Alprazolam 0.125 mg PO/NG QAM TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: -new medication: sildenafil, for pre-capillary pulmonary hypertension confirmed on right heart catheterization -maintain resting O2 sat >93% to decrease pulmonary vasculature resistance -evidence of worsened lymphadenopathy on CT imaging. Recommend continued evaluation for treatment of CLL as outpatient. -check Chem7 on ___. Pt on higher dose furosemide (40mg) with intention of euvolemia. -Please emphasize importance of recording daily weights, calling PCP if there is a change >3lbs in weight -discharge Cr 1.0 -discharge weight: 61.6kg -Full code -HCP, husband, ___ ___ ___ on Admission: The Preadmission Medication list is accurate and complete. 1. Furosemide 20 mg PO DAILY 2. Omeprazole 20 mg PO DAILY 3. CloniDINE 0.1 mg PO TID 4. Atenolol 50 mg PO DAILY 5. Hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg PO DAILY 6. ALPRAZolam 0.125 mg PO QAM 7. Loratadine 10 mg PO DAILY
summary: You were recently admitted with fatigue and shortness of breath. You were treated with medications to help you urinate off the fluid call diuretics, which you will continue at home. You underwent testing with CT scans, ultrasound of your heart (echocardiography), and a measurement of the pressures in your heart, known as right heart catheterization. Based on the results of this test, you were started on a new medication, sildenafil to help treat your lung disease, which is worsening your heart failure. You are also on a higher dose of furosemide, your water pill. You should continue on home oxygen to maintain a saturation above 93% at rest.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ with history of hypertension, hyperlipidemia, COPD, referred in by PCP after being found in acute heart failure exacerbation and atrial fibrillation with RVR. Prior to admission, patient was started on metoprolol as well as rivaroxaban as an outpatient. ACTIVE ISSUES ============= # Acute Heart Failure with Reduced EF: On admission, patient had new small right pleural effusion. Patient's physical examination was notable for elevated JVD. TTE on ___ showed severe global left ventricular hypokinesis (LVEF = 20 %). Thought to be secondary to tachyarrhythmia in the setting of atrial fibrillation with RVR. However, admission EKG was concerning for old infarct, and the patient should have an ischemic workup as an outpatient. She received 40 mg IV boluses of furosemide with subsequent transition to furosemide 80 mg PO daily at the time of discharge. Also increased dose of metoprolol succinate to 75 daily. Discharged on lisinopril 40 daily. Please consider adding spironolactone as an outpatient. The pt will need a f/u TTE and encouragement to stop smoking. # Atrial Fibrillation: During hospitalization, patient had atrial fibrillation with RVR. She was started on diltiazem gtt due to difficult to control heart rate. This was stopped given concern for low EF. She was transitioned to metoprolol tartrate 50 mg Q6H, however, given continued tachycardia, patient was loaded with digoxin. She underwent TEE/electrical cardioversion on ___ with return to normal sinus rhythm. To prevent return to atrial fibrillation, digoxin was discontinued and she was started on amiodarone during hospitalization. She will continue amiodarone loading 400 mg PO Q12H with end date ___ (one dose on ___, for a total of 6g loading. She will then be on amiodarone 200 mg PO daily starting on ___. As she is on Amiodarone, will need TFTs and PFTs as outpatient. Metoprolol as above, and rivaroxaban was continued at 20 daily. Aspirin stopped as it had been given for primary prevention but she is not anticoagulated with rivaroxaban. CHADSVASc score calculated to be 4. CHRONIC ISSUES: =============== #Hypertension: Lisinopril 40 daily held on ___ in the setting of SBP in ___ but restarted on ___. Home HCTZ and amlodipine were dc'd as BP controlled on solely lisinopril and preferred ACEi as first line agent in HF. #Hyperlipidemia: continued home pravastatin 40 #Depression/Anxiety: continued home citalopram, lorazepam PRN #GERD: continued home omeprazole 20 BID #Fibromyalgia: #Low Back Pain: treated with standing APAP Q8H and Cyclobenzaprine 10 mg PO TID:PRN. #Seasonal Allergies: continued home Fluticasone Propionate NASAL 1 SPRY NU DAILY and DiphenhydrAMINE 25 mg PO TID TRANSITIONAL ISSUES =================== [ ] Discharge Weight: 73.8 kg (162 pounds) [ ] Discharge Creatinine: 1.0. [ ] Please obtain daily weights. [ ] Diuretic Regimen: furosemide 80 mg PO daily. [ ] discuss utility of aspirin as an outpatient. [ ] Please check chemistry at next outpatient appointment. [ ] Please discuss need for spironolactone as outpatient given low EF. [ ] Please obtain repeat TTE within 6 weeks to assess for interval change in EF. [ ] Please perform ischemic evaluation as an outpatient given evidence of heart failure with reduced ejection fraction. [ ] Since patient is on amiodarone, please monitor LFT's and TFT's as an outpatient. **Medication Changes** Added: 1. Amiodarone. 400 mg BID until ___ (one dose on ___, 200 mg daily thereafter 2. Rolling Walker Changed: 1. Metoprolol Succinate XL increased from 25 to 75 mg PO DAILY 2. Furosemide 40 mg PO DAILY to Furosemide 80 mg PO DAILY 3. Potassium Chloride 30 mEq PO DAILY to Potassium Chloride 40 mEq PO DAILY Stopped: 1. amLODIPine 2.5 mg PO DAILY 2. hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg oral 2 tablets PO daily 3. ASA 81 daily
summary: You were admitted to ___ due to shortness of breath. During your hospitalization, you were noted to be in an abnormal rhythm called atrial fibrillation. In order to treat the atrial fibrillation, you underwent a cardioversion. This allowed your rhythm to return to a normal rhythm. You also underwent an ultrasound of your heart called an echocardiogram. This showed that your heart was very weak. It is possible that your heart became this weak due to the heart rhythm you were in. Our hope is that with improvement of your heart rhythm, your heart strength can improve. To prevent fluid buildup in your lungs, you were started on a medication called furosemide. Please take furosemide 80 milligrams EVERY DAY. You will also require potassium supplementation while you are on the furosemide. You were started on a medication called amiodarone. Please continue to take amiodarone 400 millligrams EVERY 12 HOURS with end date ___. On ___, please start taking amiodarone 200 milligrams EVERY DAY. Your blood pressure medications were adjusted as noted below. Your weight at the time of discharge was 162 pounds.

text: Brief Hospital Course: PATIENT SUMMARY =============== ___ with a history of DM2, HTN, HLD, hypothyroidism, and report of "leaky heart valve" on twice a week Lasix who presents with weakness and dyspnea and hypoxic respiratory failure, requiring BiPAP in the ICU. She was found to have evidence of volume overload and new onset systolic heart failure, as well as A.fib with RVR. MICU COURSE ----------- The patient was admitted to the MICU with hypoxia and atrial fibrillation complicated by RVR. Her troponins were negative X 2. The etiology of her hypoxia (O2 sat to the ___, requiring BiPAP) appeared to be volume overload in the setting of a CHF exacerbation. She was diuresed with IV lasix with good effect. She also had sustained atrial fibrillation with RVR, likely worsened by her underlying volume overload. This was managed with metoprolol in the ED but given high rates despite ample metoprolol, diltiazem was started. Her rates improved and she had several liters of fluid diuresed off. She was then transferred to the floor for further management. She also received 2 days of CTX due to c/f UTI but cultures were negative so this was stopped. ACUTE MEDICAL ISSUES ==================== #New Onset Acute Systolic CHF (EF ___: New onset resulting in hypoxic respiratory failure requiring brief BiPAP in the ICU. Unclear precipitant though could be tachyarrhythmia induced cardiomyopathy from a. fib with RVR as noted below. TTE with EF of ___, global left ventricular hypokinesis, normal LV cavity, 3+ TR. Diuresed with IV Lasix gtt/boluses, to dry weight of 96.8 kg/213 lbs. Discharged on 40mg torsemide PO BID. She was also started on metoprolol and lisinopril. #New Afib with RVR: Symptomatic with rates in the 160s+. Patient had difficult to rate control in the ICU and required diltiazem drip and metoprolol. She was amiodarone loaded but continues to be in atrial fibrillation. She was anticoagulated on apixaban 5mg BID. She will be discharged on diltiazem 240 mg XL, metoprolol 200 mg XL for rate control, and amiodarone 400mg po BID, with discharge rates in the 70-90's. Plan is for cardioversion after 3.5 more weeks of anticoagulation. ___: on presentation, Cr 1.5, baseline 0.8. Improved with diuresis with Cr 1.3 on discharge. CHRONIC MEDICAL ISSUES ====================== #Hypothyroidism: home levothyroxine continued #Diabetes: ISS while in house; restarted on oral hypoglycemic TRANSITIONAL ISSUES ===================== DRY WEIGHT: 96.8kg [] Patient will be cardioverted in 3.5 weeks after discharge. This will be set up by Dr. ___ she ___ be admitted to ___. [] TTE: Repeat TTE in 3.5 weeks at hospitalization to reassess systolic function. [] SPIRONOLACTONE: EF is depressed at ___ consider initiating spironolactone [] LABS: Please check BUN, Cr, and K+ on ___ and fax to Dr. ___ office at ___ [] New medications: -Apixiban 5 mg BID -Amiodarone 400 mg BID to end ___ and switch to amiodarone 400 mg daily until admission to ___ -Metoprolol succinate XL 200 mg daily -Diltiazem XL 240 daily -Lisinopril 5 mg daily -Torsemide 40mg po BID # Communication: HCP: ___ (daughter) ___ (H), ___ (c) # Code: Full, confirmed
summary: You were admitted because you had trouble breathing and you were found to have fluid in your lungs because your heart's ability to pump blood was weak. In addition, your heart's rhythm was found to be abnormal. While you were in the hospital, you had an ultrasound of your heart, which showed that it was not pumping properly. You had a chest x ray, which showed that because of your heart, you had fluid in your lungs. You were given medication to remove the extra fluid from your lungs. This made your breathing better. Once the extra fluid was gone, you were given water pills to keep the fluid from coming back. It is important that you continue to take these at home. Your heart's rhythm was abnormal, so you were started on several medicines to slow down your heart rate and control the rhythm. You were also started on a blood thinner that helps prevent stroke for people with rhythms like yours. It is very important you take these every single day exactly as prescribed. When you came in, your kidney function was lower than usual, most likely because of the extra fluid in your body. This improved with the medications to remove fluid.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Mr. ___ is a ___ year old male with Cerebral palsy, mild MR, seizure d/o, systolic CHF, with recent echocardiogram showing worsening Mitral Regurg, mod pulm HTN, and biventricular dysfunction. Patient had catheterization today showing diffuse CAD with elevated PCWP and pulmonary edema. # Systolic Heart Failure: Patient has low EF and elevated PCWP. Patient denied symptoms; however, his exam was positive for diffuse crackles with decreased breath sounds at bases and elevated JVP. He had adequate oxygen saturations on 2L NC, and his BP was stable. He was fluid overloaded on exam and the overall goal was to diurese. He responded well to Lasix 40mg IV. His blood pressure decreased to low-normotensive and his creatinine was 1.2. Low doses of furosemide were adequate to diurese. The patient's outpatient provider should consider starting a beta-blocker pending normal heart rate and blood pressure in the future as the patient has systolic congestive heart failure with a low ejection fraction. . #CAD: Cath showed 3 vessel disease with likely ischemic cardiomyopathy. Last echo showed EF between ___. Patient denies symptoms this admission, but noted years of anginal symptoms in the past. He has been considered for an ICD in the past but deferred given comorbidities and lack of heart failure symptoms at that time. However, since then, has worsened EF and development of sx from HF; could benefit from CRT given widened QRS. Seen by C surg who does not believe he is a candidate for surgery. He underwent PCI on ___ and had successful drug eluting stenting of LMCA and LCx arteries using Tandem Heart support. Also there was balloon angioplasty of the RCA. He will be discharged on dual antiplatelet therapy with clopidogrel and aspirin for his drug eluting stents. . #Acute on Chronic Kidney Failure: Patient presented at baseline creatinine, but had jump to 1.7 from 1.1. Most likely etiology was prerenal secondary to fluid overloaded state and poor CO. Other possibilties included contrast nephropathy, however the bump in creatinine was too soon after the cath to be attributed to the dye. FeUrea is 29%, suggestive of prerenal azotemia. He had a creatinine of 1.2 at time of discharge. . #Seizure disorder: Unknown when last had a seizure. Reportedly refractory to Keppra. In house he was on phenobarbital without incident. . #Osteoarthritis: no current complaints of joint pain. NSAIDs were not given in the setting of aspirin use and need for anitplatelet agents in this patient with new drug eluting stents. Tylenol prn was used for pain control. .
summary: You were admitted to us after an ultrasound evaluation of your heart showed decreased function, in addition to your symptoms of shortness of breath. A diagnostic cardiac catheterization allowed us to evaluate the coronary arteries and showed you had several blockages in your vessels that were causing your symptoms and decreased your heart function. We performed a therapeutic catheteriation procedure placing several stents in your left and right coronary arteries to relieve the blockages. This also required us to hook you up to a heart pump during the procedure that helped circulate your blood during the procedure. All the main blockages were relieved, with no complications.

text: Brief Hospital Course: PATIENT SUMMARY ================== ___ y/o male with a history of cryptogenic cirrhosis c/b GI bleed ___ years ago and varices s/p multiple bandings, essential thrombocytosis, metastatic prostate cancer (___) s/p XRT (ended ___, and prior transverse myelitis who presented from ___ for PVT who underwent lysis/thrombectomy and TIPS procedure in ICU. He was bridged with lovenox to warfarin and discharge to rehab. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES ==================== [ ] New anticoagulation with warfarin goal ___ (lifelong). [ ] New lactulose and rifaximin to prevent hepatic encephalopathy post TIPS procedure. [ ] PTH elevated during admission in the setting of low phosphate. Please continue workup for primary hyperparathyroidism. #Portal vein thrombosis s/p TIPS #Concern for SMV clot Presented with two weeks of abdominal pain, found to have PVT. Etiology likely ___ underlying cirrhosis, essential thrombocytosis, and underlying malignancy with less likely component from prior radiation therapy. Initially started on heparin gtt, then treated with lysis catheter, thrombectomy and TIPS. Imaging before discharge showed patent TIPS with velocities ranging between 50-57 cm/s. He was bridged with lovenox to warfarin for expected lifelong anticoagulation with a goal INR 2.5-3. He was stable on 1 mg warfarin daily on discharge. His nadolol was stopped on discharge since he had a TIPS and it is no longer indicated. #Hypoxia Intubated for ___ procedures and had difficulty weaning due to presumed atelectasis. Slow wean from supplemental oxygen, but was on room air for 3 days preceding discharge. Patient had a previous diagnosis of CHF, O2 requirement and not on home O2. Ddx includes CHF based on clinical symptoms, but echo prior to TIPS from outside hospital and echo with bubble here showed no evidence of CHF. Hypoxia had improved and was thought secondary to atelectasis and volume overload. He was discharged on his home spot dosing of 20 mg furosemide as needed. #Cryptogenic cirrhosis History of cirrhosis with reportedly negative work up. Possibly related to prior ETOH use though not significant. C/b varices requiring recurrent banding. No history of HE or ascites. MELD-Na 17, Childs Class A on admission. Currently not being worked up for transplant. He underwent TIPS as above, and was started on rifaximin and lactulose to prevent encephalopathy. His nadolol was disconintued post TIPS, and he was only on spot dosing of Lasix 20 PO as needed for swelling. He will need q6month screening for HCC. #Transaminitis AST, ALT mildly elevated; Tbili peaked at 5.4. Likely initially ___ PVT and then worsened by serial procedures. On discharge these had normalized, and only alk phos was elevated on day of discharge. #Leukoctyosis Persistently elevated. Likely a stress response from thrombosis. Low concern for true infection. It was stable at 11 on discharge. #Weakness Noted to have diffuse weakness following extubation. History of transverse myelitis but was not weak upon arrival to the hospital. Low concern for intracranial etiology. CK mildly elevated but not consistent with rhabdo and has since resolved. Suspect ___ hospitalization/intubation, ICU myopathy as well as mild encephalopathy. He was discharged to rehab to improve strength. #Malnutrition #Low phosphate Likely causing low phosphate levels. Nutrition was consulted and said that tube feeds were a possibility, however the patient preferred to increase his oral intake. He was started on PO phos repletion as well as thiamine and vitamins. #Concern for primary hyperparathyroidism PTH elevated during this admission in the setting of low phosphate. Plan to continue workup as an outpatient CHRONIC ISSUES ============== #Essential thrombocytosis Presented on ASA and hydroxyurea. Held aspirin indefinitely given need for ongoing anticoagulation. Continued home hydroxyurea. Has follow up with hematologist.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital because you had a clot in one of the veins by your liver. You had a procedure done to remove the clot from your vein. You then had a TIPS (transjugular intrahepatic portosystemic shunt) procedure so that blood passes over your liver instead of through your liver. This means that your liver cannot process toxins as well as before. You were started on a new blood thinner medication (warfarin also called Coumadin) that prevents clots. You will need to take this for the rest of your life. You were started on few new medications that will cause you to have more bowel movements. This will help get rid of toxins and prevent confusion since your liver cannot filter toxins very well any more after the TIPS. You improved and were ready to leave the hospital.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ yo F with hx of afib not on coumadin, hypothyroid who presented with dyspnea and volume overload, course complicated by occult upper GI bleed, pneumonia, flash pulmonary edema, and hypotension. # Hypoxia/dyspnea: Multifactorial, Patient's hypoxia/dyspnea was cotributed to by an exacerbation of CHF, and also healthcare associated pneumonia, as discussed below. # Acute on chronic diastolic heart failure exacerbation: Patient arrived on the floor and appeared to be volume overloaded. She was given IV diuresis, placed on 2g sodium and 1.5L fluid restriction and her symptoms initially improved. On hospital day 2 she developed worsening anemia as discussed below, and developed flash pulmonary edema ___ the setting of her 3rd unit of pRBCs, requiring further aggressive diuresis and brief CCU stay. She was started on 20mg PO furosemide daily and was felt to be euvolemic on this at the time of discharge. Discharge weight 53kg. # Bacterial pneumonia: On hospital day 4 patient developed copious purulent sputum and leukocytosis. CXR showed pulmonary edema but no discrete consolidation. Sputum culture grew moraxella catarrhalis and she was narrowed from vancomycin/cefepime to augmentin for a total 7 day course (will finish ___, and her sputum decreased and leukocytosis resolved. # Upper GI bleed: Hemoglobin dropped from 7 to 5 (acute on chronic anemia) on second hospital day, confirmed on recheck. She was noted to have marroon-orange guaiac positive stools and required a total of 4 units of pRBC, with infusion of the ___ unit complicated by transfusion-related cardiac overload as discussed above. GI was consulted and she was started on a PPI. Endoscopy was not felt to be within goals of care per discussion with family. # Hypotension: Patient developed hypotension to the ___ and was minimially responsive ___ the setting of aggressive diuresis for flash pulmonary edema. This improved without intervention, and she was able to return to the floor from the ICU within a few hours. # Acute delirium: A week into her hospitalization patient became intermittently more agitated, frequently self-dialoging and tangential thinking, this was also documented on prior discharge summaries. Infectious work up was negative except for pneumonia as below and etiology felt to be primarily hospital-induced delirium. She started to improve somewhat after unteathering (removal of telemetry and foley catheter). # Atrial fibrillation: Currently rate controlled. Not on warfarin due to prior GI bleeds. Continued aspirin. Did not start beta blocker as rate already <110, no known coronary disease, and no proven indication for patients with diastolic heart failure. # Hyponatremia: Likely hypervolemic hyponatremia. Resolved with diuresis. # Hypothyroidism: continued levothyroxine # Goals of care: Given patient's advanced heart failure and age, as well as her previously expressed wishes, aggressive or heroic measures are not within this patients goals of care. When the patient developed flash pulmonary edema and had to go to the ICU for several hours for hypoxia and hypotension, a family meeting was held and code status was transitioned from full to DNR/DNI, ok for ICU only if needs Bipap while diuresing, no endoscopy or pressors. HCP is ___ (___) ___. Transitional Issues: - Patient should see PCP ___ ___ days of discharge from rehab - Last day of Augmentin is ___ - Please weigh patient daily and call MD if increases by more than 3 pounds ___ 3 days
summary: You were admitted to the hospital with shortness of breath and found to have an exacerbation of your heart failure which caused fluid to build up ___ you lungs. We treated you with medications to remove fluid and your symptoms improved. While you were here you developed bleeding ___ your GI tract. This was treated conservatively with acid-reducing medications and blood transfusions and the bleeding appears to have stopped. After one of these transfusions you developed acute worsening of the fluid ___ your lungs,which can be a rare but dangerous side effect of blood transfusion. You required a brief ICU stay for additional support while we were treating this complication. You tolerated your next transfusion well. You were also diagnosed with a pneumonia during this admission, but your infection improved with antibiotics.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Assessment and Plan: ___ year old male with a history of OSA, COPD and RV failure presents with syncopal episode in setting of having a bowel movement. . # Syncope - Occurred in the setting of a bowel movement, so this most likely represents a vasovagal episode. He has had a prolonged PR in the past, as well as a high degree AV block, however none of these findings are apparent on EKG. cardiology EP was consulted and felt EKG was consistent with Aflutter w/ variable block, unrelated to syncopal episodes. He was ruled out for MI. He was monitored on telemtry, which demonstrated Aflutter with variable block. Started on ranitidine, and discontinued omeprazole given it's drug-drug interaction with celexa (QTc prolongation) . # Altered mental status - The patient's altered mental status was likely due to a UTI. His hypercarbia was not very profound on blood gas; HCO3- is also not too elevated suggesting that he may not be a chronic CO2 retainer; this mild elevation in CO2 may thus be contributing to his mental status but seems too low to be the sole explanation. The +UA (had >180 WBCs in urine) and has grown out pan-senstive pseudomonas (now on Cipro). Improved with initally ceftriaxone and vanco, which have no been narrowed to cipro 500 BID. . # Respiratory distress - The respiratory status was due to volume overload. The patient was diuresis 5L length of stay and is now marketly improved. He was restarted on torsemide 40mg daily. His weight should be obtained daily. His dry weight on discharge was 242 lbs. He was instructed to take an extra 20mg of torsemide daily if his weight is greater than 245. Given his right sided heart failure, imdur is a poor choice in medication and was stopped. His ace inhibitor was held given his ___. He was discharge on his baseline of 2L of supplemental O2 via nasal canual. He will need to continue his CPAP at night. . # Hypertension - At home is on ACE inhibitor, metoprolol, and imdur. As noted above, Imdur not the best choice given his RV failure. His HTN may have led to flash pulmonary edema. He was also volume overloaded and he was aggressively diuresis and his blood pressure improved. He was normotensive on metoprolol and lisinopril 10mg (lower dose) was restarted at discharge since his kidney function was near baseline (1.8, baseline 1.6-1.7) # Urinary tract infection - Significant pyuria on admission. Grew >100,000 colonies of pan-senstive pseudomonas. The pateint was initially started on ceftriaxone and vanco, but was narrowed to Cipro 500mg BID for a 14 day course. He will need 13 additional days after discharge. . # Renal failure - Decreased renal function was likely secondary to poor forward flow in the setting of RV failure and volume overload. He was aggressively diuresis and was -5L length of stay and his lisinopril was held. His returned to near baseline at 1.8 (baseline 1.6-1.7) at the time of discharge. . # Dementia - Continue aricept, ropinarole, and celexa throughout hospital course
summary: You were admitted for fainting. The fainting was likely due to a condition called vasovagal syncope. You can get this from straining while trying to use the bathroom. We have started medications to soften your stools and stopped the iron supplements, which can make you constipated. We also found that you had too much fluid in your body, which was caused by your heart failure. We have adjusted your medications to improve your heart function. You should have your weight obtained every day and if your weight is greater than 245 lbs, you need to take an one dose torsemide 20mg by mouth in addition to your daily dose of torsemide.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ PMH CKD s/p renal transplant, DM2, CAD, p/w 3 days onset of dyspnea in the setting of medication non-compliance and dietary indiscrestion, transferred to the CCU for presumed CHF exacerbation. Active Issues: # CONGESTIVE HEART FAILURE. Likely new onset CHF given DOE with pulmonary edema on CXR and elevated BNP, which was likely caused by medication non-compliance and and dietary indiscretions. Acute ischemia which is a concern given her evalulated troponins, though EKG unremarkable for ST elevations but some depressions in V5 and V6. The patient was diuresed with IV furosemide and IV nitroglycerin gtt. She was admitted to the MICU for further evaluation, as etiology included possible infectious sources as well (which were ruled out). She was diuresed in the MICU with IV furosemide as well as nitroglycerin gtt. She required CPAP to maintain SpO2 for a short time (<1 day), when she was weaned to O2 NC. She was transferred to the CCU for management of CHF exacerbation. She was diuresed, weaned from O2, and started on PO Lasix. # TROPINEMIA. Likely demand ischemia in the setting of CKD. EKG not remarkable for STEMI though ST depression in V5 and V6 in the distrubution in the prior MI. The patient was without chest pain and remained hemodynamically stable. Her cardiac enzymes were trended to peak (CK-MB max 12, Trop 0.16, 0.23, 0.25 and serial EKGs were monitored. # FEVER. The patient was found to be febrile on ___ to Tm 101.5F, with no complaints of abdominal pain, dysuria, chest pain, or worsening of shortness of breath. APAP 1g PO was given and the fever resolved. Blood cultures were collected and showed no growth CXR performed showed interval improvement in pulmonary edema & pleural effusions, however infiltrate was not able to be ruled out. CBC did not demonstrate leucocytosis. UA was clean and culture was sent as well. Her physical exam was unchanged, though she did complain of dry cough. She also received influenza vaccination today. As the patient's fever broke to APAP and she appeared clinically stable, fever was attributed to immune response to vaccination. Uruine and blood culture final results were negative Chronic Issues: # DIABETES MELLITUS, TYPE II. Patient on Lantus and ISS humalog at home. Continued home insulin regimen. # CHRONIC KIDNEY DISEASE. Stage III per OMR records. Creatine is near baseline. Tacrolimus level was checked and found to be therapeutic. Tacrolimus, TMP-SMX SS and MMF were continued. Her Tacrolimus dose was decreased to 3 mg bid upon discharge, with follow up lab work scheduled for ___. # HYPERLIPIDEMIA. Will start atorvastain 80 mg in stead of paravastatin. She was changed back to Pravastatin upon discharge # HYPERTENSION. Home antihypertensives were held while the patient was on IV nitroglycerin gtt and restarted once stable. She was discharged on her home regimen # ANEMIA. Appears to be at her baseline. Likely chronic given history CKD. CBCs were trended daily. # CORONARY ARTERY DISEASE. Her ASA was continued, but metoprolol was held in the setting of CHF until the patient was recovered. She was transitioned back to her home Metoprolol dose prior to DC. # ATRIAL FIBRILLATION: Ms ___ developed asymptomatic paroxysmal aftrial fibrialltion with ventricular rates briefly to the 120's. This was possibly exacerbated by initially holding her BB and then resuming it at less than home dose. She was discharged on her full home dose of Metoporol Succ 200 mg daily. She was also started on Coumadin 5 mg. Her INR will be followed by her PCP, ___.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital with an exacerbation of your heart failure because your Lasix was not taken as prescribed and your diet contained a lot of salt while on vacation. Initially, you were diuresed with IV Lasix and required supplemental oxygen. Over 24 hrs, your symptoms improved and you were weaned from the oxygen. You also had a fever, which resolved after several hrs. No source of infection was found. Please weigh yourself everyday at the same time and using the same scale. Report a weight gain of ___ lbs over several days to your cardiologist. Follow a low salt diet. ___ services were suggested to help you monitor your weight, diet and medications, but you declined these services at this time. You also developed an abnormal heart rhythm called atrial fibrillation. This rhythm can increase your risk of a stroke. Because of this, you were started on a blood thinner called warfarin. You will need frequent blood checks to make sure that you are on the correct dose of warfarin. Your dose may change depending on the results of this blood test (called an INR). Please obtain blood work on ___ to check your INR level and Tacrolimus level, and kidney function.

text: Brief Hospital Course: #. Volume status: Patient has a history of systolic HF, and is lasix dependent at baseline. Patient appeared total body volume overloaded on initial exam. His elevated creatinine implied that he was intravascularly depleted. He has an oxygen requirement that according to his wife had been present for ~2 months around his recent hospitalizations. Per prior notes, he required IVF boluses for low UOP, and was likely overloaded from that. He responds well to 20mg IV Lasix. Pt. was actively diuresed daily and his creatinine increased significant approximately ___. He was fluid restricted to <1.5L, and his general edema and particularly the edema in his right hand greatly improved. . #. New oxygen requirement: Pt.'s oxygen requirement abated with diuresis, incentive spirometer. Likely a combination of fluid overload and post-operative atelectasis. . #. RUE clot: PICC line out, not needed per surgery. Was placed on Heparin then lovenox bridge to Coumadin which he will need for 3 months with goal INR ___. . #. ___ associated UTI- Has a history of E. coli in ___ resistant to cipro, and bactrim. Also with funguria on UA before and after ___ changed during admission. Pt. was started on cefriaxone ___ and was given 10 day course with resolved UA/Uctx. . #. ARF- Likely intravascularly dry. Held further IVF in the setting of fluid overload. Ulytes inconclusive with FE urea approximately 40%. . #. Distended loops of bowel: No nausea, having BMs and passing gas. Though KUB concerning, asymptomatic. Cdiff negative though had multiple episodes of diarrhea. Abdominal exams were benign over course of stay. . #. DM: BG stable during admission; Continued home glargine/Lispro sliding scale, monitored QACHS fingersticks, diabetic diet. . #. Peripheral vascular disease/left BKA: No active surgical issues, vascular surgery and wound care consult followed. . #. Coronary artery disease: Patient had been discharged on plavix 75mg daily and aspirin 81mg daily. Increased aspirin to full strength as the patient is ___ month s/p a drug-eluting-stent. Changed metoprolol to Toprol XL given survival benefit in acute systolic CHF. Pt. was started on Lisinopril during the hospitalization. -Unable to tolerate a statin . #. Chronic systolic heart failure: Continue Toprol XL as above. . #. Myasthenia ___/ Statin induced myopathy, confirmed with neurologist: Continue Prednisone 5 mg daily and added Cellcept, which the patient should be on per his neurologist. . #. Hypertension: changed from Metoprol tartrate to Toprol XL. . #. Gastro-esophageal reflux disease: Continue home dose of Ranitidine HCl 150 mg daily . #. Hypothyroidism: Continue home dose of Levothyroxine 88 mcg daily . #. chronic anemia- Stable, HCT at baseline, d/c'd ferrous sulfate as labs with high ferritin, low iron showed ACD -may benefit from outpatient epogen . #. BPH- Continue home regimen of tamsulosin . #. Depression- Continue home regimen of venlafaxine . FEN: Diabetic diet, fluid restrict to 1.5 L . PPX: -DVT tx with coumadin -Pain management with home regimen of dilaudid . ACCESS: heparin dependent port . CODE STATUS: Full (confirmed) . Communication: patient, wife ___ ___ ___ on Admission: Tamsulosin 0.4 mg qhs Venlafaxine 75 mg daily Albuterol Sulfate q6H prn Ipratropium nebs q6H prn Docusate Sodium 100 mg BID Ferrous Sulfate 325 mg daily Furosemide 20 mg daily Clopidogrel 75 mg daily Ranitidine HCl 150 mg daily Levothyroxine 88 mcg daily Prednisone 5 mg daily Aspirin 81 mg daily Metoprolol Tartrate 25 mg BID Miconazole Nitrate 2 % Powder as needed Hydromorphone ___ q3H prn pain Insulin Glargine 5 units qhs Lispro sliding scale . . ALLERGIES: Penicillins Hydrocodone Sulfa (Sulfonamide Antibiotics) Haldol (Injection) (Haloperidol Lactate) Vicodin (Oral) (Hydrocodone Bit/Acetaminophen) Morphine Atorvastatin
summary: You were admitted to the hospital because of swelling of your body and fevers. While you were in the hospital you were found to have a urinary tract infection and you were started on antibiotics. This infection was probably what was causing your fever since you subsequently did not have a fever after starting treatment. Your urinary catheter was taken out because this can sometimes increase risks of urinary infections. It was also found that you had a blood clot in your vein near your PICC line so your PICC line was removed and you were started on medicine to keep your blood thin. You will have to be on Coumadin and have your blood levels checked on this medicine for 3 months. You were given lasix multiple times which improved your swelling.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Mr. ___ is a ___ year old male with a history of morbid obesity, non-ischemic cardiomyopathy with an unknown EF, ICD placement for unknown arrhythmia, OSA, who presents with a several day history of dyspnea with heart failure exacerbation. # CCU COURSE ___-: In order to further management his heart failure exacerbation, right heart cath was performed with subsequent transfer to the CCU for further monitoring and treatment of presumed cardiogenic shock. In cath lab, patient's RV pressures were 70/28. PA 71/44/55. PCW 43/44/41. RA ___. Initial MVO2 was 25. In the CCU, patient reported feeling "numb" since have shock on ___. He reported being unable to lie flat. He was started on a nitroprusside drip, and later switched to oral captopril, imdur, hydralazine as an afterload reducing agent in the context of his CHF. He subseqeuntly had imrpoved urinary output and his mixed venous O2 increased to the 60's. For further diuresis, patient was switched to bumex drip from lasix with increasing doses of metolazone as well. By ___ patient could better tolerate lying down flat, and had started to diurese more than his IVF (from bumex drip) and become net negative. Patient was transferred to CCU to general cardiology floor on ___, on regimen of lisinopril, imdur, hydralazine and bumex drip. He did complain of some ankle pain consistent with gout. He continued to diurese well and was transitioned to PO Bumex BID on discharge. Potassium was stable at discharge. # CHF with nonischemic cardiomyopathy s/p AICD: The exact trigger of his CHF decompensation was beleived to be secondary to dieatry nonccompliane and recent URI as reported per patient. He did not present with any focal infectious symptoms. # Ankle Pain: tender R ankle with diuresis, warm to the touch typical of gout. Patient treated with colchicine with effect. # Hyperlipidemia: Continued home rosuvastatin # Depression: Continued home sertraline # OSA: Continued home CPAP # Hypokalemia: aggressive repletion performed with both IV and PO given diuresis. Stabilized and discharged on home regimen. Transitional Issues - If readmission, place on ppx colchicine with diuresis - please consider psych follow-up given psychosocial issues and anxiety - social concerns regarding housing, please assist - multiple new medications/dosing, please ensure adherence - Dry weight: 172.8 prior to discharge
summary: You were admitted for shortness of breath and found to have excess fluid due to your heart failure. You had a right heart catheterization which confirmed this diagnosis. You were placed on medications to help relax your blood vessels as well as diuretics to help aggressively take off fluid. You responded very well to the IV diuretics and were transitioned to diuretics by mouth. Your blood pressure was well controlled on the regimen you began in the hospital. You are starting new medications. You will be starting bumetanide, which is a diuretic to help remove fluid and prevent fluid on your lungs. You will aslo be starting isosorbide dintrate, which will help to relax your blood vessels and prevent fluid movement our of blood vessels. You are being prescribed fluticasone, which is a medication to improve your symptoms of shortness of breath. We have increased your hydralazine dosing to 75mg daily, which will improve your blood pressure. We have also increased your spironolactone, which will take off fluid while maintaining your potassium. Please weigh yourself every morning and record your weight. Please adhere to the diet and fluid restriction below.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ admitted with CHF exacerbation and new slow AFIB, ? infiltrative disease on echo. #. Dyspnea: Pt's one week of dyspnea with weight gain and increased lower extremity edema consistent with CHF exacerbation. Repeat echo this admission showed preserved EF but possible infiltrative process. Symptoms improved initially with diuresis. No obvious triggers although symptoms may have been precipitated by new AF as below. MI ruled out with ___iomarkers (troponin elevated but flat in setting of renal dysfunction, MB normal). Prior to discharge, he received multiple fluid boluses for borderline hypotension so was more volume overloaded again with increased lower extremity edema but had less symptomatic dyspnea. His home lasix dose was increased to 60mg daily to attempt to get him back to his dry weight. He was instructed to check daily weights and call PCP if had weight gain >3 pounds. He will have repeat labs and lytes checked at follow-up visit. Oxygen sats remained mid ___ on room air. #. AF - ECG on admission shows new slow afib with variable conduction and LBBB. CHADs score 3 (age, HTN, CHF) so anticoagulation was started with heparin drip and bridge to coumadin. On admission, it was unclear how long he had been in afib since he was not clearly symptomatic. Atenolol was held as this was though to be contributing to his bradycadia. EP was consulted and recommended TEE cardioversion which was performed during admission. He initially converted to a flutter but then to sinus rhythm, however, he converted back to afib prior to DC. His HR remained bradycardic at night ___ but was mostly in ___ at rest during the day. HR increased to 150s with ambulation. He was started on metoprolol which was uptitrated to 25 po BID which he tolerated well and HR was then similar at rest and 110 with exertion. TSH abnormal but free T4 within normal limits. Enzymes cycled but flat x 3 sets. Per EP, no indication for pacemaker at this time and he will follow up with Dr. ___ as an outpatient. Due to persistent hematuria and HCT drop, heaprin drip was stopped and he was discharge ___ low dose coumadin with subtherapeutic INR. #. ? Infiltrative disease on echo: Pt had elevated filling pressures and relatively low voltages on ECG c/w ? amyloidosis or infiltrative disease. He had fat pad biopsy to rule out amyloidosis which was negative. SPEP and UPEP were also checked to evaluate for multiple myeloma and SPEP showed very faint monoclonal IgG band that could not rule out bence ___ protein. Free kappa and lambda chains were subsequently ordered per lab recommendations and were pending at time of discharge but returned as borderline abnormal at the time of this summary. He should have repeat testing and follow up of this as an outpatient for further characterization. #. Hematuria: Patient had persistent hematuria while on heparin drip with HCT drop. Heparin gtt was stopped and his urine cleared prior to discharge. ___ was removed and he did not have any urinary obstruction. # Acute on chronic renal failure: (baseline creatinine 1.5). Cr elevated on admission, 1.8-1.9, improved with diuresis to 1.5, then back up to 1.8-1.9. Given volume overload on exam, likely secondary to decreased renal perfusion with CHF as well as decreased perfusion with borderline hypotensive episodes. # CAD: MI Ruled out. He did not have any complaints of chest pain during admission. he was continued on aspirin, statin, ASA 81 daily. # HTN - Patient was normotensive to hypotensive with SBPs ___ during admission. Atenolol was held on admission due to impaired clearance in renal failure and bradycardia. Valsartan was discontinued prior to DC to allow for uptitration of metoprolol for HR control. SBP ___ at discahrge and pt asymptomatic without dizziness or lightheadedness. # BPH - continue terazosin # CODE STATUS: FULL
summary: You were admitted to the hospital with shortness of breath. This was likely because your heart was not functioning well and you had fluid in your lungs. We gave you IV doses of Lasix which improved your symptoms. You also were in an irregular heart rhythm called atrial fibrillation, which may have contributed to your symptoms. We started you on blood thinning medications to prevent blood clots and strokes and your heart was converted to a regular rhythm. Subsequently, your heart was back in an abnormal rhythm so it is important for you to stay on the blood thinning medications. The level of this medication will need to be closely followed by your primary care physician.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ year female with metastatic colon cancer s/p resection now on palliative chemotherapy who presented with cholangitis 2 weeks after irinotecan treatment. # Cholangitis, Nausea with vomiting, RUQ pain: She presented with elevated bilirubin, nausea, vomiting, and right upper quadrant pain felt to be consistent with cholangitis. She was treated with cipro/flagyl and IV fluids. Blood cultures were drawn and negative to date. She underwent ERCP on ___ and was found to have biliary stricture in the distal common bile duct and stent placement and sphincterotomy were performed. She tolerated the procedure well and diet was advanced afterwards without complication. She will complete a 10 day course of cipro/flagyl. Her last day of abx therapy will be ___. # Falls, Right leg weakness: She reported multiple falls prior to admission and was found to have mild right hip flexor weakness on exam. She also endorsed right sided radicular pain down her leg to her knee. She had normal rectal tone and no sensory deficits. MRI lumbar spine was obtained which did not show any metastatic disease in her spine, but did show some foraminal narrowing at L2-L3 due to her scoliosis that may be causing her symptoms. # Hypoxia, acute on chronic CHF (diastolic): She was initially given IV fluids for cholangitis and developed mild SOB and an oxygen requirement. She responded to diuresis with IV lasix after CXR revealed pulmonary edema. She was eventually restarted on her home lasix dose and remained on room air. # Diarrhea: She had loose bowel movement on admission which improved but did not entirely cease. C diff negative. It was felt to be potentially antibiotic related # Metastatic colon cancer to liver, weight loss: She was due for her next dose of chemotherapy during her hospital stay but given her other issues, this was deferred. She will follow up with her oncologist shortly. # Hypertension: Continued her home beta blocker, lasix (as above), and ___ # CAD/Afib: Records were obtained from her PCP and she has a h/o CAD and atrial fibrillation. She was continued on her home beta blocker, aspirin, and amiodarone. # Right knee pain: She intermittently complained of right knee pain. Previous xray was consistent with arthritis vs pseudogout. It was recommended that she follow-up with rheumatology vs orthopedics in the outpatient setting. # Cataracts: S/p recent left eye cataract surgery. Continued BID prednisolone eye drops. # Anorexia: One dose of dexamethasone 2 mg was given, with no effect. Patient would benefit from nutrition consult and consideration of appetite stimulant if appropriate.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital with nausea, vomiting, and pain and were found to have an obstruction in one of the ducts leading to your liver. You were treated with antibiotics. You underwent a procedure called an ERCP and a stent was placed in the duct to keep it open. You were tolerating a regular diet after the procedure. In addition, you required oxygen for a short period of time and were given a diuretic to help remove fluid from your lungs. You also had right back, hip and leg pain and had an MRI which showed that your pain is likely caused by some narrowing of the bones in your spine. You can continue pain medications for this pain. We also recommend that you see a rheumatologist for your knee pain. You also reported poor appetite and fatigue. This may be related to cancer and deconditioning. During your rehabilitation, you should focus on regaining strength as well as improving nutrition and appetite. A nutrition consultation with a nutritionist at the ___ will be recommended. Nutrition supplementation should be considered. IMPORTANT PATIENT DETAILS 1.

text: Brief Hospital Course: SUMMARY: ========== Ms. ___ is a ___ year old with past medical history significant for RA, HTN, T2DM, HLD, active smoker, prior penetrating aortic ulcer status post TEVAR c/b subsequent type B dissection and repeat TEVAR who is presented with dyspnea on exertion, DOE, PND, and chest pressure concerning for acute heart failure exacerbation. She had a TTE which showed an LVEF of 40% from 62% in ___, and underwent cardiac catheterization which showed diffuse non-obstructive disease with no interventions performed. She was diuresed with IV Lasix with improvement in her respiratory status and transitioned to PO. Her lisinopril was increased and her metoprolol was changed to carvedilol to help with her persistent elevated blood pressure. She was discharged with cardiology follow up and extensive consuleing about weight monitoring, salt restriction, and smoking cessation. ACUTE ISSUES ============ #Acute heart failure with borderline reduced ejection fraction: #Diffuse 2 vessel CAD: Patient presented with shortness of breath at rest and with exertion, ___, and weight gain. Exam consistent with volume overload. Elevated BNP also suggestive of heart failure. Initial ischemic workup negative without clear inciting factor other than dietary indiscretions. Echo with evidence of reduced EF to 40% from ~55-60% in ___ with new wall motion abnormalities. Cardiac cath with long moderate disease in RCA and distal LCx in a co-dominant system. Both would require long stents. Patient had previously stated her wish to avoid PCI and possible issues with compliance to DAPT. As such, it was decided to medically treat her disease for now. If she has any progressive symptoms, plan is to proceed with PCI. She was diuresed with 40mg IV Lasix daily for 3 days and then switched to 40 mg PO lasix for maintenance diuresis (LVEDP 22 at the time of switch) with a discharge weight of 162 lbs. Lisinopril was increased to 40 mg PO daily and metoprolol was substituted for carvedilol 12.5 mg BID. Her atorvastatin was also increased to 80 mg PO daily given her CAD. CHRONIC ISSUES ============== #HTN -Lisinopril as above -Carvedilol as above #HLD -Increased atorvastatin to 80MG qPM #DM - Home DM meds held while inpatient, restart on discharge #DM neuropathy -Continued home gabapentin #Tobacco use disorder -Continued to encourage cessation, especially in light of now known CAD. #Rheumatoid arthritis -Resume home humira on discharge -Continued home Vicodin 7.5/325 Q6H:PRN, acetaminophen 1000mg Q8H prn -Continued home lidocaine patch #EtOH use disorder No h/o withdrawals, no drinks for past several days -Thiamine and folate supplementation -Encourage cessation TRANSITIONAL ISSUES =================== [ ] Please recheck a Chem7 at PCP appointment as patient's lisinopril was increased and she was newly started on furosemide this admission. [ ] Continue to monitor blood pressures, have remained elevated in 140-150s this admission despite medication uptitration. Continue to uptitrate carvedilol as tolerated. She may require additional agents. [ ] Continue education about the need for low sodium diet and fluid restriction. Also continue to consuel on smoking cessation, patient currently says that she might be able to cut down but isn't planning on quitting yet. [ ] Continue to reinforce good control of patient's type II diabetes. Discharge Weight: 162 lb Discharge Cr: 0.6
summary: You were admitted to the hospital because you had been feeling short of breath and you were found to have fluid in your lungs. This was felt to be due to a condition called heart failure, where your heart does not pump hard enough and fluid backs up into your lungs. You were given a diuretic medication through the IV to help you urinate the fluid out. You had a procedure called a cardiac catheterization which showed that you have some narrowing of the arteries in your heart, but none that were bad enough to require stenting. We adjusted your medications and you breathing improved considerably.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Primary Reason for Hospitalization: Mr. ___ is a ___ year old man with h/o OSA, HTN, HLD, who was transferred from ___ ___ with hypercarbic respiratory distress, s/p intubation, and presented to the MICU. He was started on Levofloxacin for pneumonia, and CPAP at night for obstructive sleep apnea. He was evaluated by physical therapy, with recommendations for rehabilitation. . #. Hypercarbic/Hypoxic respiratory failure: Patient with initial hypoxia on presentation to the OSH ED, with quick improvement on ___. He then acutely decompensated while on FiO2 75% on CPAP. Given his underlying sleep apnea, he may have been over oxygenated, causing the patient to lose his respiratory drive and become obtunded. Could also have component of obesity hypoventilation as well. There was also concern for CHF, as patient had had several weeks of increasing ___ edema. TWI and ST changes on EKG suggest possible coronary event, but CK remain flat at this time. TTE showed intact systolic function. Given concern for viral infection, he was initially started on oseltamivir, but this was discontinued once viral screen returned as negative. He treated with levofloxacin, with planned 7-day course. His oxygenation and mental status improved after intubation, but he required very high PEEPs. He diuresed well with IV lasix in the MICU. His viral antigen screen was negative for flu, so oseltamivir was stopped. Sputum cultures were negative. He was extubated on ___. OSH records of a sleep study were obtained showing severe sleep apnea. He endorsed a history of severe daytime sleepiness prior to admission. He was started on nighttime CPAP. On transfer to the floors, pulmonary was consulted. He had PFT's which showed mild mixed obstructive and restrictive defect. ABG showed mildly elevated CO2. MIP's were low, but no evidence of skeletal weakness otherwise. . #. Chest pain: On admission, patient had T-wave inversions and ST depressions inferolaterally, mildly improved since initial OSH EKG. Trop and CK flat at OSH. Trop 0.06 here, but CK/MB remained flat. Troponin leak was likely due to fluid overload and respiratory failure. He had no more chest pain on the medicine floors. . #. Acute Renal Failure: Cr 1.6 at OSH, improved to 1.2 here on admission. Creatinine was trended, and was 1.2 on the day of discharge. Lisinopril was initially held, but was restarted prior to discharge. Transitional issues: 1. CODE: FULL 2. Medical management: - Start CPAP at night 3. Pending studies: - Final PFT's interpretation pending at time of discharge 4. Follow-up: - Pulmonary as an outpatient - Sleep as outpatient - Repeat sleep study as outpatient - Repeat PFT's as an outpatient in ___ weeks
summary: You were admitted to the hospital for shortness of breath and confusion and were found to have a low blood oxygen level and a high blood carbon dioxide level. These required you to be intubated to help you breathe and you stayed in the ICU. Your breathing improved and we were able to take you off the ventilator and eventually back to room air. We feel that your obstructive sleep apnea plays a role in why this happened, but we will have to sort out the exact cause for this event with a pulmonologist and sleep specialist. In the hospital we did lung function tests and blood tests. These showed that your level of carbon dioxide was slightly high but improved and that your lungs show slight impedement of air movement both in and out. This could be related to your acute illness and should be followed up with repeat testing in ___ weeks and with evaluation as an oupatient with a sleep pulmonologist and a ___ pulmonologist.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ with hx IDDM, HFpEF, HTN, OSA, severe anxiety, presenting with fever and left foot swelling found to have bacteremia due to left third toe infection now s/p partial toe amputation for source control. # STREP SEPSIS: # TOE INFECTION: Patient transferred from ___ with gram positive cocci ___ blood. Speciation of the blood culutres showed group-b strep. His toe wound was cultured that showed group-b strep and MRSA. The patient was treated with vancomycin given MRSA (+) culture. Source control was achieved by partial amputation of his third toe (pathology pending). Blood cultures with GBS. Wound cultures GBS and Staph A. ___ this setting foot wound is likely source of entry.
