All blood cultures at ___ were negative. Patient was seen by infectious disease who arranged for outpatient IV antibiotics. The patient received a PICC line. He was discharged on vancomycin IV 1250mg Q12H. Projected End Date: ___ (4wks total). Follow-up by OPAT will be arranged after discharge by the OPAT program. Patient will need the following weekly labs: CBC with differential, BUN, Cr, Vancomycin trough, CRP. # Hypoxemia # Pulmonary Edema: # CHF: Patient developed pulmonary edema and hypoxemia during his hospitalization. A chest Xray showed pulmonary edema. The pulmonary edema and hypoxemia was likely secondary to his home torsemide being held ___ the setting of sepsis. He was hemodynamically stable so he was given IV Lasix with adequate diuresis. His oxygen requirement improved from 6L at its maximum to room air prior to discharge. He was continued on his home Torsemide 40mg BID. Discharge weight: 296.3 lb. # Anxiety: Continued home klonipin TID. Ativan QHS was given as needed for anxiety. #CVD risk: - Increased atorvastatin to 40mg from 20mg - Continued ASA #CKD: his baseline Cr is 1.2-1.4. Was at his baseline during hospitalization. Discharge creatinine: 1.1. #T2DM: Last A1C 8.6% ___ ___. Was continued on his Humalog ___ 100u TID. He had multiple episodes of low blood glucose at night to 60-70. The episodes were asymptomatic. Discharged on reduced dose of 100u, 70u, 80u, at breakfast, lunch and dinner respectively. #Venous stasis: - Continued home saran #OCD: continued fluoxetine and clonazepam with hold parameters. #GERD: continued omeprazole #OSA: Continued CPAP #Glaucoma: - continued home eye drop regimen, except those that are nonformulary. - Continued home methazolamide =================== TRANSITIONAL ISSUES =================== OUTPATIENT ANTIBIOTICS: [ ] Vancomycin 1250mg IV q12h Start Date: ___ Projected End Date: ___ (4wks) [ ] WEEKLY LABS: CBC with differential, BUN, Cr, Vancomycin trough, [CRP] ALL LAB RESULTS SHOULD BE SENT TO : ATTN: ___ CLINIC - FAX: ___ [ ] FOLLOW UP APPOINTMENTS: ID OPAT will arrange DIURETIC REGIMEN: Torsemide 40mg BID DISCHARGE WEIGHT: 296.3 lb (standing weight) MEDICATIONS CHANGED: [ ] Atorvastatin increased to 40mg from 20mg [ ] Gabapentin increased from QHS to TID dosing ITEMS FOR FOLLOW-UP: [ ] Follow-up final pathology from bone fragment [ ] Patient should be partial weight bearing on left lower extremity until specified otherwise from his podiatrist [ ] Wound care, Site: toe, Type: Surgical, Cleansing agent: Commercial cleanser, Dressing: Gauze - dry, change every other day [ ] Follow-up blood glucose and if hypoglycemia, contact PCP to adjust insulin dosing [ ] Leukocytosis: Patient with WBC ___ prior to discharge. No localizing symptoms or fevers. Please monitor patient for fevers or other localizing symptoms of infection. If he has diarrhea, low threshold to test for CDiff. Name of health care proxy: ___ Phone number: ___ Code: Full Code
summary: You were admitted to ___ ___ because you were found to have bacteria growing ___ your blood. You also had an infection of a toe on your left foot. The bacteria ___ your blood was thought to be from your toe infection, so you had part of your left third toe removed. Since you had bacteria ___ your blood, you will need to be on antibiotics for a total of four weeks. The antibiotics will need to be given through your vein so you were given something called a "PICC" line. This is a line that you will have ___ your arm until after your antibiotics are completed. For your toe, it is important that you try and only put your weight on your heel when standing up. You will be able to stand on your feet normally once your toe heals.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ with newly diagnosed non-ischemic CMP (EF 10%), Type II DM, and hyperlipidemia who presents with worsening DOE, orthopnea, PND, found go be fluid overloaded 14 pounds above dry weight, admitted for acute on chronic HFrEF exacerbation for RHC. ACTIVE ISSUES: #Heart failure with reduced ejection fraction: Presented volume overloaded at 78.5kg, dry weight thought to be 73kg. Initially put on Lasix gtt at 10mg/hr with 80mg IV bolus leading to good urine output. He was transitioned to 20mg torsemide daily (home medication was 40mg PO Lasix) when his pressures became soft and clinically felt euvolemic. Right heart cath showed RA 5, PA 35/15/24, wedge 10, CI 2.52, CO 4.89. On the day of discharge his home metoprolol succinate 12.5mg daily was restarted, and that morning his BP dropped to 87 / 63, associated with nausea and lightheadedness. ECG was unchanged. His pressure improved over the next hour and his symptoms resolved. He was discharged off metoprolol with plan to re-evaluate at follow-up with cardiology. Discharge weight: 70.9 kg. Discharge medications: torsemide 20mg daily, lisinopril 2.5mg daily. #Syncope: Syncopal event prior to admission concerning for ventricular arrhythmia. This admission he was fitted for and discharged with a Life Vest. Plan was to continue goal-directed therapy for 3 months before considering ICD implantation. #Transaminitis: RUQUS no evidence of cholelithiasis or intra-hepatic pathology. Hb Core Ab positive, surface Ab negative. Hepatitis surface antigen pending at time of discharge. Hepatitis C ab negative. Improving and almost within normal limits at time of discharge TRANSITION ISSUES: []Started on 20mg tosremide in place of previous 40mg PO Lasix []hepatitis B surface antigen pending at time of discharge. Primary team to follow and alert PCP of result []metoprolol held on discharge (see above for details), consider restarting if BPs tolerate, as directed by cardiology []Discharged with life vest. Consider ICD in future after 3 months of goal-directed therapy []Repeat LFTs in ___ weeks to ensure resolution DISCHARGE WEIGHT: 70.9kg
summary: We changed your water pill from Lasix to torsemide. You had a right heart catheterization which measured the pressures in the chambers of the heart. This procedure showed that your chamber pressures were relatively normal and that you had did not have any extra fluid on the body. We fitted you with a Life Vest, which will sense abnormal heart rhythms that might be fatal, and shock your heart to get it out of the abnormal rhythm. You should wear this at all times. We tried restarting your metoprolol on the day of discharge, but your blood pressure got too low and you had symptoms of low blood pressure. You should NOT take metoprolol until speaking to your cardiologist.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ with HFpEF, known moderate MR, transferred from ___ ___ for mgmt of hypoxemia and heart failure as well as workup of bilateral hilar enlargement with possibility and workup of cardiac sarcoidosis. # HFpEF: On transfer, patient still volume overloaded based on CXR as well as with hypoxemia and elevated JVP. Was initially diuresed with lasix gtt at 20mg/hr. ECHO revealed slightly diminished EF 45-50% on ___ and CT imaging concerning for partially calcified mediastinal and bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy compatible with granulomatous disease such as sarcoid, TB or mycobacterial infection. These lymph nodes are amenable to transbronchial biopsy. Fluid status improved on Lasix gtt and then pt transitioned to Lasix 80mg daily with appropriate urine output/fluid balance. The patient's losartan was halved to 25mg daily during this hospitalization given low blood pressures. SBPs at discharge between 89-low 100s/ 50s-60s. Initially difficult for patient to lie flat given respiratory distress. Interventional pulmonology decided to tap the pleural fluid given large unilateral effusion on CT imaging. Pt tolerated procedure well and had improved her breathing status. Pleural fluids showed transudative and cytology pending. No e/o infection in the pleural fluid. Pt was able to complete the MRI given she was able to lie flat for long enough and the MRI final read is pending at discharge. This will be followed up outpatient. Pt will also get a EBUS/lymph node biopsy for sarcoid diagnosis on ___ and PET scheduled for ___. # Possible Sarcoid: Patient with CT chest ___ with "bilateral pleural effusion, stigmata of old granulomatous disease, mediastinal and bilateral hilar adenopathy with central calcification". Her echocardiogram demonstrated LVEF 45-50% with an inferior wall motion abnormality despite recently normal coronary angiography. Her constellation of mediastinal/hilar lymphadenopathy, mildly reduced LVEF, wall motion abnormality without obstructive coronary artery disease, frequent PVCs, and LBBB are all highly suggestive of cardiac sarcoid. The read of her cardiac MRI is pending at the time of discharge. She will get an outpatient endobronchial lymph node biopsy and cardiac PET CT. # Hilar adenopathy: IP c/s as above, concern for sarcoid See above. # Pulmonary HTN: Transpulmonary gradient of 7 mmHg based on ___ RHC making Class II (LV) pulmonary hypertension, her diastolic heart failure, the overwhelmingly likely etiology. Asthma/lung disease may contribute. She was diuresed until establish new dry weight at 86.6kg, consider sleep study for presumed OSA which may be contributing as outpt. Sarcoid workup as above # Asthma: Continued on home inhalers # HTN: Continued on home losartan and metoprolol # HLD: continued on home statin *****TRANSITIONAL ISSUES***** Transitional Issues: ==================== -Plan for EBUS with lymph node biopsy on ___ with interventional pulmonary -Follow-up Cardiac MRI final read -Plan for Cardiac-PET on ___ at 8am for further work-up of cardiac sarcoid -Decreased dose of Losartan to 25mg daily in the setting of hypotension -Follow-up with EP for consideration of ICD placement pending sarcoid work-up -Follow-up with ___ clinic on ___ -Discharged on Lasix 80mg PO daily -Discharge weight: 86.4kg -Discharge Cr: 0.9 -Code: Full -Contact: ___ ___
summary: You were admitted for the shortness of breath you were experiencing. Upon admission, a chest xray showed fluid in your lungs concerning for an acute exacerbation of your heart failure. You were given medication through the IV and underwent a procedure called a thoracentesis to help remove the fluid. A CT scan of your lungs showed that you have enlarged lymph nodes, which will need to be biopsied by the interventional pulmonary team. In addition, an echo of your heart showed decreased pumping function with concern that there may be infiltration of the heart tissue due to a condition called sarcoid. To help evaluate for this condition, you underwent an MRI whose read was pending at time of discharge. You will also need a PET scan of your heart to help determine if there is active inflammation from sarcoid.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ year old woman with PMH significant for Afib with RVR, TR/MR, s/p AVR, preserved LVEF on recent echo, who presented with symptomatic bradycardia and underwent placement of a permanenet pacemaker. . # Rapid Afib with Rate Controle complicated by Symptomatic Bradycardia: Pt presented with afib with RVR and with bradycardic episodes to ___ which were symptomatic and associated with hypotension. There was initially concern about placing a permanent pacemaker given risk of ventricular dyssycnchrony in the setting of mitral and tricuspid valve disease. The benefits were seen to outweigh the risks and pt had PPM placed ___. Metoprolol was subsequently uptitated to 75 q6 and then transitioned to 150mg BID XL. Anticoagulation maintained. Digixin was restarted on ___. . # Flash pulmonary edema: Occurred on admission during bradycardic episodes, likely ___ poor forward flow. When no longer bradycardic, patient was euvolemic with clear lung sounds and no peripheral edema. Discharge weight is 108 pounds. . # ___: Cr of 1.5 on presentation from baseline 0.9-1. This was likely from poor forward flow in the setting of bradycardic episodes. Resolved at discharge. . # UTI: UCX was remarkable for ___ E coli in the context of having a foley catheter. Pt was started on Augmentin (___). . # Leukocytosis: WBC count of 15.9 with neutrophil predominence on presentation. This appears to have been chronic. Pt did not have evidence of PNA. UTI did not correlate with leukocytosis. further workup should be persued as outpatient. . # MR/TR: Seen by cards surgery but pt was not interested in surgical intervention. . # Chronic dCHF: Lisinopril and spironolactone were held during admission. Pt received daily diuresis on most days of admission. Lisinopril was restarted at lower dose but pt was unable to tolerate this medication ___ hypotensive episodes. It was determined that lisinopril not indicated b/c diastolic CHF and was discontinued. . # HTN: lisinopril, spironolactone held in the setting of low BPs. Spironolactone restarted. . # HLD: Continued simvastatin . # S/p AVR: Pt was anticoagulated with coumadin and/or heparin gtt. . # Hypercalcemia: Elevated early during this admission, then improved. Ca elevated and PTH elevated. Consider this may be primary hyper PTH. . # Diarrhea: Pt developed diarrhea. C diff neg. Subsequently improved. This was likely ___ antibiotic use in setting of UTI. . # Hyponatremia: Likely hypovolemic hyponatremia. Resolved with hydration. . >> Transitional issues: # CODE: FULL (confirmed) # CONTACT: patient, ___ (daughter) ___ # Augmentin x7 (___) for tx of UTI - Consider possibility of primary hyperparathyroidism given elevated ca and pth - Follow up blood cultures from ___ and ___
summary: You had palpitations from atrial fibrillation that was going very fast. The medicines that were given to you to slow the heart rate down sometimes slowed the heart rate too much so a pacemaker was inserted. You have tolerated the procedure well and now the medicines have been adjusted to keep your heart rate at a normal rate. Your warfarin was also adjusted while you were here. No lifting more than 5 pounds with your left arm or lift your left arm over your head for 6 weeks. Do not remove the strips of tape over the pacer site, they will fall off gradually. You will return to ___ in a few days to have the pacemaker checked. You had an exacerbation of your diastolic heart failure and needed medicine to get the fluid off. You are now back on your home dose of furosemide.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ yo M w/ COPD stage II (on 3L O2 at home) p/w dyspnea and neck discomfort for the past 2 weeks, after failing outpt therapy with avelox and prednisone, found to have acute respiratory failure, likely multifactorial, as well as secondary polycythemia. ACUTE ISSUES # Acute hypoxic respiratory failure - Pt p/w 2 week of dyspnea, failing outpt treatment with Avelox and prednisone for presumed COPD exacerbation. He was initially admitted to the ICU, and treated supportively with antibiotics of CTX/azithromycin, prednisone and nebulizers, briefly and then transferred to the floor. He was seen by the pulmonary consult service of Dr. ___ Dr. ___. He was continued on prednisone, just azithromcyin with no evidence of pneumonia on imaging, and nebulizers. He was also diuresed given concern that there was a component of volume overload leading to his worsening hypoxia. He was ultimately discharged home on a prednisone taper, ___ and increased home oxygen. ## Chronic hypoxic respiratory failure, with chronic COPD. He was admitted with acute exacerbation of chronic hypoxia. His chronic hypoxia was evaluated with chest CT, ECHO, repeat PFTs and lung scan. ECHO showed worsening pulmonary artery hypertension. Thus lung scan was performed to identify chronic thromboembolic disease as a cause of PAH, which was negative. Chest CT showed question of TBM, and bronchiolitis, and lung nodules, but not significant interestitial disease. PFTS were stable. He was discharged home with O2 at 5L, with plan for further outpt pulmonary workup. He was discharged on ___, albuterol and prednisone. # Neck discomfort - Pt has associated neck discomfort with dyspnea that did not accompany pt's last COPD exacerbation. He ruled out for MI, but did have ST depressions on EKG. Consideration could be made for stress test as outpatient. # Chronic diastolic CHF - Pt w/ EF > 55% and mild symmetric left ventricular hypertrophy with normal cavity size and moderately dilated right ventricular cavity with moderate global free wall hypokinesis. HE was diuresed with some improvement in his respiratory status, and discharged on 20 mg po daily of furosemide. # Leukocytosis - Most likely due to prednisone he was on as an outpatient as he is afebrile and non-toxic appearing and so do not have a high suspicion that this is from infection. He remained afebrile. # Polycythemia, secondary - Pt noted to have a HCT of 54.1 and unclear how long this as persisted as last HCT hasn't been since ___. Likely polycythemia due to chronic hypoxia. # Obstructive sleep apnea - Pt has hx OSA and uses CPAP at home. - cont CPAP at night. Transitional issues: 1. Lung nodules. Will need repeat Chest CT ___ months. Communicated by letter to pcp, pulmonary and patient. 2. ST depressions on ekg. Could consider stress test if respiratory status improves. 3. Chronic diastolic CHF. Discharged on higher dose of furosemide, 20 mg po daily - will need bmp in 1 week. 4. Pending tests. Blood cultures from admission pending at discharge.
summary: You were admitted with difficulty breathing. You did not have a pneumonia, and likely this was caused by a bronchitis, that made your breathing worse than usual. We did several tests to look for other causes,and to find out why your oxygen is always low, and Dr. ___ is going to continue that evaluation.\ Weigh yourself every day, and call Dr. ___ Dr. ___ your weight drops or increases by more than 2 lbs over 2 days. Your oxygen is still lower than usual, but you feel well. You will need to continue to use 5L of oxygen at all times, and especially when you are walking. Use your CPAP at night. If you get more short of breath, you should call Dr. ___ ___.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ y/o M with history of critical AS, CAD, PAD p/w shortness of breath and syncope, found to have NSTEMI. ACTIVE DIAGNOSES: 1. NSTEMI: Based on the EKG findings of ST depression and T wave flattening/inversions with positive troponins and CK-MB, he was diagnosed with an NSTEMI. During his hospitalization, he was managed medically with aspirin, heparin gtt, beta-blockers, statin therapy, and was diuresed with torsemide and IV lasix. He was not felt to be a candidate for PCI during this visit. Through his medical treatment, he had resolution of his chest discomfort as well as his shortness of breath. He had originally been undergoing an evaluation for aortic valve replacement and was seen by the C-surg team during this visit, who will likely plan for CABG and AVR after recovery from this NSTEMI. He underwent additional studies in preparation for this surgery, including ___ venous dopplers, CT chest, u/a, and will complete the evaluation with CXR and B/L ulnar/radial U/S on an outpatient basis. He will stop coumadin 5 days prior to the procedure and be admitted the night prior to start heparin. He was also evaluated by vascular surgery for possible simultaneous CEA, however given his complete right occlusion and partial left 40-59% carotid stenosis this procedure was not indicated. During this hospitalization, he remained hemodynamically stable and was discharged home 3 days after his initial presentation. He will have close follow-up with both cardiology and cardiac surgery for further management. 2. ATRIAL FIBRILLATION: During his hospital course, he remained in atrial fibrillation with slow ventricular rate. He was treated with metoprolol but temporarily held on coumadin for a heparin gtt. He was monitored on telemetry throughout his stay and remained hemodynamically stable. He was discharged back on his coumadin and will be further managed by his PCP. 3. Acute on Chronic Systolic Congestive Heart Failure: On admission to the service, he was mildly fluid overloaded with complaints of shortness of breath. After diuresis with IV lasix and negative output, his fluid status measured by JVD and ___ edema improved. He also reported symptomatic improvement of his shortness of breath and he felt his volume status to be at baseline. An echo performed during this visit showed critical aortic stenosis along with an actual improvement in his systolic function from past EF of 30% from TTE ___ to 55%. He will be discharged on beta-blockade, low sodium diet, and 1200mL per day fluid restriction. He will hold off on ace-inhibitor therapy due to his upcoming cardiac surgery and will have further management per cardiology as an outpatient. CHRONIC DIAGNOSES: 4. DM: No acute issues during this admission. Maintained on sliding scale insulin. Further management per PCP as an outpatient. 5. CKD: No acute issues during this admission. Creatinine with small fluctuations at approximate baseline of mid to upper 1s. Will have further outpatient management per PCP.
summary: You came to the hospital because you had some shortness of breath. While you were here we found that you had a small heart attack, which was likely due to increased strain on your heart while you were swimming. You also had signs of a heart failure exacerbation, which we treated with IV diuretics to take excess fluid off your body so your heart could pump more easily. While you were here, you were evaluated by the cardiac surgery team. They have scheduled you for an aortic valve replacement as well as a single-vessel coronary artery bypass surgery on ___. You will be coming into the hospital the day before your surgery as instructed by the surgery team (they will be contacted you regarding the details). You will need to stop taking coumadin on ___ in preparation for your surgery.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Summary: ___ year old lady with respiratory decline for three days who initially presented to the MICU and transferred to the floors. Assessment/Plan # Tracheobronchomalacia causing wheezing: This finding was seen on CT scan. There was concern for upper airway obstruction, but no foreign object or edema seen on ENT scope exam. There was no PNA on CXR but she did have bilateral pleural effusions and very mild pulmonary edema. She recieved a couple doses of broad Abx but this was stopped as low concern for PNA (no fever, leukocytosis). She was given a couple doses of IV lasix 10mg with -1.5L diuresis total with mild improvement (note from ___ ___, Echo ___ showed normal LVEF 55%, no DD). In addition, she was found to have episodes of Afib RVR while here so she may have mild dCHF leading to her effusions. We suspect she may have had a viral syndrome leading to reactive airways in setting TBM. There was no foriegn object in her lower airways. She will be treated with 5days of prednisone 40mg and nebs q3 ATC. By day of discharge (after steroids and gentle diuresis), her breathing was much improved and she no longer appeared in distress. She will continue nebulizers until her wheezing resolves. . Acute exacerbation of diastolic heart failure: The patient was noted to have pulmonary edema, however recent echo from ___ showed no diastolic or systolic dysfunction. The patient most likely episodes of atrial fibrillation with RVR which caused fluid to be retained in her lungs. She was diuresed with lasix and her atrial fibrillation was rate controlled as below. . Atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular rate: The patient has a history of atrial fibrillation. She was noted to have an irregular heart rate with vitals and started on diltiazem. She should be weaned to a lower dose of diltiazem as tolerated. Another issue is that she has a h/o Afib and several strokes. We are not sure if coumadin was ever addressed, she is currently on ASA 325mg only. Given her poor prognosis and unlikely she can have further loss of function beyond where she is now, coumadin may not be entirely beneficial, but will defer this to outpatient. G tube leaking: The patient's G tube was found leaking. The leakage stopped with tubefeedings. Surgery evaluated the G tube and thought there was no infection around it. Wound consult evaluated the patient and suggested to "cleanse the ___ G-tube tissue with normal saline. Pat the tissue dry. Apply Critic Aid Clear Moisture Barrier Ointment to the periwound tissue daily. Secure the tube with Flexitract -change Flexitract every ___ days or prn. Apply Allevyn Foam Trach sponge over the G-tube site to absorb drainage as needed, change daily." The area on CT scan did not look infected. Surgery felt that the erythema was likely due to irritation from gastric contents. Acute renal failure: The patient was found to have an elevated Cr to 1.4 on admission (per ___ records her baseline Cr was 0.6). Her admission Creat was 1.3 and remained stable 1.2-1.3 for the 4days she was here despite diuresis. Her baseline creat as of ___ is 0.6 so we are not sure if this was a progressive increase (CKD) or whether she has ARF. Given it is stable, likely plauteau phase of ATN, but we dont have an etiology for this (no recent contrast at ___ that we know of, no NSAIDs (except asa) or nephrotoxins, etc). Her creatinine should be monitored as an outpatient. Constipation: The patient had bowel movements while in the hospital. She has a history of constipation and should be on bowel medications. Goals of care: After discussions with the health care proxy, ___, the patient was made "Do not resusitate" but can still intubate.
summary: You were admitted to the intensive care unit with increased respiratoray rate and wheezing. You had an image of your chest (CT scan) which showed fluid in your lungs and a narrow trachea and bronchi. You were given Lasix, a medication that helps move water out of your lungs. You were also given prednisone (to help decrease inflammation in your lungs, trachea and bronchi) as well as nebulizers (to help open your airways). You were also found to have acute renal failure. Your creatinine, a test of kidney function, rose from your ___ baseline of 0.6 to 1.2. It will be monitored at your nursing home. You have a history of atrial fibrillation and your heart rate increased while in the hospital. You were given diltiazem to help decrease your heartrate. Your G tube was at first thought to be infected, but it was just leaking. Surgery evaluated you and felt that the area was not infected. Your tubefeeds were restarted without any problems. During this hospitalization, ___, your health care proxy, was informed of your clinical status. With guidance of the medical team, it was decided to not resusitate you if your heart stopped.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ yo male with diabetes, HTN and HL p/w acute onset of shortness of breath in the setting of hypertension and tachycardia concerning for flash pulmonary edema. Now found to have new systolic CHF with LVEF of 30%. # Acute congestive heart failure: Patient was noted to have pulmonary edema on x-ray and an elevated BNP therefore consitent with acute CHF. He has no history of CHF and his last ECHO in ___ was fairly unremarkable. TTE from ___ shows new reduction in LVEF from 60-70% in ___, now down to 30% without obvious valvular lesions. Global hypokinesis most consistent with hypertensive heart disease ___ medication non-compliance. Has not taken any medications other than baby ASA for past 6 months. Stress ECHO was non-diagnostic for CAD. On initial presentation he was acutely short of breath and desaturating to 80% on RA. He was briefly on BiPap in ED and diuresed with total of IV Lasix 20mg x 3. This resulted in complete resolutin of his dyspnea, and on discharge he was satting 98% on RA. He was restarted on carvedilol 25mg BID. Losartan was added initially at 50mg daily, then increased to 75mg daily. He was not discharged on a diuretic, but will likely require this in the future. Hypertension: Has had extensive w/u for secondary causes of HTN in past, which included negative urine metanephrines, normal TSH, no RAS. Per EMS reports, patient had SBPs in 200s in the field. In the ED, SBPs were in the 170s. Patient has been non-compliant with meds for past 6 months. Avoided CCBs due to significant peripheral edema in past. During admission had SBPs in 140s and DBPs in ___ to low 100s on carvedilol 25mg BID and losartan 75mg daily. As above, patient may require third agent, such as diuretic for optimal BP control and symptomatic CHF treatment. # DM2: Patient has a history of DM2. He was only on metformin however his sugars were noted to be elevated upon admission. HGBA1c found to be 11.8%. Not candidate for metformin currently in setting of new systolic dysfunction. ___ c/s was obtained and per their recommendations, he was discharged on humulin 70/30, 15 units BID. He was provided with diabetes education and educated on insulin use and home FSG checks. He will follow-up with his PCP with regards to future need for insulin therapy vs. possible return to oral hypoglycemics such as a sulfonylurea, though it is likely that he will need to remain on insulin, pending ability to comply with regimen. # Hyperlipidemia: FLP in-house showed total cholesterol 224, ___ 165, HDL 46, and LDL 145. Patient was re-started on prior dose of simvastatin 20mg. Patient was counseled extensively on the severity of his cardiac condition and the critical importance of taking his medications regularly and maintainng all scheduled appointments with his new PCP, and his cardiologist.
summary: You were admitted for shortness of breath and were found to have fluid in your lungs caused by acute heart failure. This heart failure is likely caused by your poorly controlled high blood pressure. While here you received a water pill (lasix) which helped to remove excess fluid and your breathing improved significantly. You had an ultrasound of your heart called an echocardiogram which showed that the pumping function of your heart has decreased from 60-70% (normal) in ___, down to 30%. You had a stress test of your heart, which did not show any obvious blockages in blood flow that would explain your heart failure. Your high blood pressure was relatively well controlled with the medications carvedilol and losartan. You will be discharged on these medications.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ with dementia, afib, and diastolic CHF was found down at her nursing facility and is admitted to medicine for further workup. . 1. Fall with possible syncope: Unfortunately history was extremely limited therefore differential broad. Given that she was in bathroom, consider vasovagal episode. Could have syncopized from poorly controlled atrial fibrillation. No history of seizure. Unknown whether PO intake has been poor. No history of recent illness. The patient was monitored on telemetry, and ruled out for MI (first set of troponins drawn at ___. Echo showed focal area of LV hypokinesis, which wasn't seen on previous echo in ___. After speaking with the patient's family and primary care physician, it was clear that goals of care are directed towards comfort, and no further invasive studies were pursued. . 2. Possible Posterior ligamentous injury: Orthopedic spine was consulted and evaluated the ___ MRI. They cleared her C spine and removed the cervical collar. . 3. Patient had a positive UA at ___. Urine culture pending on discharge. We treated her with Ciprofloxacin 250mg po x 3 days. -Please follow up results of urine culture . 5. Atrial fibrillation: Patient was continued on outpatient Aspirin and Metoprolol. Given history of falls she is not a candidate for warfarin. She was monitored on telemetry. 6. CHF: Patient appeared euvolemic on admission. Lasix was held on admission. Repeat echo showed new LV hypokinesis. -Please continue to monitor patient's fluid status. If appears volume overloaded, can restart low dose lasix.
summary: You were admitted after being found down in your nursing home. An MRI was done of your neck at ___. Orthopedics evaluated your neck and felt that you no longer required a collar for your neck. We made sure that you didn't have a heart attack. You had an Ultrasound of your heart done that showed decreased movement of one of the walls of your heart. We also stopped your lasix because you may have been dehydrated which caused your to fall. After speaking with your primary care doctor and your family, it is clear that our goals of care are towards comfort, and that you would not want any further invasive procedures. We also treated you for a urinary tract infection.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms ___ is a ___ yr old female with CNS lymphoma diagnosed ___ w/ partial response after 4 cycles HD MTX admitted for cycle 13. # CNS lymphoma - Achieved CR w/ induction HD MTX thus far +s/p C12 MTX and C6 rituxan. Here for cycle ___ MTX given ___. Patient had new Cr elevation on admission thus MTX delayed for 24 hrs for IVF (doubt related to MTX toxicity as she had cleared MTX as expected and maintained normal Cr last admisison, more likely ___ dehydration/overaggressive diuresis, see ___ below). Cr improved and was able to proceed with MTX, received ___. Pt continued on PO and bicarb gtt for urine alkalinization goal urine pH>8. Started leucovorin rescue 24 hrs after MTX dose, until level <0.1. This is final biweekly dose and then plan is to extend cycles to monthly with rituxan every 3 months. Her next admission for MTX is ___. Primary oncologist is aware that pt needs optho exam and may need further workup for lymphoma, this is deferred to outpatient setting. #Acute Kidney Injury - Resolved. Suspect ___ diuretics and ACE-I use, moreover pt with hx of recent orthostatic episode in past month (required admission at that time). Held these meds on admission, UA/UC unremarkable, 24 hr urine low but may not be reliable. Cr much improved and stable after IVF. ACE-I continued to be held during clearance of MTX and was resumed on discharge. Lasix was decreased from 40mg daily to 20mg PO three times a week (___) from 40mg PO daily.and she will weigh herself daily and call MD for titration. She knows to double the dose if she notices marked leg swelling. However it seems clear that while she requires aggressive diuresis on admission with significant IVF with MTX, her home dose was much too high for her without such hydration. She has f/u with PCP for lasix titration in 1 wk. #Hx CHF/HTN - ___ ischemic cardiomyopathy, has hx of volume overload during MTX admissions when receives large volume IVF. Did have weight gain and mild hypervolemia thus give intermittent lasix. She continued on metoprolol, held valsartan as above but restarted on discharge. Lowered lasix dosing on discharge as above. # Hx memory loss - suspect early dementia (has some component of sm vessel ischemic dz on MRI) vs effect of CSN lymphoma. chronic since diagnosis, sundowns at night. continued seroquel 25mg po qhs # Coronary Artery Disease - History of CABG in ___ - Stable, no active evidence of CAD and no chest pain. on ASA at home, held with MTX, resumed at discharge # Depression - Stable. Continued on home bupropion. # HLD. Stable. Continued home simvastatin. # GERD. Stable. Continued home famotidine. # Chemotherapy induced anemia - Hgb stable, prior w/u without nutritional cause. No indication for transfusion at this time. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: Next admission for MTX is ___
summary: you were admitted for your next cycle of high dose methotrexate. This was tolerated well. Prior to starting chemotherapy your creatinine was elevated and this may be that the diuretic medication lasix is too aggressive and causing dehydration, your kidney function returned to normal with IV fluids. However in the past you have had difficulty with excess fluid during chemotherapy thus lasix is needed while you are hospitalized and receiving large amounts of fluid. Therefore, you should go home with lasix 20mg (note this is HALF the pill you were taking before) but only take this ___, ___ and ___. Weigh yourself daily.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ ___ CHF ___ echo w LVEF 45-50%), CAD sp CABG, HTN, anticoagulated on warfarin presenting with dyspnea. # aCHF exacerbation: Came in with evidence of volume overload on exam in the setting of discontinuation of lasix. Most likely ddx is CHF although lung exam and CXR underwhelming. Patient is comfortable on room air at rest but has some symptoms when ambulating. He was diuresed with IV lasix until his volume status stabilized. He was transitioned to PO lasix for maintenance. His discharge weight was 67.8 kg. He was optimized medically on lasix, metoprolol, and enalapril. Needs outpatient follow-up echo. # CAD: Has a history of CABG. Negative troponins and non-ischemic EKG. He was continued on his statin, metoprolol, and enalapril which was uptitrated. He was not on aspirin because of abdominal discomfort. # AFIB: High CHADS score (___). INR 1.9 at presentation down to 1.8 during hospitalization. 5mg warfarin once given overnight ___ and started on a lovenox bridge to therapeutic INR. At the time of discharge, his INR was therapeutic. He will need frequent INR checks to ensure that his warfarin dosing is accurate. # HTN: uptitrated on enalapril, continued on metoprolol. # HLD: continued on simvastatin. # Arthritis: continued on oxycodone. ================================================ TRANSITIONAL ISSUES ================================================ * hemodynamic monitoring of blood pressure and heart rate * daily weights 67.8 kg at the time of discharge; monitor in's and out's and daily weights, volume status * INR checks, last INR 2.2 ___ * help with medication management and administration * electrolyte checks, please check tomorrow and thereafter as needed to ensure K+ levels are >4.0 * needs outpatient echocardiogram in follow-up
summary: You came into the hospital because you were feeling short of breath. This happened because you did not take your lasix, which helps keep fluid from building up in your body. While you were here, we gave you lasix to help get rid of the fluid and improve your breathing. While you were here, we also noticed that your INR was below its goal (subtherapeutic) of ___. We gave you lovenox shots to help make your blood thin while you took coumadin to increase your INR. When you left the hospital, your INR was within its goal range of ___. We increased your enalapril to better control you blood pressure. We also noticed that you have a urinary tract infection. You will take antibiotics for 7 days. You started antibiotics on ___ and will continue it until ___.

text: Brief Hospital Course: PATIENT SUMMARY: ================ ___ with history of HTN, HLD, Afib on eliquis, who was transferred from ___ with Afib with RVR to 130s and pulmonary edema. Received IV Lasix and IV metoprolol. Now asymptomatic with HR stable in ___. Found on TTE ___ to have EF 23% with severe global biventricular hypokinesis. # CORONARIES: unknown, has had prior cath at ___ but ___ ___ records # PUMP: EF 23% (___) # RHYTHM: AFib ACUTE ISSUES: ============= #HFrEF (EF 23%): #Pulmonary edema: The patient presented with pulmonary edema, which was thought to be triggered by Afib with RVR in the setting of likely diastolic dysfunction given long-standing HTN, BNP elevated at 4375 on admission. However TTE ___ revealed EF 23% with severe global biventricular hypokinesis, mild to moderate (___) MR. ___ prior TTEs were available within our ___ system, however patient was not aware of any prior diagnosis of heart failure. He was initially given 60mg IV lasix at ___, and then transitioned to Lasix 20mg PO prn weight gain for discharge. He was also started on Lisinopril 5mg daily. And his metoprolol succinate was increased from 50mg to 100mg daily. Upon discharge, he appeared clinically back to his baseline and was ambulating without shortness of breath. This new-onset HF will need to be further worked up as an outpatient; can consider cath for possible ischemic etiology. His HF medications will also need to be uptitrated, and consider repeat TTE once HF medications are more optimized. #AFib with RVR: Patient had a new episode of RVR on chronic AF with rates to 130s. He was given 5mg IV metoprolol in the ED. Trigger is unclear, TSH was within normal limits. His metoprolol succinate was increased from 50mg daily to 100mg daily for better rate control as his HR was persistently in 100s-110s at rest. Given Afib may be contributing to poor EF, he underwent TEE with cardioversion ___. TEE showed ___ intracardiac thrombus. Unfortunately, cardioversion was unsuccessful and he remains in Afib. He was continued on apixaban 5mg BID. He should follow up with Dr. ___ other potential therapy for rhythm control. ___: On admission, the patient had a Cr 1.6, stable at 1.5 on discharge. Patient is unaware of any history of renal issues, and ___ other records are available at this time. He should follow up with his PCP ___ ___ weeks to continue to trend Cr, and if different from baseline, further work-up. CHRONIC ISSUES: =============== #HLD: Continued on home pravastatin. #HTN: Amlodipine was discontinued because of new HFrEF diagnosis. His metoprolol succinate was increased from 50mg daily to 100mg daily and he was started on Lisinopril 5mg. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: ==================== DISCHARGE WEIGHT: 91.4 kg (201.5 lb) DISCHARGE Cr: 1.5 Heart Failure: [] New diagnosis of HFrEF (EF 23%): Further workup required as an outpatient, can consider cath to look for ischemic etiology. TSH - 0.88 (normal range 0.27-4.2) [] Discharged on Lasix 20mg prn weight gain, continue to monitor volume status and Cr and titrate as necessary. [] Up-titrate beta blocker and lisinopril as tolerated [] Repeat TTE after 3 months of optimal medical management to determine if needs ICD for primary prevention EP: [] DCCV unsuccessful; consider other therapies for rhythm control. General: [] Follow up patient's Cr. Continue to trend, and if different from baseline, further work-up may be warranted. # CODE: FULL # CONTACT: HCP: ___ ___
summary: You were admitted to the hospital because you were having chest tightness and shortness of breath. You were found to have a rapid heart rate, so you were given medications through your IV to slow down your heart rate. Your dose of metoprolol was also increased to better control your heart rate. You had an ultrasound of your heart (echocardiogram) which showed that the pumping function of your heart is lower than normal. As a result, you were started on a new medication (Lisinopril). You also were found to have some fluid in your lungs (pulmonary edema), likely as a result of the rapid heart rate and decreased pumping function. You were given a medication through your IV to get rid of the fluid. You were then started on an oral medication to help keep the fluid off (Lasix). You had a procedure called a transesophageal echocardiogram to look for clots in the heart, and there were ___ clots found. You then had a procedure called a cardioversion to try to shock your heart into a normal rhythm. Unfortunately, this procedure was not successful and you are still in atrial fibrillation.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ w/ HFpEF on torsemide w/ gradually worsening nocturnal cough over past ___ weeks. ACUTE ISSUES: =============== # Cough: Nocturnal cough was initially concerning for acute heart failure exacerbation given history of HFpEF. She has ___ volume overload on exam. CXR negative for PNA. BNP normal. CTA negative for PE. Last TTE in ___ w/ normal EF, e/o diastolic dysfunction; she is ___ longer on lisinopril as concern it may be contributing to cough. Her cough improved with nebs. Repeat TTE here with mod-severe MR which can sometimes cause shortness of breath but would not be reversed with nebs. Likely etiology is reactive airway disease given improvement with inhalers and can initiate PPI for possible GERD. She is discharged with albuterol inhaler, ipratropium inhaler. She can be trialed on omeprazole for GERD and continued on torsemide 5mg daily. Consider referral to cardiology as outpatient for further evaluation of mitral regurgitation and consider referral to outpatient PFTs. They were ordered but in the setting of her age and comorbidities, it is unclear if PFTs would be beneficial if empiric inhalers can be used. # Non-occlusive common femoral vein thrombus LENIS on this admission revealed non-occlusive DVT. Unclear of the timing of this thrombus although it was not noted on prior US in ___ (had clots in right leg). Recommended initiation of apixaban at reduced dose 2.5mg twice daily upon discussion with cardiology. CHRONIC ISSUES: =============== # Dementia: continue home Seroquel for now, reconsider its use long-term. Restart home melatonin on discharge. # Chondrocalcinosis: Continued colchicine # HTN: ___ longer on any agents, was previously on lisinopril. BP stable off meds. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: ==================== New medications: ---------------- Albuterol inhaler Ipratropium inhaler Apixaban Omeprazole Thickener Terbinafine [] Follow up with cough symptoms [] Continue apixaban. Would recommend continuation for life given prolonged immobility and risk of recurrent DVTs. Apixaban was given at reduced dose 2.5mg BID given age as well as weight. [] Encourage ensure as well as thickener for fluids to prevent aspiration [] Consider outpatient PFTs for definitive diagnosis of reactive airway disease [] Podiatry to follow up with feet and toenails. Prescribed terbinafine at discharge. [] Consider outpatient cardiology referral for mitral regurgitation although patient is not likely to be a candidate for replacement given comorbidities [] Please continue to discuss goals of care. While the patient is currently FULL CODE, it may be important to re-address this issue. Time spent: 45 minutes
summary: You were admitted for cough occurring at night which had worsened over the last several weeks. You were also having generalized fatigue and malaise You had an xray of your chest which did not show any infection. We also ruled out a clot in your lungs (Pulmonary embolism). We started you on inhalers which helped your cough. You did not require any oxygen and you did very well. We did an ultrasound scan of your legs which showed a nonocclusive clot in your left thigh. We decided to start you on blood thinner. Please continue to use your inhalers to help with your cough. You can also take some robitussin before bed. You may also take omeprazole to help with heartburn if that is related to your cough. We recommend that you continue to take a blood thinner.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ with HFrEF (EF 28% per pt) s/p ICD, CAD s/p angioplasty ___, AFib warfarin, DM2, and asthma presenting with presyncope and symptomatic bradycardia. # BRADYCARDIA/History of Ventricular tachycardia: Patient was recently started on amiodarone for a history of ventricular tachycardia which possibly contributed to bradycardia. He underwent a successful electrophysiology study and VT ablation. Amiodarone and digoxin were discontinued during the hospitalization as they are no longer needed. Patient will follow up with Dr. ___ and in device clinic. # CHF, systolic, chronic. Most recent EF 28% per patient. Cause is ischemic cardiomyopathy after remote MI. Not currently decompensated although he gained 20 lbs in ___ due to being off furosemide from pharmacy issue. Appeared dry on exam. Initially furosemide was held as he had positive orthostatics and ___. However, by discharge was restarted at a lower dose of lasix 80mg QD instead of BID. He had positive orthostatic vital signs. Home Lasix was held for 2 days and gentle fluids were administered given CHF. Patient appeared euvolemic with resolution ___ and lightheadedness. He was restarted on furosemide 80mg QD which is down from 80mg BID he was taking at home. Was also restarted on home lisinopril and metoprolol at discharge which was initially held due to ___. Patient's weight and kidney function labs should be followed up. # AFIB. Slow afib here likely due to multiple nodal agents. CHA2DS2VASc score is 4 from CHF, HTN, DM2, MI. summary: You were admitted to us for evaluation of your history of slow heart beat and arrhythmia. While here you underwent a procedure called a VT ablation which found the area of the heart that was causing the abnormal heart rhythm and it was ablated. As a result, you no longer need to take amiodarone and digoxin. We also found that you were a little dehydrated on admission which is a possible cause of you feeling lightheaded. We decreased your furosemide(Lasix) dose to just 80mg once a day and you will be monitored as an outpatient. You should have blood work checked on ___. The script is attached. Please see your appointments and medications below.

text: Brief Hospital Course: HOSPITAL COURSE ___ M w/complicated past medical history including ischemic cardiomyopathy (last EF ___, s/p ICD placement for hx of V Tach and V fib, on coumadin for Afib, and a whipple for biliary dysplasia in ___. He was recently found to have Klebsiella and Enterococcus bacteremia on ___ admission to ___ and discharged with ___ for ___/cipro regiment, completing his course ___. He presented to the ED ___ with nausea, feeling dizzy, and three loose stools. He had a low grade temp of 100.8 and leukocytosis of 11.3. Following this original presentation and labs, his symptoms improved overnight, he did not have any recurrent temperatures, and his WBC downtrended from 11.3 to 6.6 (___). There was no growth on urine culture or five blood cultures. He did have a mild transaminitis with his alk phos remaining elevated around 200 throughout his stay. A RUQ US was performed without any obvious signs of dilitation. An ERCP was again recommended by the ID team and Geriatric team, as it had been during his last admission. After extensive consideration with his SO, the ERCP was deferred and the patient will seek a second opinion as well as follow-up with Dr. ___ as an outpatient. ACTIVE ISSUES # transaminitis - Mild transaminitis with elevated alk phos, 224 on ___ . Dilitation of pancreatic duct noted on CT during last admission, but pt declined ERCP. RUQ US during this admission ___ was w/out evidence of duct dilitation or masses. Ddx includes infx corresponding w/leukocytosis and nausea, biliary obstruction given elevated alk phos but less likely w/out dilitation on US and Tbili wnl - Pt and S.O. will be seeking a second opinion as an outpatient regarding ERCP and discuss their decision with their PCP and Dr. ___. # leukocytosis - LG fever of 100.8 and leukocytosis on presentation. Unclear source; negative UA and CXR. No recurrent fevers and WBC continues to downtrend. DDx includes transient viral gastroenteritis, infx ___ biliary/pancreatic lesion which also corresponds with transaminitis, or endocarditis ___ enterococcus bacteremia and hx of ICD placement. Given lack of growth on blood cx now less likely to be an active biliary infection. Final rec from ID team was to send home off of antibiotics and continue to consider ERCP to explore a potential source. - Pt and S.O. will be seeking a second opinion as an outpatient regarding ERCP and discuss their decision with their PCP and Dr. ___. INACTIVE ISSUES # atrial fibrillation - H/o paroxysmal a fib, CHADS2 4 (CHF, HTN, evidence of prior stroke on head CT ___ on coumadin. s/p cardioversion. Currently in and out of afib. Continue amiodarone,warfarin w/INR trend, and metoprolol. Consider lovenox bridge depending on when ERCP is scheduled # Chronic systolic CHF: Severe ischemic cardiomyopathy with LVEF of ___. Currently appears euvolemic without evidence of edema, elevated JVD, or dyspnea. Continue home torsemide, lisinopril, and metoprolol # CAD: Stable. Continue ASA 81 # H/O VT/VF: Stable. ICD in place. Continue home amiodarone # depression: Stable. Continue bupropion and mirtazapine # hypothyroid: Stable. Continue levothyroxine # pancreatic insufficiency: Stable. Continue creon # Anemia: Stable. Continue to trend as outpatient. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES -- At the time of discharge, the patient still had a transaminitis with alk phos of 224. The decision on whether or not to have an ERCP and where to have it performed remains undecided by him and his SO. --He will require outpatient follow-up by ID with Dr. ___ to monitor for any return signs of infection from his initial Klebsiella and Enterococcus bacteremia. -- Patient was given script for full LFT, CBC and INR on ___
summary: You were admitted to ___ with a low-grade fever, some nausea, and a few episodes of loose stoools. Given your recent history of an infection in ___, you were started on ceftriaxone/vancomycin antibiotics and monitored for any return of fevers or signs in your lab work of infection. Your symptoms improved the same night of your admission, and you remained without fevers the rest of your stay. Your initial blood labs showed a slight increase in white blood cells, a marker for infection, but all the following tests have been normal with decreasing white blood cells. You have now recovered from a clinical standpoint. You were reevaluated by the Infectious Disease team and both them and your Geriatrics team still recommend having an ERCP to make sure there is nothing in your bile and pancreas system that could have caused the original infection and symptoms. One of your labs from your biliary system is elevated, showing there might still be something abnormal in that area. Per your preference, an ERCP was not performed at ___. You may continue considering this procedure and seek any additional opinions that may help you. This is not an urgent procedure as your are clinically well right now, but it should be pursued soon if you decide to do it in order to gain better understanding about what originally caused your symptoms and figure out next steps to take.

text: Brief Hospital Course: IMPRESSION AND RECOMMENDATIONS: ___ yo man who presented to CDAC on ___ with CHF exacerbation, elevated Troponin thought to be demand ischemia with HF, recent PND/DOE/edema/chest discomfort, stopped taking his meds a while back ___ insurance issues and only recently resumed outpt. He was admitted for NSTEMI, HF management and management of dry gangrenous left great toe. # Acute decompensated heart failure: EF 45-50% on TTE on ___. Dry weight is unknown as patient does not have a scale at home, office visit 3 months ago with the same weight as admission possibly reflecting furosemide 40 mg daily was insufficient. Diuresed well on Lasix gtt which was stopped on evening of ___ after 6 Liter cumulative negative output. 5 pound weight loss from admit weight. S/P cath ___ with elevated left heart filling pressure. -Continue spironolactone 25mg -Start torsemide 40 mg daily -Daily weight, 2 gram sodium diet -F/U with Dr. ___ # NSTEMI: Troponin obtained in the setting of history of CAD and nonspecific chest discomfort positive at 0.13, down trended to 0.12 and negative MB. First part of nuclear perfusion test done ___, delayed by positive troponin, volume overload. Continued to diuresis and revisited second day ___. Completed pharm nuclear ___ which showed severe perfusion defect involving RCA territory. S/P coronary angiogram ___ SVG-RCA graft occluded. No interventions done. -Continue increased carvedilol 12.5 mg BID -Continue ASA 81, atorvastatin 80 -Continue increased dose of losartan 50 mg daily for BP control # Dry gangrene left toe: Appreciate Podiatry/Vascular surgery consults. Now s/p left great toe debridement and nail removal by podiatry ___. -non-invasive studies ___ (ABI, PVR, Arterial duplex), vein mapping completed on ___ per vascular request, results above -outpatient follow-up next week for ___ angio Vascular Surgery Office: ___. -plan per vascular is to do diagnostic angiogram of Left foot as outpatient, does not need to be while inpt. ___ angio scheduled for ___ -Daily betadine dressing by ___ -Per podiatry, will see patient after vascular procedure as outpt -ID consulted re input on oral antibiotic transition at discharge; appreciate recs: discontinued iv antibiotics, no need to continue iv or po antibiotics per ID -IV Ceftriaxone discontinued ___ per ID recs -IV Vancomycin discontinued on ___ per ID recs -Home with ___ to help with dressings, wound checks and heart failure monitoring # HTN: SBP 155-188 today. BP up to ___ in the afternoon. Pt asymptomatic. His bp has been 150-170 range since admission. Was briefly on po hydral 10 mg tid at which time his bp remained 120-150's. Hydral was discontinued on ___ to give room for up titration of BB and losartan. - Continue increased carvedilol 12.5 mg bid - Continue increased dose of losartan 50 mg daily - Start hydralazine 10 mg tid # Type 2 diabetes with nephropathy, hyperglycemia, neuropathy: Had episode of hypoglycemia BG ___ while NPO. Had received full dose of NPH evening prior. Treated with glucose gel and D50 25gm for BG 115. - ___ c/s as outpt for DM management - Continue home metformin and NPH - Potentially start SGL T-2 inhibitor as outpt through ___ # Mixed dyslipidemia: - Continue Atorvastatin, started on admission on ___ # Transitional: home with ___ for dressing changes, wound checks and heart failure monitoring arranged # Dispo: Discharge today with ___
summary: You were admitted to the hospital because you had a heart failure exacerbation and you were volume overloaded (extra fluid in your body). You received diuretics through your IV to help get the extra fluid off. Your blood work showed elevated cardiac enzymes therefore you underwent a cardiac catheterization to look at the arteries in your heart. This showed one of your grafts from your prior heart surgery was blocked, but it did not require any intervention, will be managed with medications. The vascular and podiatry doctors saw ___ because of your gangrenous left toe. This was debrided. The recommendation is to have it amputated. It has been arranged for you to return on ___ for an angiogram to look at the blood flow in your leg.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ with history of severe AS s/p TAVR in ___ (s/p balloon valvuloplasty in ___, systolic HFrEF (30%), CAD (s/p cardiac cath in ___ with BMS to LAD/Diag), DMII, and hyperlipidemia presenting as a transfer from ___ here he presented on ___/ shortness of breath and malaise. Transferred to ___ after (TTE) there revealed 2 vegetations on Mitral valve concerning for endocarditis, upon repeat TTE ___, found to have similar mobile echodensities on prior studies which suggests these represent thrombi/fibrin. Patient remained afebrile, non-toxic, without sequelae, blood cultures NGTD. # Concern for infective endocarditis: Per echocardiogram review ___ concern for endocarditis, the findings represent chronic fibrin. Stopped vancomycin ___. # Acute on chronic CHF exacerbation: Likely ___ flash pulmonary edema precipitated by transiently worsened previously known MR. ___ evidence of subacute presentation. Pt seems to be compliant with medications and diet. TTE report from OSH appears somewhat improved with respect to LVEF (45% from 30% ine arly ___ and MR reported now is ___ vs 4+ earlier this month, making worsening valvular disease a less likely explanation for his worsening heart failure, but does demonstrate ability of MR to fluctuate. Asthma is also likely contributing at least to some of his respiratory distress and subsequent albuterol treatments likely contributing to tachycardia. Required minimal diuresis and euvolemic on exam. Due to lack of change in weight and sudden onset dyspnea, believe these episodes are secondary to anxiety/increased HR/afterload which can exacerbated MR causing flash pulmonary edema. Started patient on Imdur 30 mg PO QD with PRN NTG SL 0.4 mg to relieve preload and guard against functional mitral stenosis, Increased Metoprolol to XL 75 BID to control HR and prevent worsening MR, changed diuresis to Torsemide 5 mg PO QOD as patient's labs revealed he was likely at dry weight as evidenced by rising potassium and Cr, slight hemoconcentraion on CBC and improved BNP. Started Ipratropium Nebs Q6H PRN, Loratidine 10 mg PO QD, and Nasal saline spray BID for control of allergies/secretions. # CAD s/p BMS to LAD and diagonal in ___, cardiac cath on ___ showed 2 vessel moderate stenosis in the diagonal branches of LAD;these were unable to be reached and intervention was deferred. Continued ASA, crestor, increased metoprolol as above. # Normocytic Anemia: Hemoglobin 10.5 on admission, which is well below a baseline of near 12 in early ___. ___ changes in color of stool or hx of hemoptysis to suggest GIB. Hemolysis labs: LDH, haoptoglobin, Tbili not reflective of hemolysis. Iron studies: Fe, ferritin, transferrin reflective low iron stores without microcytic anemia, could be ACD (low RDW), did not require any intervention, stable at 10.5 on discharge. # Eosinophilia: pt has a hx of transiently elevated eosinophils in the past, <20 more likely to be associated with his asthma. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: - STARTED Imdur 30 mg PO QD - PRN NTG SL 0.4 mg for SOB - INCREASED Metoprolol to XL 75 BID - CHANGED diuresis to Torsemide 5 mg PO QOD - STARTED Ipratropium Nebs Q6H PRN - STARTED Loratidine 10 mg PO QD - STARTED Nasal saline spray BID - F/U with cardiology (Dr. ___ within 2 weeks
summary: You were admitted as a transfer after intially presenting to ___ ___ for acute shortness of breath. An echocardiogram performed at ___ was concerning for an infection on one of your heart valves. Upon transfer, we took blood cultures and started you on antibiotics and repeated an echocardiogram. In comparing prior studies in our system, our repeat study showed that the growths in your heart were are likely not due to an infection. Blood culture have also not grown any pathogens. We believe that you had an episode of flash edema, which is fluid building in your lungs, probably caused by a combination of allergies and anxiety, raising you blood pressues, which makes your blood back up into your lungs through your mitral valve. We started some new medications to help with these problems.

text: Brief Hospital Course: SUMMARY: ___ year old man with non-ischemic cardiomyopathy, LBBB, initially admitted to ___ ___ for acute bacterial meningitis, transferred to ___ after VFib arrest, s/p ICD placement on ___. . # Ventricular fibrillation: The patient was transferred to ___ following an OSH Vfib arrest while being treated for GBS meningitis. He was initially loaded on amiodarone, and this medication was subsequently discontinued. It was felt that the most likely etiology of his arrythmia was LBB re-entrant tachycardia, though no arrythmia was induced while in the electrophysiology lab on ___. Overnight on ___, the patient developed hemodynamically stable supraventricular tachycardia in the setting of acute urinary retention. The patient underwent a cardiac MRI and ICD placement on ___. . # Bacteremia: Blood culture from OSH was positive for Streptococcus aglacticae which was sensitive to Ampicillin and ceftriaxone. Suspected source was bacterial meningitis given results of a lumbar puncture. ID was consulted and felt that there was no contraindication to ICD placement given that blood cultures had been negative for several days. An MRI of the spine revealed the possibiliy of discitis, and ID recommended a 6 week course of ceftriaxone, to complete on ___ (2g q12 until ___, then 1g q24 until ___. . # Nonischemic Cardiomyopathy/Chronic systolic congestive heart failure. The etiology of his cardiomyopathy remains unclear. The patient's CHF was felt to be stable during his hospital course. He did require some gentle diuresis but was otherwise euvolemic. He was continued on his ACE-I and beta-blocker. Given his depressed LVEF, Spironolactone should be considered as an otpatient. . # HTN: Continued on carvedilol and lisinopril. The patient did have some orthostatic hypotension, that was felt secondary to deconditioning. . # HLD: The patient's simvastatin was initially held given transaminitis. This medication was restarted, and his LFTs continued to normalize. . # Anemia: Unclear etiology at this time. HIV negative, TSH was within normal limits. His hematocrit remained stable and there was no suggestion of acute blood loss. Will need continued work-up/follow-up as an outpatient. And age-appropriate malignancy screening should be considered. . # Transaminitis: Most likely secondary to hypotension (shock liver) during cardiac arrest. Resolved over time without intervention. Simvastatin was initially held, and LFTs continued to normalize after this medication was restarted. Other potential etioliges included drug induced transaminitis from amiodarone. ___ and HBV, HCV serologies were negative. . # Hyperglycemia: The patient did have some hyperglycemia early in the hospital course. Hb A1c was 5.6% suggesting this was most likely secondary to his acute illness . # Lower-back pain: Ongoing since pre-hospitalization, possibly exacerbated by discitis, well-controlled with prn Oxycodone and Acetaminophen . # Buttock ulcerations: At home, the patient was treated with hot packs, and developed several ulcerations on his buttock. Subsequent to transfer, the patient developed ___ additional small ulcerations. Wound care was consulted, and recommended routine wound care. These were not felt to be pressure ulcers, and the exact etiology is unclear. These need to be followed as an outpatient, and if he continues to develop new lesions, further work-up may be indicated. . ================================== Transitional issues- -Consider Spironolactone initiation given depressed LVEF. -LFTs, CBC with diff, Chem-7 and ESR and fax to ___ clinic noted on page 1. -Pull PICC line after completion of antibiotic course on ___ -Continue work-up / follow-up of stable anemia, consider age-appropriate malignancy screening. -Renal cyst noted on cardiac MRI, further work-up may be indicated -Monitor buttock ulcerations for improvement, consider work-up if more lesions develop
summary: You originally went to ___ on ___ with a high fever and diagnosed with bacterial meningitis. You were slowly getting better when you experienced an episode of cardiac arrest on ___ due to an irregular heart rhythm called ventricular fibrilation. You were stablized and then transferred to ___ on ___ for further management of this irregular heart rhythm. Several different studies were done on your heart here at the hospital, and it was decided that a pacemaker with a defibrilator should be placed to help keep your heart beating at a normal rate and rhythm. This device was placed on ___ and has been functioning appropriately. Your heart is a bit weaker than before your admission, but the medications will help. You are now doing much better and you are ready to transfer to rehab.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Non-ischemic cardiomyopathy: The patient's symptoms were attributed to acute decompensation of chronic systolic heart failure. Her initial physical exam revealed bibasilar crackles, suggesting a component of pulmonary edema. While a chest x-ray was clear, her BNP was 3419, which is elevated from prior BNPs (which often are in the ___. She was placed on a nasal cannula with a flow rate of 2L of oxygen per minute. We initially proceeded with diuresis of excess fluid by increasing her bumetanide dose to 0.5 mg daily and gave 0.5 mg on an as needed basis. Her creatinine levels remained at baseline throughout the duration of her hospitalization. Her physical exam demonstrated improvement by her third hospital day. An echocardiogram was obtained to assess LV function, and it showed no significant changes compared to the previous echo in ___. Her EF remained at 30 - 35%. The patient continued to complain of vague chest heaviness and shortness of breath throughout her hospitalization, but these were not believed to be severe as she complained that her muscle aches were worse. Additionally, the patient received her home doses of valsartan and carvedilol. Coronary artery disease: The patient has known coronary artery disease that is known to be nonocclusive. EKG's and cardiac isoenzymes were acquired upon admission to rule out an acute coronary syndrome as the cause of her symptoms. Troponins were negative times three sets and EKG showed no significant changes from prior EKG's. She received her home dose of simvastatin. Lightheadedness and weakness: The patient has chronic complaints of weakness, muscle aches and cramps, and lightheadedness. The patient said these symptoms were not any worse during her hospitalization than when she had been at home. She has a history of depression and was recently prescribed Cymbalta, but she electively discontinued the drug. Chronic kidney disease - Her creatinine upon admission was 1.3, which is her baseline. She is known to be sensitive to diuresis, so bumetanide was used conservatively for her symptoms. Her creatinine ranged from 1 - 1.3 during her hospitalization. Nephrotoxic drugs were avoided. Hypertension - The patient was maintained on valsartan and carvedilol. Systolic blood pressures often ranged high (as much as 171 on one vital sign check), but were also normal. For this reason, no adjustment was made to her antihypertensive regimen to avoid inducing a hypotensive episode. History of iron deficiency anemia - Hemoglobin on admission was 11.9 g/dl, which is the highest it has been since ___ (per ___ records). She was continued on iron supplementation as well as B12 supplementation.
summary: You were admitted to the ___ on ___. As you recall, you were experiencing difficulty breathing and what you described as "chest heaviness." We were concerned that these symptoms might have been related to your heart, so you were admitted by the cardiology team. During your stay, blood tests we performed did not show any damage to your heart. This is good news! We also obtained an echocardiogram (an ultrasound image of the heart) to assess your heart's function. It showed no significant changes from your last echocardiogram in ___. This means that your heart function has remained stable, a good sign. Your medications remain unchanged.

text: Brief Hospital Course: In summary, Mr. ___ is a ___ male with history of atrial fibrillation, s/p CABG (LIMA-LAD, SVG-OM, SVG-PDA, SVG-D), and CHF with EF ~15% who underwent AICD/pacer revision 4 weeks ago for lead fracture and presented on this admission with generalized weakness and lightheadedness. . #Lightheadedness: After serial EKGs, telemetry monitoring and formal pacemaker interrogation review patient's pacemaker found to be functioning normally. Given his severe CAD he was also ruled out for a silent MI with negative cardiac enzymes. Orthostatic testing after admission was within normal limits and patient denied any recent diarrhea or illnesses of note so this episode is unlikely to have been related to dehydration. Unclear etiology for his lightheadedness but his history and symptoms most consistent with a vaso-vagal episode. He was asked to return to the ED or call his physician should he have any additional repeat episodes as a more detailed neurological workup may be warranted if these episodes persist. At time of discharge he had no complaints and he had no recurrent lightheadedness or dizziness for the entirety of his hospital course. . # Coronaries: Mr. ___ is status ___ CABG (___-LAD, SVG-OM, SVG-PDA, SVG-D) done in ___ and he had a LCX stent placed in ___. Currently chest pain free throughout entire hospital stay. He was continued on his usual aspirin, beta blocker, valsartan and simvastatin. No concerning ischemic changes on EKGs and he had negative cardiac enzymes x 3 ( ___ set at OSH). . # Pump Function: Mr. ___ also has severe ischemic cardiomyopathy with EF 15% and ___ mitral regurgitation per ECHO. He was euvolemic on his physical exam and denied recent weight gain, worsened lower extremity edema or new shortness of breath. He was continued on his carvedilol, digoxin, valsartan and his BID home lasix regimen. . #Hypertension: He had some short-lived hypertension at the outside hospital setting which was attributed to stress response as he explained that he was anticipating a big shock from his pacemaker. After recent malfunction and having been shocked by his AICD over 20 times he had much anxiety when he initially arrived to emergency room. After he his lightheadedness resolved and he was transferred to ___ he remained largely normotensive so his blood pressure medications were not altered in any way. Continued on beta blocker, ___, lasix. . # Rhythm: He was noted to be V-paced at 80 bpm on telemetry and EKG. Background native rhythm was NSR. No sign of pacemaker malfunction on telemetry. The electrophysiology specialists were able to do a pacemaker interrogation which showed no history of any preceding VT/VF and patient's pacemaker was updated to pick up any evidence of wide QRS VT/VF activity after 133 bpm, this was lowered from his usual programmed threshold of 150bpm. He will plan to follow-up in the outpatient clinic next week for close follow-up. He was continued on his usual coumadin therapy for his history of atrial fibrillation. He will continue with his usual INR level checks in the outpatient setting. At discharge, INR therapeutic at 2.4. . . #Fluids, Electrolytes and Nutrition: Continued on cardiac low salt diet and electrolytes monitored and repleted as needed. . #Prophylaxis: Coumadin with therapeutic INR noted. . #Code: full code status during stay, confirmed with patient .
summary: You were admitted after having an episode of lightheadedness. Because of your recent pacemaker malfunction about 4 weeks ago your pacemaker was interrogated by the electrophysiology staff and found to be functioning normally. Also, on EKG and telemetry monitoring your heart appeared to have normal function as well. The cardiology team felt that you had a fleeting episode of lightheadedness from a condition called vasovagal dizziness. You are set up for close follow-up with Dr. ___ the ___ Clinic as outlined below. Due to your history of congestive heart failure please be sure to weigh yourself every morning, call MD if you weigh > 3 lbs above usual baseline weight. Also, please adhere to 2 gm sodium diet and less than 2L fluids daily.

text: Brief Hospital Course: 1. Dyspnea- Patient presented with recent diagnosis of pneumonia while hospitalized from ___. Started on antibiotics prior to admission (levoquin) and then got vanc/ceftaz/azithromycin initially on admission before switching back to levoquin. Last dose of levoquin was on ___- patient was stable. Possible etiologies of shortness of breath are pneumonia, fluid overload in the setting of diastolic HF or CO2 retention. Chest x-ray showed basilar atelectasis and pleural effusions. Patient started on levoquin and vancomycin. BNP- 3013. Placed on 4L O2 NC and was satting well. Had episode of desaturation to 70% on 4L and was triggered. Was briefly placed on NRB to get sats back up. Then placed on 4L O2 NC and 35% facemask. Stabilzed and satted well throughout the night. ABG drawn after trigger event- pO2 93, pCO2, pH- 7.26. Showed CO2 retention. She was also given Lasix 20mg IV x 1 for gentle diuresis with the goal of -500ml-1000ml fluid balance. Patient's symptoms did not improve despite our interventions. She remained comfortable on the 4L O2/35% face mask. 2. Chest pain- Patient has history of diastolic CHF, afib (on aspirin for anticoagulation- was on coumadin but patient is high risk for falls), and RVR (rate controlled on metoprolol). EKG and cardiac enzymes done. All three sets of enzymes are negative. TTE performed in ___ showed "Normal biventricular cavity sizes with preserved global and regional biventricular systolic function. Mild pulmonary artery systolic hypertension. Dilated ascending aorta. Compared with the prior study (images reviewed) of ___, the findings are similar." Patient was monitored on telemetry overnight. Telemetry was discontinued the following morning following discussion with patient's family regarding goals of care. 3. Altered mental status- Patient had extensive history of AMS and mild dementia. She was not currently on any sedating medications. Considered toxic/metabolic and infectious causes. Cultures have been drawn. Patient not febrile. No elevated WBC count but patient did have a left-shift (N- 83.7). Could related to pneumonia or other infection. Also, may be ___ CO2 narcosis/retention. We kept the patient on IV medications and NPO. Giving ABG results showing high level of CO2 retention, it is thought that the patient's mental status is due to that primarily. 4. Chronic diastolic HF - Last ECHO ___, preserved EF. CXR with effusions consistent with fluid overload. BNP of 3013 on this admission. We gently diuresed patient with 20mg lasix IV one time per day before comfort care measures were implemented. There was minimal symptomatic improvement after diuresis. 5. Afib- Was rate-controlled with metoprolol. Was monitored on telemetry overnight the first night. Anticoagulated with aspirin (warfarin discontinued a few months ago due to fall risk). 6. Goals of care: ___ admissions in last 3 months. Patient is DNR/DNI. Goals of care were discussed with attending physician and family. Daughter ___ is patient's proxy. After a extensive discussion, her daughter decided to pursue comfort measures only with the goal of sending the patient home with hospice care. This was a decision the family was comfortable with. All medications, vital sign monitoring and lab draws were discontinued. Patient was started on IV morphine PRN pain and IV ativan PRN anxiety. Palliative care was consulted and case managers set up hospice services for the patient.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital for shortness of breath and altered mental status. While in the hospital your oxygen sats were low and you were temporarily placed on a 100% non-rebreather to raise your oxygen saturations. After you were stabilized, we placed you back on the 4L nasal cannula and added a 35% face mask for additional oxygenation. You also required some gentle diuresis to remove some of the fluid from your lungs. Despite our interventions, your condition did not improve to the degree we would have liked. We discussed goals of care with your daughter ___. After a lengthy discussion, it was decided that your comfort was the most important priority.

text: Brief Hospital Course: SUMMARY: ___ with HFrEF ___, history of VT s/p dual-chamber ICD, pAFib on warfarin, and hypothyroidism, who presented with hypoxia secondary to acute on chronic systolic CHF. # Acute on Chronic Systolic Heart Failure: Clinically volume overloaded. Etiology was unclear, but there was no evidence of recent ischemic event. She was started with aggressive diuresis with furosemide IV drip followed by boluses and metolazone. She also received acetazolamide for metabolic alkalosis. Diuresis was limited by hypotension, although she reached what was felt to be euvolemia and was then transitioned to PO torsemide 20mg a day. On the day of discharge, torsemide was changed to 20mg BID due to rising weight. She did not tolerate an ACE due to hypotension. She was continued on metoprolol. She was also evaluated by EP regarding possible pacemaker upgrade to biventricular device, and will follow up in clinic as an outpatient after amiodarone effects are no longer present. # Ventricular Tachycardia: noted to have multiple episodes of non-sustained VT on telemetry. She also had several episodes of sustained wide complex tachycardia, which following device interrogation were felt to be atrial fibrillation with aberrancy. VT was likely secondary to scar from prior MI. She was initially loaded with amiodarone, which was stopped due to concern for toxicity and rate slowing since her ICD was unable to recognize rates < 170.
