Electrolytes were aggressively repleted, and home metoprolol was increased from 25mg daily to 25mg Q6h. # GI bleed: multiple episodes of maroon colored stool, some of which were in the setting of a supratherapeutic PTT. Hemoglobin downtrended from 11.1 at time of admission to a low of 7.9. She remained hemodynamically stable. She was evaluated by GI, who felt that colonoscopy would be high risk and low yield in terms of identifying any potentially intervenable lesion. The patient herself strongly desired to avoid colonoscopy. She was tranfused 2 units of PRBCs on ___ for symptomatic anemia with a hemoglobin of 7.9 and had an appropriate response. Warfarin was held in the setting of a GI bleed. After bleeding stabilized, she was restarted on heparin for bridging. # Atrial Fibrillation: Home metoprolol was increased as above from 25mg daily to Q6h. Warfarin was held in the setting of a GI bleed. After bleeding stabilized, she was restarted on heparin for bridging. Warfarin was restarted on ___. # Anemia: likely multifactorial, with chronic anemia secondary to CKD with acute blood loss anemia secondary to GI bleed. Iron studies and folate were normal, but she was started on B12 supplementation given macrocytosis and borderline low levels. She was transfused 2 units of PRBCs as above. # UTI: urine culture grew > 100,000 colonies/ml of pansensitive citrobacter. Given hypotension she was treated with a 7 day course of ceftriaxone for complicated UTI due to presence of foley catheter. # CKD: recently baseline of 1.8-1.9. Creatinine did increase slightly with diuresis, peaking at 2.1, but improved to 1.3 post volume resuscitation with blood products. # CAD: continued aspirin, atorvastatin # Hypothyroidism: continued levothyroxine Transitional Issues: ==================== Transitional Issues: - discharge weight 105.8 kg/233 lb - torsemide 20mg BID at discharge. When euvolemic, can transition to 20mg daily - metoprolol uptitrated from 25mg daily to 25mg Q6h, and home amiodarone was stopped - should check daily CBCs to monitor for ongoing blood loss - check BID electrolytes. K should be repleted to 4 and Mg to 2 - please check daily INRs. Heparin drip can be discontinued when INR therapeutic for two consecutive days - would recommend outpatient screening colonoscopy given recent GI bleed - needs to follow up in electrophysiology clinic in 1 month with Dr. ___ regarding pacemaker upgrade. We were unable to schedule this appointment before discharge. Please assist patient with scheduling. Phone number is ___
summary: You came in with shortness of breath. This was likely from too much fluid in the lungs. After the fluid was removed your breathing improved. You also had episodes of an abnormal heart rhythm. We increased your metoprolol to help prevent more of these episodes. You were also evaluated by the electrophysiology doctors. ___ felt that upgrading your pacemaker might help your heart function. You can see them in clinic as an outpatient. You also had some blood in your stools while you were on a blood thinner called heparin. The GI doctors did not think you needed an emergency colonoscopy, but you should consider having a screening colonoscopy as an outpatient. This is recommended for everyone over age ___. Your weight at discharge was 233 lb. You should try to limit your salt intake, and try not to drink more than 2 liters of fluid a day. You should weigh yourself daily, and call Dr. ___ your weight increases by more than 3 pounds.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ year old male with history of CAD s/p CABGx3 and multiple PCI's, CHF with EF 30%, diastolic and systolic HF, CRI, HTN, now presenting with SOB likely ___ CHF. Pt was intubated in ED and sent to the MICU. He was extubated the following day and transferred out to the Cardiology floor. # Respiratory distress: Respiratory distress likely combination of COPD and CHF, but more CHF given bilateral lower exttremity edema, CXR finding of fluid overload, and overload on exam initially. Mr. ___ has a known LVEF of 25% based on ECHO here. Patient was intubated in the ED and transferred to the MICU. He was much improved the following day and was extubated successfully. He was treated with IV Furosemide during this time. He was transferred to the Cardiology Service and was placed on a Lasix drip for further diuresis. Given his new onset worsening left ventricular function, he was sent for cardiac cath which was significant for 3VD and is now s/p stenting of ostial and mid RCA with DES and POBA to ostial PDA. #CHF: Systolic acute on chronic CHF exacerbation as above. Patient was to continue carvedilol 12.5 mg BID, isosorbide dinitrate 20mg TID. Furosemide was incresed to 80mg BID . #CAD: CABG x 3 in ___ (LIMA-LAD, SVG-OM, SVG-PDA) with only LIMA-LAD patent multiple PCI's and multiple stents placed. Patient has tropopin leak up to 0.25 up from 0.03. This was thought to be due to demand ischemia as CK levels were not elevated. Patient was sent for Cardiac Cath as above. He is to continue home regimen of clopidogrel 75mg daily, ASA 325mg daily, simvastatin 80mg daily, isosorbide dinitrate 20mg TID. Pt started on Carvedilol 12.5 mg BID. # Rhythm: Atrial fibrillation: Pt not anticoagulated ___ massive GI bleed; rate controlled only with nondihydropyridine nifedipine at home. Switched to carvedilol this admission per cardiology. Patient was monitored for bronchospasm given hx of COPD. He did not have any adverse reaction and was discharged on Carvedilol for management of his A-fib and CHF. # COPD: Pt has known obstructive lung disease ___ extensive smoking history. He is to continue on his home Combivent. . # CRI: Baseline Cr (1.7-2.2), now elevated to 2.6 and remained there upon discharge. ACE-I was held and will be restarted by Dr. ___ in clinic if kidney function improves. . # HTN: Patient is to continue Carvedilol, Isosorbide dinitrate, Amlodipine # Diabetes mellitus: Cont home glipizide . # Dyslipidemia: Continued simvastatin 80 daily. # Phase ___lock: Patient has been seen by Dr. ___ ___ placement. This should be follow up by his PCP.
summary: You were admitted into ___ treatment of your Congestive Heart Failure. You were in severe respiratory distress on arrival and you were intubated and placed on a breathing machine for 24 hours. Your heart failure has been treated successfully with Intravenous Diuretics. An Ultrasound of the heart was done which showed worsening heart function. A cardiac catheterization was done to evaluate your arteries. You had a new occlusion of your right coronary artery which was opened with a drug eluting stent. A balloon was also used to open up a second artery. Please stop taking your Lisinopril for the time being. Your kidney function has slightly worsened with the diuresis and you should not take your Lisinopril as it may contribute to worsening kidney function. Your kidney function will be reevaluated by Dr. ___ at your visit with him. Your Lasix has been increased from Lasix 60mg daily to Lasix 80mg twice per day.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ M PMHx SLL/CLL w pulmonary and sinus involvement s/p chemotherapy, hypogammaglobulinemia w prior recurrent Hflu on ppx with doxycycline, history of recurrent pneumonias, atrial fibrillation on Coumadin and amiodarone, transferred to ___ from ___ with fever, hypotension. # Hypotension/Cough/Wheezing: Viral syndrome vs PNA with hypotension due to sepsis versus volume depletion. Initially on broad spectrum antibiotics and oseltamivir. Flu swab negative, and no clear consolidation on imaging. Only minimal continued sputum production and no systemic symptoms. Most likely viral process i.e. viral bronchitis. s/p aztrenonam/vanc/cipro, but these were stopped as cultures have shown no growth. Patient was given nebulizers prn for symptomatic relief, given history of asthma, which may be exacerbated by viral infection. We restarted home doxycycline (Hflu PPX) ___. Patient was sat-ing well on room air. # Right Upper Extremity Swelling: Patient with diffuse swelling of R upper extremity, vascular u/s did not demonstrate e/o of DVT; this was attributed to infiltrated IV and treated conservatively w good response. # Nonischemic Cardiomyopathy: Patient had ECHO at ___ showing improvement with EF 35-40 %. His weight per webOMR was 152 in ___ with admission weight of 176 lbs. He reports feeling "puffy" and fluid overloaded. There was a concern for CHF given recent fluid resucitation and mild signs of overload on exam. Ischemia was felt to be unlikely at ___ with admission ECG at ___ showing known LBBB. However, we diuresed patient with torsemide with good effect. # Paroxysmal Atrial Fibrillation: He attempted to get out of bed on ___ and was told his HR was in the 140s; he was in afib with RVR. The RVR resolved with metoprolol, and his home metoprolol was uptitrated from 25mg succinate to 12.5mg tartrate TID. He was in sinus rhythm following that episode. Coumadin was restarted (it had been held due to concern for causing a supratherapeutic INR while the patient was on antibiotics). Amiodarone was continued. # Diarrhea: Pt developed diarrhea s/p broad spectrum antibiotics. Cdiff negative. Resolved without intervention. # Hypertension: Held lisinopril in setting of acute illness. # Anemia: HCT stable after drop from 35 on ___ to 29 on ___, 31 on ___. No active bleeding source seen. He was likely hemoconcentrated on admission with artifical decrease after hydration. A secondary consideration would be AIHA from CLL, but labs not c/w hemolysis. # CLL/SLL: Status post chemotherapy. Counts were largely reassuring overall. Monitored counts as inpatient. # Hematoma at IJ Site / Subcutaneous Emphysema: Seen on chest CT and likely from attempted IJ placement x 3 at ___. Improved during admission. # NEW Pleural Thickening Seen on CT: Noncon chest CT should be obtained in 6 months. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES OUT OF ICU ** new pleural thickening deep in pulmonary sulcus on the right, a noncontrast chest CT should be obtained in 6 months for follow up (roughly ___ - patient was full code during this admission
summary: You were admitted to the hospital with cough, fever, and low blood pressure. Your blood pressure came up to normal after we gave you IV fluids. Our CT scan showed that you did not have a pneumonia. You tested negative for the flu. We think you had a viral bronchitis. Your symptoms were improving at the time of discharge. We also noticed you had some weight gain which was likely due to all the fluids you received to take care of your low blood pressure. The last 2 days of your hospital stay we gave you torsemide to help your body excrete some of the extra fluid. We saw an area of thickening on the outside of the lung when we did the CT scan. It is very likely this is nothing concerning.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Mr. ___ is a ___ with a PMH significant for HTN, atrial fibrillation, mitral and tricuspid valve regurgitation and repair of both ~2 months prior, CHF, and gout evaluated due to a supertherapeutic INR of 4.8, 8 weeks of downtrending Hct, and joint pain. He was admitted due to his above issues as well as pericardial effusion and anemia found on initial evaluation. Workup notable for a hemolytic anemia (likely mechanical due to recent valve repair), moderate-sized pericardial effusion without tamponade physiology, and no evidence of UTI. No hemodynamic instability or lab work consistent with DIC. Hemoglobin stabilized prior to discharge, and supra therapeutic INR resolved after correction with Vitamin K and holding warfarin for several days (restarted prior to discharge). Folate/iron/B complex vitamins provided for his anemia. Given patient continued to complain of moderate left ankle joint on day of discharge, he was discharged on an extra 5 days of colchicine 0.6 mg PO BID. ================= TRANSITIONAL ISSUES ================= # CODE: presumed full # CONTACT: cousin ___ # ___ # Patient will need repeat echocardiogram in 7 days and then frequency to be determined thereafter. THIS NEEDS TO BE SCHEDULED PLEASE. # MEDICATION CHANGES: - Added colchicine (0.6mg daily), to be continued for a total of 6 months. - Added ferrous sulfate 325mg daily. - Discontinued furosemide. Can be restarted if clinically volume overloaded. - To continue taking warfarin indefinitely given prior Afib and anticoagulation risk. Start with 1mg daily, titrate to goal INR ___. # FOLLOW-UP LABS: - Please monitor CBC once weekly to ensure stability and evaluate for worsening hemolysis. - Please check INR, goal ___. # WARFARIN DOSING: Came in supratherapeutic on warfarin 2mg daily. Please titrate to goal ___ as above. Discharge weight: 84.8 kg Discharge Hg: 7.7 Discharge Cr: 0.9 Discharge INR: 1.4
summary: You were seen at our hospital because you had joint pain and high levels of your blood thinner, as well as some signs of low blood counts ("anemia"). Your joint pain was likely due to another gout flare; we added a new medicine ("colchicine") which helped your gout. Your low blood counts were related to your recent heart valve repair; we gave you a small amount of blood, and you did not have any active blood loss before you went home. We held your blood thinner ("warfarin") for a few days given some concern for blood loss, but restarted it before you left. You also had some fluid around your heart ("pericardial effusion") that we saw on a picture of your heart. This fluid was not making your heart worse, and not related to bleeding. This can happen after heart surgery, and the heart surgeons believe that it will likely resolve on its own. Initially we thought you had a urinary tract infection, based on a test of your urine. Repeat testing did not show an infection. We briefly gave you antibiotics, but stopped them as you did not appear to have an infection.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ with HTN, diastolic CHF, COPD, ___, who was recently admitted from ___ for healthcare associated pneumonia, admitted from his SNF for acute onset SOB and chest pain. . # SOB: The acute onset of SOB suggests flash pulmonary edema, possibly in the setting of hypertension. An aspiration event is also possible considering that ___ had just eaten. The CXR was suggestive of pulmonary edema, as well as chronic pulmonary fibrosis. ___ continues to have a mildly productive cough, but has been afebrile, w/o leukocytosis, so his pneumonia appears to have been adequately treated. ___ was not tachycardic, and there was low clinical suspicion for PE. ___ was diuresed with IV lasix with improvement in his symptoms, and ___ is ___ on 2L at rest (pt discharged from last admission to rehab on 2L NC). ___ is still mildly SOB when ambulating. Much of his SOB is likely due to his underlying COPD and deconditioning. Would consider another swallowing evaluation should ___ appear to be aspirating. . # DIASTOLIC CHF: Recent echo showed a dilated left atrium, markedly dilated right atrium, mild symmetric LVH, LVEF 70%, and mild MR. ___ was mildly volume overloaded on exam, was diuresed with IV lasix 80mg BID and is now euvolemic. ___ was discharged on an increased dose of lasix (80mg PO daily) which can be adjusted by his rehab physicians; consider increase to bid dosing if weight rising. Discharge weight is 101.4 kg. . # PNEUMONIA: Completed course of vancomycin on ___ and completed course of cefepime on ___. ___ has been afebrile and w/o leukocytosis. . # COPD: Continued albuterol and ipratropium nebs. . # CHEST PAIN: His pain is reproducible on exam and is worse with coughing and movement, suggesting a musculoskeletal etiology. Three sets of cardiac enzymes were flat at 0.12-0.13 (patient with ___ and EKG was unchanged from prior. His pain was well controlled with tylenol. . # HTN: Continued imdur and tamsulosin. On previous discharge, atenolol 50mg daily had been changed to metoprolol 12.5mg tid, and lisinopril 20mg daily and valsartan 80 mg daily held. On this admission, metoprolol was increased to 25mg TID and changed back to outpatient atenolol 50mg daily dose on discharge given good BP control. We also restarted lisinopril at 5mg daily, which can be uptitrated prn. We continued to hold valsartan which can be resarted as needed for blood pressure control. . # HLD: Chol 134, Trig 156, HDL 29, LDL 74. Continued atorvastatin 20mg daily. . # ANEMIA: Secondary to ___. Hct was stable at ___, which appears to be his baseline. Resumed Aranesp 80 mcg weekly upon discharge. . # ___ Stage IV: Creatinine has been stable at his baseline ~2.5-2.9. . # DIABETES: Monitored via FSBS and regular ISS. Blood sugars were well controlled. . # H/o CVA: Patient with residual loss of fine motor function in his left hand but no other deficits. Continue plavix and statin. . # CODE STATUS: DNR/DNI, confirmed with the patient's son. # CONTACT INFO: Son (HCP): ___ ___
summary: You were admitted with shortness of breath. This is likely due to several things including mild exacerbations of your congestive heart failure and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease. It is also possible that you aspirated some food. You have finished the course of antibiotics for your pneumonia and have remained afebrile. Please eat your food slowly and discuss with your physician about getting another swallow evaluation if you cough after eating or have any difficulty swallowing food. For your congestive heart failure, you should restrict yourself to a 2 gram sodium diet and 1.5 L (6 cups) of fluids daily. Your weight should be monitored daily. Should your weight go up by more than 3 lbs or you have increased oxygen requirement, your lasix dose may need to be adjusted by your rehab physician.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ year old woman with a history of possible CHF, HTN, and arthritis with R knee replacement one week ago who presents with hypoxia and dyspnea on exertion. *Acute Issues # Hypoxia/DOE: Patient underwent right knee replacement at ___. Since the surgery patient noted shortness of breath with exertion or talking. She was able to breath comfortably at rest. She notes that her oxygen saturation dipped to the high ___ wih moderate exertion. Patient's initial ABG on admission showed elevated Aa gradient but repeat ABG on ___ after exertion showed normal Aa gradient. CXR was negative for pnuemonia/effusions. V/Q and CTA was negative for PE as well as pneumonia or other primary lung process. No interstitial changes noted on the imaging. Patient has a questional history of diastolic heart failure, and was very gently diuresed to attempt improvement in hypoxia, though there were no convincing signs on exam or labs (low BNP) to suggest decompensated HF/fluid overload. ECHO unremarkable other than focal apical right ventricular hypokinesis of uncertain origin with no sign of pulmonary edema on CXR. ECHO with bubble study notable for PFO which is only significant during valsalva maneuver. PFT showed mild decrease in FVC and DsbHb, but pulmonology felt it was not significant cause patient's hypoxemia, and results were essentially within normal limits. On the day of discharge, patient underwent cardiac stress testing and showed average exercise tolerance with no drop in O2 saturation with exercise. Patient did no desaturate with valsalva manuever. Patient not discharged with oxygen given symptomatic improvement and no objective evidence of desaturation. # CHF: EF 65% but with new area of right ventricular hypokinesis, however, chamber size normal without dilation. Patient was briefly diuresed with 20mg IV Lasix x1, and her weight was down from prior to admission. This did not significantly change her oxygenation status. Her home medications including pravastatin, lisinopril, and home lasix 20mg daily were continued. *Chronic/Stable Issues # Anemia: Most recent Hct at 37.4. Hct on admission remains somewhat lower than her baseline of 41.2. Denies BRBPR or dark stools; last colonoscopy in ___ with Grade 1 internal hemorrhoids, diverticulosis, polyp s/p polypectomy. Stools guaiac negative. Likely had some blood loss related to her knee surgery. Borderline low serum iron but normal ferritin were found. # Osteoarthritis s/p knee replacement. Healing well. Warfarin continued with INR therapeutic to prevent DVT. ___ was consulted and saw the patient for post-surgical training/mobility. Home oxycontin and oxycodone were continued for pain. # GERD: Chronic, continued with pantoprazole. # Glaucoma: Chronic, continued with Fluorometholone and Latanoprost *Transitional Issues: None.
summary: You were admitted due to shortness of breath and low oxygen saturation. You had a CT scan of the chest which showed no pulmonary embolism, or clot, as well as showing no sign of pneumonia or other active lung process. You had an EKG which showed some nonspecific T wave flattening. You had an echocardiogram which showed your heart function was normal, except one area in the right ventricle that was not pumping as well as the rest of the muscle. You had pulmonary function tests which showed mild decrease in lung function. You had a "bubble study" which showed a small opening in the atrium of your heart (Patent Foramen Ovale). We did not think that these results were significant contributors to your low oxygen levels when walking. You received a dose of IV Lasix to help remove fluid in case this shortness of breath could be related to accumulating fluid from heart failure. Your blood gases normalized and your oxygen saturation with ambulation improved. You had a cardiopulmonary stress test which showed your oxygenation status to be excellent, and there was no desaturation with exercise. You were therefore discharged without need for oxygen. The transient desaturation was likely related to sub-radiographic clots within the lungs, that have resolved at this point.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ y/o male with a history of multiple UTIs and renal stones with incomplete voiding and intermittent catheterization at home presents with fevers chills and new afib with RVR found to have an obstructing stone on CTA/P. #Urosepsis: Patient with h/o UTIs treated with doxy/keflex at home and recurrent fevers/chills with new tachycardia elevated WBC and CTU with hydronephrosis and obstructing stone suggesting a urinary source. Patient had a right-sided primary percutaneous nephrostomy placed on ___. UCx and blood cultures grew Klebsiella. Patient was initially treated with meropenem and switched to IV ceftriaxone on sensitivities returned. He was then converted to IV cipro and PO upon discharge to complete a 2 week course of therapy from the date of last urine culture positivity (last day of rx. ___ unless otherwise directed by urology, Dr. ___. Pt with follow up with urology within 2 week's time and keep his nephrostomy tube in place until then. He had a foley catheter while inpatient but converted to his home regimen of self-cath 5x daily upon discharge. #Atrial Fib/Flutter with RVR: No prior history and likely ___ to urosepsis above. CHADS score of 2 for HTN and DM. Cardiology consulted and patient was placed on PO metoprolol 25 mg q6 hours with adequate rate control, and ultimately converted to sinus rhythm. Transitioned to metoprolol succinate 200 mg daily. Patient was started on warfarin. INR was 1.9 on discharge with instructions for him to continue current dose. # ___ on CKD III - Patient has baseline Cr of 1.3-1.4. On admission was 2.0 and improved to 1.5 by discharge. #sCHF: EF 40-45%. Etiology unclear but may be ___ tachycardia-induced cardiomyopathy vs ischemic cardiomyopathy vs sepsis; in discussion with cardiology this may have been underestimated EF given non sinus rhythm during echocardiogram. Patient was continued on metoprolol and given lasix, but latter stopped when creatinine began to rise again with diuresis. #HTN: Had been on metoprolol tartrate 25 mg PO BID at home for tachycardia but not Afib/flutter - adjusted as above given new afib/flutter. #HLD: continued home meds #DM: stopped metformin given CKD and ? ___. Changed to Glipizide XL as recommended by ___ consultation. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: -full code -should have repeat TTE in coming months to re-eval EF -will need to f/u within ___ d with urology as outpatient for definitive treatment of the stone -ongoing management of warfarin by primary MD with ___ measurement of INR (ordered, confirmed with primary MD that they will monitor by faxed results).
summary: You were treated for a blood stream infected related to a kidney and bladder infection. You were also found to have kidney stone causing a blockage. For this, you were treated with antibiotics which you will need to continue to complete a 2 week course of therapy. You had a "nephrostomy tube" placed to help your kidney drain and this will need to remain in place until your evaluation with Dr. ___. You were found to have a fast irregular heart rate called "atrial flutter" when you were in the ICU. You were evaluated by the cardiologists who recommended that you start a blood thinner (coumadin) and a medication called metoprolol to slow down your heart rate.

text: Brief Hospital Course: # Systolic Heart Failure: The patient was admitted to the floor to rule out an acute MI and initiate a work-up for her heart failure. Her cardiac enzymes remained negative throughout the rule-out. An echocardiogram was performed which demonstrated that the patient has an ejection fraction that is lower normal (LVEF 50%), and increased left ventricular filling pressures and pulmonary artery systolic hypertension consistent with congestive heart failure. The patient was diuresed with multiple doses of IV lasix until her lung exam improved. Her lower extremity edema slowly improved over the course of her hospitalization. The patient was encouraged to keep her legs elevated and to wear compression stockings to improve redistribution of the fluid in her legs. Additionally, the patient was advised to do frequent leg exercises when sitting in her chair. During the course of her admission, her lisinopril/hydrochlorothiazide combination pill was held throughout admission for mildly low blood pressures. We attempted to start a beta-blocker, but were unable to administer the medication because her systolic blood pressure was in the 100s - 110s throughout her stay. The patient was stable for discharge home with ___ services on hospital day five. She was discharged on PO lasix and lisinopril, and told not to resume her lisinopril/HCTZ pill until following up with her primary care doctor and new cardiologist, Dr. ___ provided). The patient should further discuss with her cardiologist the possibility of starting a beta blocker. # Hematuria: The patient had a Foley placed for strict monitoring of her input and output. After approximately 48 hours, the patient had macroscopic hematuria. A urinalysis was sent which showed red blood cells in the urine. This was thought to be due to trauma from the Foley catheter. She should ___ with her primary care physician to ensure resolution of this hematuria. # Hyponatremia: Likely related to fluid overload. Resolved after IV lasix.
summary: You were admitted to ___ on ___ with fluid overload because your heart is not pumping effectively. Electrocardiograms of your heart were performed and were consistent with a prior heart attack some time in the past. Ultrasound of the heart confirmed that it was not pumping as well as prior. We gave you medications to help with your fluid overload. Your breathing symptoms had improved at the time of discharge. It may take some time for the swelling in your legs to improve. You should keep your legs elevated as much as possible. You should also wear your compression stockings to help reduce the swelling. While you were in the hospital, you had a temporary catheter to help collect your urine. Your urine tests showed that you may have had some trauma from that foley catheter. You should have your urine rechecked when you see your primary care provider to ensure you no longer have blood in the urine.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ year-old man with a history of aortic stenosis s/p Core valve ___, dilated cardiomyopathy (EF 30%), CAD s/p CABG ___, Type 2 Diabetes, HTN, non-hodgkins lymphoma, s/p permanent pacer placement ___ who presents with dyspnea, found to be obtunded in the ED, intubated, and admitted to the CCU for further management, clinically improved and transferred to the floor on ___. # Respiratory failure: The patient presented to ___ ED lethargic and was intubated for airway protection. Etiology of his presentation was likely due to underlying CHF with superimposed altered mental status causing a decreased respiratory drive. Lethargy and CHF managed as below. Patient had an infectious work-up that was negative. At the time discharge, the patient is breathing comfortably on room air. # Acute on chronic congestive heart failure: Prior to admission, patient's most recent ECHO in ___ showed EF 25%. On admission, patient had evidence of volume overload on exam, laboratory findings notable for BNP doubled from prior, and chest X-ray with pulmonary edema. The etiology of his CHF exacerbation was thought likely secondary to underdiuresis. He was diuresed successfully with IV furosemide, after which he was switched to oral lasix. He is being discarged on furosemide 20mg PO daily. His home metoprolol was increased from 50mg to 75mg daily. # Hypoglycemia: Patient presented with fasting hypoglycemia to glucose 40-50s requiring dextrose boluses. The Endocrinology team was consulted and confirmed that hypoglycemia was secondary due to decreased excretion of home glimeperide in the setting of worsening renal function. As the patient was extubated and started eaint, patient's hypoglycemia resolved. At the time of discharge, blood glucose ranged from 100-150s off insulin/home diabetes medications. He is being discharged home off glimeperide and other antihyperglycemic agents. Per ___ consultant recommendations, the patient can consider sitagliptin or Trajenta as an outpatient as needed. # Altered mental status: The patient presented with Likely secondary to hypoglycemia and now resolved with resolution of hypoglycemia. Infectious workup negative. # Acute kidney injury: Patient was found to have an elevated creatinine to 2.3 on admission from a baseline of 1.3. This was likely secondary to cardio-renal syndrome/poor forward flow. After diuresis, patient's creatinine improved and was 1.2 on the day of discharge. # Hypotension: On admission, the patient was hypotensive in the setting of hypoglycemia and sedation briefly required vasopressor support. This resolved after cessation of sedating medications and normalization of blood sugars. # Atrial fibrillation s/p ablation: The patient remained predominantly A-paced throughout this admission. He had an episode of atrial fibrillation ___ overnight with overlying pacemaker firing that resolved spontaneously. The patient was continued on home amiodarone, metoprolol was increased to 75mg daily. Warfarin was held in the setting of supratherapeutic INR and restarted at the time of discharge. # Critical Aortic Stenosis s/p Transaortic valve replacement: Stable. Patient was continued on his home aspirin and clopidogrel. # Coronary Artery Disease status post PCI & CABG: Stable. The patient was continued on his home ontinue aspirin and clopidogrel. # Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus: In the setting of fasting hypoglycemia (see above), patient's home antihyperglycemics were held. Per above, the patient was not discharged on any agents. # Hypothyroidism: Stable. Patient was continued on his home levothyroxine. ===================== TRANSITIONAL ISSUES ===================== - Glimepiride discontinued on discharge - Can consider starting sitagliptin - Lasix increased to 20mg daily on discharge - Metoprolol increased to 75 daily - Will need INR check and chemistries on ___ - Also needs EP eval for pacer management as outpatient given intermittent tachyarrhythmias while in house
summary: You were admitted for shortness of breath and was found to have an exacerbation of your heart failure. You required a breathing machine to help you breathe initially. We gave you medicines to remove fluid from your lungs and supported your blood pressures with medicines. You improved over time and we were able to remove the breathing tube. You were also found to have low blood sugars, so we discontinued your glimepiride. You should not take this medication at home until you discuss it further with your endocrinologist. Additionally, we have increased your lasix dose to 20mg daily and your metoprolol dose to 75. You will need to have your INR and chemistry panel checked on ___ at your primary care provider's office.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Mr ___ is a ___ yo M with persistent AF (s/p cardioversion in ___ on Eliquis), HTN, type II DM, CVA L ___, gout, gunshot wound/lung injury and ___ filter placed in ___ who presented to PCP office on ___ with increasing shortness of breath and lower extremity edema, along with increased weight and transferred from ___ for cardiac catheterization. #Acute on chronic systolic heart failure. Patient has low-normal EF on most recent echo with considerable beat-to-beat variability of the left ventricular ejection fraction due to an irregular rhythm/premature beats. LVEF at OSH was reported as being low. Patient had pleural effusion tapped at OSH; findings were consistent with CHF. Patient presented volume overloaded and with an O2 requirement and CO2 retention. Diuresis was gradually performed with IV lasix. Patient was discharged on 80 mg PO lasix in the morning and 40 mg PO lasix in the evening. The patient was discharged with home oxygen given desaturation with ambulation and this should be weaned gradually over time. #Chest wall hematoma Patient presented with very large R lower back chest wall hematoma s/p thoracentesis at OSH. Hemoglobin was relatively low (7.7) with possible active bleed and he was given 1unit of PRBCs. He had appropriate response to Hgb 9.4, but then dropped to 8.1 within less than a day. Repeat H/H were stable. CTA chest showed large hematoma but no evidence of active extravasation. Patient should have CBC check at time of follow up as outpatient. # Afib: CHADS 4. s/p failed cardioversion. Apixaban held initially given hematoma and H/H drop as above until reassured that no more active bleeding occurring. It was restarted at time of discharge. #Thrombocytopenia Patient noted to be thrombocytopenic on admission to the 130's with elevated INR and low albumin with some suspicion for cirrhosis. Patient should be considered for outpatient cirrhosis work up. Platelets normalized prior to discharge. # HTN: continued home losartan # BPH: continued home tamsulosin and doxazosin # Diabetes: Continued ISS. # Gout: continued allopurinol Transitional issues: -patient noted to have anemia this hospitalization. Please check CBC at time of follow up (Hg 9.4 on day of discharge) -Lasix 80 mg every morning and 40 mg every evening started this hospitalization -please check chem-7 to monitor electrolytes and renal function at time of follow up -patient discharged with home O2 at time of ambulation given desaturation to 85% and should be weaned over time -- consider outpatient ___ given thrombocytopenia, low albumin, and elevated INR (all signs of synthetic liver dysfunction)
summary: You were transferred to this hospital for cardiac catheterization to evaluate the causes of your heart failure. We did not find significant blockages in the arteries that supply your heart. When you were admitted we also noted that you had a large area of swelling on your back where you had previously had a procedure to remove fluid from your lungs. We monitored your blood counts, which dropped some, and gave you a transfusion. We temporarily stopped giving you Eliquis, because it can increase the risk of bleeding. In addition, we gave you lasix to help remove extra fluid from your body. It is very important that you continue to take these. All of your medications are detailed in your discharge medication list.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Mr. ___ is a ___ man with a h/o remote anterior wall MI with reduced LV function for many years (EF ~30%), who presented with an episode of sustained VT leading to PEA arrest. He was stabilized prior to transfer from ___ and arrived on amiodarone drip for further evaluation. . # Sustained VT followed by PEA arrests: This was thought to be ___ scar from old MI's. CE elevation (TnT peak 2.05, CKMB peak 80) was thought to be most likely ___ chest compressions in the field. Heparin was started given ddx including ACS (small STE in V1-3 and elevated TnT as above). Pt was also loaded with plavix. Catheterization showed no new blockages and ECHO showed old LV dysfunction. PE was also considered as a possibility as an etiology but was thought to be less likely. Patient had no evidence of DVT on ___ imaging. Heparin gtt and plavix were discontinued after cardiac cath showed no evidence of new ischemic disease to account for PEA arrest. An ICD was placed on ___. Pt was transitioned to PO amiodarone. . #CAD/CHF: As above, there was no evidence of new ischemia. Pt's statin was changed to atorvastatin 40 mg in setting of amiodarone use. Aspirin and carvedilol were coNtinued. Nitro patch was held on arrival. Pt was also started on captopril for CHF. Echo showed: severe regional left ventricular systolic dysfunction with akinesis of the anterior wall, septum and apex (c/w prior LAD infarction). LVEF = ___ with moderate MR. ___ was started on spironolactone 12.5 daily. Lasix should be every other day instead of daily before admission for worsened renal failure. Weight at discharge is 142 pounds. . # PNA: On ___, pt was noted to be febrile to 100.8 with some evidence of confusion/delirium. There were no focal neurological deficits, blood and urine CX were sent but there was no evidence of UTI on UA. Pt received olanzapine 5 mg SL x 1 for delerium. CXR was concerning for pulmonary edema with hypoxemia and pt was diuresed with Lasix IV. Given some concern for possible aspiration PNA, Levofloxacin was started for a 7d course to treat aspiration PNA (___). . # Atrial Fibrillation: Pt was noted to have one brief episode of atrial fibrillation but reverted spontaneously to sinus rhythm. This was thought to be in the setting of electrolyte abnormality (hypokalemia) and did not recur. He was started on anticoagulation with warfarin. . # Acute Kidney injury: Cr was elevated during admission, this was thought to be ___. FeNA (<1%) was suggestive of pre-renal process but FeUN (55) suggested intra renal process (and pt was on diuretics at the time). There were trace leukocytes and rare eosinophils on UA. Cr peaked at 1.8. Day of discharge is 1.7. . # Hyponatremia: Pt was noted to be hyponatremic with Na of 129 during the hospitalization. This was thought to be ___ CHF and pt was diuresed with lasix IV, with improvement in Na. . # HYPERLIPIDEMIA: Switched simvastatin to atorvastatin 40mg in the setting of amiodarone use. . #ESOPHAGEAL REFLUX: Continued Omeprazole 40 mg daily . # BPH with urinary rention: foley placed on admission with failed voiding trial in CCU with 1L of urine noted in bladder. Foley was replaced and repeat voiding trial approx 3d later again failed. Talked with urology who reported pt needs to maintain foley at least 7d after retaining 1L of urine. Foley replaced ___ and should be kept in 7d. . TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: CODE STATUS: Full EMERGENCY CONTACT: ___ (wife) - Please note, on amiodarone, pt will need yearly TFT and CXR - Please continue Amiodarone PO 400 TID x 3d (___), 200 TID (___) and then transition to amiodarone 200mg po qd until follow-up with cardiology # FOLLOW-UP with PCP/cardiologist (not made) # Follow up in device clinic for ICD (made) # Follow-up with OP urologist approx ___ for voiding trial, this appt has not been made due to holiday weekend.
summary: You had an episode of a dangerous heart rhythm called ventricular tachycardia which then led to a cardiac arrest. You developed a small pneumonia during this arrest. An internal cardiac defibrillator was placed which will shock your heart out of this rhythm in the future. Call ___ if your ICD fires and you feel sick. A cardiac catheterization was done which did not show any change in the blockages in your heart arteries. You also had some fluid buildup in your lungs that was treated with diuretic medicines. Your kidneys were worse after the cathterization but are improving now. When the foley catheter was removed, you were unable to urinate. The foley was replaced and you will need to keep it in until ___. Dr. ___ office is making an appt with a urologist for you to see around that time.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Patient is a ___ y/o ___ F, h/o CHF, PAF on Coumadin, HTN, Asthma, CRF, DM comes in with worsening SOB and CP since discharged on ___. . DYSPNEA: Pt has a history of CHF, Asthma and a-fib all of which could have been contributing to her shortness of breath. Pt had elevated BP on arrival to the hospital likely exacerbating her a-fib and making her more short of breath. Initial blood pressures were 170/80s while on a nitro gtt. She was placed on her home medications for her asthma and initially given albuterol nebulizer treatments Q6hrs, which were later increased to Q4hrs to help control her dyspnea. She was restarted on Nifedipine for blood pressure control. Pt was given lasix for fluid overload seen on radiologic imaging and physicial exam. Initially she was diuresed with Lasix 80mg IV daily. (Her home dose was Lasix 80mg daily). She was diursed over 3L of fluid and then creatinine increased to 3.1 from 2.4. Lasix was held and should be restarted at a dose of 40mg PO daily once creatinine has stabilized. Her Nifedipine should continue. She was not restarted on an ACE inhibitor (was on Enalapril 20mg PO BID prior to last hospitalization) due to elevated creatinine during this hospitalization, but once creatinine is stable, this could be trialed. Blood pressure was well controlled during the remainder of her hospitalization. She should continue on Lasix 40mg PO daily on discharge. . ATRIAL FIBRILLATION: The patient was in atrial fibrillation on amiodarone on admission. She was successfully cardioverted on ___. She was continued on coumadin. Initially she was subtherapeutic with an INR of 1.5 and her coumadin dose was continued at the same level. She was bridged with lovenox until INR was 2.3 and it was then discontinued. Her INR should be checked in 2 days. Continue amiodarone. . CORONARY ARTERY DISEASE: Patient was continued on beta-blocker, statin and aspirin. Cardiac enzymes were negative and ECG unchanged. . ASTHMA: She was continued on Fluticasone and given nebulizers. . DIABETES MELLITUS: Oral hypoglycemics were held during admission and she was given insulin for her diabetes. Oral medications were resumed on discharge. . CHRONIC KIDNEY DISEASE: Creatinine ranged from 2.5-3.1 during this admission. She was initially diuresed and then IV lasix was stopped. She was placed on PO Lasix on discharge. She should have creatinine checked in 2 days. . CLOUDY URINE: UA was contaminated. Urine culture pending at the time of discharge. . ANEMIA: She was continued on B12 and iron. HCT was stable during admission. . TO FOLLOW-UP ON: 1. Check Electrolytes in 2 days to assess renal function while on lasix. 2. Assess cardiopulmonary status to determine if she needs additional lasix. 3. Continue coumadin at current dose, check INR in 2 days. 4. Urinalysis pending at the time of discharge. Please repeat UA if symptomatic or patient is having fevers.
summary: You are being discharged from ___ ___. You were admitted for SOB and chronic palpitations. You were last discharged on the ___ and you stated that your symptoms have just gotten worse since that admission. You presented to us with very high BP and we adjusted your medication in order to bring your blood pressure into better control. We also noted that you were still in a-fib and that the treatments you have been receiving were not adequate at this time. The next step in therapy is cardioversion in order to stop the atrial fibrillation and bring your heart back to a normal rhythm. You received this cardioversion on ___. There were many changes to your medications at your last admission and we modified your medications again.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ yo M with history of sCHF (EF 40% in ___, HTN, CAD (RCA stent in ___ and NSCLC (lobectomy ___ presented to ___ on ___ with progressively worsening SOB, ankle edema and 10 pound weight gain, found to have decreasing EF to <20% and biventricular concentric hypertrophy, concerning for amyloidosis. Transferred to ___ for cardiac catheterization. # Acute on chronic sCHF- Worsening SOB and DOE over past two week. Acutely decreased EF to ~20%. Also having newly discovered atrial flutter at office visit at end of ___. Potential etiologies include amyloid, tachycardia-induced cardiomyopathy, new ischemia. Cardiac enzymes negative at OSH. Repeat ECHO at ___ showed EF 30% with biventricular concentric hypertrophy and speckled pattern concerning for amyloid. Cardiac catheterization showed concordance of LV and RV pressures and nonequalization of pressures. At presentation, patient states cannot ambulate to his bathroom without SOB. Patient treated symptomatically with diuresis with 40-80mg IV lasix daily. Symptoms improved and patient can ambulate down the hall without SOB at discharge. OSH UPEP showed no monoclonal proteins or M spike. Mildly elevated kappa light chain but kappa/lamda ratio only 1.8 (upper limit normal 1.6). Consider outpatient Cardiac MRI to further eval for infiltrative process. Consider fat pad biopsy. Patient DC home on lasix, ASA, lisinopril, metoprolol. # RHYTHM: Patient in a-flutter. HR 90-100s at rest and up to 140s with activity. TEE showed sludge in the ___, so cardioversion not attempted. Will continue Pradaxa for 4 more weeks followed by elective cardioversion. DC home on Toprolol 100 daily and Pradaxa. Amiodarone initially started in the hospital but then stopped after TEE to prevent patient from converting to sinus rhythm with possible ___. # Elevated LFT: Patient with newly developed jaundice. Elevated LFT and mixed hyperbilirubinemia. Elevated INR. DDx- viral hepatitis, hepatic congestion ___ CHF, amyloid. Hepatitis serologies negative. ___ negative. Liver ultrasound with no focal lesions and no fatty liver. Follow up as outpatient. # Hypothyroidism- TSH elevated, T4 normal, free t3 mildly decreased, TPO negative. Holding off on levothyroxine in the setting of atrial flutter and tachycardia. # ___- Cr 1.4-1.5 on admission. Cr rise to 1.9 ___ aggressive diuresis. Discharging home on home dose lasix. Will recheck chem 7 on ___. # CORONARIES: RCA stent in ___. Cardiac cath on ___ showed patent RCA. Cath showed LAD and LCx with 50% stenosis, no need for intervention. Continue metoprolol, ASA 81, lisinopril. # HTN: SBP in 100-110s. Continue metoprolol, low dose lisinopril. # Transitional issues: - Outpatient follow up and eval of liver disease. - Consider treatment for hypothyroidism once converted to sinus rhythm. - Further workup of amyloid as outpatient. - Repeat Chem 7 to follow up Creatinine ___
summary: You were transferred to ___ for cardiac catheterization for evaluation of your heart failure. Your catheterization showed no significant coronary disease. Part of your heart failure is caused by your fast heart rate (a rhythm called atrial flutter). We had planned for a procedure called cardioversion to shock your heart back into regular rhythm. However, there is a clot in your heart which would increase the risk of stroke with this procedure. You will continue on anticoagulation with Pradaxa and plan for cardioversion in 4 weeks. The electrophysiology team will call you to schedule you for this procedure. Also, we found your thyroid hormone to be low. This can cause you to feel tired.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ with a history of 3-vessel CABG in ___, CHF, COPD, p/w acute orthopnea and SOB at 5am, p/w troponin elevation at 1.29, wo EKG changes, currently free of SOB and CP. Orthopnea/SOB: Most likely etiology was STEMI leading to ventricular wall stiffness leading to acute on chronic heart failure. The patient's symptoms probably resolved with heparin IV reducing the clot burden, Lasix diuresing excess fluid. We continued heparin gtt, continued ASA, switching from atenolol to metoprolol 12.5mg PO BID. Started atorvastatin 80mg PO Daily, continued his ___. Patient had mild volume overload given pedal edema, congestion on CXR, and elevated BNP. He did eventually develop Q waves in his precordial leads just prior to his catheterization. Patient went to cath lab which showed occluded LMCA, LAD with 90% stenosis proximal to graft touchdown site, and 70% stenosis distal to touchdown site. Cx was diffusely diseased and RCA was known to be occluded. A bare metal stent was placed in the proximal LAD, proximal to the graft touchdown point. He also underwent angioplasty of the LCx graft feeding the OM1, which was thought to supply the LAD territory. He did have a likely periprocedural MI with CKMB of 15 and MB index of 14 which started downtrending prior to discharge. He otherwise tolerated the procedure well. The patient remained free of chest pain, shortness of breath, or orthopnea for the remainder of the hospitalization. He will need to be on Aspirin and Plavix for life. HTN: Normotensive during the admission. We continued home antihypertensives. HLP: Excellent lipid panel. Continued high dose statin. COPD: No wheezing on exam, no cough, not SOB. Not active. Abdominal Aortic Aneurysm: No active issues. Controlled BP.
summary: You were transferred from an outside hospital after having some complaints of shortness of breath and chest discomfort. You were found to have had a heart attack. You were placed on continuous heart monitors and treated with blood thinning medications. You underwent a cardiac catheterization procedure which showed a blocked coronary vessel (Lateral Anterior Descending artery). This blockage was opened up by the placement of a cardiac stent. As outlined below, it is critically important that you continue to take daily aspirin and Plavix ( clopidogrel) therapy in order to prevent any clots from developing around the area of the stent.
